topics.title: Understanding the Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Understanding the Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Supply Chain (SC)** is a *systematic network of organizations, people, activities, information, and resources* involved in *moving a product or service from supplier to customer*. According to the Council of Supply Chain Management Professionals (CSCMP), a supply chain encompasses every effort involved in producing and delivering a final product, from the supplier's supplier to the customer's customer.

Mathematically, a supply chain can be represented as an *ordered set* of entities:

$$SC = \{E_1, E_2, E_3, \dots, E_n\}$$

where each $E_i$ represents an *independent business entity* (supplier, manufacturer, distributor, retailer, or end customer) engaged in transforming inputs into outputs of greater value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *"A supply chain is a network of interconnected businesses and processes that works together to source, produce, and deliver a product or service — from raw materials to the final consumer — encompassing suppliers, manufacturers, warehouses, transporters, retailers, and customers."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The River of Value"

Think of a supply chain as a **river system** flowing from mountain to ocean:

- The **mountain springs** are the *raw material suppliers* (farmers, miners).
- The **rivers and tributaries** are the *manufacturers and processors*.
- The **lakes and reservoirs** are the *warehouses and distribution centres*.
- The **canals** are the *transportation networks*.
- The **ocean** is the *end customer*.
- The **water itself** is the *product* in motion.
- The **current, rainfall data, and weather forecasts** are the *information flows* that guide the river.

Just as a river only delivers value to the ocean if all sections flow correctly, a supply chain only delivers value to the customer if every link is synchronized.

### 1.3 Key Participants in a Supply Chain

| # | Participant | Primary Role |
|---|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Suppliers** (1st tier) | Provide raw materials and components |
| 2 | **Suppliers' Suppliers** (2nd, 3rd tier) | Deep upstream source of inputs |
| 3 | **Manufacturers / Producers** | Transform inputs into finished goods |
| 4 | **Warehouses / DCs** | Store inventory between stages |
| 5 | **Distributors / Wholesalers** | Break bulk and resell to retailers |
| 6 | **Retailers** | Sell directly to end customers |
| 7 | **Customers** (B2B / B2C) | Final consumers of the product |
| 8 | **Logistics Providers** | 3PL/4PL transport and warehousing partners |
| 9 | **Reverse Logistics** | Handle returns, recycling, and disposal |

> [!NOTE]
> Every organization, whether a small grocery store or a multinational like Apple, operates within a supply chain. The size and complexity differ — but the fundamental flow is identical.

### 1.4 Supply Chain vs. Supply Chain Management

| Aspect | Supply Chain (SC) | Supply Chain Management (SCM) |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **What it is** | The network of entities | The art and science of managing the network |
| **Nature** | Static structure | Dynamic process |
| **Focus** | *What* the system is | *How* the system is coordinated |
| **Goal** | Connecting entities | Optimizing flows and value |
| **Example** | Apple, Foxconn, DHL, Best Buy together | Apple's strategy of synchronized product launches with its partners |

> [!TIP]
> **Easy Memory Trick:** *Supply Chain = the stage. Supply Chain Management = the play being performed on that stage.*

### 1.5 Why Study Supply Chains?

- They account for **more than 50%** of the total cost of many products.
- Effective SCM can reduce costs by **20%–30%** while improving service levels.
- They directly influence a firm's **competitive advantage** through responsiveness, efficiency, and resilience.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Flows in a Supply Chain

Every supply chain simultaneously manages three critical flows:

#### (i) Product / Material Flow
- The physical movement of goods from suppliers → manufacturer → customer.
- Includes raw materials, work-in-process (WIP), and finished goods.
- Direction: **Downstream** (forward, towards the customer).
- Returns flow **upstream** (reverse logistics).

#### (ii) Information Flow
- The bidirectional exchange of data: orders, forecasts, inventory levels, shipment status, demand signals.
- Modern SCs use **EDI, ERP, RFID, IoT, Blockchain, and Cloud Platforms**.
- Direction: **Bidirectional** (upstream + downstream).
- A *bullwhip effect* occurs when small demand variations get amplified upstream due to poor information sharing.

#### (iii) Financial Flow
- The movement of money, credit terms, invoices, and payment schedules.
- Direction: Mostly **upstream** (customer → supplier).
- Includes procurement budgets, working capital, and payment cycles (e.g., Net 30, Net 60).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Explain the three flows in a supply chain."* — Always draw the diagram with three arrows between every two entities (one for each flow).

### 2.2 Stages of a Supply Chain (End-to-End View)

A typical supply chain can be broken into **four to six functional stages**:

1. **Sourcing / Procurement** — Identifying and acquiring raw materials.
2. **Production / Manufacturing** — Converting inputs into finished products.
3. **Warehousing & Storage** — Buffering inventory to balance supply and demand.
4. **Distribution & Transportation** — Moving products to the next stage.
5. **Retail / Sales** — Final point of customer interaction.
6. **Reverse Logistics** — Handling returns, repairs, recycling, and end-of-life disposal.

### 2.3 The Three Decision Phases in Supply Chain Management

KTU examiners frequently test this framework. The decisions in an SC are classified by *time horizon* and *level of aggregation*:

| Decision Phase | Time Horizon | Level | Typical Decisions |
|----------------|--------------|-------|-------------------|
| **Strategic (Supply Chain Strategy)** | Years | Long-term | Plant location, supplier selection, product design, network design, IT infrastructure |
| **Tactical (Planning)** | Quarters / Months | Medium-term | Production planning, inventory policies, transportation modes, contract negotiation |
| **Operational (Execution)** | Days / Hours | Short-term | Order fulfilment, daily production scheduling, truck loading, route scheduling |

> [!NOTE]
> Strategic decisions are made first; they set *constraints*. Tactical decisions operate *within* those constraints. Operational decisions are the *day-to-day execution* of tactical plans.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Quantitative Relationships

Although SCM is a management discipline, several quantitative relationships form the foundation of supply chain analytics. The following table consolidates the high-yield formulas that frequently appear in numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Meaning |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Total Supply Chain Cost (TSCC)** | $TSCC = C_{mat} + C_{prod} + C_{inv} + C_{trans} + C_{fac} + C_{info}$ | Currency | Sum of all costs across the chain |
| 2 | **Value Added (VA)** | $VA_i = P_{out,i} - C_{in,i}$ | Currency | Value created at stage $i$ |
| 3 | **Cumulative Value** | $V_{cum} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} VA_i$ | Currency | Total value at the end of chain |
| 4 | **Inventory Turnover (IT)** | $IT = \dfrac{COGS}{Average\ Inventory}$ | Times/year | Efficiency of inventory use |
| 5 | **Days of Supply (DoS)** | $DoS = \dfrac{Average\ Inventory}{Daily\ Demand}$ | Days | How long current stock will last |
| 6 | **Order Cycle Time (OCT)** | $OCT = T_{order} + T_{process} + T_{ship} + T_{receive}$ | Days | Time from order placement to delivery |
| 7 | **Fill Rate (FR)** | $FR = \dfrac{Orders\ Filled}{Orders\ Received} \times 100\%$ | % | Service level metric |
| 8 | **Perfect Order Rate (POR)** | $POR = FR \times On\text{-}Time \times Damage\text{-}Free \times Correct\ Docs$ | % | Gold-standard SC performance metric |
| 9 | **Cash-to-Cash Cycle (C2C)** | $C2C = D_{SO} + D_{INV} - D_{PO}$ | Days | Time between paying suppliers and receiving customer payment |
| 10 | **Bullwhip Variance Ratio** | $BWR = \dfrac{Var(Order)}{Var(Demand)}$ | Dimensionless | Ratio \mid{} 1 indicates bullwhip effect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat on Units:** Production cost $C_{prod}$ is often expressed as cost per unit. Total cost = unit cost $\times$ number of units produced. Always carry units through calculations.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Supply Chain Concept

- **Apple Inc.** — Operates one of the most efficient SCs globally, sourcing components from 43 countries, manufacturing in China (Foxconn), and distributing worldwide — all synchronized via *just-in-time* principles.
- **Amazon** — Pioneered *two-day (and now same-day) delivery* via a hyper-optimized DC network and predictive demand analytics.
- **Reliance Jio / Bigbasket** — In the Indian/Kerala context, companies leverage hyperlocal SCs to deliver perishables within hours.
- **COVID-19 Disruption (2020–2022)** — Exposed fragility in global SCs, prompting firms to shift from *lean* to *resilient* and *agile* strategies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Total Supply Chain Cost Framework

The Total Supply Chain Cost (TSCC) framework is the *quantitative backbone* of supply chain decision-making. The following derivation builds the framework from first principles.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Six Cost Components

The supply chain is decomposed into six cost-generating activities:

$$
C_1 = \text{Material Cost} \quad
C_2 = \text{Production Cost} \quad
C_3 = \text{Inventory Cost}
$$
$$
C_4 = \text{Transportation Cost} \quad
C_5 = \text{Facility Cost} \quad
C_6 = \text{Information Cost}
$$

**Explanation:** These represent the *value-adding and value-aggregating activities* that consume organizational resources at every stage.

#### Step 2 — Express Each Component as a Function

Each cost component is a function of decision variables. For instance, inventory cost includes holding, ordering, and shortage costs.

$$
C_3 = C_{hold} + C_{order} + C_{shortage}
$$

where:
- $C_{hold} = H \cdot \dfrac{Q}{2}$ (holding cost per period)
- $C_{order} = S \cdot \dfrac{D}{Q}$ (ordering cost per period)
- $C_{shortage}$ = penalty cost from unmet demand

**Explanation:** This is the classical Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) decomposition. The symbol $H$ is the holding cost per unit per period, $S$ is the ordering cost per order, $D$ is annual demand, and $Q$ is the order quantity.

#### Step 3 — Sum All Cost Components

$$
TSCC(Q) = C_1 + C_2 + C_3(Q) + C_4(Q) + C_5 + C_6
$$

**Explanation:** Cost components 3 and 4 are typically variable with respect to $Q$ (order quantity), while $C_1, C_2, C_5, C_6$ are largely fixed in the short run.

#### Step 4 — Optimize (EOQ Derivation)

Differentiating $C_3$ with respect to $Q$ and setting the derivative to zero:

$$
\frac{d}{dQ}\left[\frac{HQ}{2} + \frac{SD}{Q}\right] = \frac{H}{2} - \frac{SD}{Q^2} = 0
$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{SD}{Q^2}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
Q^2 = \frac{2SD}{H}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2SD}{H}}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the *classic EOQ formula* (Economic Order Quantity / Wilson Formula). It gives the optimal lot size that minimizes the total of ordering and holding costs.

#### Step 5 — Total Cost at Optimum

Substituting $Q^*$ back into $C_3$:

$$
C_3^* = \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2SD}{H}} + S\cdot\frac{D}{\sqrt{\frac{2SD}{H}}}
= \sqrt{2SHD} + \sqrt{2SHD} = 2\sqrt{SHD}
$$

**Explanation:** At the optimum, ordering cost exactly equals holding cost, and the total variable inventory cost simplifies to the elegant closed-form expression $\sqrt{2SHD}$.

#### Step 6 — Generalize to Full Supply Chain

The complete supply chain cost function is therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,TSCC = C_1 + C_2 + \sqrt{2SHD} + C_4(Q) + C_5 + C_6\,}
$$

**Explanation:** This formulation gives a *decision-maker* the ability to evaluate trade-offs. Increasing facility cost might reduce transportation cost. Increasing information cost might reduce inventory cost. SCM is fundamentally about managing these trade-offs.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Component-to-Decision Mapping

The following analytical matrix maps each cost component to the decision phase that primarily controls it. This is a high-yield KTU exam framework.

| Cost Component | Primary Driver | Decision Phase | Example Lever |
|----------------|----------------|----------------|---------------|
| $C_1$ Material | Procurement, supplier mix | Strategic | Long-term contracts, supplier base optimization |
| $C_2$ Production | Process design, labour | Strategic / Tactical | Automation, lean manufacturing |
| $C_3$ Inventory | Order policy, safety stock | Tactical | EOQ, just-in-time (JIT) |
| $C_4$ Transportation | Mode, routing, network | Tactical / Operational | Modal shift (road → rail), route optimization |
| $C_5$ Facility | Plant / DC location & size | Strategic | Network redesign, greenfield/brownfield |
| $C_6$ Information | IT systems, integration | Strategic | ERP deployment, EDI, IoT, blockchain |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A company sells 12,000 units per year of a product. The unit cost is **₹50**, ordering cost is **₹200 per order**, and holding cost is **₹10 per unit per year**. The company currently orders in lots of 1,000 units. The plant operates 250 days/year.

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ.**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 200 \times 12000}{10}} = \sqrt{480000} \approx 692.82 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Inventory Cost at EOQ.**

$$
C_3^* = \sqrt{2 \times 200 \times 10 \times 12000} = \sqrt{48000000} \approx 6928.20\ \text{₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Annual Inventory Cost at Current Lot Size Q = 1000.**

$$
C_3(Q=1000) = \frac{10 \times 1000}{2} + \frac{200 \times 12000}{1000} = 5000 + 2400 = 7400\ \text{₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Number of Orders and Cycle Time.**

$$
N^* = \frac{12000}{692.82} \approx 17.32 \text{ orders/year}
$$
$$
T^* = \frac{250}{17.32} \approx 14.43 \text{ days/order}
$$

**Step 5 — Cost Savings.**

$$
\text{Savings} = 7400 - 6928.20 = 471.80\ \text{₹/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

By switching from Q = 1000 to the EOQ of 692.82, the firm reduces annual inventory cost by ₹471.80 and increases order frequency — typical of the *cost–service trade-off* in SCM.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark questions, always show a *diagram* (e.g., U-shaped total cost curve) alongside the numerical table to claim full marks on the presentation component.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — End-to-End Supply Chain

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *functional architecture* of a typical supply chain, showing the eight sequential stages and the value flow between them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[T1 Suppliers\nRaw Material Source] --> B[T2 Suppliers\nProcessed Inputs]
    B --> C[Manufacturer\nProduction Unit]
    C --> D[Warehouse\nStorage Buffer]
    D --> E[Distribution Centre\nConsolidation Hub]
    E --> F[Retailer\nFinal Outlet]
    F --> G[Customer\nEnd Consumer]
    G --> H[Reverse Logistics\nReturns and Recycling]
    H --> A
    C --> I[Quality Inspection\nCompliance Gate]
    I --> D
    D --> J[Inventory Tracking\nERP System]
    J --> E
    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style B fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style E fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457
    style F fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00838f
    style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style H fill:#efebe9,stroke:#4e342e
    style I fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#4527a0
    style J fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#558b2f
```

**Interpretation:**
- Top-level flow = *forward supply chain* (left to right).
- Bottom-right loop = *reverse logistics* (returns feeding back to the supplier).
- Quality Inspection and Inventory Tracking are *cross-cutting enablers* that span multiple stages.

---

### 4.2 The Three Flows in a Supply Chain

The following diagram visualizes the *three concurrent flows* between two entities (Supplier and Manufacturer) and onward to the Customer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Supplier[Supplier Node] -- Product Flow Downstream --> Mfg[Manufacturer Node]
    Mfg -- Product Flow Downstream --> DC[Distribution Centre]
    DC -- Product Flow Downstream --> Customer[Customer Node]
    Customer -- Financial Flow Upstream --> DC
    DC -- Financial Flow Upstream --> Mfg
    Mfg -- Financial Flow Upstream --> Supplier
    Customer -- Demand Information Upstream --> DC
    DC -- Forecast Upstream --> Mfg
    Mfg -- Order Information Upstream --> Supplier
    Supplier -- Capacity Information Downstream --> Mfg
    Mfg -- Production Status Downstream --> DC
    DC -- Delivery Status Downstream --> Customer
    style Supplier fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style Mfg fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style DC fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style Customer fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **product flow** moves left-to-right (downstream).
- The **financial flow** moves right-to-left (upstream).
- The **information flow** is *bidirectional* (both directions).

---

### 4.3 The Three Decision Phases — Hierarchical View

The following diagram illustrates how supply chain decisions cascade from *strategic* (broadest) to *operational* (most specific).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Strategic[Strategic Phase Years Horizon]
        S1[Network Design]
        S2[Supplier Selection]
        S3[Facility Location]
        S4[Product Portfolio]
    end
    subgraph Tactical[Tactical Phase Quarters Horizon]
        T1[Production Planning]
        T2[Inventory Policy]
        T3[Transportation Mode]
        T4[Contract Negotiation]
    end
    subgraph Operational[Operational Phase Days Horizon]
        O1[Order Fulfillment]
        O2[Daily Scheduling]
        O3[Route Optimization]
        O4[Shipment Tracking]
    end
    Strategic --> Tactical
    Tactical --> Operational
    style Strategic fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style Tactical fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff6f00
    style Operational fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
    style S1 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#01579b
    style S2 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#01579b
    style S3 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#01579b
    style S4 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#01579b
    style T1 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#ff6f00
    style T2 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#ff6f00
    style T3 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#ff6f00
    style T4 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#ff6f00
    style O1 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#880e4f
    style O2 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#880e4f
    style O3 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#880e4f
    style O4 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#880e4f
```

**Interpretation:**
- The strategic layer *constrains* the tactical layer.
- The tactical layer *constrains* the operational layer.
- Decisions cascade downward — a small strategic error can propagate thousands of small operational failures (the *bullwhip effect*).

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Cross-Functional SC Enablers

A simplified functional architecture showing how IT, finance, and HR *enable* the supply chain at every stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CoreFlow[Core Material and Product Flow]
        P1[Procurement] --> P2[Production] --> P3[Distribution] --> P4[Sales] --> P5[Returns]
    end
    subgraph ITLayer[Information Technology Enabler]
        I1[ERP System]
        I2[Forecasting Tools]
        I3[IoT and Tracking]
    end
    subgraph FinanceLayer[Financial Enabler]
        F1[Procurement Budget]
        F2[Working Capital]
        F3[Payment Cycles]
    end
    subgraph PeopleLayer[Human Capital Enabler]
        H1[Supply Chain Manager]
        H2[Logistics Analyst]
        H3[Procurement Officer]
    end
    ITLayer -.-> CoreFlow
    FinanceLayer -.-> CoreFlow
    PeopleLayer -.-> CoreFlow
    style CoreFlow fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#1a237e
    style ITLayer fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064
    style FinanceLayer fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style PeopleLayer fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define supply chain. List any four participants in a supply chain.**

**Model Answer:**
A supply chain is a network of organizations, people, activities, information, and resources involved in moving a product or service from the supplier's supplier to the customer's customer. The four key participants are:
1. **Suppliers** — provide raw materials.
2. **Manufacturer** — converts raw materials into finished goods.
3. **Distributor/Wholesaler** — warehouses and transports goods in bulk.
4. **Retailer/Customer** — final point of sale to the end consumer.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**What is the difference between supply chain and supply chain management?**

**Model Answer:**
| Basis | Supply Chain | Supply Chain Management |
|-------|--------------|--------------------------|
| Meaning | The network of entities and processes | The coordination of the network |
| Focus | Structure of the network | Optimization of flows |
| Goal | Linking entities | Maximizing customer value at minimum cost |
| Nature | Static | Dynamic and continuous |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **KTU Pattern Note:** In the 2024 scheme, Part B questions are *essay-type*, typically with two sub-parts worth 7 marks each. Below are *two alternative questions* (Question A OR Question B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model 1]`

**(a)** Define supply chain. With a neat diagram, explain the various stages of a supply chain. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the three types of flows in a supply chain with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A supply chain is the connected network of organizations, resources, and activities that produces and delivers a product or service — from raw-material suppliers to the end customer.

**Stages of Supply Chain (5 marks):**

1. **Sourcing / Procurement:** Identifying and acquiring raw materials from suppliers. *Example:* Apple sourcing rare-earth metals from Chile.
2. **Production / Manufacturing:** Converting raw materials into finished products. *Example:* Foxconn assembling iPhones in China.
3. **Warehousing & Storage:** Holding inventory between production and consumption to absorb supply-demand mismatches.
4. **Distribution & Transportation:** Moving products from warehouses to retailers via trucks, ships, planes, or rails.
5. **Retail:** Final point of sale to the consumer — physical (Big Bazaar) or e-commerce (Amazon).
6. **Reverse Logistics:** Managing returns, repairs, recycling, and disposal. *Example:* Amazon's "no-questions-asked" return policy.

*Neat Diagram (mandatory for full marks):* Draw a linear flowchart with arrows from supplier → manufacturer → warehouse → distributor → retailer → customer. Add a *return arrow* from customer to manufacturer for reverse logistics.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

The three flows in a supply chain are:

1. **Product/Material Flow** *(2.5 marks):*
   - Direction: Downstream (from supplier to customer).
   - Includes raw materials, WIP, and finished goods.
   - *Example:* A laptop moving from a Bangalore factory → Chennai warehouse → Kochi retail outlet → customer.

2. **Information Flow** *(2.5 marks):*
   - Direction: Bidirectional.
   - Includes orders, forecasts, inventory levels, and shipment status.
   - *Example:* A Point-of-Sale (POS) terminal at a Big Bazaar checkout transmits real-time sales data to the central warehouse, which triggers replenishment orders to the manufacturer.

3. **Financial Flow** *(2 marks):*
   - Direction: Upstream (from customer to supplier).
   - Includes invoices, credit terms, and payments.
   - *Example:* A customer pays Flipkart → Flipkart pays the courier → courier pays the distributor → distributor pays the manufacturer → manufacturer pays its suppliers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse *information flow* as one-directional. **It is bidirectional** — orders go upstream, and shipment status goes downstream. Marks are deducted if the bidirectionality is not stated.
> - Many students miss the **reverse logistics** stage in the diagram. Always include a backward arrow from customer to manufacturer and label it clearly.
> - For numerical questions, forgetting to **convert annual demand to daily demand** is the single most common error in EOQ-type problems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model 2]`

**(a)** Explain the three decision phases of supply chain management with examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Distinguish between supply chain and supply chain management. List the key drivers of supply chain performance. *(7 Marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The three decision phases in supply chain management are:

1. **Strategic Phase (2.5 marks):**
   - Time horizon: Years.
   - Long-term decisions affecting the entire network.
   - *Examples:* Location of factories (e.g., Tesla's Gigafactory in Bengaluru), choice of suppliers, product design decisions, IT architecture.

2. **Tactical Phase (2.5 marks):**
   - Time horizon: Quarters to months.
   - Mid-term planning within strategic constraints.
   - *Examples:* Production scheduling, inventory levels, choosing transportation mode (road vs. rail), contract negotiations with suppliers.

3. **Operational Phase (2 marks):**
   - Time horizon: Days to hours.
   - Day-to-day execution of tactical plans.
   - *Examples:* Order picking in a warehouse, daily truck route allocation, dispatch scheduling.

*Visual Aid (mandatory):* Draw a pyramid/hierarchy with strategic at the top, tactical in the middle, operational at the base.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Difference (3 marks):** The supply chain is the *network* itself; supply chain management is the *process of planning, implementing, and controlling* the network to deliver value to the customer at minimum cost.

**Key Drivers of Supply Chain Performance (4 marks):**

1. **Facilities** — Location, capacity, and flexibility of factories/warehouses.
2. **Inventory** — Quantity and location of stock across the chain.
3. **Transportation** — Mode and routing decisions (air, road, rail, sea).
4. **Information** — IT systems, data analytics, real-time visibility.
5. **Sourcing** — Insource vs. outsource; choice of suppliers.
6. **Pricing** — Pricing strategy influences demand patterns and revenue.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> - Writing only *two* drivers instead of all six. **Memorize all six drivers** — Facilities, Inventory, Transportation, Information, Sourcing, Pricing.
> - Confusing **Sourcing** with **Suppliers** — sourcing is the *strategic decision* of make-vs-buy; suppliers are the *entities*.
> - Not drawing the **pyramid diagram** for the three decision phases. A diagram carries 1–2 marks on its own.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Understanding the Supply Chain**

- **Definition:** A supply chain is the *network* of organizations, people, activities, information, and resources that moves a product from raw material to the end consumer.
- **Supply Chain ≠ SCM:** SC is the *structure*; SCM is the *process* of managing it.
- **Key Participants:** Suppliers → Manufacturer → Warehouse → Distributor → Retailer → Customer (+ Reverse Logistics).
- **Three Flows (Master these!):**
  - *Product Flow:* Downstream (supplier → customer).
  - *Information Flow:* Bidirectional.
  - *Financial Flow:* Upstream (customer → supplier).
- **Three Decision Phases:**
  - *Strategic* (years) — network, facility, sourcing.
  - *Tactical* (quarters) — production, inventory, transportation mode.
  - *Operational* (days/hours) — order fulfilment, routing, scheduling.
- **Six Cost Components of TSCC:** Material + Production + Inventory + Transportation + Facility + Information.
- **EOQ Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2SD}{H}}$ — a *must-know* quantitative tool.
- **Six Performance Drivers:** Facilities, Inventory, Transportation, Information, Sourcing, Pricing.
- **Bullwhip Effect:** Variance amplification of demand upstream due to poor information sharing. Always mention this in any SC overview question.
- **Reverse Logistics:** Often forgotten by students — *always* include it in diagrams.
- **Visual Aids:** In KTU 14-mark questions, a *neat labelled diagram* can account for 2–3 marks. Never answer without one.
- **Real-World Anchors:** Use examples like *Apple-Foxconn* (global SC), *Amazon* (e-commerce SC), *Reliance Jio* (Indian SC) to show applied understanding.
- **Board Favourite Frameworks:** SCOR model, three flows, three decision phases, six cost components, six drivers.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** Always frame your answer in the structure *Definition → Stages/Components → Diagram → Example → Conclusion*. Examiners reward clarity and structure, not length.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Achieving Strategic Fit in a Supply Chain: Supply Chain Drivers and Metrics, Analysing and Designing the Supply Chain Network
Models for Designing Regional Network Configuration, Impact of Globalization on Supply Chain Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Models for Designing Regional Network Configuration, Impact of Globalization on Supply Chain Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Models for Designing Regional Network Configuration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):**
> *Regional Network Configuration* refers to the strategic design decisions that a firm makes regarding the **number**, **location**, **size**, and **assignment of facilities** (plants, warehouses, distribution centers) within a regional or continental supply chain to **minimize total landed cost** while meeting customer service level requirements.

A regional network configuration model answers four fundamental questions simultaneously:
1. **How many** facilities should be operated?
2. **Where** should each facility be located geographically?
3. **What capacity** (size) should each facility have?
4. **Which markets and products** should be assigned to each facility?

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Post Office Problem":**
> Imagine Kerala Postal Service deciding where to place regional sorting hubs to serve all districts. They must decide:
> - *How many* hubs (1? 3? 6?) — affects fixed cost vs. delivery speed
> - *Where* to put them — affects transportation cost
> - *How big* each should be — affects capacity vs. land cost
> - *Which districts* each hub serves — affects last-mile distance
>
> Regional Network Configuration is exactly this logic, but for an industrial firm's plants, warehouses, and customer zones. **The trade-off is always between fixed facility cost and variable transportation/service cost.**

---

### 1.2 Impact of Globalization on Supply Chain Networks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):**
> *Globalization Impact on Supply Chain Networks* refers to the strategic restructuring of facility location, sourcing, and distribution decisions across **international borders** in response to variations in **factor costs** (labor, land, capital), **trade policies** (tariffs, duties), **risk factors** (political, currency, supply disruption), and **customer expectations** (responsiveness, sustainability).

The five global drivers that reshape regional networks are:
- **Market-seeking** (locate near customers)
- **Cost-seeking** (low-cost country sourcing)
- **Resource-seeking** (access raw materials)
- **Knowledge-seeking** (R&D hubs near talent)
- **Risk-diversification-seeking** (geographic redundancy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Apple iPhone Story":**
> Apple **designs** in Cupertino (knowledge-seeking), **sources** components from Korea, Japan, Taiwan (cost/resource-seeking), **assembles** in China and India (cost-seeking + risk diversification), and **sells** in 175+ countries (market-seeking). A single product's supply chain spans the planet. This is **globalized network design** in action.

---

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Network Design

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fixed Facility Cost | $f_i$ | ₹/year | Capital recovery per facility |
| Variable Production Cost | $v_i$ | ₹/unit | Operating cost per unit output |
| Transportation Cost | $c_{ij}$ | ₹/unit shipped | Lane cost between nodes $i \to j$ |
| Tariff / Duty Rate | $t_{ij}$ | % of FOB value | Cross-border duty on flow $i \to j$ |
| Demand at Market | $D_j$ | units/year | Annual customer consumption |
| Capacity of Facility | $K_i$ | units/year | Maximum throughput |
| Response Time | $T_j$ | days | Customer service level |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **regional** with **global** networks. *Regional = within a country or trade bloc (e.g., EU, ASEAN).* *Global = across continents with currency, tariff, and political risk layers.* Both use similar optimization logic, but global networks add **multi-currency**, **tariff**, and **lead-time uncertainty** dimensions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Strategic Role of Regional Network Design

A regional supply chain network converts **forecasted demand** into **served demand** through a hierarchy of facilities. The model must balance three competing cost buckets:

1. **Fixed Cost of Facilities** — incurred regardless of volume (rent, depreciation, overhead)
2. **Variable Operating Cost** — scales with production volume (labor, utilities, materials)
3. **Transportation Cost** — scales with shipments between echelons and to customers

> **The Engineering Logic:** As the **number of facilities increases**, fixed cost rises but transportation cost falls (shorter customer distances). The *optimal* facility count lies at the **intersection** of the rising fixed-cost curve and the falling transportation-cost curve — this is the classic *U-shaped total cost curve* in facility location theory.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Regional Network Models

| Model Class | Decision Variables | Typical Application | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Center-of-Gravity (CoG)** | Single $(x, y)$ coordinate for one facility | Quick warehouse placement | Low |
| **Weber's Problem** | Single facility minimizing weighted distance | Plant location on a plane | Medium |
| **p-Median Model** | Location of $p$ facilities to minimize weighted distance | Multiple warehouse placement | Medium |
| **Capacitated Plant Location (CPLP)** | Number, location, capacity, assignment | Multi-plant production networks | High |
| **Gravity-Friction Analogy (GFA)** | Gravity pull between demand sources | Multi-country trade flow estimation | High |
| **Mixed-Integer Linear Program (MILP)** | Binary (open/close) + continuous (flow) | Strategic global networks | Very High |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations. They appear in KTU ESE every year in some form.**

#### Equation 1 — Euclidean Distance Between Facility and Customer

$$d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$$

Where $d_{ij}$ is the distance between facility $i$ at $(x_i, y_i)$ and customer $j$ at $(x_j, y_j)$. Used in *load-distance* cost models.

---

#### Equation 2 — Center-of-Gravity (CoG) Coordinates

$$x^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot x_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j} \qquad y^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot y_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}$$

Where $w_j$ is the demand (weight) at customer $j$. Yields the *single* facility location that minimizes total weighted distance.

---

#### Equation 3 — Load-Distance (L-D) Objective Function

$$Z_{LD} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot d_{ij} \cdot y_{ij}$$

Where $w_j$ = demand at customer $j$, $d_{ij}$ = distance, $y_{ij}$ = assignment indicator (1 if customer $j$ served by facility $i$, else 0). Minimized in **p-median** formulations.

---

#### Equation 4 — Capacitated Plant Location Model (Total Landed Cost)

$$\min \; Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} f_i \cdot y_i \; + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}$$

Subject to:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = D_j \quad \forall j \in J \quad \text{(Demand satisfaction)}$$

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \le K_i \cdot y_i \quad \forall i \in I \quad \text{(Capacity constraint)}$$

$$y_i \in \{0, 1\}, \quad x_{ij} \ge 0$$

Where:
- $y_i$ = 1 if facility $i$ is opened, 0 otherwise
- $x_{ij}$ = units shipped from facility $i$ to customer $j$
- $f_i$ = annual fixed cost of opening facility $i$
- $c_{ij}$ = unit shipping cost from $i$ to $j$
- $D_j$ = annual demand at customer $j$
- $K_i$ = annual capacity of facility $i$

---

#### Equation 5 — Tariff-Inclusive Global Cost

$$C_{ij}^{global} = (p_i + c_{ij}) \cdot (1 + t_{ij}) + H_{ij}$$

Where $p_i$ = FOB unit production cost at source $i$, $c_{ij}$ = unit freight, $t_{ij}$ = tariff rate (decimal), $H_{ij}$ = holding/inventory carrying cost in transit. Used in **global network** decisions.

---

#### Equation 6 — Gravity-Friction Analogy (GFA) Flow Estimator

$$F_{ij} \;\propto\; \frac{(G_i \cdot G_j)}{R_{ij}}$$

Where $F_{ij}$ = forecasted trade flow, $G_i, G_j$ = "gravitational masses" (e.g., GDP or capacity), $R_{ij}$ = "friction" (e.g., cost/distance/tariff). Analogous to Newton's gravitational law.

---

#### Equation 7 — Response Time Constraint (Service Level)

$$T_j = \frac{d_{i^*j}}{v_{truck}} + t_{proc} \le T_{max}$$

Where $T_j$ = lead-time to customer $j$, $i^*$ = assigned facility, $v_{truck}$ = average truck speed, $t_{proc}$ = order processing time, $T_{max}$ = customer-acceptable threshold (typically **24–48 hours** for regional, **5–15 days** for global).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business

- **Automotive industry** uses MILP-based regional models to decide engine plant vs. assembly plant locations.
- **E-commerce giants** (Amazon, Flipkart) use CoG + p-median hybrids for last-mile warehouse placement.
- **Pharmaceutical MNCs** apply GFA to model cross-border medicine flows under varying tariffs.
- **Automotive OEMs** like Toyota and Hyundai use load-distance minimization for green-field plant site selection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Center-of-Gravity Formula from Load-Distance Minimization

**Goal:** Find the $(x^*, y^*)$ that minimizes total weighted distance to $n$ demand points.

**Starting objective (single facility, continuous plane):**

$$Z(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot d_{j}(x, y)$$

**Step 1** — Substitute the Euclidean distance:

$$Z(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot \sqrt{(x - x_j)^2 + (y - y_j)^2}$$

**Step 2** — Take the partial derivative with respect to $x$ and set to zero for the minimum:

$$\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot \frac{(x - x_j)}{\sqrt{(x - x_j)^2 + (y - y_j)^2}} = 0$$

**Step 3** — Separate the terms with $x$ and those with $x_j$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot \frac{x}{\sqrt{(x - x_j)^2 + (y - y_j)^2}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot \frac{x_j}{\sqrt{(x - x_j)^2 + (y - y_j)^2}}$$

**Step 4** — Since $d_j = \sqrt{(x - x_j)^2 + (y - y_j)^2}$, rewrite as:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{w_j \cdot x}{d_j} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{w_j \cdot x_j}{d_j}$$

**Step 5** — Solve for $x$:

$$x^* \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{w_j}{d_j} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{w_j \cdot x_j}{d_j}$$

$$x^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{w_j \cdot x_j}{d_j}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{w_j}{d_j}}$$

**Step 6** — **Note:** This is the exact (non-linear) form. For KTU problems, we use the **classical simplification** where $d_j$ is ignored (or treated as constant for an initial estimate):

$$\boxed{\; x^* \approx \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot x_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j} \;}$$

**Step 7** — By symmetry, the $y$-coordinate:

$$\boxed{\; y^* \approx \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot y_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j} \;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The classical CoG formula is what is tested in Part A (3-mark) questions. The full nonlinear version appears rarely and only in Part B derivations.

---

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Capacitated Plant Location

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Dec 2022 pattern):**

A firm is evaluating opening **warehouses** at 3 candidate sites ($A$, $B$, $C$) to serve 2 markets ($M_1$, $M_2$).

| Site | Fixed Cost ₹/yr | Capacity (units) | Unit Cost to $M_1$ | Unit Cost to $M_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 100,000 | 500 | 20 | 25 |
| $B$ | 150,000 | 800 | 15 | 22 |
| $C$ | 120,000 | 600 | 22 | 18 |

**Demand:** $M_1$ = 400 units/yr, $M_2$ = 350 units/yr.

**Find:** Optimal set of sites to open and the assignment.

---

**Step 1 — Total Demand:**
$D_{total} = 400 + 350 = 750$ units/yr.

**Step 2 — Capacity Constraint Check:** Any single facility can serve total demand (max capacity = 800). So we compare single-facility and dual-facility options.

**Step 3 — Evaluate Single-Facility Options:**

**Option 1: Open only $A$ (capacity 500, but demand = 750)** → INFEASIBLE.

**Option 2: Open only $B$ (capacity 800):**
$$Z_B = 150{,}000 + (400 \times 15) + (350 \times 22)$$
$$Z_B = 150{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 7{,}700 = 163{,}700$$

**Option 3: Open only $C$ (capacity 600):** → INFEASIBLE.

**Step 4 — Evaluate Dual-Facility Options:**

**Option 4: Open $A$ + $B$** (combined capacity 1,300):
$$Z_{AB} = 100{,}000 + 150{,}000 + \min(400 \times 20 + 350 \times 15, \; 400 \times 15 + 350 \times 22)$$
$$Z_{AB} = 250{,}000 + \min(8{,}000 + 5{,}250, \; 6{,}000 + 7{,}700) = 250{,}000 + \min(13{,}250, 13{,}700)$$
$$Z_{AB} = 250{,}000 + 13{,}250 = 263{,}250$$

**Option 5: Open $A$ + $C$:**
$$Z_{AC} = 100{,}000 + 120{,}000 + \min(400 \times 20 + 350 \times 18, \; 400 \times 22 + 350 \times 25)$$
$$Z_{AC} = 220{,}000 + \min(8{,}000 + 6{,}300, \; 8{,}800 + 8{,}750) = 220{,}000 + 14{,}300 = 234{,}300$$

**Option 6: Open $B$ + $C$:**
$$Z_{BC} = 150{,}000 + 120{,}000 + \min(400 \times 15 + 350 \times 18, \; 400 \times 22 + 350 \times 22)$$
$$Z_{BC} = 270{,}000 + \min(6{,}000 + 6{,}300, \; 8{,}800 + 7{,}700) = 270{,}000 + 12{,}300 = 282{,}300$$

**Step 5 — Compare All Feasible Options:**

| Option | Open Sites | Total Annual Cost ₹ |
|---|---|---|
| $B$ only | $\{B\}$ | **163,700** ✓ |
| $A+B$ | $\{A, B\}$ | 263,250 |
| $A+C$ | $\{A, C\}$ | 234,300 |
| $B+C$ | $\{B, C\}$ | 282,300 |

**Step 6 — Optimal Solution:**

$$\boxed{\; \text{Open only Site } B. \quad \text{Assignment: } B \to M_1 \text{ (400 units), } B \to M_2 \text{ (350 units).} \;}$$
$$\boxed{\; Z^* = ₹\;163{,}700 \text{ per year} \;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Single large facility beats multiple smaller ones here because fixed-cost savings outweigh transportation savings. *This is exactly why global firms consolidate into mega-DC (Distribution Centers) like Amazon's 1-million-sq-ft fulfillment hubs.*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Globalization Impact Categories

| Driver | Impact on Facility Count | Impact on Facility Location | Impact on Inventory |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lower labor cost abroad** | Increase (more sites in low-cost countries) | Shift toward Asia/Africa/LATAM | May increase (longer pipeline) |
| **High tariff barriers** | Increase (regional local plants) | Within trade bloc (EU, ASEAN) | Increase (need buffer stock) |
| **Currency volatility** | No direct change | Diversify across currency zones | Increase (hedge via stock) |
| **Faster customer responsiveness** | Increase (many small local DCs) | Near major population centers | Decrease (shorter lead time) |
| **ESG / Sustainability mandates** | Decrease (consolidation) | Near renewable energy hubs | Variable |
| **Supply disruption risk (COVID-like)** | Increase (resilience via redundancy) | Geographic diversification | Increase (safety stock) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Regional Network Configuration Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Strategic Inputs] --> A1[Forecasted Demand Dj by zone]
    A0 --> A2[Cost Parameters fi, vij, cij]
    A0 --> A3[Service Level Constraints Tj max]
    A0 --> A4[Capacity Limits Ki]
    A0 --> A5[Tariffs and Duties tij for global]

    B0[Model Selection Layer] --> B1[Center of Gravity for single site]
    B0 --> B2[p Median for multi site]
    B0 --> B3[Capacitated Plant Location MILP]
    B0 --> B4[Gravity Friction Analogy for global]

    B1 --> C0[Optimization Solver]
    B2 --> C0
    B3 --> C0
    B4 --> C0

    C0 --> D1[Number of Facilities m star]
    C0 --> D2[Location coordinates xi star yi star]
    C0 --> D3[Capacity Allocation Ki star]
    C0 --> D4[Market Assignment xij star]
    C0 --> D5[Total Landed Cost Z star]

    D1 --> E0[Strategic Output to Management]
    D2 --> E0
    D3 --> E0
    D4 --> E0
    D5 --> E0

    E0 --> F1[Facility Opening Decision]
    E0 --> F2[Supply Chain Network Map]
    E0 --> F3[Investment Budget]
    E0 --> F4[Service Level SLA]

    style A0 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style C0 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style E0 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.2 Globalization Impact Flow on Supply Chain Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Domestic Network Single Country] -->|Add Global Drivers| G2[Regional Network Trade Bloc]
    G2 -->|Add Tariff and Currency| G3[Global Network Multi Continent]

    G3 --> H1[Offshoring Low Cost Country Sourcing]
    G3 --> H2[Reshoring Risk Reduction]
    G3 --> H3[Nearshoring Geographic Proximity]
    G3 --> H4[Friend Shoring Allied Countries]

    H1 --> I1[Lower Variable Cost]
    H2 --> I2[Higher Resilience]
    H3 --> I3[Lower Lead Time]
    H4 --> I4[Lower Geopolitical Risk]

    I1 --> J1[Total Landed Cost Equation 5]
    I2 --> J1
    I3 --> J1
    I4 --> J1

    J1 --> K0[Network Reconfiguration Decision]

    style G1 fill:#bbdefb,color:#000000
    style G3 fill:#ffccbc,color:#000000
    style J1 fill:#fff59d,color:#000000
    style K0 fill:#388e3c,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.3 p-Median Facility Location Logic (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Inputs p Desired Facilities, n Customers, wij] --> P1[Enumerate Candidate Sites]
    P1 --> P2[For Each Subset of Size p]
    P2 --> P3[Assign Each Customer to Nearest Open Site]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Load Distance Score]
    P4 --> P5{Is This Subset Best So Far}
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Store New Best Solution]
    P5 -->|No| P7[Discard Subset]
    P6 --> P8{All Subsets Checked}
    P7 --> P8
    P8 -->|No| P2
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Output Optimal Locations and Assignments]

    style P0 fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
    style P5 fill:#f9a825,color:#000000
    style P9 fill:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** *Define regional network configuration in supply chain management. List the four key decisions it addresses.*

**Model Answer:**
Regional network configuration is the strategic design of facility number, location, capacity, and market assignment to minimize total landed cost while meeting service level targets.

Four key decisions:
1. **How many** facilities to operate (count)
2. **Where** to locate each facility (geography)
3. **What capacity** each facility should have (size)
4. **Which markets and products** each facility will serve (assignment)

> ---

**Q2. [3 Marks]** *State and explain the Center-of-Gravity (CoG) formula used in facility location.*

**Model Answer:**
The Center-of-Gravity formula gives the $(x^*, y^*)$ coordinate of a single facility that minimizes total weighted Euclidean distance to a set of customers.

$$x^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot x_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j} \qquad y^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot y_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}$$

Here $w_j$ = demand weight at customer $j$, $(x_j, y_j)$ = customer $j$ coordinates. It is derived by minimizing the load-distance objective. Used for first-cut warehouse or plant placement.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern: Two Alternatives]**

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[Regional Network Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the Capacitated Plant Location Model (CPLM) with its objective function and constraints. Why is it called a "mixed-integer" formulation?*

**Model Answer:**

The Capacitated Plant Location Model (CPLM) is a strategic supply chain optimization model that simultaneously decides:
- Which candidate facilities to **open** (binary decision)
- How much **flow** to ship on each lane (continuous decision)
- All while respecting **capacity** limits at each facility and **demand** satisfaction at each market.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$\min \; Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} f_i \cdot y_i + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}$$

**Subject to:**

**Demand satisfaction** (every customer $j$ is fully served):
$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = D_j \quad \forall j$$

**Capacity constraint** (no facility ships more than its capacity):
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \le K_i \cdot y_i \quad \forall i$$

**Variable types:**
$$y_i \in \{0, 1\}, \quad x_{ij} \ge 0$$

**Why "Mixed-Integer":**
Because the formulation contains **both** binary variables ($y_i$, takes only values 0 or 1) **and** continuous variables ($x_{ij}$, can take any non-negative real value). Such problems require specialized solvers (branch-and-bound, branch-and-cut, CPLEX, Gurobi).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A company is evaluating two candidate warehouse sites P and Q to serve three markets A, B, C. Annual fixed costs are ₹4,00,000 for P and ₹5,00,000 for Q. Per-unit shipping costs (₹) and demand are tabulated below. If P has capacity 900 units and Q has capacity 1,200 units, determine the optimal configuration.*

| From \ To | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | 20 | 35 | 40 |
| Q | 30 | 25 | 28 |
| **Demand (units)** | 400 | 350 | 300 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Demand:**
$D_{total} = 400 + 350 + 300 = 1{,}050$ units.

**Step 2 — Single-Site Feasibility:**

- *P only:* capacity 900 < 1,050 → INFEASIBLE
- *Q only:* capacity 1,200 > 1,050 → FEASIBLE

$$Z_Q = 5{,}00{,}000 + (400 \times 30) + (350 \times 25) + (300 \times 28)$$
$$Z_Q = 5{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 8{,}750 + 8{,}400 = 5{,}29{,}150$$

**Step 3 — Both Sites Open:**
Cheaper shipping lane wins each market:
- A: min(20, 30) = 20 (from P)
- B: min(35, 25) = 25 (from Q)
- C: min(40, 28) = 28 (from Q)

$$Z_{PQ} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + (400 \times 20) + (350 \times 25) + (300 \times 28)$$
$$Z_{PQ} = 9{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 8{,}750 + 8{,}400 = 9{,}25{,}150$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

| Option | Total Cost ₹ |
|---|---|
| Open Q only | **5,29,150** ✓ |
| Open both P and Q | 9,25,150 |

**Optimal Decision:** Open **only Q**. Assign all three markets to Q. Total Annual Cost = **₹ 5,29,150**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - Students often forget to **check feasibility first** (P alone cannot serve 1,050 units).
> - Some forget to include **fixed cost** $f_i$ in $Z_Q$.
> - Some assign customers to *non-cheapest* lanes — always pick min cost lane **per market** when both sites are open.
> - Not stating the optimal assignment clearly in the conclusion loses **1 mark**.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[Globalization Impact]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the impact of globalization on supply chain network design. Explain with examples how offshoring, reshoring, and nearshoring reshape regional networks.*

**Model Answer:**

Globalization fundamentally transforms regional supply chains by introducing new cost, risk, and service layers. Three structural shifts dominate:

**1. Offshoring — Relocating Production to Low-Cost Countries**
- *Definition:* Moving manufacturing or services to distant countries with lower factor costs.
- *Example:* Apple assembling iPhones in Shenzhen, Foxconn setting up in Vietnam.
- *Impact on Network:* Increases facility count abroad, lengthens pipeline, raises safety-stock, but lowers unit cost.

**2. Reshoring — Bringing Production Back Home**
- *Definition:* Reversing offshoring by relocating production to the firm's home country.
- *Drivers:* COVID-19 supply shocks, US-China trade war, ESG pressure, rising wages in China.
- *Example:* General Motors reshoring EV motor production to Michigan, USA.
- *Impact on Network:* Reduces facility count abroad, shortens lead-time, increases domestic capacity, raises unit cost but reduces risk.

**3. Nearshoring — Relocating to Geographically Close Countries**
- *Definition:* Moving production to a nearby country (not home, not far) to balance cost and responsiveness.
- *Example:* US firms moving production from China to Mexico; German firms shifting from Asia to Eastern Europe.
- *Impact on Network:* Maintains geographic diversification but reduces lead-time and tariff exposure.

**Summary Impact Matrix:**

| Strategy | Facility Count | Lead-Time | Cost | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Offshoring | High (Asia) | Long | Low | High |
| Reshoring | High (Domestic) | Short | High | Low |
| Nearshoring | Medium (Region) | Medium | Medium | Medium |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A firm in Germany is deciding whether to source a component from Supplier A in Germany (local) or Supplier B in Vietnam (global). The FOB unit cost is €12 at A and €6 at B. Freight is €2/unit from A and €4/unit from B. Tariff on Vietnam imports is 8%. Annual demand is 50,000 units. Holding cost is 20% of unit cost per year. Lead-time is 7 days (A) and 35 days (B). Which supplier minimizes total landed cost per year?*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Unit Landed Cost for Supplier A (Local):**
Tariff = 0 (intra-EU/domestic)
$$C_A = (12 + 2)(1 + 0) = €\;14 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Unit Landed Cost for Supplier B (Vietnam):**
$$C_B = (6 + 4)(1 + 0.08) = 10 \times 1.08 = €\;10.80 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 3 — Annual Purchase + Freight + Tariff Cost:**
- Supplier A: $50{,}000 \times 14 = €\;700{,}000$
- Supplier B: $50{,}000 \times 10.80 = €\;540{,}000$

**Step 4 — Pipeline Inventory Holding Cost (using 20% of unit landed cost × lead-time in years):**
- Supplier A: $14 \times 0.20 \times (7/365) = €\;0.054$ per unit-year → $50{,}000 \times 0.054 = €\;2{,}700$
- Supplier B: $10.80 \times 0.20 \times (35/365) = €\;0.207$ per unit-year → $50{,}000 \times 0.207 = €\;10{,}350$

**Step 5 — Total Annual Landed Cost:**
- Supplier A: $700{,}000 + 2{,}700 = €\;702{,}700$
- Supplier B: $540{,}000 + 10{,}350 = €\;550{,}350$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

$$\boxed{\; \text{Choose Supplier B (Vietnam). Savings} = €\;152{,}350 \text{ per year.} \;}$$

Even after accounting for tariff and 5× longer lead-time, Supplier B remains cheaper. *However*, if lead-time exceeded 90 days or tariff rose above 30%, Supplier A would win — illustrating the **trade-off frontier** in global network design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - Students frequently **omit the tariff multiplier** $(1 + t_{ij})$ — losing 2 marks.
> - Forgetting **pipeline holding cost** (which depends on lead-time) loses another 2 marks.
> - Converting lead-time correctly to **fraction of a year** (divide by 365) is essential.
> - Always state **both** the per-unit cost and the **annual total** in the final answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- ☐ **Regional Network Configuration** answers **4 decisions**: *count, location, capacity, assignment*.
- ☐ **Center-of-Gravity (CoG)** formula uses **demand weights** $w_j$ and customer coordinates $(x_j, y_j)$.
- ☐ **Load-distance objective** = sum of (weight × distance × assignment) across all customer-facility pairs.
- ☐ **Capacitated Plant Location Model (CPLM)** is a **Mixed-Integer Linear Program (MILP)** because it has binary $y_i$ + continuous $x_{ij}$.
- ☐ **Total landed cost** in global networks = production + freight + tariff + in-transit holding.
- ☐ **Tariff-inclusive cost equation:** $C = (p + f)(1 + t)$.
- ☐ **Lead-time affects holding cost** — longer global supply chains need more pipeline inventory.
- ☐ **Offshoring** reduces cost, increases risk; **Reshoring** reverses it; **Nearshoring** balances both.
- ☐ **Global drivers** of network design: factor costs, tariffs, currency, lead-time, ESG, geopolitical risk.
- ☐ **Gravity-Friction Analogy (GFA):** $F_{ij} \propto (G_i \cdot G_j) / R_{ij}$ — used to estimate trade flows.
- ☐ **Service-level constraint:** lead-time must satisfy customer threshold (24–48 hr regional, 5–15 days global).
- ☐ Always **check feasibility** of single-facility options against demand + capacity before comparing costs.

> **Final Mantra:** *Network design = minimize (Fixed + Variable + Transportation + Tariff + Risk) cost subject to (Demand + Capacity + Service) constraints.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demand Forecasting in Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Demand Forecasting in Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Demand Forecasting** is the process of estimating future customer demand for a product or service over a defined horizon (short, medium, or long term), using historical data, market intelligence, statistical models, and managerial judgment, in order to support supply chain planning decisions such as inventory control, production scheduling, procurement, capacity planning, and logistics.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, demand forecasting is positioned as the **critical upstream driver** of the entire Supply Chain Management (SCM) planning hierarchy. Every downstream decision — from raw material ordering (MRP) to distribution network design — is anchored to a demand forecast. The **forecast horizon** is therefore a primary classifier:

- **Short-range forecast:** horizon of ≤ **3 months**; drives daily/weekly production and replenishment orders.
- **Medium-range forecast:** horizon of **3 months to 1 year**; drives aggregate production planning, workforce sizing, and supplier contracts.
- **Long-range forecast:** horizon of **> 1 year**; drives strategic decisions such as facility location, capacity expansion, technology investment, and new product introduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle (KTU Board Favourite):** *Forecasts are never perfect. The objective of demand forecasting is not to produce a single number but to bound uncertainty — i.e., to characterise a probability distribution over future demand — so that supply chain planners can build safety stock, safety lead time, and safety capacity to absorb forecast error.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you are a **tea stall owner in Thiruvananthapuram**. Every morning you must decide how much tea powder, milk, sugar, and biscuits to stock. You don't know the *exact* number of customers tomorrow, but from past experience you know:

- Monday mornings are slow, Friday evenings are busy.
- During Onam, demand for *kuzhalappam* and *payasam* ingredients rises sharply.
- A thunderstorm at 4 PM will halve your evening sales.

A good shopkeeper builds a *mental forecast* using these patterns plus weather news and local events. **Demand Forecasting in a Supply Chain is exactly this — but formalised, quantified, automated, and applied at scale across thousands of SKUs and geographies.** The same logic (pattern recognition + causal reasoning + judgment) is wrapped in mathematical models and IT systems to support billion-rupee decisions.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics of a Forecast (KTU Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The forecast must be evaluated, not merely produced. KTU examiners expect students to distinguish:
> - **Bias:** systematic over- or under-forecasting (measured by **Mean Error (ME)** or **Tracking Signal**).
> - **Accuracy:** closeness of forecast to actual demand (measured by **MAD, MSE, MAPE, RMSE**).
> - **Forecast Error:** $e_t = D_t - F_t$ where $D_t$ is actual demand and $F_t$ is forecast.
> - **Cumulative Error (Running Sum of Forecast Errors, RSFE):** $\sum_{t=1}^{n}(D_t - F_t)$.

**Units & Conventions used throughout these notes:**
- Demand $D_t$ in units (e.g., cases, kg, pieces).
- Time index $t$ in periods (days, weeks, months).
- Smoothing constant $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ are **dimensionless** with $0 \le \alpha,\beta,\gamma \le 1$.
- Trend $T_t$ in units per period.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Forecasting Methods (Theory Breakdown)

Forecasting methods fall into two macro-families, and every KTU question on this topic maps onto this taxonomy.

**A. Qualitative Methods (Judgmental / Subjective)** — used when historical data is absent (new product launch) or unreliable.

- **Executive Opinion (Top-Down):** Senior management pools judgment.
- **Sales Force Composite (Bottom-Up):** Field salespeople estimate demand from their territory.
- **Delphi Method:** Iterative, anonymous expert panel; avoids group-think.
- **Market Research & Customer Surveys.**
- **Analogous (Comparable) Product Method:** Use life-cycle of a similar product.

**B. Quantitative Methods (Mathematical / Statistical)** — used when reliable historical data exists.

- **Time-Series Models** (assume future = function of past):
  1. Naïve Forecast
  2. Simple Moving Average (SMA)
  3. Weighted Moving Average (WMA)
  4. Exponential Smoothing (SES)
  5. Trend-Adjusted Exponential Smoothing (Holt's Method)
  6. Trend & Seasonality-Adjusted (Holt-Winters)
- **Causal / Associative Models** (assume future = function of *external* drivers):
  1. Linear Regression
  2. Multiple Regression
- **Simulation & AI Models:** Monte Carlo simulation, neural networks, ARIMA.

### 2.2 Seven Pillars of Demand (Chopra & Meindl Framework — KTU Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> The seven pillars explain *what* the forecast must capture: (i) Historical demand, (ii) Lead time of product, (iii) Planned marketing/promotions, (iv) Planned price changes, (v) Economic indicators, (vi) Customer preferences, (vii) Competitor actions.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **examinable** in both Part A (3-mark) derivations and Part B (14-mark) numerical problems. Memorise the syntax and the units.

**Table 1 — Forecast Models and Formulas**

| # | Model Name | Forecast Equation | Key Parameters / Notes |
|---|------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Naïve | $F_{t+1} = D_t$ | Last period is the best guess; baseline benchmark |
| 2 | Simple Moving Average (SMA, n-period) | $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} D_{t-i}$ | $n$ = window length; equal weights |
| 3 | Weighted Moving Average (WMA) | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} w_i \cdot D_{t-i}, \quad \sum w_i = 1$ | Higher weight on recent periods |
| 4 | Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) | $F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha (D_t - F_t) \equiv \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$; only level tracked |
| 5 | Holt's Method (Trend-Adjusted ES) | $\begin{aligned} L_t &= \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1}+T_{t-1}) \\ T_t &= \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1} \\ F_{t+\tau} &= L_t + \tau T_t \end{aligned}$ | Captures level + linear trend |
| 6 | Linear Trend Regression | $\hat{y} = a + b x$ | Causal / time-index model |
| 7 | Linear Regression Slope | $b = \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^{2} - (\sum x)^{2}}$ | $y$ = demand, $x$ = period or driver |
| 8 | Regression Intercept | $a = \bar{y} - b \bar{x}$ | Anchors the line |
| 9 | Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) | $\text{MAD} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert$ | $\vert\cdot\vert$ = absolute value |
| 10 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^{2}$ | Penalises large errors heavily |
| 11 | Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as demand |
| 12 | Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) | $\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{\vert e_t \vert}{\vert D_t \vert} \;\%$ | Scale-free; comparable across SKUs |
| 13 | Tracking Signal (TS) | $\text{TS} = \dfrac{\text{RSFE}}{\text{MAD}}, \quad \text{RSFE}=\sum e_t$ | $\vert TS \vert \le 4 \Rightarrow$ in control |
| 14 | Forecast Bias (Mean Error) | $\text{ME} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum e_t$ | $\text{ME}=0$ ⇒ unbiased |

**Table 2 — Selecting the Smoothing Constant $\alpha$**

| $\alpha$ Value | Behaviour | When to Use |
|----------------|-----------|-------------|
| $\alpha \to 0$ | Slow response; heavy smoothing | Stable demand, low noise |
| $\alpha \to 1$ | Fast response; tracks noise | Volatile demand, recent trend matters |
| Typical KTU value | $\alpha \in [0.1,\; 0.4]$ | General-purpose supply chains |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility (Why Industry Cares)

- **Manufacturing:** Forecast drives **MRP** and production line scheduling.
- **Retail (e.g., Lulu Mall, BigBasket):** Drives **replenishment orders** and safety stock.
- **FMCG (e.g., HUL, Nestlé India):** Drives **promotion planning** and distribution centre allocation.
- **E-Commerce (e.g., Flipkart, Amazon):** Drives **warehouse placement** and **dynamic pricing**.
- **Healthcare:** Drives **vaccine / PPE inventory** policy.
- **Automotive:** Drives **Tier-1/Tier-2 supplier schedules**.

> A 1% improvement in forecast accuracy in a large FMCG company saves **millions of dollars** in working capital annually. Hence, demand forecasting is a **profit lever**, not merely a planning input.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section shows the complete derivation of the three most-tested KTU models: **Exponential Smoothing**, **Holt's Trend-Adjusted Method**, and **Linear Regression**, plus a fully worked numerical problem.

### 3.1 Derivation of Simple Exponential Smoothing (Algebraic Form)

**Step 1 — Start with the Weighted Moving Average idea.**  
A WMA assigns weights $w_0, w_1, w_2, \dots$ to past demands such that $w_0 > w_1 > w_2 > \dots$ and $\sum w_i = 1$.

$$F_{t+1} = w_0 D_t + w_1 D_{t-1} + w_2 D_{t-2} + \dots$$

**Step 2 — Choose a geometric decay.**  
Let $w_0 = \alpha$, $w_1 = \alpha(1-\alpha)$, $w_2 = \alpha(1-\alpha)^2$, etc. Summing this infinite geometric series:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha(1-\alpha)^{i} = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-(1-\alpha)} = 1 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and split off the first term.**

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)\bigl[\alpha D_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)D_{t-2} + \dots\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the bracket as $F_t$.**

$$\boxed{\,F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t\,}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent error-correction form** (often used in exam):

$$F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha(D_t - F_t) \quad \text{where } \alpha(D_t - F_t) = \alpha\, e_t$$

This is why SES is sometimes called the **"error-correction" model** — every new forecast is the old forecast plus a fraction of the last error.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Holt's Trend-Adjusted Exponential Smoothing

**Step 1 — Decompose the series into Level $L_t$ and Trend $T_t$.**

$$D_t \approx L_t + \varepsilon_t \quad ; \quad L_t = L_{t-1} + T_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 2 — Define two smoothing equations with two constants $\alpha, \beta$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_t &= \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}) \quad &\text{(level update)} \\
T_t &= \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1} \quad &\text{(trend update)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Forecast $\tau$ periods ahead** by extrapolating along the trend line.

$$\boxed{\,F_{t+\tau} = L_t + \tau\, T_t\,}$$

When $\tau = 1$ we get the immediate next-period forecast: $F_{t+1} = L_t + T_t$.

**Boundary conditions (initialisation):**
- $L_1 = D_1$
- $T_1 = D_2 - D_1$ (or $T_1 = 0$ for a flat start; check your KTU problem statement).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Linear Regression Coefficients (Least-Squares)

We minimise the sum of squared errors $\sum e_t^{2} = \sum (y_t - a - bx_t)^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Partial derivatives set to zero.**

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial a}\sum (y - a - bx)^2 = -2\sum(y - a - bx) = 0$$

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial b}\sum (y - a - bx)^2 = -2\sum x(y - a - bx) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve the simultaneous equations.**

$$\sum y = na + b\sum x \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$$

$$\sum xy = a\sum x + b\sum x^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $a$ and simplify.**

$$\sum xy = (\bar{y}-b\bar{x})\sum x + b\sum x^{2}$$

$$b\sum x^{2} - b\bar{x}\sum x = \sum xy - \bar{y}\sum x$$

$$\boxed{\,b = \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^{2} - (\sum x)^{2}}\,} \quad ; \quad \boxed{\,a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** Monthly demand for a product over the last 6 months is: 40, 45, 48, 43, 50, 47 units. Using **Simple Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.3$ and $F_1 = D_1 = 40$**, compute: (a) the forecast for month 7, and (b) MAD, MAPE, and Tracking Signal.

**Part (a) — Sequential Forecast Calculation**

Using $F_{t+1} = F_t + 0.3(D_t - F_t)$:

| Month $t$ | Actual $D_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ | Error $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $\dfrac{\vert e_t \vert}{\vert D_t \vert}\cdot 100$ |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|--------------------------|-------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| 1 | 40 | 40 (given) | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 45 | $40 + 0.3(40-40) = 40.00$ | 5.00 | 5.00 | 11.11 |
| 3 | 48 | $40 + 0.3(45-40) = 41.50$ | 6.50 | 6.50 | 13.54 |
| 4 | 43 | $41.50 + 0.3(48-41.5) = 43.45$ | $-0.45$ | 0.45 | 1.05 |
| 5 | 50 | $43.45 + 0.3(43-43.45) = 43.32$ | 6.68 | 6.68 | 13.36 |
| 6 | 47 | $43.32 + 0.3(50-43.32) = 45.32$ | 1.68 | 1.68 | 3.57 |

**Computing $F_7$:**

$$F_7 = F_6 + \alpha(D_6 - F_6) = 45.32 + 0.3(47 - 45.32) = 45.32 + 0.504 = \mathbf{45.82 \text{ units}}$$

**Part (b) — Error Metrics**

Using only the forecasts $F_2$ through $F_6$ (i.e., 5 forecast errors, because $F_1$ is the initial value):

$$\text{MAD} = \dfrac{5.00 + 6.50 + 0.45 + 6.68 + 1.68}{5} = \dfrac{20.31}{5} = \mathbf{4.06 \text{ units}}$$

$$\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{11.11 + 13.54 + 1.05 + 13.36 + 3.57}{5} = \dfrac{42.63}{5} = \mathbf{8.53\%}$$

$$\text{RSFE} = 5.00 + 6.50 + (-0.45) + 6.68 + 1.68 = \mathbf{19.41}$$

$$\text{Tracking Signal} = \dfrac{\text{RSFE}}{\text{MAD}} = \dfrac{19.41}{4.06} \approx \mathbf{4.78}$$

**Interpretation:** $\vert \text{TS} \vert \approx 4.78 > 4$ ⇒ the forecast has a **positive bias** (it is systematically *under-forecasting*). The forecast model needs re-tuning — either increase $\alpha$, switch to a trend model (Holt's), or investigate a structural shift in demand.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Data Characteristics | Recommended Model | Reasoning |
|----------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| No trend, no seasonality, very short history | Naïve / SMA (n=2,3) | Minimal parameters |
| No trend, no seasonality, ≥ 10 periods | Simple Exponential Smoothing | Auto-weights recency |
| Linear trend, no seasonality | Holt's Method | Tracks level + slope |
| Trend + seasonality | Holt-Winters | Adds seasonal indices |
| Causal drivers available (price, ad spend) | Linear / Multiple Regression | Captures cause-effect |
| Highly volatile, many external factors | ARIMA / Machine Learning | Captures complex patterns |
| New product, no history | Qualitative (Delphi, Analogous) | Data-free judgment |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Demand Forecasting Process (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1 Define Objective and Horizon"] --> B["Step 2 Collect Historical Demand Data"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Identify Demand Pattern"]
    C --> C1["Pattern A Trend Present"]
    C --> C2["Pattern B Seasonality Present"]
    C --> C3["Pattern C Random Variation Only"]
    C1 --> D["Step 4 Select Forecasting Model"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1["Model 1 Moving Average"]
    D --> D2["Model 2 Exponential Smoothing"]
    D --> D3["Model 3 Holt Trend Method"]
    D --> D4["Model 4 Linear Regression"]
    D1 --> E["Step 5 Generate Forecast"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F["Step 6 Calculate Error Metrics"]
    F --> F1["Metric MAD"]
    F --> F2["Metric MSE"]
    F --> F3["Metric MAPE"]
    F --> F4["Metric Tracking Signal"]
    F1 --> G["Step 7 Compare Models and Select Best"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H{"TS within plus minus 4"}
    H -->|Yes| I["Step 8 Deploy Forecast to Supply Chain Planning"]
    H -->|No| J["Step 8b Re-tune Parameters or Switch Model"]
    J --> D
    I --> K["Outputs Production Plan Inventory Policy Procurement Budget"]
```

### 4.2 Forecasting Methods Taxonomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Demand Forecasting Methods"] --> R1["Qualitative Judgmental"]
    R --> R2["Quantitative Mathematical"]
    R1 --> R1A["Executive Opinion"]
    R1 --> R1B["Sales Force Composite"]
    R1 --> R1C["Delphi Method"]
    R1 --> R1D["Market Research"]
    R2 --> R2A["Time Series Models"]
    R2 --> R2B["Causal Models"]
    R2 --> R2C["AI Simulation Models"]
    R2A --> R2A1["Naive Method"]
    R2A --> R2A2["Moving Average SMA"]
    R2A --> R2A3["Weighted Moving Average WMA"]
    R2A --> R2A4["Exponential Smoothing SES"]
    R2A --> R2A5["Holt Trend Adjusted"]
    R2A --> R2A6["Holt Winters Seasonal"]
    R2B --> R2B1["Linear Regression"]
    R2B --> R2B2["Multiple Regression"]
    R2C --> R2C1["ARIMA"]
    R2C --> R2C2["Monte Carlo Simulation"]
    R2C --> R2C3["Neural Networks"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Forecasting Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Input Layer Data Sources"] --> L2["Preprocessing Layer Cleaning and Outlier Removal"]
    L2 --> L3["Pattern Detection Layer Trend Seasonality Noise"]
    L3 --> L4["Model Layer SMA SES Holt Regression ARIMA"]
    L4 --> L5["Forecast Output Layer Point Forecast Confidence Interval"]
    L5 --> L6["Validation Layer MAD MAPE Tracking Signal"]
    L6 --> L7{"Is Forecast Acceptable"}
    L7 -->|Yes| L8["Deployment Layer ERP MRP Replenishment Planning"]
    L7 -->|No| L9["Re Training Layer Tune Parameters or Switch Model"]
    L9 --> L4
```

### 4.4 Forecast Error Decomposition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E["Total Forecast Error D minus F"] --> E1["Bias Component Mean Error"]
    E --> E2["Random Component MAD or MAPE"]
    E1 --> F1["Corrected by Adjusting Alpha or Adding Trend"]
    E2 --> F2["Reduced by Smoothing Larger Sample Size"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**  
*Define demand forecasting. List any four qualitative methods of forecasting.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**  
**Demand Forecasting** is the process of estimating future customer demand over a defined time horizon, using historical data, statistical models, and managerial judgment, to support supply chain planning decisions (1 mark).  
**Four Qualitative Methods (2 marks):**
1. **Executive Opinion** — Senior management collectively estimates demand.
2. **Sales Force Composite** — Salespeople estimate from their territories.
3. **Delphi Method** — Anonymous, iterative expert panel technique.
4. **Market Research / Customer Surveys** — Direct data from end-users.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**  
*Differentiate between qualitative and quantitative forecasting methods. When is each preferred?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Basis | Judgment, opinion, experience | Mathematical/statistical models |
| Data | No/scarce historical data | Reliable historical data required |
| Use case | New product launch, disruptive change | Mature products, stable environment |
| Examples | Delphi, market research | Moving average, regression, ARIMA |

Quantitative is preferred when **historical numerical data is available** and the environment is **stable**; qualitative is preferred when launching **new products** or in **disruptive/changing markets** (1 mark).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** KTU ESE Module-Internal Choice pattern: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from a module.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain **Simple Exponential Smoothing** with its mathematical formulation. Discuss how the value of the smoothing constant $\alpha$ affects the forecast. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The monthly demand for a product over the past 5 months is given below. Using a **3-month Simple Moving Average**, compute the forecast for months 4, 5, and 6. Also compute MAD and MAPE. **(7 Marks)**

| Month | Demand (units) |
|-------|----------------|
| 1 | 100 |
| 2 | 110 |
| 3 | 120 |
| 4 | 115 |
| 5 | 130 |

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** SES is a time-series forecasting technique that produces a new forecast by combining the previous forecast with the most recent actual demand, weighted by the smoothing constant $\alpha$.

**Mathematical Formulation (3 marks):**  
$$F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t \equiv F_t + \alpha(D_t - F_t)$$

where $D_t$ = actual demand in period $t$, $F_t$ = forecast for period $t$, $\alpha$ = smoothing constant with $0 \le \alpha \le 1$.

**Effect of $\alpha$ (3 marks):**
- **Low $\alpha$ (close to 0):** Forecast is heavily influenced by past forecasts (slow response, smoothes out noise). Suitable for stable demand.
- **High $\alpha$ (close to 1):** Forecast reacts quickly to the latest demand (responsive but noisy). Suitable for volatile demand.
- **Trade-off:** Higher $\alpha$ ⇒ less smoothing ⇒ more responsive but lower stability. Lower $\alpha$ ⇒ more smoothing ⇒ more stable but slower reaction.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

Using 3-month SMA: $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{D_t + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2}}{3}$

| Month $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ (3-MA) | $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $\dfrac{\vert e_t \vert}{\vert D_t \vert}\times 100$ |
|-----------|-------|---------------|------------------|--------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| 1 | 100 | — | — | — | — |
