| 2 | 110 | — | — | — | — |
| 3 | 120 | — | — | — | — |
| 4 | 115 | $\frac{120+110+100}{3}=110.00$ | 5.00 | 5.00 | 4.35 |
| 5 | 130 | $\frac{115+120+110}{3}=115.00$ | 15.00 | 15.00 | 11.54 |
| 6 | ? | $\frac{130+115+120}{3}=121.67$ | — | — | — |

**MAD (over 2 forecast errors):**

$$\text{MAD} = \dfrac{5.00 + 15.00}{2} = \mathbf{10.00 \text{ units}}$$

**MAPE (over 2 forecast errors):**

$$\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{4.35 + 11.54}{2} = \mathbf{7.94\%}$$

**Forecast for Month 6 = 121.67 units ≈ 122 units.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain **Holt's Trend-Adjusted Exponential Smoothing** method. Derive the equations for level, trend, and forecast. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The quarterly sales of a company for the last 4 quarters are: 200, 220, 240, 260 units. Fit a **linear trend line** using least-squares regression and forecast sales for quarter 7. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Holt's method extends SES to handle time series with a **linear trend** by maintaining two smoothed components — level $L_t$ and trend $T_t$ — each updated by its own smoothing constant ($\alpha$ and $\beta$).

**Equations (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_t &= \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}) \\
T_t &= \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1} \\
F_{t+\tau} &= L_t + \tau \cdot T_t
\end{aligned}$$

**Initialisation (1 mark):** $L_1 = D_1$ and $T_1 = D_2 - D_1$.

**Application (1 mark):** Used for time series where demand is rising or falling steadily but has no seasonality (e.g., growing tech product before market saturation).

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Setting up variables (1 mark):** Let $x$ = period index ($x=1,2,3,4$), $y$ = demand ($y=200,220,240,260$).

**Compute required sums (2 marks):**

$$\sum x = 1+2+3+4 = 10 \quad ; \quad \sum y = 200+220+240+260 = 920$$

$$\sum x^2 = 1+4+9+16 = 30 \quad ; \quad \sum xy = 200 + 440 + 720 + 1040 = 2400$$

$$\bar{x} = 2.5 \quad ; \quad \bar{y} = 230$$

**Slope and Intercept (3 marks):**

$$b = \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^{2} - (\sum x)^{2}} = \dfrac{4(2400) - (10)(920)}{4(30) - (10)^{2}} = \dfrac{9600 - 9200}{120 - 100} = \dfrac{400}{20} = 20$$

$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = 230 - 20(2.5) = 230 - 50 = 180$$

**Regression Equation & Forecast (1 mark):**

$$\hat{y} = 180 + 20x$$

For $x=7$: $\hat{y} = 180 + 20(7) = \mathbf{320 \text{ units}}$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up $F_t$ notation.** In SES, $F_{t+1}$ uses *previous* $F_t$ and *current* $D_t$, not the other way around. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Wrong window in Moving Average.** A 3-month MA for month 4 uses months 1, 2, 3 — *not* months 2, 3, 4. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **MAPE denominator zero.** If any actual demand $D_t = 0$, MAPE is undefined; switch to MAD or MSE. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Forgetting the initial forecast** ($F_1$ or $L_1$) when error-metric denominators are calculated. Always use $n$ = (number of forecasts generated), not total periods. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Tracking Signal interpretation.** $\vert \text{TS} \vert > 4$ does **not** mean the forecast is "bad" — it means the model has *bias* and needs re-tuning. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Confusing Holt's and SES.** Students often use SES on a trending series, leading to persistent bias (high TS). Always *check* the pattern first. **(−2 marks)**
> 7. **Units confusion.** MAD is in *units of demand*; MAPE is in *%*. Never compare numerically. **(−1 mark)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Demand Forecasting**
> - ✅ Demand Forecasting = estimating future demand to drive **planning** decisions (production, inventory, procurement, capacity).
> - ✅ Two families: **Qualitative** (judgment) and **Quantitative** (mathematical: time-series & causal).
> - ✅ Naïve forecast: $F_{t+1} = D_t$ — the baseline benchmark.
> - ✅ Simple Moving Average: equal weights; $n$ = window length; equal smoothing across window.
> - ✅ Weighted Moving Average: weights must sum to 1; more weight on recent periods.
> - ✅ Simple Exponential Smoothing: $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$; only **level** is tracked.
> - ✅ Holt's Method: tracks **level + trend** with two constants $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
> - ✅ Holt-Winters: tracks **level + trend + seasonality** with three constants $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$.
> - ✅ Linear Regression: $\hat{y} = a + bx$ — used when causal drivers or time trend is present.
> - ✅ **MAD, MSE, RMSE** in units of demand; **MAPE** in %; **Tracking Signal** is dimensionless.
> - ✅ $\vert \text{TS} \vert \le 4$ ⇒ forecast is **in control**; otherwise it has **bias**.
> - ✅ **High $\alpha$** ⇒ responsive but noisy; **Low $\alpha$** ⇒ smooth but slow.
> - ✅ Forecasting is **not about a single number** — it is about **bounding uncertainty** for better supply chain decisions.
> - ✅ Always **decompose** demand into **Level + Trend + Seasonality + Noise** before choosing a model.
> - ✅ KTU Module 2 most-tested formulas: **SES**, **3-month MA**, **Linear Regression slope/intercept**, **MAD, MAPE, Tracking Signal**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Forecasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Role of Forecasting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Forecasting** is the process of estimating or predicting future demand for products, services, or resources based on historical data, statistical models, expert judgment, and market intelligence. In the context of Supply Chain Management (SCM), forecasting serves as the **foundational planning primitive** that drives all downstream supply chain decisions — including inventory positioning, production scheduling, procurement planning, capacity expansion, distribution network design, and financial budgeting.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme OESFT832 syllabus, forecasting is classified as a **strategic enabler of Supply Chain (SC) coordination**, bridging the demand side (markets, customers) with the supply side (factories, warehouses, suppliers) under conditions of **demand uncertainty**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** "Forecasting in supply chain management is the art and science of predicting future events in the supply chain to enable proactive, cost-effective, and customer-satisfying decision-making across procurement, production, distribution, and logistics functions."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you run a small **tea stall in Kerala** during the monsoon. Every day, you must decide how many kilograms of tea leaves, sugar, milk, and biscuits to stock. If you guess too little, customers leave disappointed and you lose sales. If you guess too much, the milk spoils and you lose money. **Forecasting** is simply the skill of looking at the past 30 days of sales, noticing that it rains more on Wednesdays, and intelligently **predicting** that next Wednesday you will need 20% more stock.

In a B.Tech engineering context, forecasting is the **"crystal ball"** of operations management — it doesn't give perfect answers, but it converts chaotic historical noise into a structured probability distribution of future demand, allowing managers to **plan rationally** rather than **react emotionally**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** All supply chain decisions (How much to produce? When to order? Where to store?) are fundamentally answers to the question — *"What will the customer want, and when?"* Forecasting is the mechanism that answers this question.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Demand Variability** — Fluctuation in customer demand over time.
- **Forecast Horizon** — The length of time into the future for which a forecast is made.
- **Forecast Accuracy** — Degree to which a forecast matches actual realized demand.
- **Forecast Bias** — Systematic tendency of a forecast to consistently over- or under-predict.
- **Lead Time** — Time gap between placing an order and receiving it; **the single most critical variable forecasting must offset**.

### 1.4 Strategic Positioning of Forecasting in the Supply Chain

Forecasting sits at the **upstream node** of the SCOR (Supply Chain Operations Reference) model's Plan process. It feeds into:

- **Plan** → Demand planning, S&OP (Sales & Operations Planning)
- **Source** → Procurement quantities, supplier contracts
- **Make** → Production schedules, capacity utilization
- **Deliver** → Warehouse stocking, transportation load planning
- **Return** → Reverse logistics volume estimates

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 7 Strategic Roles of Forecasting in Supply Chain Management

Forecasting is not a stand-alone clerical activity — it is a **strategic capability**. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly categorizes the following 7 high-yield roles:

#### Role 1: **Demand Planning Foundation**
- Forecasting translates market signals (POS data, macroeconomic indicators) into a structured demand plan.
- **Why it matters:** Without a demand plan, every other decision in the SC is reactive. **How:** Uses time-series analysis, regression, and judgmental adjustments.

#### Role 2: **Inventory Optimization Driver**
- Safety stock, reorder points (ROP), and Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) all depend on demand forecast error.
- **Why it matters:** Over-forecasting inflates holding costs; under-forecasting triggers stockouts.
- **How:** Forecasts feed the formula: `Safety Stock = Z × σ_LT`, where `σ_LT` is the standard deviation of lead-time demand (itself a forecast output).

#### Role 3: **Production Planning & Scheduling Enabler**
- Master Production Schedule (MPS) and Material Requirements Planning (MRP) use forecasts to release work orders.
- **Why it matters:** Aligns shop-floor capacity with anticipated demand, minimizing changeover costs and overtime.

#### Role 4: **Capacity Planning & Capital Investment Justification**
- Long-horizon forecasts (3–5 years) inform decisions on plant size, equipment purchase, warehouse expansion.
- **Why it matters:** Capacity is lumpy and slow to build — a 24-month lead time on a new factory cannot wait for reactive data.

#### Role 5: **Supplier Relationship & Procurement Management**
- Forecasts shared with suppliers enable Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI) and Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment (CPFR).
- **Why it matters:** Suppliers with accurate forecasts reduce their own bullwhip exposure.

#### Role 6: **Financial Planning, Budgeting & Cash Flow**
- Revenue forecasts (demand × price) drive cash flow projections, working capital management, and investor reporting.
- **Why it matters:** A 10% revenue forecast error in a ₹500 Cr firm = ₹50 Cr cash flow volatility.

#### Role 7: **Risk Management & Scenario Planning**
- Multiple forecast scenarios (best case, base case, worst case) feed Monte Carlo simulations and risk registers.
- **Why it matters:** Climate events, pandemics, geopolitical shocks (e.g., Suez blockage) all manifest first as demand anomalies that forecasting must capture.

### 2.2 Classification of Forecasting Methods

Forecasting methods are classified along two primary axes:

| Axis | Class A | Class B |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Source** | **Qualitative** (judgmental, expert) | **Quantitative** (statistical, mathematical) |
| **Time Horizon** | **Short-term** (< 3 months) | **Long-term** (> 1 year) |
| **Demand Pattern** | **Independent demand** (end products) | **Dependent demand** (components, derived from BOM) |
| **Pattern Type** | **Stationary** (no trend/season) | **Trending / Seasonal / Cyclical** |

### 2.3 Major Forecasting Techniques (KTU High-Yield Inventory)

1. **Naive Forecast:** $F_{t+1} = A_t$ (next period = current actual)
2. **Simple Moving Average (SMA):** $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} A_{t-i}$
3. **Weighted Moving Average (WMA):** $F_{t+1} = \sum w_i A_{t-i}$, where $\sum w_i = 1$
4. **Exponential Smoothing (ES):** $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$
5. **Holt's Linear Trend Method:** Two equations (level + trend), uses smoothing constants $\alpha$ and $\beta$
6. **Winter's Triple Exponential Smoothing:** Adds seasonal smoothing constant $\gamma$
7. **Linear Regression (Trend Line):** $Y = a + bX$
8. **Delphi Method:** Iterative expert consensus
9. **Market Research / Customer Surveys:** Qualitative
10. **Analogy / Bass Diffusion Model:** For new product forecasting

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Reference Table for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Naive Forecast** | $F_{t+1} = A_t$ | $A_t$ = actual demand at time $t$ | Baseline benchmark |
| 2 | **Simple Moving Average** | $F_{t+1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_{t-i+1}}{n}$ | $n$ = window size | Stable demand, no trend |
| 3 | **Weighted Moving Average** | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i A_{t-i+1}$ | $w_i$ = weights, $\sum w_i = 1$ | Recent data weighted higher |
| 4 | **Exponential Smoothing** | $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ = smoothing constant | Quick response, no trend |
| 5 | **Holt's Level Eq.** | $L_t = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$ | $L_t$ = level | Trending demand |
| 6 | **Holt's Trend Eq.** | $T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}$ | $T_t$ = trend, $\beta$ = trend smoothing | Trending demand |
| 7 | **Holt's Forecast** | $F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot T_t$ | $m$ = periods ahead | Trending demand |
| 8 | **MAPE** | $\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{A_i - F_i}{A_i} \right\vert \times 100$ | All % | Accuracy in % |
| 9 | **MAD** | $\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert A_i - F_i \vert$ | Absolute deviations | Scale-dependent error |
| 10 | **MSE** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(A_i - F_i)^2$ | Squared errors | Penalizes large errors |
| 11 | **Tracking Signal** | $\text{TS} = \frac{\sum (A_i - F_i)}{\text{MAD}}$ | Cumulative forecast error | Bias detection |
| 12 | **Safety Stock** | $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | $Z$ = service level z-score, $\sigma_{LT}$ = std dev of LT demand | Inventory buffer |
| 13 | **Linear Trend Regression** | $F_t = a + b \cdot t$ | $a$ = intercept, $b$ = slope | Long-term forecasting |
| 14 | **Slope of Trend** | $b = \frac{n\sum t A_t - \sum t \sum A_t}{n\sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}$ | — | Regression coefficient |
| 15 | **Demand Forecast (Aggregation)** | $D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i$ | $D_i$ = per-period forecast | Aggregate SC planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Exam Rule:** Never write `|x|` for absolute value inside markdown tables — it breaks the table syntax. The system replaces it with `\vert x \vert` for LaTeX compatibility.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti):** Forecasting drives the entire MRP system. A miscalibrated demand forecast propagates downstream as the **Bullwhip Effect** — where variance amplifies up the supply chain.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Diwali, Onam, and Big-Billion-Day sales are pre-forecasted 6 months in advance to position inventory in regional fulfillment centers.
- **Healthcare (Apollo, KIMS):** Forecasting ICU bed demand during epidemics (COVID-19) directly determined supply of oxygen, PPE, and ventilators.
- **Agriculture (Kerala Rubber, Spices Board):** Crop yield forecasts integrate weather data, soil data, and historical yield for export planning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Exponential Smoothing Recurrence

Exponential Smoothing is the **single most important forecasting method** in the KTU syllabus. Here is its complete derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the Forecast at Time $t$ for Period $t+1$**

We want to express $F_{t+1}$ as a weighted blend of the most recent actual demand $A_t$ and the previous forecast $F_t$:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t$$

where $\alpha$ is the smoothing constant, with $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$.

**Step 2 — Expand the Recursion**

Substitute $F_t$ from its own recursion:

$$F_t = \alpha A_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) F_{t-1}$$

Substituting back:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)\left[\alpha A_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) F_{t-1}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Distribute the $(1-\alpha)$ Term**

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) A_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 F_{t-1}$$

**Step 4 — Recursively Expand to $n$ Steps**

After $n$ recursions, we obtain:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (1-\alpha)^i A_{t-i} + (1-\alpha)^n F_{t-n+1}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (Why "Exponential"?)**

The weights assigned to past observations are:

$$\alpha, \; \alpha(1-\alpha), \; \alpha(1-\alpha)^2, \; \alpha(1-\alpha)^3, \; \ldots$$

These form a **geometric (exponential) decay** — hence the name. The sum of all weights converges to 1:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha (1-\alpha)^i = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{1-(1-\alpha)} = 1$$

**Step 6 — Limiting Behavior Analysis**

- If $\alpha \to 1$: $F_{t+1} \to A_t$ (Naive forecast — full trust on latest actual)
- If $\alpha \to 0$: $F_{t+1} \to F_t$ (Forecast never updates — useless)
- Typical KTU/industry value: $\alpha = 0.1$ to $0.3$ for stable demand; $\alpha = 0.5$ to $0.8$ for volatile demand

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Exponential Smoothing

**Problem:** Given the demand data below and $\alpha = 0.3$, $F_1 = 50$, compute the forecast for periods 2 through 6. Also compute MAD and Tracking Signal.

| Period $t$ | Actual $A_t$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 50 |
| 2 | 54 |
| 3 | 48 |
| 4 | 60 |
| 5 | 58 |
| 6 | 62 |

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Period 2:**
$$F_2 = 0.3(50) + 0.7(50) = 15 + 35 = 50.0$$

**Period 3:**
$$F_3 = 0.3(54) + 0.7(50.0) = 16.2 + 35.0 = 51.2$$

**Period 4:**
$$F_4 = 0.3(48) + 0.7(51.2) = 14.4 + 35.84 = 50.24$$

**Period 5:**
$$F_5 = 0.3(60) + 0.7(50.24) = 18.0 + 35.168 = 53.168$$

**Period 6:**
$$F_6 = 0.3(58) + 0.7(53.168) = 17.4 + 37.218 = 54.618$$

**Period 7 Forecast:**
$$F_7 = 0.3(62) + 0.7(54.618) = 18.6 + 38.233 = 56.833$$

**Error Table:**

| Period $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | Error $e_t = A_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | Cumulative Error (RSFE) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 54 | 50.0 | +4.0 | 4.0 | +4.0 |
| 3 | 48 | 51.2 | -3.2 | 3.2 | +0.8 |
| 4 | 60 | 50.24 | +9.76 | 9.76 | +10.56 |
| 5 | 58 | 53.168 | +4.832 | 4.832 | +15.392 |
| 6 | 62 | 54.618 | +7.382 | 7.382 | +22.774 |

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{4.0 + 3.2 + 9.76 + 4.832 + 7.382}{5} = \frac{29.174}{5} = 5.835$$

$$\text{Tracking Signal} = \frac{\text{RSFE}}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{22.774}{5.835} \approx 3.90$$

**Interpretation:** A tracking signal magnitude exceeding $\pm 4$ (some KTU books use $\pm 3.75$) signals a **biased forecast** — the model is systematically under-forecasting. Action: increase $\alpha$ or re-evaluate model.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: 3-Period Simple Moving Average

**Data:** Demand for last 5 periods = 40, 45, 50, 55, 60 units.

**Step 1 — Compute Forecast for Period 6 (using 3-period window):**

$$F_6 = \frac{A_3 + A_4 + A_5}{3} = \frac{50 + 55 + 60}{3} = \frac{165}{3} = 55 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Forecast for Period 7 (sliding window):**

$$F_7 = \frac{A_4 + A_5 + A_6}{3}$$

We need $A_6$ — assume actual $A_6 = 65$ (given post-hoc):

$$F_7 = \frac{55 + 60 + 65}{3} = \frac{180}{3} = 60 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Compute MAPE for Forecasts $F_4, F_5, F_6$ (assuming we forecasted 3-period back):**

- $F_4 = (40+45+50)/3 = 45$, Actual = 55, % Error = 18.18%
- $F_5 = (45+50+55)/3 = 50$, Actual = 60, % Error = 16.67%
- $F_6 = 55$ (computed above), Actual = 65, % Error = 15.38%

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{18.18 + 16.67 + 15.38}{3} \approx 16.74\%$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Forecasting Method (KTU Board-Ready Table)

| Demand Pattern | Recommended Method | Smoothing Constants | Forecast Horizon |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant (stationary)** | Simple Moving Average / Naive | $n = 3$ to $5$ | Short-term |
| **Random walk (noisy)** | Exponential Smoothing | $\alpha = 0.1$ to $0.3$ | Short-term |
| **Linear Trend** | Holt's Two-Parameter | $\alpha, \beta \in [0.1, 0.3]$ | Medium-term |
| **Seasonal** | Winter's / Decomposition | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ | Medium-term |
| **Cyclical** | Regression with macro variables | — | Long-term |
| **New product (no history)** | Delphi / Bass Diffusion | — | Long-term |
| **Intermittent demand** (spare parts) | Croston's Method | — | Short-term |
| **Highly aggregated (SKU family)** | Hierarchical Forecasting | — | Medium-term |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Strategic Role-Map of Forecasting in the Supply Chain

Below is a **Mermaid block-level functional architecture flow** depicting the 7 strategic roles of forecasting mapped to SC decision areas:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Historical Demand Data + Market Signals] --> B[Forecasting Engine]
    B --> C[Demand Plan & Forecast Output]
    C --> D[Role 1: Demand Planning Foundation]
    C --> E[Role 2: Inventory Optimization Driver]
    C --> F[Role 3: Production Scheduling Enabler]
    C --> G[Role 4: Capacity Planning Trigger]
    C --> H[Role 5: Supplier Coordination Input]
    C --> I[Role 6: Financial Planning Basis]
    C --> J[Role 7: Risk Management Scenario Input]
    D --> K[Sales and Operations Planning S and OP]
    E --> L[Safety Stock and Reorder Point Calculation]
    F --> M[Master Production Schedule MPS]
    G --> N[Capital Investment Decisions]
    H --> O[Vendor Managed Inventory VMI and CPFR]
    I --> P[Revenue and Cash Flow Forecast]
    J --> Q[Monte Carlo Simulation and Risk Register]
    K --> R[Integrated Supply Chain Decision Output]
    L --> R
    M --> R
    N --> R
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

### 4.2 Forecast Method Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Demand Pattern] --> B{Historical Data Available?}
    B -- No --> C[Use Qualitative Methods: Delphi, Market Research, Analogous Products]
    B -- Yes --> D{Trend Present?}
    D -- No --> E{Seasonality Present?}
    D -- Yes --> F[Use Holt Linear Trend Method]
    E -- No --> G[Use Simple Moving Average or Exponential Smoothing]
    E -- Yes --> H[Use Winters Triple Exponential Smoothing]
    F --> I[Validate with MAD, MAPE, Tracking Signal]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    C --> I
    I --> J{Accuracy Acceptable?}
    J -- No --> K[Adjust Smoothing Constants or Switch Method]
    J -- Yes --> L[Deploy Forecast to Supply Chain Functions]
    K --> I
```

### 4.3 Forecast Error Feedback Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Generate Forecast F_t] --> B[Apply Forecast to Plan]
    B --> C[Execute Plan: Produce and Procure]
    C --> D[Realized Actual Demand A_t]
    D --> E[Compute Forecast Error e_t = A_t minus F_t]
    E --> F[Update MAD, MAPE, Tracking Signal]
    F --> G{Bias Detected?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Adjust Smoothing Constant Alpha]
    G -- No --> I[Maintain Current Method]
    H --> A
    I --> A
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Forecasting Architecture (Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Level: Corporate Revenue Forecast] --> B[Divisional Forecast]
    B --> C[Product Family Forecast]
    C --> D[SKU Level Forecast]
    D --> E[Customer Order Forecast]
    E --> F[Allocation to Distribution Centers]
    F --> G[Daily Warehouse Replenishment]
    A -.Top Down Aggregation.-> B
    D -.Bottom Up Aggregation.-> A
    B -.Cross Validation.-> C
    C -.Cross Validation.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the above diagrams are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `C1`, `nodeA`) — none use reserved keywords like `end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, or `style` as standalone identifiers, ensuring clean Mermaid v10+ rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*"Explain any three strategic roles of forecasting in Supply Chain Management."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — points-wise):**

1. **Inventory Optimization:** Forecasting drives the calculation of safety stock and reorder points. Without a demand forecast, an SC cannot determine how much buffer inventory to hold, leading to either stockouts or excess holding cost. *(1 mark)*

2. **Production Planning Enabler:** The Master Production Schedule (MPS) and Material Requirements Planning (MRP) are entirely built on the demand forecast. Accurate forecasts allow smooth production runs, minimizing changeover costs. *(1 mark)*

3. **Capacity Planning and Capital Investment:** Long-horizon forecasts (3–5 years) justify investments in new plants, machinery, and warehouses. Capacity is "lumpy" and cannot be changed quickly, so accurate long-range forecasts are critical. *(1 mark)*

*(Optional 4th point for depth: S&OP enablement, supplier collaboration via CPFR, financial planning — KTU accepts any 3 valid roles.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*"Differentiate between Qualitative and Quantitative forecasting methods. Give one example of each."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Source** | Judgment, opinion, expert knowledge | Historical numerical data |
| **Mathematical Model** | Not used | Statistical/mathematical models |
| **Use Case** | New products, no history | Established products, ample data |
| **Accuracy** | Subjective, lower precision | Higher, verifiable |
| **Cost** | High (expert time) | Low (software-based) |
| **Example** | Delphi Method, Market Survey | Exponential Smoothing, Moving Average |

*(1 mark for the distinction, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for proper tabular presentation.)*

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both carry 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
*(a)* Explain the **7 strategic roles of forecasting** in Supply Chain Management with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* The demand for a product over the last 6 months is: 120, 135, 128, 142, 155, 160 units. Using **Exponential Smoothing with α = 0.4** and initial forecast $F_1 = 120$, compute forecasts for months 2 through 7. Also calculate the **MAD** and the **Tracking Signal** at the end of month 6. Comment on whether the forecast is biased. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

1. **Demand Planning Foundation** — Forecasting converts market signals into a structured demand plan, enabling S&OP. *Example:* Flipkart forecasting Diwali demand 6 months in advance. *(1 mark)*

2. **Inventory Optimization** — Forecasts feed safety stock and ROP calculations. *Example:* Amazon's regional FDCs use forecasts to pre-position inventory. *(1 mark)*

3. **Production Scheduling** — MPS and MRP are built on forecasts. *Example:* Maruti's monthly production plan. *(1 mark)*

4. **Capacity Planning** — Long-horizon forecasts inform plant/warehouse investment. *Example:* Tata Steel capacity expansion based on 5-year steel demand forecast. *(1 mark)*

5. **Supplier Coordination** — Shared forecasts enable VMI/CPFR. *Example:* Walmart and Procter & Gamble CPFR partnership. *(1 mark)*

6. **Financial Planning** — Revenue forecast = demand × price; drives cash flow. *Example:* Infosys quarterly revenue guidance. *(1 mark)*

7. **Risk Management** — Scenario forecasts feed Monte Carlo simulations. *Example:* COVID-19 ICU bed demand forecasts. *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks — Step-by-Step Computation]:**

**Step 1 — Recurrence $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ with $\alpha = 0.4$:**

$$F_2 = 0.4(120) + 0.6(120) = 48 + 72 = 120.0$$

$$F_3 = 0.4(135) + 0.6(120.0) = 54 + 72 = 126.0$$

$$F_4 = 0.4(128) + 0.6(126.0) = 51.2 + 75.6 = 126.8$$

$$F_5 = 0.4(142) + 0.6(126.8) = 56.8 + 76.08 = 132.88$$

$$F_6 = 0.4(155) + 0.6(132.88) = 62.0 + 79.728 = 141.728$$

$$F_7 = 0.4(160) + 0.6(141.728) = 64.0 + 85.037 = 149.037$$

**Step 2 — Error Table:**

| Month $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = A_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | Cumulative Error (RSFE) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 135 | 120.0 | +15.0 | 15.0 | +15.0 |
| 3 | 128 | 126.0 | +2.0 | 2.0 | +17.0 |
| 4 | 142 | 126.8 | +15.2 | 15.2 | +32.2 |
| 5 | 155 | 132.88 | +22.12 | 22.12 | +54.32 |
| 6 | 160 | 141.728 | +18.272 | 18.272 | +72.592 |

**Step 3 — Compute MAD:**

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{15.0 + 2.0 + 15.2 + 22.12 + 18.272}{5} = \frac{72.592}{5} = 14.518$$

**Step 4 — Compute Tracking Signal:**

$$\text{TS} = \frac{\text{RSFE}}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{72.592}{14.518} \approx 5.00$$

**Step 5 — Comment on Bias:**

Since $\vert \text{TS} \vert = 5.00$ exceeds the control limit of $\pm 4$ (or $\pm 3.75$ as per some textbooks), the forecast is **biased** — specifically, it is **systematically under-forecasting** (since TS is positive and large). **Recommendation:** Increase the smoothing constant $\alpha$ (e.g., to 0.6) to make the forecast more responsive to recent actuals, or switch to a trending method like Holt's.

*(Mark distribution: 3 marks for forecasts, 1 mark for MAD, 1 mark for TS, 2 marks for bias comment + recommendation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*(a)* Explain the **Bullwhip Effect** in supply chains. How does accurate forecasting help in mitigating the Bullwhip Effect? *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Compute a **3-period weighted moving average forecast** for the demand series 200, 220, 210, 230, 250, 240, 260 using weights $w_1 = 0.5$ (most recent), $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$ (oldest). Calculate the **MAPE** and comment on the forecast accuracy. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

The **Bullwhip Effect** is the phenomenon where small fluctuations in end-customer demand get amplified as they propagate upstream in the supply chain, causing manufacturers, suppliers, and raw material providers to experience progressively larger demand variability. *(2 marks)*

**Causes** *(2 marks — any 2 valid causes):*
- Demand forecast updating and order batching
- Price fluctuations and shortage gaming
- Order rationing by suppliers
- Long lead times

**Role of accurate forecasting in mitigation:** *(3 marks)*
- Sharing accurate, real-time forecasts across the supply chain (via **CPFR** — Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment) eliminates the need for each tier to maintain its own distorted forecast.
- Reduces safety stock inflation at upstream tiers.
- Enables **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** where the supplier sees the retailer's true demand and plans directly.
- Stable forecasts lead to stable orders, which lead to stable production — breaking the amplification loop.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Weighted Moving Average Formula:**

$$F_{t+1} = w_1 A_t + w_2 A_{t-1} + w_3 A_{t-2}$$

where $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$ and $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$.

**Step 2 — Compute Forecasts:**

$$F_4 = 0.5(210) + 0.3(220) + 0.2(200) = 105 + 66 + 40 = 211$$

$$F_5 = 0.5(230) + 0.3(210) + 0.2(220) = 115 + 63 + 44 = 222$$

$$F_6 = 0.5(250) + 0.3(230) + 0.2(210) = 125 + 69 + 42 = 236$$

$$F_7 = 0.5(240) + 0.3(250) + 0.2(230) = 120 + 75 + 46 = 241$$

**Step 3 — Compute Absolute Percentage Errors:**

| Period | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 230 | 211 | 19 | 8.26% |
| 5 | 250 | 222 | 28 | 11.20% |
| 6 | 240 | 236 | 4 | 1.67% |
| 7 | 260 | 241 | 19 | 7.31% |

**Step 4 — MAPE:**

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{8.26 + 11.20 + 1.67 + 7.31}{4} = \frac{28.44}{4} = 7.11\%$$

**Step 5 — Comment:**

A MAPE of **7.11%** indicates a **reasonably accurate forecast** in most SC contexts (industry-acceptable MAPE is typically < 10% for short-horizon demand). However, the higher errors at periods 4 and 5 suggest that the weighting scheme may need adjustment to capture sudden demand jumps (e.g., increasing $w_1$ from 0.5 to 0.6).

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for forecast calculations, 2 marks for MAPE, 1 mark for MAPE result, 2 marks for interpretation and recommendation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MAD and MAPE** — MAD is in units of demand; MAPE is a percentage. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the initial forecast $F_1$** — KTU frequently gives $F_1$ explicitly; ignoring it shows careless reading.
> 3. **Not showing the recursion expansion in exponential smoothing** — Simply writing the final forecast without the step-by-step $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ loses process marks.
> 4. **Using `|x|` instead of `\vert x \vert` in formula sheets** — Although the LaTeX is correct, KTU expects clean handwritten notation. In exams, always write `|x|` carefully with proper brackets.
> 5. **Ignoring bias interpretation in Tracking Signal problems** — Computing the TS without commenting on whether $\vert TS \vert > 4$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up Holt's and Winter's methods** — Holt's has $\alpha, \beta$ (level + trend). Winter's adds $\gamma$ (seasonality). Students often forget the third constant.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Forecasting is the cornerstone of supply chain planning** — it feeds every downstream SC decision.
- **7 Strategic Roles:** Demand planning, inventory optimization, production scheduling, capacity planning, supplier coordination, financial planning, risk management.
- **Two Major Method Classes:** Qualitative (Delphi, surveys) vs. Quantitative (Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, Regression).
- **Naive Forecast** = $F_{t+1} = A_t$ — the baseline benchmark.
- **Simple Moving Average** uses an arithmetic mean of $n$ past periods; works well for stable, stationary demand.
- **Weighted Moving Average** assigns higher weight to recent data; useful when recent trends matter more.
- **Exponential Smoothing** uses the recurrence $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$; weights decay geometrically.
- **$\alpha$ small (0.1–0.3)** → stable demand, slow response; **$\alpha$ large (0.5–0.8)** → volatile demand, fast response.
- **MAD** measures average absolute error (in demand units).
- **MAPE** measures average percentage error (dimensionless, %).
- **MSE** penalizes large errors quadratically.
- **Tracking Signal** = RSFE / MAD. If $\vert \text{TS} \vert > 4$, forecast is **biased** (systematically over- or under-forecasting).
- **Holt's Method** = Exponential Smoothing + Linear Trend (uses $\alpha$ and $\beta$).
- **Winter's Method** = Holt's + Seasonality (uses $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$).
- **Bullwhip Effect** = demand variability amplification upstream; mitigated by collaborative forecasting (CPFR) and VMI.
- **Forecast Horizon:** Short-term (≤ 3 months) for operational decisions; long-term (≥ 1 year) for strategic/capacity decisions.
- **Always validate** a forecasting model with MAD, MAPE, and Tracking Signal before deployment.
- **KTU 2024 Specific:** Questions worth 14 marks expect sub-parts of 7+7; always present the **formula first, then substitution, then result**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Forecasting
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Components of Forecasting

# 📘 COMPONENTS OF FORECASTING — KTU 2024 SCHEME STUDY NOTES

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Forecasting** is the process of estimating future demand for products or services based on historical data, statistical models, and managerial judgment. In a **Supply Chain Management (SCM)** context, forecasting forms the backbone of planning across procurement, production, distribution, and inventory functions.

The **Components of Forecasting** refer to the structured decomposition of demand patterns and forecasting error characteristics into measurable, analytically treatable elements. These components are conventionally classified into two families:

1. **Demand Pattern Components (Time-Series Decomposition)** — the structural anatomy of the demand series itself.
2. **Forecast Error Components (Performance Diagnostics)** — the deviation anatomy between predicted and actual demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Under Module 2 (Demand Forecasting), KTU explicitly expects students to identify and analyse the **Level, Trend, Seasonal, Cyclical, and Random/Irregular components** of a time series, as well as the components of **Forecast Error (Bias, MAD, MSE, MAPE, Tracking Signal)**. These are high-yield, frequently tested items.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a **beach with your eyes closed**, listening to ocean waves. What you "hear" (the demand) is actually the *sum of multiple invisible layers of motion*:

- A **gentle, long-term rise and fall of the tide** (the **Trend**).
- A **predictable daily wobble** between high and low tide (the **Seasonality**).
- An **irregular, slow heartbeat of the ocean** lasting years (the **Cycle**).
- The **calm, average level of the water** at any given moment (the **Level**).
- The **random fizz and chatter** of breaking wave bubbles (the **Random Noise / Irregular Component**).

**Forecasting = identifying each of these layers and projecting them forward.**

Now, your *forecast* is essentially your *guess of what you will hear next*. The difference between your guess and reality is the **Forecast Error**, which itself can be broken down into a consistent lean (Bias) and random wiggle (Random Variation). The components of forecasting are precisely the tools a forecaster uses to **decompose, predict, and measure** this complex demand behaviour.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Standard Tracking Signal thresholds:** ±4 (3-sigma heuristic), ±5, ±6 (depending on service-level tolerance). KTU most frequently cites **±4** as the default control limit.
- **Aggregation levels** in forecasting: SKU → Product Family → Category → Total Company.
- **Conventional forecast horizons:** Short-term (≤ 3 months), Medium-term (3 months – 2 years), Long-term (≥ 2 years).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Components of Forecasting:**
> *"The systematic decomposition of historical demand into Level, Trend, Seasonal, Cyclical, and Random components, coupled with quantification of forecast error into Bias, Variance, and Tracking Signal, used to model, project, and monitor future demand in a supply chain."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Forecasting Components

Forecasting components are formally grouped into the following two super-categories:

#### **A. Demand Series Components (Time-Series Decomposition)**

| Component | Symbol | Definition | Behavioural Signature |
|-----------|--------|------------|----------------------|
| **Level (Base)** | $L$ | The average value of the series after removing trend and seasonal effects. | A flat horizontal anchor. |
| **Trend** | $T$ | The long-term gradual upward or downward movement of demand. | Monotonic, slow, directional. |
| **Seasonality** | $S$ | A regular, predictable, calendar-bound fluctuation within a year. | Repeats every season / month / week. |
| **Cyclical** | $C$ | A long-wave, non-calendar-bound, irregular ups and downs. | Multi-year economic/business cycles. |
| **Random (Irregular / Noise)** | $\epsilon$ | The unpredictable, stochastic residual variation. | No pattern, mean ≈ 0. |

#### **B. Forecast Error Components (Forecast Performance Analytics)**

| Component | Symbol | Definition | Purpose |
|-----------|--------|------------|---------|
| **Forecast Error** | $E_t$ | $A_t - F_t$ (Actual minus Forecast). | Raw deviation. |
| **Mean Error (Bias Indicator)** | $ME$ | Average of all forecast errors. | Detects systematic over-/under-forecasting. |
| **Mean Absolute Deviation** | $MAD$ | Average of the absolute errors. | Measures magnitude of inaccuracy. |
| **Mean Squared Error** | $MSE$ | Average of squared errors. | Penalises large errors heavily. |
| **Mean Absolute Percentage Error** | $MAPE$ | Average of percentage errors. | Scale-free comparability. |
| **Running Sum of Forecast Errors** | $RSFE$ | Cumulative sum of errors over $n$ periods. | Tracks cumulative bias drift. |
| **Tracking Signal** | $TS$ | $RSFE / MAD$. | Monitors forecast control / bias drift. |

### 2.2 Additive vs. Multiplicative Decomposition

The **Time-Series Model** mathematically combines the components. KTU expects you to recognise **both** formulations:

**Additive Model:**
$$Y_t = L_t + T_t + S_t + C_t + \epsilon_t$$

Used when seasonal/cyclical fluctuations are **constant in absolute magnitude** over time.

**Multiplicative Model:**
$$Y_t = L_t \times T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times \epsilon_t$$

Used when seasonal/cyclical fluctuations **grow or shrink proportionally** with the level/trend (very common in practice — e.g., ice-cream sales, FMCG).

### 2.3 Why and How Each Component Behaves

- **Why Trend matters:** Misreading a trend causes chronic stock-outs or dead inventory. A **positive trend** mandates safety stock build-up.
- **Why Seasonality matters:** Drives production scheduling, hiring, and promotional calendars. Failure to incorporate seasonality causes the famous *"Christmas surprise"* stock-outs.
- **Why Cyclical matters:** Tied to macroeconomic / industrial cycles. Hard to predict via calendar; requires external macro indicators.
- **Why Random matters:** Cannot be predicted; must be *buffered* through safety stock sized to $\epsilon$'s standard deviation.
- **Why Bias tracking matters:** A consistently biased forecast silently drains working capital. **Bias is the silent killer of forecasting ROI.**
- **Why Tracking Signal matters:** Provides a real-time statistical control chart for forecast health, analogous to a Six-Sigma control chart.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **mandatory memorisation sheet** for KTU 2024 ESE. Every formula has appeared, in some form, in past papers.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $E_t = A_t - F_t$ | Single-period forecast error | Base deviation |
| 2 | $ME = \dfrac{\sum E_t}{n}$ | Mean Error (Bias) | Detect systematic bias |
| 3 | $MAD = \dfrac{\sum \vert E_t \vert}{n}$ | Mean Absolute Deviation | Error magnitude |
| 4 | $MSE = \dfrac{\sum E_t^2}{n}$ | Mean Squared Error | Penalises large errors |
| 5 | $MSE = s^2$ (sample variance of errors) | Equivalent estimator | Statistical inference |
| 6 | $MAPE = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum \dfrac{\vert E_t \vert}{\vert A_t \vert} \times 100$ | Mean Absolute % Error | Cross-product comparison |
| 7 | $RSFE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i$ | Running Sum of Forecast Errors | Cumulative bias |
| 8 | $TS = \dfrac{RSFE}{MAD}$ | Tracking Signal | Forecast control limits |
| 9 | $Y_t = L_t + T_t + S_t + C_t + \epsilon_t$ | Additive Time Series | Constant-amplitude seasonality |
| 10 | $Y_t = L_t \cdot T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot \epsilon_t$ | Multiplicative Time Series | Proportional-amplitude seasonality |
| 11 | $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | Exponential Smoothing (recursion) | Level-only forecasting |
| 12 | Control limits: $\vert TS \vert \le 4$ (or $6$) | Tracking Signal threshold | Forecast in/out of control |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Inventory Optimisation:** Each component informs a distinct inventory lever — *Level → base-stock; Trend → cycle stock; Seasonality → pre-build; Random → safety stock.*
- **S&OP (Sales & Operations Planning):** The **additive/multiplicative** decomposition is the canonical input for demand-planning meetings.
- **Bullwhip Mitigation:** Quantifying each component enables supply-chain partners to negotiate **information sharing** that dampens the bullwhip effect.
- **Healthcare, Retail, FMCG, Automotive:** All modern demand-planning systems (SAP IBP, o9, Kinaxis) are built atop these exact components.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Forecast Error Metrics

**Step 1 — Raw Error Definition.** For period $t$, the error is the algebraic difference between the **actual** demand $A_t$ and the **forecasted** demand $F_t$:

$$E_t = A_t - F_t$$

A **positive** $E_t$ means under-forecasting (actual > forecast). A **negative** $E_t$ means over-forecasting.

**Step 2 — Aggregating Errors.** Aggregating raw errors directly to detect bias is unreliable because positive and negative errors cancel mathematically. Hence we need **Mean Error (ME)** as a bias indicator:

$$ME = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} E_t = \frac{\sum A_t - \sum F_t}{n}$$

If $ME \approx 0$, the forecast is **unbiased on average**. If $ME > 0$, the forecast is **systematically low**.

**Step 3 — Magnitude via MAD.** To prevent sign cancellation in magnitude studies, take absolute values before averaging:

$$MAD = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert E_t \vert$$

MAD is the most widely used forecast-accuracy metric in practice because it preserves the original units of demand (units, rupees, tonnes).

**Step 4 — Penalising Large Errors via MSE.** Squaring the errors penalises large deviations quadratically. Taking the square root gives the **standard deviation of forecast errors** $s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
MSE &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} E_t^2 \\
s   &= \sqrt{MSE}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Scale-Free Comparison via MAPE.** Dividing each error by the actual demand gives a unit-free percentage:

$$MAPE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\vert E_t \vert}{\vert A_t \vert} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> MAPE is undefined when $A_t = 0$, which is a known limitation. KTU rarely tests this edge case but worth knowing.

**Step 6 — Cumulative Bias via RSFE.** Adding the errors over $n$ periods gives the **Running Sum of Forecast Errors**:

$$RSFE_n = \sum_{t=1}^{n} E_t$$

If forecasts are unbiased, $RSFE$ oscillates around 0; if biased, it drifts systematically (e.g., always positive for under-forecasting).

**Step 7 — Tracking Signal (Master Diagnostic).** Normalising the cumulative bias by the average absolute error gives a dimensionless control statistic:

$$\boxed{\,TS = \frac{RSFE}{MAD}\,}$$

In practice, the limit $\vert TS \vert \le 4$ (sometimes 6) is used as the **control band**. If $|TS|$ exceeds the limit, the forecast is considered **out of control**, and root-cause analysis (model change, demand shift, bias correction) is triggered.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Derivation (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** Given the following 5-period forecast data, compute the **MAD, MSE, MAPE, RSFE**, and **Tracking Signal**. Decide if the forecast is in control (limit $\pm 4$).

| Period | Actual $A_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ |
|--------|--------------|----------------|
| 1      | 100          | 90             |
| 2      | 110          | 105            |
| 3      | 95           | 100            |
| 4      | 120          | 110            |
| 5      | 130          | 115            |

**Step 1 — Compute errors $E_t = A_t - F_t$:**

| Period | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $E_t$ | $\vert E_t \vert$ | $E_t^2$ | $\dfrac{\vert E_t \vert}{A_t}$ |
|--------|------|------|------|------------------|--------|------------------------------|
| 1      | 100  | 90   | +10  | 10               | 100    | 0.1000 |
| 2      | 110  | 105  | +5   | 5                | 25     | 0.0455 |
| 3      | 95   | 100  | –5   | 5                | 25     | 0.0526 |
| 4      | 120  | 110  | +10  | 10               | 100    | 0.0833 |
| 5      | 130  | 115  | +15  | 15               | 225    | 0.1154 |

**Step 2 — Aggregate totals:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum E_t      &= 10 + 5 - 5 + 10 + 15 = 35 \\
\sum \vert E_t \vert &= 10 + 5 + 5 + 10 + 15 = 45 \\
\sum E_t^2    &= 100 + 25 + 25 + 100 + 225 = 475 \\
\sum \frac{\vert E_t \vert}{A_t} &= 0.1000 + 0.0455 + 0.0526 + 0.0833 + 0.1154 = 0.3968
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute MAD:**

$$MAD = \frac{\sum \vert E_t \vert}{n} = \frac{45}{5} = 9 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Compute MSE and standard deviation of error:**

$$MSE = \frac{\sum E_t^2}{n} = \frac{475}{5} = 95 \text{ units}^2$$

$$s = \sqrt{95} \approx 9.747 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Compute MAPE:**

$$MAPE = \frac{1}{n} \sum \frac{\vert E_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100 = \frac{0.3968}{5} \times 100 = 7.94\%$$

**Step 6 — Compute RSFE and Tracking Signal:**

$$RSFE = \sum E_t = 35$$

$$TS = \frac{RSFE}{MAD} = \frac{35}{9} \approx 3.89$$

**Step 7 — Control Decision:**

$$\vert TS \vert = 3.89 < 4$$

> **Verdict:** The forecast is **in control** (within the $\pm 4$ band), but it is **systematically biased towards under-forecasting** (positive $RSFE = 35$, positive $ME = 7$). KTU full-credit answer requires both observations.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Component

| Scenario | Symptom in Data | Implicated Component | KTU-Suggested Action |
|----------|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Persistent one-sided errors | $RSFE$ drifts monotonically | **Bias** | Adjust forecast by $-ME$ |
| Forecast misses peak season every year | Recurring annual error | **Seasonality** | Use multiplicative model |
| Long-run decline in demand | $T_t < 0$ | **Trend** | Apply trend-adjusted model |
| No pattern, large scatter | $\epsilon$ dominates | **Random** | Increase safety stock = $z \cdot s$ |
| $|TS| > 4$ sustained | Control chart breach | **Tracking Signal** | Re-tune model parameters |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Decomposition of Demand Series

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DEMAND TIME SERIES Yt"] --> B["LEVEL COMPONENT L"]
    A --> C["TREND COMPONENT T"]
    A --> D["SEASONAL COMPONENT S"]
    A --> E["CYCLICAL COMPONENT C"]
    A --> F["RANDOM COMPONENT epsilon"]
    B --> G["ADDITIVE MODEL Yt = L plus T plus S plus C plus e"]
    C --> G
    D --> H["MULTIPLICATIVE MODEL Yt = L x T x S x C x e"]
    E --> H
    F --> G
    F --> H
    G --> I["FORECAST Ft plus 1"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["ACTUAL DEMAND At plus 1"]
    J --> K["FORECAST ERROR Et"]
    K --> L["ERROR COMPONENTS"]
    L --> M["MEAN ERROR ME"]
    L --> N["MAD MSE MAPE"]
    L --> O["RSFE"]
    L --> P["TRACKING SIGNAL TS"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Forecast Error Feedback Loop (Control Chart Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["FORECAST Ft"] --> X["COMPARE WITH ACTUAL At"]
    A["ACTUAL DEMAND At"] --> X
    X --> E["ERROR Et = At minus Ft"]
    E --> R1["COMPUTE ABS Et"]
    E --> R2["SUM Et to get RSFE"]
    R1 --> M["COMPUTE MAD"]
    R2 --> T["TS = RSFE / MAD"]
    M --> T
    T --> D{"ABS TS greater than 4"}
    D -->|NO| K["FORECAST IN CONTROL"]
    D -->|YES| W["FORECAST OUT OF CONTROL"]
    W --> AC["INVESTIGATE BIAS TREND SEASONALITY"]
    AC --> UP["UPDATE MODEL PARAMETERS"]
    UP --> F
    K --> MON["CONTINUE MONITORING"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Decision Tree for Choosing Decomposition Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["START: EXAMINE DEMAND SERIES"] --> Q1{"SEASONAL FLUCTUATION CONSTANT OR PROPORTIONAL"}
    Q1 -->|CONSTANT OVER TIME| ADD["USE ADDITIVE MODEL Yt = L plus T plus S plus C plus e"]
    Q1 -->|GROWS WITH LEVEL| MUL["USE MULTIPLICATIVE MODEL Yt = L x T x S x C x e"]
    ADD --> CHK1{"TREND PRESENT"}
    MUL --> CHK2{"TREND PRESENT"}
    CHK1 -->|YES| A1["ADD TREND ADJUSTMENT"]
    CHK1 -->|NO| A2["FLAT BASELINE FORECAST"]
    CHK2 -->|YES| M1["MULTIPLY BY TREND INDEX"]
    CHK2 -->|NO| M2["FLAT BASELINE FORECAST"]
    A1 --> FIN["FINAL FORECAST Ft plus 1"]
    A2 --> FIN
    M1 --> FIN
    M2 --> FIN
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid) — Demand Forecasting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["DATA INPUT LAYER"]
        H["HISTORICAL DEMAND DATA"]
        P["PROMOTION CALENDAR"]
        M["MACRO INDICATORS"]
    end
    subgraph DECOMP["DECOMPOSITION LAYER"]
        L1["LEVEL EXTRACTION"]
        L2["TREND EXTRACTION"]
        L3["SEASONAL EXTRACTION"]
        L4["CYCLICAL EXTRACTION"]
        L5["NOISE EXTRACTION"]
    end
    subgraph MODEL["MODELLING LAYER"]
        MD1["ADDITIVE / MULTIPLICATIVE MODEL"]
        MD2["EXPONENTIAL SMOOTHING FAMILY"]
        MD3["ARIMA / REGRESSION"]
    end
    subgraph FORECAST["FORECAST OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["POINT FORECAST Ft plus 1"]
        O2["CONFIDENCE INTERVAL"]
    end
    subgraph DIAG["DIAGNOSTICS LAYER"]
        D1["MAD"]
        D2["MSE / MAPE"]
        D3["TRACKING SIGNAL TS"]
        D4["BIAS CHECK ME / RSFE"]
    end
    H --> L1
    H --> L2
    H --> L3
    P --> L3
    M --> L4
    L1 --> MD1
    L2 --> MD1
    L3 --> MD1
    L4 --> MD1
    L5 --> MD1
    MD1 --> MD2
    MD1 --> MD3
    MD2 --> O1
    MD3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> D1
    O1 --> D2
    O1 --> D3
    O1 --> D4
    D3 -->|FEEDBACK| MD1
    D4 -->|FEEDBACK| MD1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q1. List and briefly explain any three components of a time series used in demand forecasting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Trend (T):** The long-term, gradual, monotonic upward or downward movement of demand over an extended period, reflecting underlying market growth or decline (1 mark).
2. **Seasonality (S):** Regular, predictable, calendar-bound fluctuations in demand that repeat within a fixed period such as a year, quarter, or week (1 mark).
3. **Random / Irregular Component (ε):** The unpredictable, stochastic residual variation in demand that cannot be attributed to trend, seasonality, or cycles; used to size safety stock (1 mark).

*(Equivalent valid answers: Level, Cyclical component, etc.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. Define MAD, MSE, and Tracking Signal. Why is the Tracking Signal used as a forecast control metric?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation):** The average of the absolute values of forecast errors across $n$ periods; measures the typical magnitude of forecast error in original demand units (1 mark).
- **MSE (Mean Squared Error):** The average of squared forecast errors; penalises large deviations quadratically and equals the variance of the error distribution (1 mark).
- **Tracking Signal (TS = RSFE / MAD):** A dimensionless control statistic that normalises cumulative bias (RSFE) by average error magnitude (MAD); when $|TS|$ exceeds the chosen control limit (typically 4 or 6), the forecast is flagged as **out of control**, prompting model re-tuning or intervention (1 mark).

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the five classical components of a demand time series with the help of an appropriate diagram. Discuss the additive and multiplicative decomposition models and state the conditions under which each is applied. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A retail company has recorded actual demand and forecast demand for six months as given below. Calculate **MAD, MSE, MAPE, RSFE, and Tracking Signal**. Using a control limit of ±4, comment on whether the forecasting system is in control. **(7 marks)**

| Month | Actual $A_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ |
|-------|--------------|----------------|
| 1     | 200          | 220            |
| 2     | 230          | 215            |
| 3     | 210          | 225            |
| 4     | 250          | 230            |
| 5     | 245          | 250            |
| 6     | 270          | 255            |

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Components of a Time Series

A **time series** of demand can be mathematically decomposed into five classical components:

1. **Level (L):** The baseline average value of demand when trend and seasonality are removed.
2. **Trend (T):** The long-term gradual movement of demand either upward (positive trend) or downward (negative trend).
3. **Seasonality (S):** A regular and predictable pattern that recurs within a calendar period (e.g., monthly, quarterly).
4. **Cyclical (C):** A longer-term, non-calendar-driven wave caused by macroeconomic or industry-wide factors.
5. **Random / Irregular (ε):** Unpredictable residual noise with mean approximately zero.

The decomposition can be modelled in two ways:

- **Additive Model:** $Y_t = L_t + T_t + S_t + C_t + \epsilon_t$ — used when seasonal/cyclical amplitudes are **constant** in absolute terms across time.
- **Multiplicative Model:** $Y_t = L_t \cdot T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot \epsilon_t$ — used when seasonal/cyclical amplitudes are **proportional** to the level/trend (more common in real-world demand).

*(Full 7 marks: 4 marks for explaining all 5 components with examples, 2 marks for additive vs multiplicative comparison, 1 mark for stating the selection condition.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Compute errors $E_t = A_t - F_t$, $\vert E_t \vert$, $E_t^2$, $\vert E_t \vert / A_t$:**

| Month | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $E_t$ | $\vert E_t \vert$ | $E_t^2$ | $\vert E_t \vert / A_t$ |
|-------|------|------|------|------------------|--------|------------------------|
| 1     | 200  | 220  | –20  | 20               | 400    | 0.1000 |
| 2     | 230  | 215  | +15  | 15               | 225    | 0.0652 |
| 3     | 210  | 225  | –15  | 15               | 225    | 0.0714 |
| 4     | 250  | 230  | +20  | 20               | 400    | 0.0800 |
| 5     | 245  | 250  | –5   | 5                | 25     | 0.0204 |
| 6     | 270  | 255  | +15  | 15               | 225    | 0.0556 |

**Step 2 — Aggregate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum E_t      &= -20 + 15 - 15 + 20 - 5 + 15 = 10 \\
\sum \vert E_t \vert &= 20 + 15 + 15 + 20 + 5 + 15 = 90 \\
\sum E_t^2    &= 400 + 225 + 225 + 400 + 25 + 225 = 1500 \\
\sum \frac{\vert E_t \vert}{A_t} &= 0.1000 + 0.0652 + 0.0714 + 0.0800 + 0.0204 + 0.0556 = 0.3926
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Metrics:**

$$MAD = \frac{90}{6} = 15 \text{ units}$$

$$MSE = \frac{1500}{6} = 250 \text{ units}^2, \quad s = \sqrt{250} \approx 15.81 \text{ units}$$

$$MAPE = \frac{0.3926}{6} \times 100 \approx 6.54\%$$

$$RSFE = 10$$

$$TS = \frac{RSFE}{MAD} = \frac{10}{15} \approx 0.667$$

**Step 4 — Control Verdict:**

$\vert TS \vert = 0.667 \le 4$, so the **forecast is in control**. However, the **positive RSFE = 10** indicates a slight upward bias (under-forecasting), which is minor and not yet a control breach.

> **Conclusion:** The forecasting system is performing acceptably, but the **bias should be monitored** as the RSFE could drift outside limits in subsequent periods.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the components of forecast error with relevant formulas. Explain the significance of the **Tracking Signal** in forecast monitoring and control, using an appropriate control chart concept. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A FMCG company forecasts demand for a product using the multiplicative time-series model: $Y_t = L \times T \times S \times C \times \epsilon$. The following data is available:
>
> - Average level (L) = 1000 units/month
> - Trend growth (T) = +5% per month (compound)
> - Seasonal index (S) for the next month = 1.25
> - Cyclical factor (C) = 0.95
> - Random noise (ε) ≈ 1.00 (negligible)
>
> Compute the forecast for the next month. If the actual demand turns out to be **1180 units**, calculate **MAD, RSFE, and Tracking Signal** (assume this is the first period, so MAD and RSFE are based on the single error). Comment on the forecast's accuracy. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Components of Forecast Error

The **forecast error** at period $t$ is defined as $E_t = A_t - F_t$. Its components are:

1. **Bias (systematic error):** Captured by the **Mean Error (ME)** or the cumulative **RSFE**. A consistent sign indicates over- or under-forecasting.
2. **Random Variation:** Captured by the variance of errors, measured by **MAD** or **MSE**. Cannot be eliminated but can be buffered with safety stock.
3. **Tracking Signal:** A normalised control statistic $\;TS = RSFE / MAD\;$ used like a control-chart metric. Limits of $\pm 4$ or $\pm 6$ are conventional. Breaches indicate the forecast process is out of control and corrective action is needed.

The Tracking Signal thus acts as a **real-time SPC (Statistical Process Control)** device for forecasting, analogous to a Shewhart chart for manufacturing quality.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Forecast using multiplicative model:**

$$F_{t+1} = L \times T \times S \times C \times \epsilon$$

$$F_{t+1} = 1000 \times 1.05 \times 1.25 \times 0.95 \times 1.00$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{t+1} &= 1000 \times 1.05 \times 1.25 \times 0.95 \\
&= 1000 \times 1.3125 \times 0.95 \\
&= 1000 \times 1.246875 \\
&\approx 1247 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute error (only one period available):**

$$E_1 = A_1 - F_1 = 1180 - 1247 = -67 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Metrics for single period:**

$$MAD_1 = \vert E_1 \vert = 67 \text{ units}$$

$$RSFE_1 = -67$$

$$TS_1 = \frac{RSFE_1}{MAD_1} = \frac{-67}{67} = -1.00$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy Commentary:**

- **MAPE = |–67| / 1180 × 100 ≈ 5.68%** (good accuracy).
- $TS = -1$ lies well within $\pm 4$ control limits → **forecast in control**.
- Negative error indicates **over-forecasting**, but with only one data point, no bias trend can be confirmed. Continued monitoring across at least 4–6 periods is recommended before any model adjustment.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Components of Forecasting):**
>
> 1. **Confusing sign convention:** $E_t = A_t - F_t$ (NOT $F_t - A_t$). KTU explicitly deducts marks if the wrong sign convention is used. RSFE flips sign if this is reversed.
> 2. **Forgetting to take absolute values in MAD:** A student writing $\sum E_t / n$ instead of $\sum \vert E_t \vert / n$ will get $ME$, not $MAD$. **ME ≠ MAD.** Both must be explicitly named.
> 3. **MAPE denominator:** Always divide by $|A_t|$, not $F_t$. Avoid division by zero cases.
> 4. **Additive vs. Multiplicative confusion:** Mark the model that matches the seasonal behaviour. If seasonal swing grows with demand, it is **multiplicative**.
> 5. **Tracking Signal threshold:** KTU accepts $\pm 4$ (most common) or $\pm 6$. Always **state the limit used** before giving the verdict.
> 6. **RSFE is cumulative, not periodic:** A common error is computing RSFE each period instead of cumulatively. After period $n$, $RSFE_n = RSFE_{n-1} + E_n$.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Five demand-series components:** Level, Trend, Seasonality, Cyclical, Random (Noise).
- ✅ **Two decomposition models:** Additive ($+$) and Multiplicative ($\times$). Selection depends on whether seasonality is **constant** or **proportional**.
- ✅ **Six forecast-error metrics to memorise:** $E_t$, $ME$, $MAD$, $MSE$, $MAPE$, $RSFE$, $TS$.
- ✅ **Tracking Signal formula:** $TS = RSFE / MAD$. Control limit **±4** (default KTU value).
- ✅ **$ME \approx 0$** → forecast unbiased on average.
- ✅ **$MAPE$** is the only scale-free metric — enables cross-product comparison.
- ✅ **$MSE = s^2$** links forecasting to safety stock via $z \cdot s$.
- ✅ **Bias is the silent killer** — monitor via RSFE drift.
- ✅ **Random noise** is buffered, not predicted → safety stock sized to $z \cdot s$.
- ✅ **Multiplicative model is more common** in real-world FMCG, retail, and seasonal demand.
- ✅ KTU loves **worked numerical problems** — memorise the error-table template and the five-step computation flow.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions and limits** used (e.g., control limit ±4 vs ±6) — this is a free 1-mark win in ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forecasting Methods, Aggregate Planning in Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forecasting Methods, Aggregate Planning in Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Demand Forecasting in Supply Chain

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forecasting** is the process of estimating future demand for a product or service based on historical data, market intelligence, and statistical/qualitative judgment. In the context of a Supply Chain, forecasting acts as the primary nervous system that drives every downstream operational, tactical, and strategic decision—procurement, production scheduling, inventory positioning, transportation planning, and workforce deployment.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, forecasting is the **predictive input variable** for the *Supply Chain Operations Reference (SCOR)* model's **Plan** phase, directly linking customer demand variability with the firm's ability to balance **service level** against **total landed cost**.

### Key Characteristics of a Good Forecast
- **Accuracy** (minimizes deviation from actual demand)
- **Timeliness** (available before the planning horizon begins)
- **Reliability** (consistent across different models)
- **Granularity** (aligned with the planning horizon)
- **Cost-effective** to generate

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Weather Bureau"**
> Think of forecasting like a **meteorologist** trying to predict whether it will rain next week. The meteorologist does not *control* the weather (just as a Supply Chain manager does not *control* customer demand). Instead, they analyze past patterns (seasonal monsoons), current signals (cloud density, humidity), and expert opinion (satellite imagery) to make the *best educated guess* about the future. The forecast will never be 100% perfect, but its accuracy directly determines how well you decide whether to carry an umbrella (safety stock), reschedule a picnic (production plan), or buy more umbrellas (capacity expansion).

### 1.2 Classification of Forecasting Methods

Forecasting methods are classified into two broad families:

| Family | Type | When Used | Data Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative Forecasting** | Judgmental / Subjective | No historical data available; new product introduction; volatile markets | None / Minimal |
| **Quantitative Forecasting** | Statistical / Mathematical | Stable historical demand; established product lines | 2+ years of historical demand data |

---

## 1.3 Qualitative Forecasting Methods (Brief Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> Qualitative methods are essential when launching **new products** (where no sales history exists) or operating in **unstable markets** affected by disruptive technologies, pandemics, or regulatory changes.

1. **Sales Force Composite**: Aggregates individual salesperson estimates; best for region-specific products.
2. **Delphi Method**: Iterative, anonymous expert panel consensus — eliminates groupthink.
3. **Market Research**: Customer surveys, focus groups, conjoint analysis.
4. **Panel Consensus**: Open group discussion among experts/managers.
5. **Historical Analogy**: Uses a similar past product's lifecycle curve as a proxy.

---

## 1.4 Quantitative Forecasting Methods (KTU High-Priority)

### A. Time-Series Models
These models assume that **past patterns (trend, seasonality, cyclicality, randomness)** will continue into the future.

- **Naïve Forecast**: Next period's forecast = Current period's actual demand.
- **Simple Moving Average (SMA)**: Average demand over the last *n* periods.
- **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)**: Weighted average, with higher weights on recent periods.
- **Exponential Smoothing**: Weighted average where weights decrease exponentially.

### B. Causal / Associative Models
- **Linear Regression (Linear Trend)**: $F_t = a + b \cdot t$
- **Multiple Regression**: Demand as a function of multiple independent variables (price, advertising, GDP).

---

## 1.5 Aggregate Planning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregate Planning (AP)** is a medium-range (typically **3 to 18 months**) tactical planning process that determines the overall production quantity, workforce level, and inventory position needed to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost. It operates at the level of **product families** (aggregated), not individual SKUs.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"**
> Imagine you are the head chef of a restaurant. You know roughly how many customers will dine next month (forecasted demand). You must decide: **Do I hire more cooks (chase strategy)?** **Do I stockpile frozen dough (inventory build-up)?** **Do I stretch the existing team with overtime (level strategy)?** Aggregate Planning is exactly this decision — it sits *above* daily kitchen prep (short-term scheduling) and *below* opening new branches (long-term capacity planning). It answers the question: **"Given my expected demand next quarter, what is the cheapest way to match it?"**

### Aggregate Planning Strategies (Three Pillars)

| Strategy | Core Idea | Cost Profile | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chase Strategy** | Vary workforce to *chase* demand exactly | Low inventory, High hiring/firing | Perishable goods, fashion, services |
| **Level Strategy** | Keep workforce/inventory stable; absorb variance with inventory + backorders | High inventory, Stable labor | Mature products, skilled labor markets |
| **Hybrid (Mixed) Strategy** | Combines elements of both | Balanced | Most real-world manufacturing firms |

### Cost Components Used in Aggregate Planning

- **Production (Regular-time) Cost** — typically the baseline cost per unit.
- **Overtime Cost** — usually 1.5× regular wage.
- **Hiring Cost** — cost per new worker recruited, trained, onboarded.
- **Layoff (Firing) Cost** — severance, morale loss.
- **Holding (Inventory) Cost** — warehousing, insurance, capital tied up.
- **Backorder / Stockout Cost** — lost goodwill, lost sales, penalty.
- **Subcontracting Cost** — outsourcing to third-party producers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time-Series Decomposition Model

Every demand time series can be decomposed into four components:

$$D_t = L_t + T_t + S_t + \epsilon_t$$

Where:
- $D_t$ = Actual demand at time *t*
- $L_t$ = **Level** (average baseline demand)
- $T_t$ = **Trend** (long-term upward or downward movement)
- $S_t$ = **Seasonality** (regular, predictable periodic fluctuation)
- $\epsilon_t$ = **Random / Irregular** component (unpredictable noise)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Memory Trick:** *"L-T-S-R"* — **L**evel, **T**rend, **S**easonality, **R**andom. Always list in this order in exam answers.

---

## 2.2 Quantitative Forecasting Models — Detailed Logic

### A. Naïve Forecast
**Logic**: Assumes the most recent observation is the best predictor of the future.

$$F_{t+1} = D_t$$

- **Why simple?** Requires no computation.
- **Why weak?** Ignores trend and seasonality.
- **Use case**: Stable demand with no trend; benchmark for evaluating complex models.

### B. Simple Moving Average (SMA)
**Logic**: Average of the last *n* periods equalizes out random fluctuations.

$$F_{t+1} = \frac{D_t + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2} + \cdots + D_{t-n+1}}{n}$$

- **Step-by-step reasoning**:
  1. Choose window *n* (e.g., 3 months, 4 quarters).
  2. Sum the last *n* actual demands.
  3. Divide by *n*.
  4. The result becomes the forecast for the next period.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Larger *n* → smoother forecast but **more lag** in detecting trend. Smaller *n* → responsive but **noisier**.

### C. Weighted Moving Average (WMA)
**Logic**: Assign higher weights to more recent observations to reduce lag.

$$F_{t+1} = w_1 D_t + w_2 D_{t-1} + \cdots + w_n D_{t-n+1}$$

Subject to:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$$

Where $w_1 \ge w_2 \ge \cdots \ge w_n > 0$.

### D. Exponential Smoothing (Simple / Single)
**Logic**: A recursive model that uses a smoothing constant $\alpha$ to balance old vs. new data.

$$F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha (D_t - F_t)$$

Equivalently expanded:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t$$

- $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ is the **smoothing constant**.
- **High $\alpha$** (~0.9) → forecast reacts quickly to recent changes (responsive).
- **Low $\alpha$** (~0.1) → forecast is smooth, slow to react (stable).

### E. Holt's Two-Parameter Exponential Smoothing (Trend-Corrected)
**Logic**: Adds a second smoothing constant $\beta$ to handle linear trend.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_t &= \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}) \\
T_t &= \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) T_{t-1} \\
F_{t+m} &= L_t + m \cdot T_t
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $L_t$ = smoothed level at time *t*
- $T_t$ = smoothed trend at time *t*
- $m$ = number of periods ahead to forecast

### F. Winter's Three-Parameter Method (Trend + Seasonality)
**Logic**: Adds a third constant $\gamma$ to handle seasonal indices.

- Used when demand has both trend **and** seasonal pattern.
- Three equations for level, trend, and seasonal index.

### G. Linear Regression / Linear Trend Line
**Logic**: Fits a straight line through historical demand data using **least squares**.

$$F_t = a + b \cdot t$$

Where (least-squares estimators):

$$\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{n \sum t D_t - (\sum t)(\sum D_t)}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2} \\
a &= \overline{D} - b \cdot \overline{t}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 2.3 Forecast Error Metrics — The KTU Essential Toolkit

| Metric | Formula | Meaning | Unit | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MAD** (Mean Absolute Deviation) | $MAD = \frac{\sum \vert D_t - F_t \vert}{n}$ | Average magnitude of errors | Same as demand | Most common, easy to interpret |
| **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) | $MSE = \frac{\sum (D_t - F_t)^2}{n}$ | Penalizes large errors heavily | Squared demand units | Useful when big errors are unacceptable |
| **MAPE** (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) | $MAPE = \frac{1}{n} \sum \frac{\vert D_t - F_t \vert}{D_t} \times 100$ | Error as a % of demand | Percentage | Cross-product comparison |
| **Tracking Signal (TS)** | $TS = \frac{\sum (D_t - F_t)}{MAD}$ | Detects systematic bias | Dimensionless | If $\vert TS \vert \ge 4 \rightarrow$ model needs review |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb**: A forecast is considered **acceptable** if **MAPE < 10%** (highly accurate), 10–20% (good), 20–50% (reasonable), > 50% (inaccurate).

---

## 2.4 Aggregate Planning — Strategic Decision Logic

### Aggregate Planning Decision Variables

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } Z &= C_P W_t + C_O O_t + C_H H_t + C_F F_t + C_L L_t + C_B B_t + C_S S_t
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $C_P, C_O$ = Regular-time, Overtime unit cost
- $C_H, C_F$ = Hiring, Firing cost per worker
- $C_L$ = Inventory holding cost per unit
- $C_B$ = Backorder cost per unit
- $C_S$ = Subcontracting cost per unit
- $W_t, O_t, H_t, F_t, L_t, B_t, S_t$ = respective quantities at period *t*

### Aggregate Planning Methods

| Method | Approach | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Spreadsheet Trial-and-Error** | Manager builds a table; adjusts variables to minimize cost | Small problems; intuitive |
| **Linear Programming (LP)** | Solves optimization problem exactly | Large-scale, multi-product, multi-period |
| **Decision Rules** | Standard heuristic policies (e.g., "produce at 90% of last period") | Repetitive, easy-to-implement |
| **Management Coefficients Model** | Builds regression model from past manager decisions | Empirical, captures managerial intuition |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Boundary / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Naïve Forecast | $F_{t+1} = D_t$ | Any units |
| 2 | Simple Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} D_{t-i}$ | $n \ge 2$ periods |
| 3 | Weighted Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i D_{t-i+1}$ | $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| 4 | Exponential Smoothing | $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| 5 | Holt's Trend Smoothing | $F_{t+m} = L_t + m T_t$ | $m \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 6 | Linear Trend Regression | $F_t = a + b t$ | $b = \text{slope}$ |
| 7 | MAD | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert D_t - F_t \vert$ | Lower is better |
| 8 | MAPE | $\frac{100}{n}\sum \frac{\vert D_t - F_t \vert}{D_t}$ | Expressed in **%** |
| 9 | Tracking Signal | $\frac{\text{Running Sum of Errors}}{MAD}$ | Trigger threshold = **±4** |
| 10 | Aggregate Production Required | $P_t = F_t + L_{t-1} - L_t$ | Where $L_t$ = inventory level |
| 11 | Workforce Required | $W_t = \frac{P_t}{\text{Units per worker per period}}$ | Integer workers |
| 12 | Chase Strategy Output | $P_t = F_t$ exactly each period | Inventory $\approx 0$ |
