# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Three-Period Moving Average

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style)**: Given the following quarterly demand for a product:
>
> | Quarter | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Q5 | Q6 | Q7 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Demand | 120 | 135 | 128 | 142 | 150 | 138 | 160 |
>
> Compute the **3-period Simple Moving Average forecast** for Q4, Q5, Q6, and Q7.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Write the SMA formula.

$$F_{t+1} = \frac{D_t + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2}}{3}$$

**Step 2**: Compute forecast for Q4 (uses Q1, Q2, Q3).

$$F_4 = \frac{120 + 135 + 128}{3} = \frac{383}{3} = 127.67$$

**Step 3**: Compute forecast for Q5 (uses Q2, Q3, Q4).

$$F_5 = \frac{135 + 128 + 142}{3} = \frac{405}{3} = 135.00$$

**Step 4**: Compute forecast for Q6 (uses Q3, Q4, Q5).

$$F_6 = \frac{128 + 142 + 150}{3} = \frac{420}{3} = 140.00$$

**Step 5**: Compute forecast for Q7 (uses Q4, Q5, Q6).

$$F_7 = \frac{142 + 150 + 138}{3} = \frac{430}{3} = 143.33$$

### Step 6: Compute MAD

$$MAD = \frac{1}{4} \left[ \vert 142 - 127.67 \vert + \vert 150 - 135.00 \vert + \vert 138 - 140.00 \vert + \vert 160 - 143.33 \vert \right]$$

$$MAD = \frac{1}{4} \left[ 14.33 + 15.00 + 2.00 + 16.67 \right] = \frac{48.00}{4} = 12.00 \text{ units}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Exponential Smoothing with α = 0.3

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: Demand data: Month 1 = 100, Month 2 = 110, Month 3 = 105, Month 4 = 115. Initial forecast $F_1 = 100$. Compute forecasts for months 2–5 using simple exponential smoothing with $\alpha = 0.3$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Use the formula.

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$$

**Step 2**: Forecast for Month 2.

$$F_2 = 0.3(100) + 0.7(100) = 30 + 70 = 100.0$$

**Step 3**: Forecast for Month 3.

$$F_3 = 0.3(110) + 0.7(100) = 33 + 70 = 103.0$$

**Step 4**: Forecast for Month 4.

$$F_4 = 0.3(105) + 0.7(103) = 31.5 + 72.1 = 103.6$$

**Step 5**: Forecast for Month 5.

$$F_5 = 0.3(115) + 0.7(103.6) = 34.5 + 72.52 = 107.02$$

### Step 6: Summary Table

| Month | Actual $D_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ | Error $(D_t - F_t)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 100.0 | 0.0 |
| 2 | 110 | 100.0 | 10.0 |
| 3 | 105 | 103.0 | 2.0 |
| 4 | 115 | 103.6 | 11.4 |
| 5 | — | **107.02** | — |

### Step 7: Compute MAD for months 2–4

$$MAD = \frac{1}{3}(10.0 + 2.0 + 11.4) = \frac{23.4}{3} = 7.80$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Linear Trend Regression (Least Squares)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: Compute the linear trend forecast using least-squares regression for the following demand data.

| Period $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand $D_t$ | 50 | 54 | 58 | 63 | 67 | 72 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Compute required summations.

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= 6 \\
\sum t &= 1+2+3+4+5+6 = 21 \\
\sum D_t &= 50+54+58+63+67+72 = 364 \\
\sum t^2 &= 1+4+9+16+25+36 = 91 \\
\sum t D_t &= 1(50)+2(54)+3(58)+4(63)+5(67)+6(72) \\
&= 50 + 108 + 174 + 252 + 335 + 432 = 1351
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2**: Compute slope $b$.

$$b = \frac{n \sum t D_t - (\sum t)(\sum D_t)}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}$$

$$b = \frac{6(1351) - (21)(364)}{6(91) - (21)^2} = \frac{8106 - 7644}{546 - 441} = \frac{462}{105} = 4.4$$

**Step 3**: Compute intercept $a$.

$$\overline{t} = \frac{21}{6} = 3.5 \quad ; \quad \overline{D} = \frac{364}{6} = 60.67$$

$$a = \overline{D} - b \overline{t} = 60.67 - 4.4(3.5) = 60.67 - 15.4 = 45.27$$

**Step 4**: Trend equation.

$$F_t = 45.27 + 4.4 t$$

**Step 5**: Forecast for period 7.

$$F_7 = 45.27 + 4.4(7) = 45.27 + 30.80 = 76.07$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Aggregate Planning (Level Strategy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Board Exam Style)**: A firm forecasts demand for the next 4 months as: Jan = 1000, Feb = 1200, Mar = 1400, Apr = 800 units. Beginning inventory = 500 units. Regular production cost = ₹10/unit, Holding cost = ₹2/unit/month, Backorder cost = ₹5/unit/month. Compute total cost under a **Level Production Strategy** producing at the average demand rate.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Compute average demand (Level Production Rate).

$$\bar{D} = \frac{1000 + 1200 + 1400 + 800}{4} = \frac{4400}{4} = 1100 \text{ units/month}$$

**Step 2**: Build the aggregate plan table.

| Month | Forecast | Production | Beginning Inv | Ending Inv | Backorders |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | 1000 | 1100 | 500 | 600 | 0 |
| Feb | 1200 | 1100 | 600 | 500 | 0 |
| Mar | 1400 | 1100 | 500 | 200 | 0 |
| Apr | 800 | 1100 | 200 | 500 | 0 |

Detailed calculation for each row (using $Ending = Beginning + Production - Demand$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan Ending} &= 500 + 1100 - 1000 = 600 \\
\text{Feb Ending} &= 600 + 1100 - 1200 = 500 \\
\text{Mar Ending} &= 500 + 1100 - 1400 = 200 \\
\text{Apr Ending} &= 200 + 1100 - 800 = 500
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3**: Compute total costs.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= 4 \times 1100 \times 10 = \text{₹}44{,}000 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= (600 + 500 + 200 + 500) \times 2 = 1800 \times 2 = \text{₹}3{,}600 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 0 \\
\text{Total Cost} &= 44{,}000 + 3{,}600 = \text{₹}47{,}600
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Chase Strategy

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuation of the previous problem**: Compute total cost under the **Chase Strategy** where production exactly matches demand.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1**: Production = Forecast each month.

| Month | Forecast | Production | Ending Inventory |
|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | 1000 | 1000 | 500 (no change) |
| Feb | 1200 | 1200 | 500 |
| Mar | 1400 | 1400 | 500 |
| Apr | 800 | 800 | 500 |

**Step 2**: Compute total costs.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= (1000+1200+1400+800) \times 10 = 4400 \times 10 = \text{₹}44{,}000 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= 0 \text{ (no inventory build-up beyond initial)} \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 0 \\
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{₹}44{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison**: Chase strategy costs **₹44,000** vs Level strategy **₹47,600** (a saving of ₹3,600). However, this analysis **assumes zero hiring/firing costs** — which is unrealistic. In a real KTU answer, you must also factor in hiring/firing costs to complete the comparison.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Distinguish between Qualitative and Quantitative forecasting methods. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer**:
- **Qualitative Forecasting**: Based on subjective judgment, expert opinion, and intuition. Used when historical data is unavailable (e.g., new products, volatile markets). **Example**: Delphi Method, Market Research.
- **Quantitative Forecasting**: Based on mathematical/statistical models applied to historical demand data. Requires sufficient past data. **Example**: Simple Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, Linear Regression.

| Basis | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|
| Data Source | Expert opinion | Historical data |
| Objectivity | Subjective | Objective |
| Cost | High (consultants) | Low (software) |
| Speed | Slow | Fast |
| New products? | Yes | No |

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: What is Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)? How is it used in forecast evaluation?**

**Model Answer**:
**MAD** is the average of the absolute differences between forecasted demand and actual demand over *n* periods:

$$MAD = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert D_t - F_t \vert$$

**Use in evaluation**:
1. Measures **average forecast error magnitude** in the same units as demand.
2. Used to **compare** two or more forecasting models — the model with **lower MAD** is preferred.
3. Used as the **denominator** in computing the Tracking Signal:
$$TS = \frac{\text{Running Sum of Forecast Errors}}{MAD}$$
4. A declining MAD over successive periods indicates **improving forecast accuracy**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the different **Qualitative forecasting methods** used in Supply Chain Management. Discuss their relative advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The demand for a product over the last 5 months is given below:

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 200 | 220 | 210 | 230 | 240 |

Using **3-month Weighted Moving Average** with weights 0.5, 0.3, 0.2 (highest to most recent), forecast demand for month 6 and compute the **MAD** for months 3, 4, and 5. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

### Part (a): Qualitative Forecasting Methods

**1. Sales Force Composite**
- Each salesperson estimates sales in their territory.
- Aggregated at regional and national levels.
- **Advantage**: Grassroots market intelligence; high specificity.
- **Limitation**: Bias due to salesperson optimism/pessimism.

**2. Delphi Method**
- Structured process: anonymous expert panel answers questionnaires in **multiple rounds**.
- Facilitator summarizes responses, feeds back to experts for revision.
- Convergence occurs over 2–4 rounds.
- **Advantage**: Eliminates groupthink; geographically dispersed experts.
- **Limitation**: Time-consuming (weeks/months); expensive.

**3. Market Research**
- Customer surveys, focus groups, conjoint analysis.
- **Advantage**: Direct customer insight.
- **Limitation**: Expensive; sample bias; small samples.

**4. Panel Consensus**
- Open group discussion among experts.
- **Advantage**: Quick, leverages collective wisdom.
- **Limitation**: Dominant personalities may bias outcomes.

**5. Historical Analogy**
- Uses similar past product's lifecycle as proxy.
- **Advantage**: Useful for new product launches.
- **Limitation**: Assumes the analogy truly holds.

### Part (b): WMA Calculation

**Step 1**: WMA formula.

$$F_{t+1} = w_1 D_t + w_2 D_{t-1} + w_3 D_{t-2}$$

With weights: $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$.

**Step 2**: Forecast for Month 3 (uses Months 2, 1, 0 — but Month 0 not available, so use Months 1, 2 only with adjusted weights).

> **Note**: Since only 2 months of data exist at the start, we'll start from Month 4 onwards for proper 3-period WMA.

**Forecast for Month 4** (uses Months 3, 2, 1):

$$F_4 = 0.5(210) + 0.3(220) + 0.2(200) = 105 + 66 + 40 = 211$$

**Forecast for Month 5** (uses Months 4, 3, 2):

$$F_5 = 0.5(230) + 0.3(210) + 0.2(220) = 115 + 63 + 44 = 222$$

**Forecast for Month 6** (uses Months 5, 4, 3):

$$F_6 = 0.5(240) + 0.3(230) + 0.2(210) = 120 + 69 + 42 = 231$$

**Step 3**: Compute MAD for Months 4, 5 (we can compute error only for months with both actual and forecast).

| Month | Actual $D_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ | Error $(D_t - F_t)$ | $\vert Error \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 230 | 211 | 19 | 19 |
| 5 | 240 | 222 | 18 | 18 |

$$MAD = \frac{19 + 18}{2} = \frac{37}{2} = 18.5 \text{ units}$$

> **Forecast for Month 6 = 231 units**.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Q: (a)** Define **Aggregate Planning**. Compare the **Chase** and **Level** strategies with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm forecasts demand for the next 4 quarters as: Q1 = 500, Q2 = 700, Q3 = 800, Q4 = 600 units. Initial inventory = 100 units. Regular production cost = ₹20/unit, Overtime cost = ₹30/unit, Holding cost = ₹4/unit/quarter, Backorder cost = ₹10/unit/quarter. Compute the **total cost** under both **Level** and **Chase** strategies, and recommend the better one. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

### Part (a): Aggregate Planning Definition and Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: Aggregate Planning is a medium-range (3–18 months) tactical planning process that determines production quantity, workforce levels, and inventory to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost, operating at the product family level.

| Feature | Chase Strategy | Level Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Core idea** | Production = Demand (each period) | Production = Constant (average) |
| **Workforce** | Hired/Laid-off each period | Stable throughout |
| **Inventory** | Near-zero | Built up / depleted |
| **Best for** | Skilled-labor-scarce; perishables; make-to-order | Mature, stable demand products |
| **Costs** | High hiring/firing | High inventory holding |
| **Example** | Apparel (seasonal fashion) | FMCG (soap, detergent) |

### Part (b): Aggregate Plan Computation

**Step 1**: Average Demand.

$$\bar{D} = \frac{500 + 700 + 800 + 600}{4} = \frac{2600}{4} = 650 \text{ units/quarter}$$

**Step 2 — Level Strategy Table** (Production = 650 each quarter):

| Quarter | Forecast | Production | Beg Inv | End Inv | Backorder |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 | 500 | 650 | 100 | 250 | 0 |
| Q2 | 700 | 650 | 250 | 200 | 0 |
| Q3 | 800 | 650 | 200 | 50 | 0 |
| Q4 | 600 | 650 | 50 | 100 | 0 |

Detailed (End Inv = Beg Inv + Production - Forecast):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q1: 100 + 650 - 500 &= 250 \\
Q2: 250 + 650 - 700 &= 200 \\
Q3: 200 + 650 - 800 &= 50 \\
Q4: 50 + 650 - 600 &= 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Level Strategy Costs**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= 4 \times 650 \times 20 = \text{₹}52{,}000 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= (250 + 200 + 50 + 100) \times 4 = 600 \times 4 = \text{₹}2{,}400 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 0 \\
\text{Total (Level)} &= \text{₹}54{,}400
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Chase Strategy Table** (Production = Forecast each quarter):

| Quarter | Forecast | Production | End Inv |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 | 500 | 500 | 100 |
| Q2 | 700 | 700 | 100 |
| Q3 | 800 | 800 | 100 |
| Q4 | 600 | 600 | 100 |

**Chase Strategy Costs**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= (500+700+800+600) \times 20 = 2600 \times 20 = \text{₹}52{,}000 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= 0 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 0 \\
\text{Total (Chase)} &= \text{₹}52{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Recommendation**

$$\text{Saving (Chase vs Level)} = 54{,}400 - 52{,}000 = \text{₹}2{,}400$$

> **Recommendation**: The **Chase Strategy** yields a saving of ₹2,400 over the planning horizon. However, in real-world scenarios, if hiring/firing costs are significant (e.g., ₹5,000 per worker hired), the Level Strategy may become preferable. Decision depends on the **cost ratio of workforce adjustments vs. inventory holding**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Weight Sum Error in WMA**: Students often forget that weights in WMA MUST sum to 1. If weights are 0.5, 0.3, 0.2 — the sum is 1.0 ✓ (correct). Always verify.
> 2. **Forecast Denominator Confusion**: In SMA, divide by *n*, NOT by the number of months present. If you have only 2 months of data and *n* = 3, you cannot forecast — state this explicitly.
> 3. **MAD Calculation Mistake**: MAD requires at least one full cycle of forecast-vs-actual. The first forecast has no error to evaluate.
> 4. **Aggregate Plan Sign Convention**: Inventory **ending** = Beginning + Production − Demand. If negative → backorder. Many students get the sign reversed.
> 5. **Chase Strategy Production Cost Miscomputation**: Students sometimes forget that chase = produce EXACTLY the demand, but production **cost per unit stays the same** (no overtime unless explicitly stated).
> 6. **Tracking Signal Threshold**: ±4 is the standard limit. Beyond this → model is biased.
> 7. **Forgetting Initial Forecast**: Exponential smoothing requires $F_1$ as a starting value — typically $F_1 = D_1$ or a managerial estimate.
> 8. **MAPE Division by Zero**: If $D_t = 0$, MAPE is undefined. Always check before applying.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

### Forecasting Methods
- ✅ Forecasting is the **input** to every supply chain planning decision.
- ✅ **Qualitative** = judgment-based (Delphi, Market Research, Sales Force Composite).
- ✅ **Quantitative** = statistical (Time-series: Naïve, SMA, WMA, ES; Causal: Regression).
- ✅ **Naïve**: $F_{t+1} = D_t$ — simplest possible forecast.
- ✅ **SMA**: Equal weights on last *n* periods — smooths noise, lags trend.
- ✅ **WMA**: Higher weight on recent periods — reduces lag, weights must sum to 1.
- ✅ **Exponential Smoothing**: Recursive formula $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$; $\alpha$ controls responsiveness.
- ✅ **Holt's Method**: Two parameters ($\alpha, \beta$) — for **trend**.
- ✅ **Winter's Method**: Three parameters ($\alpha, \beta, \gamma$) — for **trend + seasonality**.
- ✅ **Linear Regression**: $F_t = a + b t$; slope $b$ via least squares.

### Forecast Error Metrics
- ✅ **MAD** — most common, same units as demand.
- ✅ **MSE** — penalizes large errors.
- ✅ **MAPE** — percentage; cross-product comparable.
- ✅ **Tracking Signal** — bias detector; limit = **±4**.

### Aggregate Planning
- ✅ Aggregate Planning operates at the **product family** level (NOT individual SKUs).
- ✅ Planning horizon: **3–18 months** (medium-range tactical).
- ✅ Three strategies: **Chase** (vary workforce), **Level** (constant workforce), **Hybrid** (mixed).
- ✅ Seven cost components: regular-time, overtime, hiring, firing, holding, backorder, subcontracting.
- ✅ Level Strategy → high inventory holding cost, low hiring/firing cost.
- ✅ Chase Strategy → low inventory, high hiring/firing cost.
- ✅ Methods: Spreadsheet trial-and-error, Linear Programming, Decision Rules, Management Coefficients Model.

### Final KTU Tips
- ✅ Always **define variables and units** before solving numerical problems.
- ✅ Show **every algebraic step** — partial marks depend on the working.
- ✅ In aggregate planning tables, **label each row clearly** (Forecast, Production, Beg Inv, End Inv).
- ✅ For comparison questions, compute BOTH strategies and recommend with justification.
- ✅ Remember the L-T-S-R decomposition: **Level, Trend, Seasonality, Random**.
- ✅ KTU emphasizes the **why** behind each formula — explain the logic, don't just substitute.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Trade-offs in Aggregate Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Trade-offs in Aggregate Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Aggregate Planning (AP)** is a medium-range (typically 3–18 months) operations management and supply chain strategy that establishes the overall production, workforce, and inventory levels to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost, while respecting capacity constraints and desired service levels. It translates long-range strategic plans into a tactical roadmap of *quantity* and *timing* of output.

A **Trade-off** in aggregate planning refers to the deliberate balancing of two or more competing cost or operational levers (e.g., inventory vs. backlogs, hiring vs. layoffs, overtime vs. idle time) so that the **total relevant cost** is minimized without violating capacity, policy, or service-level constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> "Aggregate Planning is fundamentally about *making trade-offs* between demand-side levers (price, promotion, backorders) and supply-side levers (workforce, inventory, overtime, subcontracting). The objective is NOT to find the perfect plan, but the **least-cost feasible plan** that balances conflicting objectives."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Balance" Intuition

Imagine you are a **fisherman** who must decide how many boats to deploy each month to catch fish for a coastal village:

- If you keep **all boats active** every day, you catch more than needed in some months (excess fish spoils = *inventory cost*) but never run short.
- If you **add or remove boats** each month based on the forecast (hire fishermen in summer, lay them off in monsoon), you save on storage but pay heavy hiring/training costs and risk a demoralized workforce.
- If you **process the surplus fish into dried/canned stock** (subcontracting/preservation), you pay a preservation premium but avoid spoilage.
- If you **tell customers "come back next week"** when fish is scarce (backlog/backorder), you avoid waste but lose goodwill.

**The fisherman, like an operations manager, constantly trades off:**
- **Stable workforce vs. Demand-matching workforce**
- **Inventory holding vs. Backorders**
- **Regular-time production vs. Overtime**
- **In-house production vs. Subcontracting**

Every aggregate plan is essentially this *bucket-balancing* exercise across time.

### 1.3 The Six Core Aggregate Planning Levers

| # | Lever | Type | Primary Cost Driver |
|---|-------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Workforce size** (hire/layoff) | Supply | Hiring & Layoff Cost |
| 2 | **Production rate** (level/chase) | Supply | Idle/Overtime Cost |
| 3 | **Inventory** (carry/stockout) | Buffer | Holding & Backlog Cost |
| 4 | **Overtime / Undertime** | Capacity | Premium Labour Cost |
| 5 | **Subcontracting** | External | Vendor Unit Cost |
| 6 | **Pricing / Promotion** | Demand | Lost Margin / Stimulation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Constants in AP Models (KTU Board Reference Values):**
> - Holding cost $\approx$ **15%–30% of unit cost per year**
> - Backorder cost $\approx$ **2–5× holding cost**
> - Overtime premium $\approx$ **1.25–1.5× regular wage**
> - Hiring cost $\approx$ **1–3 months' salary per worker**
> - Layoff cost $\approx$ **0.5–2 months' salary per worker**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Trade-offs in Aggregate Planning

Aggregate planning trade-offs can be classified along three orthogonal dimensions:

#### **Dimension A: Cost Magnitude vs. Service Quality**
- Holding inventory ⇒ **↑ Cost**, **↑ Service Level**
- Allowing backorders ⇒ **↓ Holding Cost**, **↓ Service Level**

#### **Dimension B: Workforce Stability vs. Responsiveness**
- Stable workforce (level strategy) ⇒ **↓ Hiring/Layoff Cost**, **↑ Inventory Cost**
- Flexible workforce (chase strategy) ⇒ **↓ Inventory Cost**, **↑ Hiring/Layoff Cost**

#### **Dimension C: Internal Capacity vs. External Sourcing**
- Use overtime ⇒ **↑ Labour Premium**, **↓ Subcontracting Cost**
- Use subcontracting ⇒ **↑ Unit Cost**, **↓ Capacity Pressure**

### 2.2 The Six Fundamental Trade-offs (Expanded)

#### **Trade-off 1: Inventory vs. Backorders**
$$TC_{IB} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left( c_h \cdot I_t + c_b \cdot B_t \right)$$

where $c_h$ = holding cost per unit per period, $c_b$ = backorder cost per unit per period.

**Why it matters:** A unit held one extra month costs $c_h$; the same unit backordered costs $c_b$. If $c_b > c_h$, hold inventory; if $c_b < c_h$, allow backlog.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering application:** Just-in-Time (JIT) systems in Toyota famously choose *very low inventory* even at the cost of occasional stockouts in supplier networks — because $c_h$ (capital tied up) was historically larger than $c_b$ in Japanese manufacturing economics.

#### **Trade-off 2: Hire/Layoff vs. Overtime/Undertime**
$$TC_{WL} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left( c_{hire} \cdot H_t + c_{lay} \cdot L_t + c_{reg} \cdot W_t + c_{ot} \cdot O_t - c_{ut} \cdot U_t \right)$$

- $H_t$, $L_t$ = workers hired and laid off in period $t$
- $O_t$, $U_t$ = overtime and undertime hours in period $t$
- $c_{hire}$, $c_{lay}$ = cost per worker hired/laid off
- $c_{reg}$, $c_{ot}$ = regular and overtime wage rates

#### **Trade-off 3: Regular Production vs. Subcontracting**
$$TC_{PS} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left( c_r \cdot P_t + c_s \cdot S_t \right)$$

where $c_r$ = in-house unit cost, $c_s$ = subcontractor unit cost (typically $c_s > c_r$).

#### **Trade-off 4: Level vs. Chase Production**
- **Level:** $P_t = \bar{D}$ (constant) ⇒ inventory swings absorb demand variance.
- **Chase:** $P_t = D_t$ ⇒ workforce swings absorb demand variance.

The choice is governed by which variance-absorption mechanism is cheaper: **inventory carry** or **workforce churn**.

#### **Trade-off 5: Demand Smoothing via Pricing vs. Production Smoothing via Inventory**
- High price in peak season ⇒ demand falls ⇒ less production pressure.
- Low price off-season ⇒ demand rises ⇒ better capacity utilization.

Revenue impact must be subtracted from production cost to get **net trade-off value**.

#### **Trade-off 6: Capacity Utilization vs. Flexibility**
High utilization ($>90\%$) lowers unit cost (fixed-cost amortization) but increases *queue time*, *lead time*, and *missed due dates*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute-value notation:** Use `\vert` (not the markdown table pipe) for absolute values. Example: `$\vert I_t \vert$` will render correctly inside tables.

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Typical Units** |
|------------|-------------|-------------------|
| $D_t$ | Forecasted demand in period $t$ | units |
| $P_t$ | Regular-time production in period $t$ | units |
| $O_t$ | Overtime production in period $t$ | units |
| $S_t$ | Subcontracted production in period $t$ | units |
| $I_t$ | Inventory at end of period $t$ | units |
| $B_t$ | Backlog at end of period $t$ | units |
| $W_t$ | Workforce size in period $t$ | workers |
| $H_t$ | Workers hired in period $t$ | workers |
| $L_t$ | Workers laid off in period $t$ | workers |
| $k$ | Productivity (units per worker per period) | units/worker/period |
| $c_r$ | Regular production cost | ₹/unit |
| $c_o$ | Overtime production cost | ₹/unit |
| $c_s$ | Subcontracting cost | ₹/unit |
| $c_h$ | Holding cost | ₹/unit/period |
| $c_b$ | Backorder cost | ₹/unit/period |
| $c_{hire}$ | Hiring cost | ₹/worker |
| $c_{lay}$ | Layoff cost | ₹/worker |
| $w$ | Wage rate | ₹/worker/period |

**Core Balance Equation (Inventory Flow):**
$$I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t + O_t + S_t - D_t$$

**Workforce Flow Equation:**
$$W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - L_t$$

**Production Capacity Equation:**
$$P_t = k \cdot W_t \quad \text{(level case)}$$

**Production Capacity Equation with OT:**
$$P_t + O_t = k \cdot W_t + \text{OT hours} \cdot k_{ot}$$

**Total Cost Function (Generic Linear Programming Objective):**
$$TC = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \Big[ c_{hire} H_t + c_{lay} L_t + w W_t + c_r P_t + c_o O_t + c_s S_t + c_h I_t + c_b B_t \Big]$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Aggregate Planning Cost Trade-off Equation

We derive the **Economic Trade-off Condition** that determines the *optimal balance* between two competing cost levers.

#### **Setup**
Consider a single-period decision: produce $Q$ units in-house at cost $c_r$, or produce them via subcontracting at cost $c_s$, where $c_s > c_r$. The extra capacity gained from subcontracting $S$ units is $S$, and we save on **overtime** which would otherwise cost $c_o$ per unit, or on **inventory carry** for $c_h$ per unit, or on **backorder** at $c_b$ per unit.

#### **Step 1: Define the Marginal Cost of In-house Production**
The marginal cost of producing one extra unit in-house during peak period is:
$$MC_{in\text{-}house} = c_r + (c_o - c_r) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{overtime}} + c_h \cdot \Delta I$$

#### **Step 2: Define the Marginal Cost of Subcontracting**
Subcontracting replaces the need for in-house production but at a unit premium:
$$MC_{sub} = c_s$$

#### **Step 3: Substitute Only When Marginal Benefit Exceeds Marginal Cost**
The optimal subcontracting quantity satisfies:
$$MC_{sub} \leq MC_{in\text{-}house}$$
$$c_s \leq c_r + \text{(avoided overtime premium)} + \text{(avoided holding cost)}$$
$$c_s \leq c_r + (c_o - c_r) + c_h \cdot t_{wait}$$

Simplifying, the **trade-off threshold condition** becomes:
$$c_s - c_r \leq c_o - c_r + c_h \cdot t_{wait}$$
$$c_s \leq c_o + c_h \cdot t_{wait}$$

#### **Step 4: Generalize to Multi-Period Setting**
For $T$ periods, summing over all periods:
$$\sum_{t=1}^{T} c_s \cdot S_t \leq \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left( c_o \cdot O_t + c_h \cdot I_t + c_b \cdot B_t \right) + \sum_{t=1}^{T} (c_s - c_r) \cdot S_t^{critical}$$

**Interpretation:** Subcontracting is economically justified **only when** the unit premium $c_s - c_r$ is less than the combined cost of:
- Overtime it displaces
- Inventory holding it displaces
- Backorders it prevents

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Trade-off Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU 2024 Scheme standard numerical pattern. Master this template.**

**Problem:** A firm faces demand for the next 3 months as $D_1 = 1200$, $D_2 = 1800$, $D_3 = 1500$ units. Current workforce $W_0 = 50$ workers, productivity $k = 30$ units/worker/month. Evaluate the **inventory vs. subcontracting trade-off**.

**Given Costs:**
- Regular production: $c_r =$ **₹50/unit**
- Overtime premium: $c_o =$ **₹75/unit**
- Subcontracting: $c_s =$ **₹90/unit**
- Holding cost: $c_h =$ **₹8/unit/month**
- Hiring cost: $c_{hire} =$ **₹2,000/worker**
- Backorder cost: $c_b =$ **₹30/unit/month**

**Step 1: Calculate Average Demand**
$$\bar{D} = \frac{1200 + 1800 + 1500}{3} = 1500 \text{ units/month}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Level Production Needed**
$$P_{level} = \bar{D} = 1500 \text{ units/month}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Required Workforce for Level Strategy**
$$W_{level} = \frac{P_{level}}{k} = \frac{1500}{30} = 50 \text{ workers}$$

Since $W_0 = 50 = W_{level}$, **no hiring/layoff is needed**. This is the cheapest workforce option.

**Step 4: Calculate Inventory Build-Up Under Level Strategy**

Starting inventory $I_0 = 0$:

$$I_1 = I_0 + P_1 - D_1 = 0 + 1500 - 1200 = +300 \text{ units (stockpile)}$$

$$I_2 = I_1 + P_2 - D_2 = 300 + 1500 - 1800 = 0 \text{ units}$$

$$I_3 = I_2 + P_3 - D_3 = 0 + 1500 - 1500 = 0 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Holding Cost for Level Strategy**
$$TC_{hold} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} c_h \cdot I_t = 8 \times (300 + 0 + 0) = \text{₹}2{,}400$$

**Step 6: Evaluate Chase Strategy (Alternative)**

In chase strategy, workforce changes each period:
$$W_1 = \frac{D_1}{k} = \frac{1200}{30} = 40 \text{ workers} \Rightarrow L_1 = 10$$

$$W_2 = \frac{D_2}{k} = \frac{1800}{30} = 60 \text{ workers} \Rightarrow H_2 = 20$$

$$W_3 = \frac{D_3}{k} = 1500 / 30 = 50 \text{ workers} \Rightarrow L_3 = 10$$

**Step 7: Calculate Workforce Adjustment Cost**
$$TC_{WL} = c_{lay} \cdot L_1 + c_{hire} \cdot H_2 + c_{lay} \cdot L_3$$

Assuming $c_{lay} =$ ₹1,500/worker and $c_{hire} =$ ₹2,000/worker:
$$TC_{WL} = 1500 \times 10 + 2000 \times 20 + 1500 \times 10 = \text{₹}70{,}000$$

**Step 8: Compare the Two Strategies**

| Strategy | Production Cost | Inventory Cost | Workforce Cost | **Total** |
|----------|----------------|----------------|----------------|-----------|
| **Level** | ₹50 × 4500 = ₹2,25,000 | ₹2,400 | ₹0 | **₹2,27,400** |
| **Chase** | ₹50 × 4500 = ₹2,25,000 | ₹0 | ₹70,000 | **₹2,95,000** |

**Conclusion:** The **Level Strategy saves ₹67,600** over the Chase Strategy. The **inventory-vs-workload trade-off decisively favors inventory carry** because hiring/layoff costs dominate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity Rule:** If $c_{hire} + c_{lay}$ were ₹500/worker instead, the chase strategy would cost only ₹15,000 in workforce adjustments, and the *trade-off would flip*. This is why the relative magnitudes of the cost coefficients — not absolute values — drive the trade-off decision.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Lever

| **If the cost driver is high...** | **Use the substitute lever...** | **Trade-off realized** |
|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| Holding cost $c_h$ | Backorders ($c_b$ if cheaper) | Inventory vs. Service |
| Hiring cost $c_{hire}$ | Overtime ($c_o$) | Stability vs. Capacity |
| Overtime cost $c_o$ | Subcontracting ($c_s$) | Internal vs. External |
| Subcontracting cost $c_s$ | Inventory build-up ($c_h$) | External vs. Internal buffer |
| Layoff cost $c_{lay}$ | Maintain workforce + Backorders | Morale vs. Service |
| Backorder cost $c_b$ | Inventory build-up ($c_h$) | Service vs. Capital |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Six Trade-off Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Aggregate Planning Decision] --> B{Forecast Demand Variability?}
    B -- Low Variability --> C[Use LEVEL Strategy]
    B -- High Variability --> D{Flexible Workforce Available?}

    D -- Yes --> E[Use CHASE Strategy]
    D -- No --> F{Capacity Slack Exists?}

    F -- Yes --> G[Use OVERTIME]
    F -- No --> H{Inventory Buffer Feasible?}

    H -- Yes, c_h is low --> I[Build INVENTORY]
    H -- No, c_h is high --> J{Subcontractor Reliable?}

    J -- Yes --> K[Use SUBCONTRACTING]
    J -- No --> L[Allow BACKORDERS]

    G --> M[Cost Premium: c_o > c_r]
    I --> N[Cost: c_h per unit per period]
    K --> O[Cost Premium: c_s > c_r]
    L --> P[Cost: c_b per unit per period]

    M --> Q[Compare Total TC - Choose Minimum]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q

    E --> R[Cost: c_hire, c_lay]
    C --> S[Cost: c_h, c_b]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Trade-off Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SupplySideLevers
        A1[Workforce Size]
        A2[Overtime Hours]
        A3[Subcontracting Volume]
    end

    subgraph BufferLevers
        B1[Inventory Level]
        B2[Backlog Level]
    end

    subgraph DemandSideLevers
        C1[Pricing Strategy]
        C2[Promotion Spend]
    end

    A1 -- Trade-off --> B1
    A1 -- Trade-off --> A2
    A2 -- Trade-off --> A3
    B1 -- Trade-off --> B2
    B2 -- Trade-off --> C1
    C1 -- Trade-off --> C2
    B1 -- Trade-off --> A3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cost Component Flow in Aggregate Plan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Aggregate Plan Period t]) --> P1[Production in Period t]
    Start --> W1[Workforce Adjustment]
    Start --> I1[Inventory Decision]

    P1 --> P2[Regular Time Cost: c_r times P_t]
    P1 --> P3[Overtime Cost: c_o times O_t]
    P1 --> P4[Subcontract Cost: c_s times S_t]

    W1 --> W2[Hiring Cost: c_hire times H_t]
    W1 --> W3[Layoff Cost: c_lay times L_t]
    W1 --> W4[Regular Wage: w times W_t]

    I1 --> I2[Holding Cost: c_h times I_t]
    I1 --> I3[Backorder Cost: c_b times B_t]

    P2 --> Total[Total Cost TC]
    P3 --> Total
    P4 --> Total
    W2 --> Total
    W3 --> Total
    W4 --> Total
    I2 --> Total
    I3 --> Total

    Total --> Decision{Optimal?}
    Decision -- Yes --> End([Execute Plan])
    Decision -- No --> Revise[Re-optimize using LP / Simulation]
    Revise --> Start
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **"Define aggregate planning. List the three core categories of trade-offs in aggregate planning."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks: 1 + 2 split):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Aggregate planning is a medium-range tactical planning process that determines the production quantity, workforce levels, and inventory positions to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost over a planning horizon of typically 3 to 18 months.

**Three Core Trade-off Categories (2 marks):**
1. **Cost vs. Service Level Trade-off** — Holding inventory improves service but raises cost; allowing backorders saves cost but hurts service.
2. **Workforce Stability vs. Responsiveness Trade-off** — Maintaining a stable workforce minimizes hiring/layoff costs but forces inventory swings; chasing demand minimizes inventory but increases workforce adjustment costs.
3. **Internal Capacity vs. External Sourcing Trade-off** — Using overtime absorbs demand surges in-house at premium cost; subcontracting transfers production outside at a per-unit premium.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often list "strategies" (level, chase, hybrid) instead of trade-off *categories*. Always phrase as a **balancing between two competing levers**, not a strategy choice.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **"Explain the trade-off between holding inventory and allowing backorders. State the economic decision rule."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

When forecasted demand exceeds production capacity in a given period, the manager faces a binary choice: (a) build inventory in earlier periods to absorb the shortfall, paying **holding cost** $c_h$ per unit per period; or (b) allow customers to wait (backorder), paying **backorder cost** $c_b$ per unit per period.

**Holding Inventory:**
- Cost = $c_h \times I_t$
- Benefit = High service level, immediate fulfillment
- Drawback = Capital tied up, obsolescence risk

**Allowing Backorders:**
- Cost = $c_b \times B_t$
- Benefit = No inventory carry, lower working capital
- Drawback = Lost goodwill, customer attrition

**Economic Decision Rule:**
$$\text{Hold inventory if } c_h \leq c_b \cdot p_{stockout}$$
$$\text{Allow backorder if } c_h > c_b \cdot p_{stockout}$$

In practice, since $c_b$ is typically 2–5× $c_h$, **inventory holding is preferred unless holding cost is exceptionally high** (e.g., perishable goods, fashion items, high-tech components).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students forget to qualify the rule with the *probability of stockout* factor. Always state: holding is preferred *unless* the holding cost exceeds the *expected* backorder cost, which includes the probability of occurrence.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation, students must answer ONE question from a choice of TWO. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

> **(a)** *Discuss in detail the six major aggregate planning trade-offs with suitable examples. State the cost equation governing each trade-off.* **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** *A manufacturer produces a single product. Forecasted demand for the next 4 months is:*
> *Jan = 800, Feb = 1200, Mar = 1500, Apr = 1100 units.*
> *Starting workforce = 40 workers; productivity = 25 units/worker/month; starting inventory = 0.*
> *Costs: regular production ₹40/unit; overtime ₹60/unit; subcontracting ₹70/unit; holding ₹5/unit/month; hiring ₹3000/worker; layoff ₹2000/worker.*
> *Compare the LEVEL strategy with the CHASE strategy. Which is cheaper and by how much?* **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — The Six Trade-offs (7 Marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Aggregate planning decisions revolve around balancing *conflicting objectives* — typically cost minimization vs. service-level maximization. There are six canonical trade-offs:

**Trade-off 1: Inventory vs. Backorders (1 mark)**
$$\min \sum_t (c_h I_t + c_b B_t)$$
*Example:* Pharmaceutical companies hold very low inventory due to expiry risk → accept some backorders. Consumer electronics hold inventory because backorder cost (lost sale) is very high.

**Trade-off 2: Hire/Layoff vs. Overtime/Undertime (1 mark)**
$$\min \sum_t (c_{hire} H_t + c_{lay} L_t + c_o O_t - c_{ut} U_t)$$
*Example:* Seasonal apparel brands use overtime in pre-festive months rather than hire-then-lay-off. Auto firms do the opposite — hire permanent workers for the model year and accept some idle time off-season.

**Trade-off 3: Regular Production vs. Subcontracting (1 mark)**
$$\min \sum_t (c_r P_t + c_s S_t)$$
*Example:* Toyota keeps subcontractor capacity as a buffer during demand spikes; in-house production handles the base load.

**Trade-off 4: Level vs. Chase Production (1 mark)**
$$\min \sum_t \left[ c_r P_t + c_h I_t + c_b B_t + c_{hire} H_t + c_{lay} L_t \right]$$
*Example:* Honda uses a hybrid — level base production plus chase via overtime.

**Trade-off 5: Pricing/Promotion vs. Production Adjustment (1 mark)**
Revenue lost from price discount must be ≤ cost saved by smoothing demand.
*Example:* Off-season airline pricing reduces capacity pressure.

**Trade-off 6: Capacity Utilization vs. Flexibility (1 mark)**
High utilization lowers per-unit cost but increases lead-time variance.
*Example:* Semiconductor fabs run at >90% utilization; job shops operate at <70% to accommodate mix changes.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The "best" plan is not the one that eliminates all trade-offs but the one that optimizes the weighted combination under the prevailing cost structure.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Numerical Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Given Data Summary (½ mark):**

| Month | Demand $D_t$ |
|-------|--------------|
| Jan | 800 |
| Feb | 1200 |
| Mar | 1500 |
| Apr | 1100 |
| **Total** | **4600** |

$W_0 = 40$ workers, $k = 25$ units/worker, $I_0 = 0$

**Step 1: Average Demand (½ mark)**
$$\bar{D} = \frac{800 + 1200 + 1500 + 1100}{4} = 1150 \text{ units/month}$$

**Step 2: LEVEL Strategy — Production (1 mark)**
$$P_{level} = 1150 \text{ units/month}$$
$$W_{level} = \frac{1150}{25} = 46 \text{ workers}$$
Since $W_0 = 40$, hire $H_1 = 6$ workers.

**Step 3: LEVEL Strategy — Inventory Build-up (1 mark)**

$$I_1 = 0 + 1150 - 800 = +350$$
$$I_2 = 350 + 1150 - 1200 = +300$$
$$I_3 = 300 + 1150 - 1500 = -50 \text{ (backorder)}$$
$$I_4 = -50 + 1150 - 1100 = 0$$

**Step 4: LEVEL Strategy — Total Cost (1 mark)**
- Hiring cost: $3000 \times 6 =$ ₹18,000
- Production cost: $40 \times 4600 =$ ₹1,84,000
- Holding cost: $5 \times (350 + 300) =$ ₹3,250
- Backorder cost: assuming ₹15/unit (standard) ⇒ $15 \times 50 =$ ₹750

$$TC_{level} = 18000 + 184000 + 3250 + 750 = \text{₹}2{,}06{,}000$$

**Step 5: CHASE Strategy — Workforce each month (1 mark)**
$$W_1 = \lceil 800/25 \rceil = 32 \Rightarrow L_1 = 8$$
$$W_2 = \lceil 1200/25 \rceil = 48 \Rightarrow H_2 = 16$$
$$W_3 = \lceil 1500/25 \rceil = 60 \Rightarrow H_3 = 12$$
$$W_4 = \lceil 1100/25 \rceil = 44 \Rightarrow L_4 = 16$$

**Step 6: CHASE Strategy — Total Cost (1 mark)**
- Production cost: $40 \times 4600 =$ ₹1,84,000
- Hiring: $3000 \times (16 + 12) =$ ₹84,000
- Layoff: $2000 \times (8 + 16) =$ ₹48,000
- Holding/Backorder: ₹0 (production matches demand)

$$TC_{chase} = 184000 + 84000 + 48000 = \text{₹}3{,}16{,}000$$

**Step 7: Comparison (½ mark)**
$$\Delta TC = TC_{chase} - TC_{level} = 316000 - 206000 = \text{₹}1{,}10{,}000$$

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
The **LEVEL strategy is cheaper by ₹1,10,000**. The inventory carry cost in the level strategy (₹3,250 + ₹750) is dramatically lower than the workforce churn cost in the chase strategy (₹84,000 + ₹48,000). This is the classic "hire/layoff costs dominate holding costs" scenario.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **(a)** *Explain with diagrams how the **Linear Programming formulation** of aggregate planning handles the trade-off between workforce stability and demand matching. Write the objective function and key constraints.* **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** *A company must choose between two aggregate plans for the next 2 months:*
> *Demand: Month 1 = 1000 units; Month 2 = 1400 units.*
> *Plan X (Level): produces 1200 units/month at ₹50/unit, holds inventory at ₹10/unit/month.*
> *Plan Y (Chase with OT): hires 10 workers at ₹500/worker and uses 200 hours overtime at ₹80/hour.*
> *Productivity = 20 units/worker/month. Calculate the total relevant cost of each plan and recommend.* **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — LP Formulation (7 Marks)**

**Conceptual Setup (2 marks):**
The Linear Programming (LP) formulation captures trade-offs by:
- Allowing the optimizer to choose *simultaneously* how much to hire, lay off, produce, hold, backorder, and subcontract.
- Each decision variable has a cost coefficient in the objective.
- Constraints (demand balance, workforce flow, capacity limits) ensure feasibility.

**Decision Variables (1 mark):**
- $W_t$ = workforce in period $t$
- $H_t, L_t$ = hires and layoffs in period $t$
- $P_t, O_t, S_t$ = regular, overtime, subcontracted production
- $I_t, B_t$ = inventory and backlog at end of period $t$

**Objective Function (2 marks):**
$$\min Z = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ c_{hire} H_t + c_{lay} L_t + w W_t + c_r P_t + c_o O_t + c_s S_t + c_h I_t + c_b B_t \right]$$

**Subject to Key Constraints (2 marks):**

**(i) Demand Balance Constraint (inventory flow):**
$$I_t - B_t = I_{t-1} - B_{t-1} + P_t + O_t + S_t - D_t$$

**(ii) Workforce Flow Constraint:**
$$W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - L_t$$

**(iii) Capacity Constraint:**
$$P_t \leq k \cdot W_t$$
$$O_t \leq O_t^{max}$$

**(iv) Non-negativity:**
$$W_t, H_t, L_t, P_t, O_t, S_t, I_t, B_t \geq 0$$

The optimizer trades off $H_t$ vs. $I_t$ vs. $O_t$ vs. $S_t$ based on their relative cost coefficients — this *is* the trade-off mechanism.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Plan Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Total Demand (½ mark)**
$$D_{total} = 1000 + 1400 = 2400 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2: Plan X (Level Strategy) — Production Cost (1 mark)**
$$TC_{prod}^X = 50 \times 2400 = \text{₹}1{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 3: Plan X — Inventory Build-up (1 mark)**
$$I_1 = 0 + 1200 - 1000 = +200 \text{ units}$$
$$I_2 = 200 + 1200 - 1400 = 0 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4: Plan X — Holding Cost (½ mark)**
$$TC_{hold}^X = 10 \times 200 = \text{₹}2{,}000$$

**Step 5: Plan X — Total Cost (½ mark)**
$$TC_X = 1{,}20{,}000 + 2{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}22{,}000$$

**Step 6: Plan Y (Chase + OT) — Workforce Production (1 mark)**
Regular production from baseline workers (assumed 50) = $50 \times 20 = 1000$ units (Month 1)
Extra workers contribute $10 \times 20 = 200$ units/month

**Step 7: Plan Y — Overtime Output (½ mark)**
OT output = $200 \text{ hours} \times \text{rate}$. Assuming 5 units/hour:
$$O_{total} = 200 \times 5 = 1000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 8: Plan Y — Total Cost (1 mark)**
- Hiring cost: $500 \times 10 =$ ₹5,000
- Overtime cost: $80 \times 200 =$ ₹16,000
- Regular production: $50 \times 2000 =$ ₹1,00,000
$$TC_Y = 5000 + 16000 + 100000 = \text{₹}1{,}21{,}000$$

**Step 9: Comparison and Recommendation (1 mark)**
$$TC_X - TC_Y = 122000 - 121000 = \text{₹}1{,}000$$

**Recommendation:** **Plan Y is marginally cheaper by ₹1,000.** However, Plan Y introduces operational complexity (hiring risk, OT fatigue, quality variation). For long-term horizons, **Plan X is recommended for stability**; for short bursts, Plan Y's flexibility justifies the small premium savings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Mistakes (Examiner Notes):**
> 1. Forgetting to include **ending inventory** at $t=2$ — must explicitly state it equals zero.
> 2. Miscomputing overtime output by ignoring the productivity conversion factor.
> 3. Failing to convert between "units" and "worker-hours" — productivity $k$ must be applied consistently.
> 4. Treating Plan Y's hiring as one-time — it must be added to total cost, not amortized.
> 5. Ignoring qualitative trade-offs (workforce morale, skill retention) — these earn partial marks in "discuss" type questions.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary — Read Before Exam:**

- ✅ Aggregate planning operates at the **tactical level**, horizon 3–18 months, in *product families or units*, not SKUs.
- ✅ The **fundamental trade-off** is between **cost minimization** and **service-level maintenance**.
- ✅ Six core trade-offs to memorize:
  1. Inventory vs. Backorders ($c_h$ vs. $c_b$)
  2. Hire/Layoff vs. Overtime/Undertime ($c_{hire}$, $c_{lay}$ vs. $c_o$)
  3. In-house vs. Subcontracting ($c_r$ vs. $c_s$)
  4. Level vs. Chase Production (inventory buffer vs. workforce buffer)
  5. Pricing vs. Production Smoothing (revenue foregone vs. cost saved)
  6. Utilization vs. Flexibility (per-unit cost vs. lead-time variance)
- ✅ The **inventory balance equation** $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t + O_t + S_t - D_t$ is non-negotiable.
- ✅ The **workforce flow equation** $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - L_t$ must always hold.
- ✅ **Decision rule for choosing strategy:** Compare total relevant cost (production + inventory + workforce adjustment + OT + subcontracting) and pick the minimum.
- ✅ **Rule of thumb:** If hiring + layoff cost ≫ holding cost ⇒ **Level strategy**.
- ✅ **Rule of thumb:** If $c_h \gg c_b$ (perishables, fashion) ⇒ **Chase strategy**.
- ✅ **LP formulation** captures all trade-offs simultaneously via the objective function coefficients.
- ✅ Always state **both quantitative (cost) and qualitative (morale, flexibility, skill)** implications in essay answers.
- ✅ Aggregate planning is **NOT** a detailed scheduling exercise — it sets the *envelope* within which short-term scheduling operates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Programming in Aggregate Planning, Coordination in Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Programming in Aggregate Planning, Coordination in Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Linear Programming (LP) in Aggregate Planning

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
**Aggregate Planning** is a medium-range (typically 3–18 months) tactical planning process that establishes a company-wide production rate, workforce levels, and inventory holdings to meet forecasted demand at minimum total cost. **Linear Programming (LP)** is a deterministic mathematical optimization technique that solves the aggregate planning problem by minimizing a linear objective function (total cost) subject to a set of linear equality and inequality constraints (demand fulfillment, capacity limits, workforce balance, inventory continuity).

The LP formulation in aggregate planning is formally classified under the **Transportation/Transshipment family of linear programs**, where periods act as origins/destinations and units of product (or labor) flow between time periods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Aggregate Planning is the *bridging mechanism* between long-range strategic planning (capacity, facility location) and short-range operational planning (scheduling, dispatching). LP is the *mathematical engine* used when the problem is too complex for the *Graphical/Charting Method* or *Trial-and-Error Heuristics* taught in introductory operations modules.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Bakery's Monthly Puzzle"
Imagine you run a bakery chain in Kerala. You know the demand for cakes every month for the next 6 months (Onam season spikes, monsoon dips, wedding boom). You must decide *how many cakes to bake*, *how many bakers to hire or lay off*, *whether to bake overtime*, and *whether to outsource to a neighboring bakery*. Each decision has a cost. **Linear Programming is the smartest spreadsheet** that crunches all these costs and constraints simultaneously and tells you the *exact cheapest way* to meet demand each month without stocking too much (which spoils) or too little (which loses customers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric used in KTU Problems:** Costs are typically expressed in **Rupees per unit (₹/cake, ₹/labor-hour)**. Planning horizon is usually **N = 4 to 12 periods** (months). Workforce productivity is assumed at a **fixed standard rate** of units per labor-hour.

## 1.2 Coordination in Supply Chain

### Formal Academic Definition
**Supply Chain Coordination** is the deliberate alignment of objectives, decisions, information flows, and incentives among all independent entities (suppliers, manufacturers, distributors, retailers) so that *system-wide (total supply chain) profit* is maximized rather than the *local profit* of any single stage. Lack of coordination produces the **Bullwhip Effect** — the amplification of demand variability as one moves upstream from the retailer to the manufacturer.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The WhatsApp Group Chain"
Picture a family WhatsApp group planning an Onam Sadhya. If the person hosting (retailer) only tells one cousin (wholesaler), who tells one uncle (distributor), who tells the vegetable vendor (manufacturer) — each person *guesses* and *exaggerates* slightly to be safe. By the time the message reaches the vendor, the order is for 10× the actual need. **Coordination is the family group video call** — where everyone sees the real guest count, plans exact quantities, and shares the cost of leftovers. Information transparency eliminates the distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept Cornerstone:** Coordination mechanisms include **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)**, **Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment (CPFR)**, **Information Sharing via EDI/Electronic Data Interchange**, and **Aligned Incentives (e.g., buyback/return contracts, quantity discounts, revenue sharing)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the LP Formulation in Aggregate Planning

The aggregate planning LP model consists of four building blocks: **Decision Variables**, **Objective Function**, **Constraints**, and **Parameters**.

### 2.1.1 Decision Variables (per period *t*)
Let the planning horizon be $T$ periods. The decision variables are:

- $P_t$ = units **produced** in regular time in period *t* (units)
- $I_t$ = units held in **inventory** at the end of period *t* (units)
- $H_t$ = number of workers **hired** at the start of period *t* (workers)
- $L_t$ = number of workers **laid off** at the start of period *t* (workers)
- $W_t$ = total **workforce size** in period *t* (workers)
- $O_t$ = units produced on **overtime** in period *t* (units)
- $S_t$ = units **subcontracted** in period *t* (units)
- $B_t$ = **backorders** (lost sales carried forward) at end of period *t* (units)

### 2.1.2 Objective Function
Minimize the **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** over the planning horizon:

$$
\min \; Z = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left( C_t^{P} P_t + C_t^{I} I_t + C_t^{H} H_t + C_t^{L} L_t + C_t^{W} W_t + C_t^{O} O_t + C_t^{S} S_t + C_t^{B} B_t \right)
$$

Where each $C$ represents the per-unit (or per-worker) cost in that category for period $t$.

### 2.1.3 Constraint Set
The LP is governed by six families of constraints:

1. **Demand Satisfaction Constraint** — Production + Inventory + Backorders must meet demand:
$$P_t + O_t + S_t + I_{t-1} - I_t + B_{t-1} - B_t = D_t$$

2. **Workforce Balance Constraint**:
$$W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - L_t$$

3. **Production Capacity Constraint** (regular time):
$$P_t \le p \cdot W_t$$
where $p$ is productivity (units per worker per period).

4. **Overtime Capacity Constraint**:
$$O_t \le \beta \cdot P_t$$
where $\beta$ is the maximum overtime fraction (typically 0.25 to 0.5).

5. **Subcontracting Capacity Constraint**:
$$0 \le S_t \le S_t^{\max}$$

6. **Non-negativity & Integer Constraints**:
$$P_t, I_t, H_t, L_t, W_t, O_t, S_t, B_t \ge 0, \quad H_t, L_t, W_t \in \mathbb{Z}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Linear?** Because all costs and capacities are assumed *constant per unit* (no economies of scale, no learning curves). When this assumption breaks, the problem becomes a *Mixed Integer Nonlinear Program (MINLP)*, which is outside KTU undergraduate scope.

## 2.2 Coordination in Supply Chain — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 The Bullwhip Effect (Lee, Padmanabhan, Whang, 1997)
Demand variance amplifies as one moves upstream. Quantitatively:

$$
\text{Bullwhip Ratio} = \frac{\text{Var}(\text{Orders placed to supplier})}{\text{Var}(\text{Customer demand})} \;\;>\; 1
$$

### 2.2.2 Four Root Causes of the Bullwhip Effect
1. **Demand Signal Processing (Forrester Effect)** —订货量调整基于局部预测
2. **Order Batching** — economic order quantities (EOQ) cause lumpy orders
3. **Price Fluctuations** — promotional discounts and forward buying
4. **Rationing & Shortage Gaming** — customers over-order when supply is scarce

### 2.2.3 Obstacles to Coordination
- ** Incentive Misalignment** — each echelon maximizes its own profit
- **Information Asymmetry** — POS data not shared
- **Operational Obstacles** — lack of IT infrastructure
- **Pricing Obstacles** — quantity discounts encourage batching
- **Behavioral Obstacles** — local optimization, fear of losing power
- **Organizational/Managerial Obstacles** — functional silos, KPIs in conflict

### 2.2.4 Managerial Levers to Improve Coordination
| Lever | Mechanism | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| Incentive Alignment | Buyback, revenue sharing, quantity discounts | P&G-Walmart CPFR |
| Information Sharing | EDI, VMI, POS data exchange | Barilla's Vendor Managed Inventory |
| Operational Improvement | Lead-time reduction, ECR | Toyota Production System |
| Pricing Strategy | Every Day Low Pricing (EDLP) | Walmart EDLP vs Hi-Lo |
| Organizational Design | Cross-functional S&OP teams | Demand-Supply balancing meetings |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_t$ | Forecast demand in period $t$ | units | Always given in question |
| $P_t$ | Regular-time production | units | ≤ capacity |
| $I_t$ | Ending inventory | units | $I_0$ is given |
| $H_t$ | Workers hired | workers | Integer variable |
| $L_t$ | Workers laid off | workers | Integer variable |
| $W_t$ | Workforce level | workers | Carries over time |
| $O_t$ | Overtime production | units | Bounded above |
| $S_t$ | Subcontracted units | units | Bounded above |
| $B_t$ | Backorders | units | Allowed or not |
| $C^P$ | Regular production cost | ₹/unit | Lowest cost source |
| $C^O$ | Overtime cost premium | ₹/unit | ≈ 1.25× to 1.5× of $C^P$ |
| $C^S$ | Subcontracting cost | ₹/unit | Highest cost source |
| $C^I$ | Inventory holding cost | ₹/unit/period | ≈ 0.10× to 0.25× of $C^P$ |
| $C^H$ | Hiring cost | ₹/worker | Includes training |
| $C^L$ | Layoff cost | ₹/worker | Includes severance |
| $p$ | Productivity | units/worker/period | Used in capacity constraint |
| $\beta$ | Overtime cap fraction | dimensionless | Usually 0.25 or 0.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** The bullwhip ratio is **NOT** the ratio of demand to variance. It is the ratio of *order variance* to *demand variance*. A ratio greater than 1 indicates amplification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Model Setup & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 LP Model Setup for a Generic Aggregate Planning Problem (4-Period Case)

### 3.1.1 Worked Construction of the Model

**Step 1 — Identify the Planning Horizon**
The KTU paper typically specifies $T = 4$ periods (quarterly planning). Here we let $T = 4$.

**Step 2 — Define Decision Variables**
For each period $t \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$:
- $P_t$ — regular production (units)
- $O_t$ — overtime production (units)
- $S_t$ — subcontracted units (units)
- $I_t$ — ending inventory (units)
- $W_t$ — workforce (workers, integer)
- $H_t$ — workers hired (workers, integer)
- $L_t$ — workers laid off (workers, integer)

**Step 3 — Write the Cost Coefficients**
Let us assume the standard cost structure (in ₹):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C^{P} &= 100 \text{ ₹/unit (regular production)} \\
C^{O} &= 130 \text{ ₹/unit (overtime premium)} \\
C^{S} &= 150 \text{ ₹/unit (subcontracting)} \\
C^{I} &= 20 \text{ ₹/unit/period (holding)} \\
C^{H} &= 5000 \text{ ₹/worker (hiring cost)} \\
C^{L} &= 8000 \text{ ₹/worker (layoff cost)} \\
C^{W} &= 0 \text{ (salary not charged, only hiring/layoff)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Build the Objective Function**

$$
\min Z = \sum_{t=1}^{4} \big( 100 P_t + 130 O_t + 150 S_t + 20 I_t + 5000 H_t + 8000 L_t \big)
$$

**Step 5 — Constraints**

(a) **Demand-balance constraints** (with initial inventory $I_0 = 100$ units):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 + O_1 + S_1 - I_1 &= D_1 - I_0 \\
P_t + O_t + S_t + I_{t-1} - I_t &= D_t, \quad t = 2, 3, 4
\end{aligned}
$$

(b) **Workforce balance constraints** (with $W_0 = 50$ workers):

$$
W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - L_t, \quad t = 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

(c) **Regular production capacity** (productivity $p = 20$ units/worker):

$$
P_t \le 20 \cdot W_t, \quad t = 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

(d) **Overtime cap** ($\beta = 0.25$):

$$
O_t \le 0.25 \, P_t, \quad t = 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

(e) **Subcontracting cap** ($S^{\max} = 200$ units):

$$
0 \le S_t \le 200, \quad t = 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

(f) **Non-negativity & integrality**:

$$
P_t, O_t, S_t, I_t \ge 0 \in \mathbb{R}; \quad H_t, L_t, W_t \ge 0 \in \mathbb{Z}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most Module-2 questions present the LP as a *formulation* exercise (10–14 marks), not as a *solve-by-Simplex* exercise. Master the formulation; the Simplex table is bonus.

## 3.2 Illustrative Numerical Solution (For Conceptual Verification)

Suppose the demand vector is $D = [600, 1200, 900, 400]$ and the optimal strategy is to keep workforce flat at 60 workers (using $H_1 = 10$, $L_4 = 0$) and produce on overtime in period 2 only.

**Step 1 — Production allocation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 20 \times 60 = 1200 \text{ units} \quad \text{(more than } D_1=600) \\
I_1 &= I_0 + P_1 - D_1 = 100 + 1200 - 600 = 700 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Period 2 (with overtime)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= 20 \times 60 = 1200 \\
O_2 &= 0.25 \times 1200 = 300 \text{ (max)} \\
I_2 &= I_1 + P_2 + O_2 - D_2 = 700 + 1200 + 300 - 1200 = 1000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Period 3 (deplete inventory)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_3 &= 20 \times 60 = 1200 \\
I_3 &= I_2 + P_3 - D_3 = 1000 + 1200 - 900 = 1300 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Period 4 (no overtime needed)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_4 &= 20 \times 60 = 1200 \\
I_4 &= I_3 + P_4 - D_4 = 1300 + 1200 - 400 = 2100 \text{ units (end-of-horizon stock)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Cost calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production cost} &= 100 \times 4800 = 4,80,000 \\
\text{Overtime cost} &= 130 \times 300 = 39,000 \\
\text{Holding cost} &= 20 \times (700+1000+1300+2100) = 20 \times 5100 = 1,02,000 \\
\text{Hiring cost} &= 5000 \times 10 = 50,000 \\
\text{Total cost } Z &= 4,80,000 + 39,000 + 1,02,000 + 50,000 = \mathbf{6,71,000 \; ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Coordination Mechanisms vs. Bullwhip Cause

| Coordination Mechanism | Demand Signal Processing | Order Batching | Price Fluctuations | Rationing Gaming |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Information Sharing (POS/EDI)** | ✅ Strong | ⚪ Weak | ⚪ Weak | ✅ Strong |
| **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)** | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | ⚪ Weak | ✅ Strong |
| **CPFR** | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong |
| **EDLP (Every Day Low Pricing)** | ⚪ Weak | ⚪ Weak | ✅ Strong | ⚪ Weak |
| **Lead-Time Reduction** | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | ⚪ Weak | ✅ Strong |
| **Aligned Incentives (Buyback)** | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | ⚪ Weak | ✅ Strong |

**Legend:** ✅ Strong mitigation effect | ⚪ Weak/Indirect effect

## 3.4 Derivation of the Bullwhip Ratio (Order Batching Case)

To understand the *order batching* contribution analytically:

**Step 1 — Assume retailer demand** $D \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Step 2 — Retailer places orders** of size $Q$ in an EOQ cycle of length $Q/D_{\text{avg}}$. Orders are placed every $L$ periods, generating **block demand** of magnitude $Q$ with inter-arrival time $L$.

**Step 3 — Variance of orders placed by retailer** (a random sum of $Q$ over cycles):

$$
\text{Var}(O_{\text{retailer}}) = \frac{Q \, \sigma^2}{D_{\text{avg}}} + Q^2 \cdot \frac{\sigma^2_{D}}{D_{\text{avg}}^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Bullwhip Ratio** (the upstream amplification):

$$
\text{Bullwhip} = \frac{\text{Var}(O_{\text{supplier}})}{\text{Var}(D)} = \frac{L \cdot \sigma^2_D / D_{\text{avg}} + (L^2 \cdot D^2_{\text{avg}})}{D^2_{\text{avg}}} \approx L + \frac{L}{Q}
$$

For $L = 4$ periods and $Q = 10$ units, the bullwhip ratio ≈ $4 + 0.4 = 4.4$ — a **340% amplification** of variance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
