# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — The Foundation of Cycle Inventory

The EOQ model minimizes the **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)**, which is the sum of **Annual Ordering Cost** and **Annual Holding Cost** (purchase cost is constant in basic EOQ and ignored during optimization).

### Step-by-Step Logic of EOQ

1. **Assume demand is constant and known** at rate $D$ units per year.
2. **A fixed cost $S$** is incurred **every time an order is placed** (administrative, transport, inspection).
3. **A holding cost $H$** is incurred **per unit per year** for storing inventory (capital, warehousing, insurance, obsolescence).
4. **Number of orders per year** = $\dfrac{D}{Q}$
5. **Annual Ordering Cost** = $\dfrac{D}{Q} \times S$
6. **Average inventory** = $\dfrac{Q}{2}$ (assumes uniform depletion)
7. **Annual Holding Cost** = $\dfrac{Q}{2} \times H$
8. **Total Relevant Cost** = Ordering Cost + Holding Cost

The **Why** behind EOQ: as $Q$ increases, ordering cost falls (fewer orders) but holding cost rises (more stock). The minimum of the sum occurs when the two costs are equal.

### The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Average Cycle Inventory** | $ACI = \dfrac{Q}{2}$ | $Q$ = order quantity (units) |
| 2 | **Annual Ordering Cost** | $AOC = \dfrac{D}{Q} \times S$ | $D$ = annual demand (units/yr), $S$ = ordering cost (Rs/order) |
| 3 | **Annual Holding Cost** | $AHC = \dfrac{Q}{2} \times H$ | $H$ = holding cost (Rs/unit/yr) |
| 4 | **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** | $TRC(Q) = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{QH}{2}$ | Rs/year |
| 5 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | units |
| 6 | **Optimal # of Orders/Year** | $N^{*} = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DH}{2S}}$ | orders/year |
| 7 | **Optimal Time Between Orders** | $T^{*} = \dfrac{Q^{*}}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{DH}}$ | years |
| 8 | **Min Total Relevant Cost** | $TRC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | Rs/year |
| 9 | **Holding Cost as % of Price** | $H = i \times C$ | $i$ = holding rate, $C$ = unit cost |
| 10 | **Joint Replenishment EOQ (Product i)** | $Q_i^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2D_i S_i}{H_i} \cdot \dfrac{\sum D_i C_i}{\sum D_i C_i + \sum K_i / T^{*2}}}$ | $K_i$ = product-specific order cost |
| 11 | **Square Root Law of Inventory (Aggregation)** | $TC_{agg} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \sum TC_i$ | $n$ = # of aggregated facilities |
| 12 | **TRC with All-Units Discount** | $TRC = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{Q \cdot i \cdot P}{2} + D \cdot P$ | $P$ = unit price with discount |
| 13 | **Reorder Point (Deterministic)** | $ROP = d \times L$ | $d$ = daily demand, $L$ = lead time (days) |
| 14 | **Reorder Point (Stochastic)** | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + SS$ | $SS$ = safety stock (units) |
| 15 | **Safety Stock (Std Dev Known, CSL-based)** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | $z$ = z-score for desired service level |
| 16 | **Std Dev of Lead Time Demand** | $\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\sigma_{L}^{2}}$ | $\sigma_d$ = std dev of daily demand |
| 17 | **Fill Rate Formula** | $\beta = 1 - \dfrac{E[z] \cdot \sigma_{LT}}{Q}$ | $E[z]$ = expected shortage per cycle |
| 18 | **Aggregate Mean (n products)** | $\bar{D}_{agg} = \sum \bar{D}_i$ | units/year |
| 19 | **Aggregate Std Dev (n products)** | $\sigma_{agg} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^{2}}$ | units |
| 20 | **Number of Kanban (CONWIP)** | $N = \dfrac{\bar{d}_{LT} + SS}{C}$ | $C$ = container size |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM RULE:** In the EOQ formula $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$, the units must be **consistent**. If $D$ is *units/year* and $S$ is *Rs/order*, then $H$ must be *Rs/unit/year*. A common KTU error is mixing $H = i \times C$ incorrectly with $D$ in different time bases.

## 2.2 Why EOQ is Used in Practice (Real-World Utility)

- **Procurement decisions** for raw materials (steel, chemicals) where demand is steady.
- **Retail replenishment** of fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) like rice, sugar, soap.
- **Spare parts management** in aerospace, railways, and power plants.
- **Hospital pharmacy** orders of consumables.
- **A base model** for SAP, Oracle, and Tally ERP inventory modules.

## 2.3 Aggregation of Multiple Products — The "Shared Truck" Logic

When a single truck visits a supplier, the cost of the trip is **shared** across all products. The fixed joint ordering cost $S$ is incurred once per trip, not per product. The total cost becomes:

$$TRC = \underbrace{\frac{S}{T}}_{\text{One order per cycle T}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \frac{D_i S_i}{Q_i} + \frac{Q_i H_i}{2} \right]$$

where $S$ is the joint (shared) cost, $S_i$ is the product-specific marginal cost, and $T$ is the joint cycle time. The optimal order frequency is:

$$n^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum D_i C_i}{S}}$$

where $C_i$ relates to the product-specific ordering and holding cost.

## 2.4 Quantity Discounts — Trade-off Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Logic for Quantity Discount Problem Solving:** When facing a quantity discount schedule, the optimal order quantity is **either** the unconstrained EOQ **or** the minimum order quantity of the next-best discount tier — whichever yields a **lower total cost**. The procedure is:
> 1. Compute $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ using the *lowest* price.
> 2. If $Q^* \geq$ minimum order quantity for that tier, that tier is optimal.
> 3. Otherwise, evaluate TRC at $Q = $ min order quantity of each tier and pick the lowest.
> 4. Compare also with the EOQ of the chosen tier.

## 2.5 Uncertainty — Why Safety Stock Exists

**Two sources of uncertainty** exist in any supply chain:
1. **Demand Uncertainty** — customers buy more or less than predicted.
2. **Lead Time Uncertainty** — the supplier takes longer or shorter than promised.

If both were deterministic, no safety stock would be needed. Because they are random, the **Reorder Point (ROP)** must be set high enough to cover demand during lead time with a high probability. The buffer is the **Safety Stock (SS)**.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

The Total Relevant Cost function is the sum of ordering and holding costs:

$$\begin{aligned}
TRC(Q) &= \text{Ordering Cost} + \text{Holding Cost} \\
TRC(Q) &= \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate TRC with respect to Q:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\, TRC(Q)}{dQ} &= \frac{d}{dQ}\left[ \frac{DS}{Q} \right] + \frac{d}{dQ}\left[ \frac{QH}{2} \right] \\
&= DS \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{Q^{2}} \right) + \frac{H}{2} \\
&= -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Set the derivative equal to zero to find the minimum:**

$$\begin{aligned}
-\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} &= 0 \\
\frac{H}{2} &= \frac{DS}{Q^{2}} \\
Q^{2} &= \frac{2DS}{H} \\
Q^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Verify it is a minimum (second derivative test):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^{2} TRC(Q)}{dQ^{2}} &= \frac{2DS}{Q^{3}} > 0 \quad \text{(positive, confirms minimum)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Q^*$ back to get the minimum total cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
TRC^{*} &= \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{\sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot H}{2} \\
&= \sqrt{DS \cdot H \cdot \frac{DS}{2DS}} + \sqrt{2DSH} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} \\
TRC^{*} &= 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} = \sqrt{2DSH}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful observation:** At the optimal $Q^*$, the **annual ordering cost equals the annual holding cost**:
> $\dfrac{DS}{Q^*} = \dfrac{Q^* H}{2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DSH}{2}}$

## 3.2 Derivation — TRC with All-Units Quantity Discount

When a discount is applied, the **unit price $P$ changes**, which changes the holding cost (since $H = i \times P$). The TRC becomes:

$$TRC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{DS}{Q}}_{\text{Ordering}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q \cdot i \cdot P}{2}}_{\text{Holding}} + \underbrace{D \cdot P}_{\text{Purchase (constant for given tier)}}$$

**Step-by-step discount problem procedure:**

1. **Compute the EOQ for the lowest price tier** (largest discount). Call this $Q^*_L$.
2. **Check feasibility:** If $Q^*_L \geq Q_{min,L}$ (minimum order quantity for that tier), it is optimal.
3. **If not feasible**, adjust upward to $Q_{min,L}$ and compute TRC.
4. **Compare TRC across all feasible tiers**, including EOQs of higher-price tiers.
5. **Pick the lowest TRC** — that is the optimal order quantity and tier.

**Example procedure template** (to be applied in exam):
Let price tiers be $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$ with $Q_{min,1} < Q_{min,2} < Q_{min,3}$.
- Compute $Q^*_3 = \sqrt{2DS/H_3}$ where $H_3 = i P_3$.
- If $Q^*_3 \geq Q_{min,3}$: Compute $TRC_3 = DS/Q^*_3 + Q^*_3 H_3/2 + D P_3$.
- If $Q^*_3 < Q_{min,3}$: Compute $TRC_3$ at $Q = Q_{min,3}$: $TRC_3 = DS/Q_{min,3} + Q_{min,3} H_3/2 + D P_3$.
- Compare with $TRC_2$ at $Q^*_2$ (or $Q_{min,2}$) and $TRC_1$ at $Q^*_1$ (or $Q_{min,1}$).
- Pick the minimum.

## 3.3 Derivation — Safety Stock for Stochastic Demand and Lead Time

Let daily demand $D$ be normally distributed with mean $\bar{d}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_d$. Let lead time $L$ be normally distributed with mean $\bar{L}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_L$.

**Lead Time Demand (LTD) Distribution:**
The demand during lead time is the sum of demand over $L$ days. Assuming $D$ and $L$ are **independent**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean of LTD} &= \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} \\
\text{Variance of LTD} &= \bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2} \\
\sigma_{LT} &= \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Reorder Point (ROP):**
To achieve a Cycle Service Level (CSL) of probability $p$, we need the inventory at reorder time to be high enough that the probability of stockout is $1 - p$. The z-score $z_p$ corresponding to $p$ is found from the standard normal table.

$$\begin{aligned}
ROP &= \text{Mean LTD} + \text{Safety Stock} \\
&= \bar{d}\bar{L} + z_p \cdot \sigma_{LT} \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{ROP = \bar{d}\bar{L} + z_p \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}}}$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**

> A retailer sells a product with mean daily demand $\bar{d} = 100$ units, std dev $\sigma_d = 20$ units. Supplier lead time has mean $\bar{L} = 5$ days, std dev $\sigma_L = 1$ day. The retailer wants a 95% Cycle Service Level. Find ROP and Safety Stock.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean LTD} &= 100 \times 5 = 500 \text{ units} \\
\sigma_{LT} &= \sqrt{5 \times 20^{2} + 100^{2} \times 1^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{5 \times 400 + 10000} \\
&= \sqrt{2000 + 10000} = \sqrt{12000} \\
\sigma_{LT} &\approx 109.54 \text{ units} \\
z_{0.95} &= 1.645 \\
SS &= 1.645 \times 109.54 \approx 180.2 \text{ units} \\
ROP &= 500 + 180.2 \approx 680.2 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}$$

So the retailer must reorder when stock falls to **680 units**, of which **180 units are pure buffer**.

## 3.4 Derivation — Risk Pooling (Square Root Law)

Suppose $n$ retail stores each face independent demand with mean $\bar{d}$ and std dev $\sigma_d$. The lead time is the same for all.

**Decentralized (independent) system — total safety stock:**

$$SS_{decentralized} = n \cdot z \cdot \sigma_d \sqrt{\bar{L}}$$

**Centralized system — total safety stock:**
The mean demand for the central warehouse is $n \bar{d}$. The variance is $n \sigma_d^{2}$ (sum of independent variances). Std dev is $\sqrt{n} \cdot \sigma_d$.

$$SS_{centralized} = z \cdot \sqrt{n} \cdot \sigma_d \sqrt{\bar{L}}$$

**Ratio (efficiency gain):**

$$\frac{SS_{centralized}}{SS_{decentralized}} = \frac{\sqrt{n}}{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Square Root Law of Inventory:** Centralizing inventory across $n$ locations **reduces the total safety stock by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$** while providing the same service level. For $n = 4$ stores, centralization needs only **half** the safety stock; for $n = 100$ stores, only **one-tenth**. This is the mathematical foundation of every Amazon-style regional warehouse.

The same logic applies to **cycle inventory** aggregation when products with synchronized demand patterns are consolidated in a single order.

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Full KTU-Style EOQ Problem

> A company has annual demand $D = 10{,}000$ units. Ordering cost $S = \text{Rs } 200$ per order. Holding cost $H = \text{Rs } 10$ per unit per year. Find EOQ, number of orders, time between orders, and total cost.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 200}{10}} = \sqrt{400000} = 632.46 \text{ units} \\
N^{*} &= \frac{10000}{632.46} = 15.81 \approx 16 \text{ orders/year} \\
T^{*} &= \frac{632.46}{10000} = 0.0632 \text{ years} \approx 23 \text{ days} \\
TRC^{*} &= \sqrt{2 \times 10000 \times 200 \times 10} = \sqrt{40000000} = 6324.56 \text{ Rs/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification (costs equal at optimum):**
- Ordering Cost = $\dfrac{10000}{632.46} \times 200 = 3162.28$ Rs
- Holding Cost = $\dfrac{632.46}{2} \times 10 = 3162.28$ Rs ✓

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define cycle inventory. Explain why it arises in supply chains and state the formula for the Economic Order Quantity.**

**Model Answer:**

Cycle inventory is the **average inventory a firm holds as a result of producing or purchasing in lot sizes** rather than in a continuous flow. It arises because supply chains cannot deliver an instantaneous, infinite stream of product — they must batch orders due to fixed ordering/transport costs, production setup costs, and economies of scale in transportation.

When a lot of size $Q$ is placed and consumed uniformly, the average inventory carried is $Q/2$. To minimize the total of annual ordering cost ($DS/Q$) and annual holding cost ($QH/2$), the optimal lot size is the Economic Order Quantity:

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

where $D$ is annual demand, $S$ is the fixed cost per order, and $H$ is the annual holding cost per unit.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: What is the Square Root Law of Inventory? Why is it important for managing uncertainty in a centralized supply chain?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Square Root Law of Inventory** states that when inventory is consolidated from $n$ independent locations (e.g., retail stores) into a single centralized location (e.g., a regional warehouse), the **total safety stock required to maintain the same service level reduces by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$**.

This occurs because the standard deviation of the *sum* of independent demands grows as $\sqrt{n}$, while the *mean* grows as $n$, so the coefficient of variation falls. Centralization therefore **reduces uncertainty** without compromising customer service.

**Importance:** It is the mathematical basis for risk pooling, just-in-time centralization, and the design of e-commerce fulfillment networks. For $n = 100$ stores, centralization needs only **1/10th** the safety stock — a dramatic cost saving on working capital.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Unit mismatch** in EOQ: If demand is in *units/year*, holding cost must be in *Rs/unit/year*. A 1-mark deduction per unit error.
> - **Ignoring the EOQ validity check** in quantity discount problems: students compute $Q^*$ at the lowest price but fail to check whether $Q^* \geq$ minimum order quantity of that tier. This costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Confusing Cycle Service Level (probability of no stockout per cycle) with Fill Rate (fraction of demand filled from stock)** — these are NOT the same. CSL uses z-tables directly; Fill Rate requires the Expected Shortage formula.
> - **Forgetting the cross-term** in $\sigma_{LT}$: students often use only $\sqrt{\bar{L}}\sigma_d$ and miss the $\bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}$ term. KTU examiners specifically look for the **complete formula**.
> - **In aggregation problems**, students compute EOQ for each product independently and ignore the **shared fixed cost** of joint ordering. This is a conceptual error worth 4 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**A retail electronics store sells a Bluetooth speaker with the following data:**
- Annual demand: $D = 12{,}000$ units
- Ordering cost: $S = \text{Rs } 300$ per order
- Holding cost: $H = \text{Rs } 15$ per unit per year
- Unit price: $P = \text{Rs } 500$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compute the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), the number of orders per year, the time between orders, and the total relevant annual cost. Verify the result by showing that ordering cost equals holding cost at the optimum.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ:**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12000 \times 300}{15}} = \sqrt{\frac{7200000}{15}} = \sqrt{480000} \approx 692.82 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Number of orders per year:**

$$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{12000}{692.82} \approx 17.32 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Step 3 — Time between orders:**

$$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{692.82}{12000} = 0.0577 \text{ years} \approx 21.08 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4 — Annual Ordering Cost (AOC):**

$$AOC = \frac{D \times S}{Q^{*}} = \frac{12000 \times 300}{692.82} = \frac{3600000}{692.82} \approx 5196.15 \text{ Rs/yr}$$

**Step 5 — Annual Holding Cost (AHC):**

$$AHC = \frac{Q^{*} \times H}{2} = \frac{692.82 \times 15}{2} = 5196.15 \text{ Rs/yr}$$

**Step 6 — Total Relevant Cost (TRC):**

$$TRC^{*} = \sqrt{2 \times 12000 \times 300 \times 15} = \sqrt{108000000} \approx 10392.30 \text{ Rs/yr}$$

**Verification:** $AOC = AHC = 5196.15$ Rs ✓ (the equality holds at the optimum, confirming the result is correct).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The supplier now offers an all-units quantity discount: orders of 1000 or more units receive a 5% discount on the unit price (P = Rs 475). Should the store accept the discount? Justify with total cost analysis.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recompute EOQ at the discounted price:**

New holding cost: $H_{new} = 15$ Rs/unit/yr (unchanged, since $H$ is given independently as Rs 15). Note: the discount affects only the **purchase cost**, not the holding cost per unit (because the problem states $H$ directly, not as a percentage of price).

$$Q^{*}_{new} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12000 \times 300}{15}} = 692.82 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Check feasibility of discount tier:**

The minimum order quantity for the discount is $Q_{min} = 1000$ units. Since $Q^*_{new} = 692.82 < 1000$, the unconstrained EOQ is **infeasible** for the discount tier. The store must order $Q = 1000$ units if it wants the discount.

**Step 3 — Total cost without discount (ordering at EOQ = 692.82):**

$$TRC_{no\_discount} = 10392.30 \text{ Rs/yr (relevant)} + 12000 \times 500 = 6000000 + 10392.30 = 6010392.30 \text{ Rs/yr}$$

**Step 4 — Total cost with discount (ordering at Q = 1000):**

$$\begin{aligned}
AOC &= \frac{12000 \times 300}{1000} = 3600 \text{ Rs/yr} \\
AHC &= \frac{1000 \times 15}{2} = 7500 \text{ Rs/yr} \\
TRC_{relevant} &= 3600 + 7500 = 11100 \text{ Rs/yr} \\
TRC_{with\_discount} &= 12000 \times 475 + 11100 = 5700000 + 11100 = 5711100 \text{ Rs/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$\Delta TRC = 6010392.30 - 5711100.00 = 299292.30 \text{ Rs/yr savings}$$

**Conclusion:** The store **should accept the discount** because it saves approximately **Rs 2,99,292 per year**, even though it must hold more cycle inventory (1000 units vs 693 units). The purchase-cost savings far outweigh the additional holding cost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**A supermarket chain has 3 retail stores that currently manage inventory independently. Each store sells a product with the following identical demand characteristics:**
- Mean daily demand per store: $\bar{d} = 80$ units
- Standard deviation of daily demand per store: $\sigma_d = 25$ units
- Lead time mean: $\bar{L} = 6$ days
- Lead time std dev: $\sigma_L = 1.5$ days
- Target Cycle Service Level: $97.5\%$ (i.e., $z = 1.96$)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the Reorder Point (ROP) and Safety Stock for a single store.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mean Lead Time Demand:**

$$\bar{LTD} = \bar{d} \times \bar{L} = 80 \times 6 = 480 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation of Lead Time Demand:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{LT} &= \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{6 \times 25^{2} + 80^{2} \times 1.5^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{6 \times 625 + 6400 \times 2.25} \\
&= \sqrt{3750 + 14400} \\
&= \sqrt{18150} \\
&\approx 134.72 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Safety Stock (SS) for 97.5% CSL ($z = 1.96$):**

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = 1.96 \times 134.72 \approx 264.05 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Reorder Point:**

$$ROP = \bar{LTD} + SS = 480 + 264.05 \approx 744.05 \approx 744 \text{ units}$$

**Result:** Each store must reorder when stock falls to **744 units**, of which **264 units** is safety stock.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The chain now centralizes inventory in a single regional warehouse serving all 3 stores. Calculate the new total safety stock required and the percentage reduction achieved. Comment on the risk pooling effect.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Aggregate Mean Demand for Central Warehouse:**

$$\bar{D}_{agg} = 3 \times 80 = 240 \text{ units/day}$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate Standard Deviation (independent demand):**

$$\sigma_{agg} = \sqrt{3} \times \sigma_d = \sqrt{3} \times 25 \approx 1.732 \times 25 \approx 43.30 \text{ units/day}$$

(For daily demand; same lead time applies for delivery to the warehouse from the supplier.)

**Step 3 — New Lead Time Demand Std Dev for Central Warehouse:**

$$\sigma_{LT,agg} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_{agg}^{2} + \bar{D}_{agg}^{2} \cdot \sigma_L^{2}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= \sqrt{6 \times 43.30^{2} + 240^{2} \times 1.5^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{6 \times 1874.89 + 57600 \times 2.25} \\
&= \sqrt{11249.34 + 129600} \\
&= \sqrt{140849.34} \\
&\approx 375.30 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — New Centralized Safety Stock:**

$$SS_{centralized} = 1.96 \times 375.30 \approx 735.59 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Decentralized Total Safety Stock:**

$$SS_{decentralized,total} = 3 \times 264.05 = 792.15 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6 — Reduction:**

$$\Delta SS = 792.15 - 735.59 = 56.56 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{Percentage Reduction} = \frac{56.56}{792.15} \times 100\% \approx 7.14\%$$

**Theoretical Cross-Check using Square Root Law:**

For 3 stores, theoretical ratio is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.5774$. So theoretical safety stock = $0.5774 \times 792.15 \approx 457.39$ units (a 42.3% reduction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the actual reduction (7.14%) differ from the theoretical 42.3%?** Because in Part (a) we had **both demand AND lead time uncertainty**, but the square root law applied to demand aggregation alone reduces only the demand-related component of safety stock. The lead-time-uncertainty component ($\bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}$) is **NOT** reduced by aggregation — it depends on the **square of the mean demand**, which *increases* in a centralized system. The net savings is therefore smaller than the pure square root law predicts. This is a sophisticated KTU-level insight worth full marks.

**Comment on Risk Pooling:** Despite the smaller-than-expected reduction, centralization still reduces total safety stock, simplifies replenishment, enables joint ordering of multiple SKUs (further savings on cycle inventory), and provides a single buffer to absorb correlated demand shocks — making the supply chain more **resilient** and **cost-efficient**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Cycle Inventory & EOQ
- Cycle inventory = average stock held due to ordering in lots = $\dfrac{Q}{2}$.
- EOQ minimizes the sum of **annual ordering cost** and **annual holding cost**.
- **EOQ formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — must use **consistent units**.
- At optimum, **ordering cost = holding cost** = $\sqrt{DSH/2}$.
- **TRC minimum** = $\sqrt{2DSH}$.
- Assumptions: constant demand, instantaneous replenishment, no stockouts, independent of price.

### Aggregation of Multiple Products
- When multiple products share a **common ordering cost $S$**, the optimal order **frequency** is $n^* = \sqrt{\sum D_i C_i / S}$.
- The **Square Root Law of Inventory** governs the savings from aggregation.
- Joint replenishment reduces the number of orders but **increases** cycle inventory of slow movers.
- Practical application: single truck, single vendor, single bill, single customs clearance.

### Quantity Discounts
- **All-units discount** applies the new price to the entire order.
- **Incremental discount** applies the new price only to units above the threshold.
- **Solution procedure:** Start from the **lowest price** tier's EOQ, check feasibility, evaluate all tiers.
- Decision is based on **minimum Total Cost**, not just minimum price.
- Holding cost $H = i \times P$ changes with price; use the correct $H$ for each tier.

### Managing Uncertainty
- **Two sources:** demand variability and lead time variability.
- **Reorder Point (ROP):** the inventory level that triggers a new order.
- **Safety Stock (SS):** buffer held to protect against stockouts.
- **ROP formula:** $ROP = \bar{d}\bar{L} + z\sigma_{LT}$.
- **Lead time demand std dev:** $\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^{2} + \bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}}$.
- **Cycle Service Level (CSL):** probability of no stockout in a cycle → uses $z$-table.
- **Fill Rate:** fraction of demand satisfied from stock → uses Expected Shortage formula.
- **Square Root Law** applies to demand aggregation — total safety stock = $\sqrt{n} \cdot SS_1$ for $n$ pooled independent sources.
- **Risk Pooling Effect:** centralization reduces safety stock, capital tied up, and obsolescence.

### High-Yield Mnemonics for the Exam Hall
- **EOQ = "OSH"** — Ordering and Setup cost in numerator, Holding cost in denominator, all under square root.
- **ROP = "DLZ"** — Demand × Lead time + Z × sigma.
- **Discount Decision = "BCC"** — Bounded by the minimum order quantity, Compare at EOQ and boundary, Choose minimum TRC.
- **Square Root Law = "Pool & Save"** — Pool $n$ locations, Safety stock scales as $\sqrt{n}$ (not $n$).

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Mixing $D$ in units/month with $H$ in units/year.
- Using price $P$ in EOQ formula (EOQ is independent of price in the basic model).
- Forgetting to add $DP$ (purchase cost) when computing total cost with quantity discounts.
- Confusing CSL with Fill Rate.
- Ignoring the $\bar{d}^{2}\sigma_L^{2}$ term in $\sigma_{LT}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Inventory: Factors Affecting the Optimal Level of Product Availability, Impact of Supply Uncertainty on Safety Inventory, Impact of Aggregation on Safety Inventory, Factors Affecting the Optimal Level of Product Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety Inventory: Factors Affecting the Optimal Level of Product Availability, Impact of Supply Uncertainty on Safety Inventory, Impact of Aggregation on Safety Inventory, Factors Affecting the Optimal Level of Product Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Safety Inventory — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Inventory (Safety Stock):** The additional quantity of an item held in inventory **beyond the expected demand** during the lead time, used as a buffer to protect against uncertainties in both demand and supply. It directly determines the **optimal level of product availability** in a supply chain.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{DLT}$$

where **$SS$** is safety stock, **$z$** is the standard normal deviate corresponding to the desired cycle service level, and **$\sigma_{DLT}$** is the standard deviation of demand during the lead time.

### 1.2 Product Availability — The Core Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Availability** is the probability that an item will be in stock when a customer wants it. A higher product availability requires more safety inventory, which raises **holding costs**. The "optimal level" is the point where the marginal cost of an additional unit of safety stock equals the marginal benefit of one fewer stockout.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rainy Day Water Tank"

Think of safety inventory as the **extra water stored in an overhead tank** above the level needed for normal daily use:

- **Expected demand during lead time** = the water you normally drink while waiting for the municipal tanker to arrive.
- **Safety stock** = the *reserve water* kept for days when you are thirstier than usual (demand surge) **OR** when the tanker is late (supply delay).
- The bigger your family (more customers, more variance), the larger the reserve must be.
- If multiple family members in different houses all maintain their own small tanks, you actually need a *much bigger combined reserve* than if you share one large community tank — this is the **aggregation effect**.

### 1.4 The Two Faces of Uncertainty Driving Safety Stock

| Uncertainty Type | Source | Effect on Safety Stock |
|---|---|---|
| **Demand Uncertainty** ($\sigma_D$) | Customer order variability | Directly increases SS |
| **Supply (Lead Time) Uncertainty** ($\sigma_L$) | Variable supplier delays | Directly increases SS |
| **Combined Uncertainty** | Both acting simultaneously | Increases SS more than either alone |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners **frequently** test whether students can distinguish between *Type 1 Service Level* (cycle service level / probability of no stockout per cycle) and *Type 2 Service Level* (fill rate / fraction of demand met from stock). Be exam-ready on both.

### 1.5 Optimal Level of Product Availability — The Managerial Decision

The "optimal" level is **not** 100% availability. It is found by equating:

$$\text{Marginal Cost of Safety Stock} = \text{Marginal Benefit of Higher Availability}$$

- Marginal cost = **holding cost per unit per year** ($h$).
- Marginal benefit = **expected saving from one fewer stockout** (lost margin, backorder cost, customer goodwill loss).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Determinants of Optimal Product Availability — A Structured Breakdown

The optimal level of product availability is shaped by **six interacting forces**:

1. **Demand Variability ($\sigma_D$)** — Higher variability forces the firm to hold more safety stock to maintain the same service level.
2. **Lead Time Variability ($\sigma_L$)** — Unreliable supply (unpredictable supplier lead times) inflates $\sigma_{DLT}$ quadratically.
3. **Average Lead Time ($\bar{L}$)** — Longer replenishment cycles expose the firm to more demand fluctuations.
4. **Targeted Service Level ($CSL$ or $\beta$)** — Higher promised availability demands disproportionately more safety stock (because the $z$-curve flattens as you approach 100%).
5. **Item Margin and Criticality ($p$, $h$, stockout cost)** — High-margin or critical items (e.g., a missing spare part in a hospital) justify higher safety stock.
6. **Degree of Aggregation / Number of Warehouses ($n$)** — Pooling reduces total safety stock via the **square-root law**.

### 2.2 The Two Standard Service Level Measures (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type 1 Service Level — Cycle Service Level (CSL):**
> $$CSL = P(\text{Demand during Lead Time} \le \text{Reorder Point})$$
> Expressed as a probability (e.g., 95%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type 2 Service Level — Fill Rate ($\beta$):**
> $$\beta = 1 - \frac{E[\text{Units short per cycle}]}{Q}$$
> Expressed as a fraction of demand satisfied from stock.

The $z$-value in $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{DLT}$ corresponds to the **Type 1 (CSL)** service level. The corresponding $z$ for a given fill rate is obtained from the **standard normal loss function** $L(z)$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{DLT}$ | Basic safety stock | $z$ = service factor, $\sigma_{DLT}$ = std. dev. of demand over lead time |
| 2 | $\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2}$ | Combined demand + lead-time uncertainty | $\bar{D}, \bar{L}$ = averages; $\sigma_D, \sigma_L$ = standard deviations |
| 3 | $\sigma_{DLT} = \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$ (special case) | When lead time is *constant* | Reduces to simple form |
| 4 | $SS_{pooled} = z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n}$ | **Square-root law of inventory** | $n$ = number of aggregated locations |
| 5 | $\text{Reduction Factor} = \sqrt{n} / n = 1/\sqrt{n}$ | Per-location safety stock reduction under aggregation | $n$ = number of locations |
| 6 | $ROP = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L} + SS$ | Reorder Point | Sum of expected demand over lead time + safety stock |
| 7 | $L(z) = \phi(z) - z[1-\Phi(z)]$ | Standard normal loss function | Used to convert fill rate to $z$ |
| 8 | $EOQ = \sqrt{2KD/h}$ | Economic Order Quantity (reference) | $K$ = ordering cost, $D$ = annual demand, $h$ = holding cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU problems often provide a fill rate and ask for safety stock. You must first invert the loss function $L(z)$ to find $z$. Memorize: $L(0) = 0.3989$, $L(1.0) \approx 0.0833$, $L(1.65) \approx 0.0206$, $L(2.0) \approx 0.0083$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Automotive Spare Parts:** A single missing bolt can ground an aircraft; safety stock prevents this.
- **Pharmaceutical Cold Chains:** Variable drug demand + cold-chain transit delays demand carefully calibrated safety stock.
- **E-commerce Warehousing:** Amazon-style fulfillment centers rely on aggregation to slash safety stock by $\sqrt{n}$.
- **Construction Project Management:** Aggregate cement/steel buffer stocks across multiple sites to exploit the square-root law.

### 2.5 Why the Square-Root Law Works — The "Why" Behind the "How"

If $n$ independent, identical retailers each carry safety stock $SS_i = z \cdot \sigma$, then total decentralised safety stock is $n \cdot z \cdot \sigma$. If demand is **pooled** at a central warehouse, the standard deviation of *aggregated* demand is $\sigma \cdot \sqrt{n}$ (because variances add, not standard deviations), so:

$$SS_{central} = z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n}$$

Total savings = $n \cdot z \cdot \sigma - z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n} = z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n}(\sqrt{n} - 1)$, which grows rapidly with $n$. For $n = 100$, savings are about 90%.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined Uncertainty Formula $\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2 \sigma_L^2}$

**Starting assumption:** Demand $D$ during lead time $L$ follows $D = D_{daily} \cdot L$, where $D_{daily}$ is daily demand and $L$ is lead time in days.

The total demand over the lead time is:

$$D_{LT} = \sum_{i=1}^{L} D_i$$

where each $D_i$ is the demand on day $i$. Both $D_i$ (random) and $L$ (random) are stochastic.

**Step 1 — Take the expectation of $D_{LT}$:**

$$E[D_{LT}] = E[E[D_{LT} \mid L]] = E[\bar{D} \cdot L] = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L}$$

**Step 2 — Take the variance, using the law of total variance:**

$$\text{Var}(D_{LT}) = E[\text{Var}(D_{LT} \mid L)] + \text{Var}(E[D_{LT} \mid L])$$

**Step 3 — Expand each term:**

$$E[\text{Var}(D_{LT} \mid L)] = E[L \cdot \sigma_D^2] = \bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^2$$

$$\text{Var}(E[D_{LT} \mid L]) = \text{Var}(\bar{D} \cdot L) = \bar{D}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$\text{Var}(D_{LT}) = \bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root to get the standard deviation:**

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case:** If lead time is *deterministic* ($\sigma_L = 0$), then $\sigma_{DLT} = \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$. This is the most frequently tested special case in KTU papers.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Square-Root Law of Inventory Aggregation

**Setup:** Consider $n$ independent retail locations, each with daily demand having mean $\bar{D}$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

**Step 1 — Safety stock at one location** (assuming constant lead time $\bar{L}$):

$$SS_1 = z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$$

**Step 2 — Total safety stock across $n$ decentralised locations:**

$$SS_{decentralised} = n \cdot z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$$

**Step 3 — Aggregated demand mean and standard deviation at a central warehouse:**

$$E[D_{aggregate}] = n \cdot \bar{D}$$

$$\sigma_{aggregate} = \sqrt{n} \cdot \sigma$$

(Variances add for independent demands: $n \cdot \sigma^2$; std. dev. is the square root.)

**Step 4 — Pooled safety stock:**

$$SS_{pooled} = z \cdot \sigma_{aggregate} \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}} = z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n} \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$$

**Step 5 — Ratio (efficiency of aggregation):**

$$\frac{SS_{pooled}}{SS_{decentralised}} = \frac{z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n} \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}}{n \cdot z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **For $n = 4$ locations**, aggregation reduces total safety stock to **50%** of the decentralised level. For $n = 16$, to **25%**. The savings are enormous, but they come with the cost of longer distance to the customer (lower **responsiveness**).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reorder Point (ROP) with Safety Stock

The reorder point triggers replenishment and equals the expected demand during lead time plus safety stock:

$$ROP = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L} + z \cdot \sigma_{DLT}$$

**Numerical example (often asked in KTU Part B):**

Given: $\bar{D} = 100$ units/day, $\bar{L} = 5$ days, $\sigma_D = 20$ units/day, $\sigma_L = 1$ day, desired $CSL = 95\%$.

**Step 1 — Find $z$ for 95% CSL:** $z \approx 1.65$.

**Step 2 — Compute $\sigma_{DLT}$:**

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{5 \cdot 20^2 + 100^2 \cdot 1^2} = \sqrt{2000 + 10000} = \sqrt{12000} \approx 109.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute $SS$:**

$$SS = 1.65 \times 109.5 \approx 180.7 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $ROP$:**

$$ROP = 100 \cdot 5 + 180.7 = 500 + 180.7 = 680.7 \text{ units}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Each Factor Affects Safety Stock

| Factor | Change | Effect on $SS$ | Magnitude | Managerial Lever |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand variability $\sigma_D$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Linear in $\sigma_D$ | Demand smoothing, forecasting |
| Average lead time $\bar{L}$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\sqrt{\bar{L}}$ scaling | Faster suppliers, local sourcing |
| Lead-time variability $\sigma_L$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Quadratic (since $\bar{D}^2 \sigma_L^2$) | Reliable suppliers, contracts with SLAs |
| Service level $CSL$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Non-linear (steep near 100%) | Set CSL per item class (ABC) |
| Number of aggregated locations $n$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ (per system) | $1/\sqrt{n}$ scaling | Centralised warehousing |
| Holding cost $h$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ (optimal) | Inverse | Reduce item cost, improve turns |
| Stockout cost $p_s$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (optimal) | Direct | Trade-off analysis |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Aggregation Impact

A firm has 9 retail stores. Each store faces $\bar{D} = 50$ units/day, $\sigma_D = 10$ units/day, lead time is constant at $L = 4$ days, target $CSL = 97\%$ ($z = 1.88$).

**Decentralised safety stock per store:**

$$SS_1 = 1.88 \cdot 10 \cdot \sqrt{4} = 1.88 \cdot 20 = 37.6 \text{ units}$$

**Total decentralised safety stock:**

$$SS_{total, dec} = 9 \cdot 37.6 = 338.4 \text{ units}$$

**Centralised safety stock:**

$$SS_{central} = 1.88 \cdot 10 \cdot \sqrt{9} \cdot \sqrt{4} = 1.88 \cdot 30 = 56.4 \text{ units}$$

**Savings:** $338.4 - 56.4 = 282$ units, an **83.3% reduction** — illustrating the dramatic power of aggregation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Factors Affecting Optimal Product Availability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Optimal Product Availability]
    nodeB[Demand Variability sigmaD]
    nodeC[Lead Time Variability sigmaL]
    nodeD[Average Lead Time Lbar]
    nodeE[Target Service Level CSL]
    nodeF[Item Margin and Criticality]
    nodeG[Degree of Aggregation n]
    nodeH[Holding Cost h]
    nodeI[Stockout Cost]

    nodeB --> nodeA
    nodeC --> nodeA
    nodeD --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeA
    nodeF --> nodeA
    nodeG --> nodeA
    nodeH --> nodeA
    nodeI --> nodeA

    nodeJ[Higher Safety Stock] --> nodeK[Higher Holding Cost]
    nodeL[Lower Safety Stock] --> nodeM[Higher Stockout Risk]
    nodeA --> nodeJ
    nodeA --> nodeL
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Impact of Supply Uncertainty on Safety Stock

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Constant Lead Time] --> nodeB[sigmaDLT equals sigmaD times sqrtLbar]
    nodeC[Variable Lead Time] --> nodeD[sigmaDLT equals sqrt of Lbar sigmaD squared plus Dbar squared sigmaL squared]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Lower Safety Stock]
    nodeD --> nodeF[HIGHER Safety Stock]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Quadratic Growth with sigmaL]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Supplier Reliability is Critical]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Aggregation (Centralised vs Decentralised)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Decentralised Warehousing]
    nodeB[Centralised Warehousing]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Each site carries Safety Stock SS equals z sigma sqrtLbar]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Pooled Safety Stock SS equals z sigma sqrt n sqrtLbar]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Total SS equals n times z sigma sqrtLbar]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Reduction Factor equals 1 over sqrt n]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Higher Total Inventory Cost]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Massive Inventory Savings]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Better Responsiveness Short Distance]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Risk Pooling Benefit]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Tradeoff Decision]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Optimal Network Design]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Safety Stock Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step 1 Estimate Demand Mean and Std Dev]
    nodeB[Step 2 Estimate Lead Time Mean and Std Dev]
    nodeC[Step 3 Compute sigmaDLT]
    nodeD[Step 4 Decide CSL or Fill Rate]
    nodeE[Step 5 Look up z from Normal Table or invert Loss Function]
    nodeF[Step 6 Compute SS equals z times sigmaDLT]
    nodeG[Step 7 Compute ROP equals Dbar Lbar plus SS]
    nodeH[Step 8 Evaluate Tradeoff and Adjust]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define safety inventory. Mention any two factors affecting the optimal level of product availability.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Safety inventory (or safety stock) is the **buffer stock** maintained to protect against uncertainties in demand and lead time, ensuring a target level of product availability. Formally, $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{DLT}$, where $z$ corresponds to the desired cycle service level and $\sigma_{DLT}$ is the standard deviation of demand during lead time.

**Two factors (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Demand variability ($\sigma_D$):** Greater demand fluctuation raises $\sigma_{DLT}$ and hence required safety stock.
2. **Lead time variability ($\sigma_L$):** Unreliable supply causes quadratic growth in $\sigma_{DLT}$, sharply increasing safety stock needs.

*(Acceptable alternatives: target service level, item margin/criticality, degree of aggregation, holding vs. stockout cost trade-off.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State and briefly explain the "square-root law" of inventory aggregation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The square-root law states that when the demand from $n$ independent locations is pooled into a single central warehouse, the **total safety stock required decreases in proportion to $\sqrt{n}$**, while maintaining the same service level.

$$SS_{pooled} = z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n} \cdot \sqrt{\bar{L}}$$

This arises because **variances of independent demands add**, so the standard deviation of pooled demand grows as $\sigma \sqrt{n}$, not $n \sigma$. Each individual location therefore needs only $1/\sqrt{n}$ times the safety stock it would carry alone, yielding major inventory savings (e.g., pooling 9 locations cuts safety stock to 33%).

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

A retail chain sells a product with the following characteristics:
- Average daily demand $\bar{D} = 80$ units, with daily demand standard deviation $\sigma_D = 15$ units.
- Average lead time $\bar{L} = 6$ days, with lead-time standard deviation $\sigma_L = 1.5$ days.
- The firm currently operates **4 independent warehouses** and wants to evaluate consolidation.

**(a)** Compute the safety stock and reorder point for a **single warehouse** if the target **cycle service level is 95%**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the total safety stock if the firm moves to **one central warehouse** serving the same demand. Comment on the savings. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Single Warehouse (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Find $z$ for $CSL = 95\%$. From standard normal table: $z = 1.65$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $\sigma_{DLT}$ using combined uncertainty formula:

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2}$$

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{6 \cdot 15^2 + 80^2 \cdot 1.5^2}$$

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{6 \cdot 225 + 6400 \cdot 2.25}$$

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{1350 + 14400} = \sqrt{15750}$$

$$\sigma_{DLT} \approx 125.5 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute safety stock:

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{DLT} = 1.65 \cdot 125.5 \approx 207.1 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute reorder point:

$$ROP = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L} + SS = 80 \cdot 6 + 207.1 = 480 + 207.1 = 687.1 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final answer — single warehouse: $SS \approx 207$ units, $ROP \approx 687$ units.

---

**Part (b) — Centralised Warehouse (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Aggregated demand mean and standard deviation across 4 warehouses:

$$\bar{D}_{pooled} = 4 \cdot 80 = 320 \text{ units/day}$$

$$\sigma_{D,pooled} = \sqrt{4} \cdot 15 = 2 \cdot 15 = 30 \text{ units/day}$$

*(Lead time is treated the same.)*

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Compute pooled $\sigma_{DLT}$:

$$\sigma_{DLT,pooled} = \sqrt{6 \cdot 30^2 + 320^2 \cdot 1.5^2}$$

$$\sigma_{DLT,pooled} = \sqrt{6 \cdot 900 + 102400 \cdot 2.25} = \sqrt{5400 + 230400} = \sqrt{235800}$$

$$\sigma_{DLT,pooled} \approx 485.6 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute pooled safety stock:

$$SS_{pooled} = 1.65 \cdot 485.6 \approx 801.2 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compare with decentralised total:

$$SS_{decentralised,total} = 4 \cdot 207.1 = 828.4 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Savings and comment:

$$\text{Savings} = 828.4 - 801.2 = 27.2 \text{ units} \approx 3.3\%$$

**Comment (1 mark included above):** The savings appear modest because the dominant source of uncertainty is **lead-time variability** ($\bar{D}^2 \sigma_L^2$ term), which scales **linearly** with $n$ in variance but is **not** reduced by the $\sqrt{n}$ rule in the same way. To fully exploit aggregation, the firm should also pursue lead-time reduction/supplier reliability alongside centralisation. In purely demand-driven uncertainty, aggregation would save $1 - 1/\sqrt{4} = 50\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

A manufacturer uses a component with the following annual data:
- Annual demand $D = 50{,}000$ units (assume 250 working days).
- Order quantity $Q = 2{,}500$ units.
- Daily demand standard deviation $\sigma_D = 12$ units.
- Lead time $L = 8$ days (deterministic).
- Desired **fill rate** $\beta = 98\%$.

**(a)** Determine the safety stock required to achieve the target fill rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If management shifts to a **cycle service level of 99%** instead, recompute the safety stock and comment on the trade-off. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Fill Rate Approach (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Compute $\sigma_{DLT}$ (deterministic lead time, so use special case):

$$\sigma_{DLT} = \sigma_D \cdot \sqrt{L} = 12 \cdot \sqrt{8} = 12 \cdot 2.828 \approx 33.94 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Relate fill rate to $z$ via the standard normal loss function. The expected shortage per cycle is:

$$E[S] = \sigma_{DLT} \cdot L(z)$$

The fill rate formula:

$$\beta = 1 - \frac{E[S]}{Q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E[S] = (1 - \beta) \cdot Q = 0.02 \cdot 2500 = 50 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Solve for $L(z)$:

$$L(z) = \frac{E[S]}{\sigma_{DLT}} = \frac{50}{33.94} \approx 1.473$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Invert loss function. From standard normal loss tables, $L(z) = 1.473$ corresponds to $z \approx 0.1$. (We can verify: $L(0) = 0.3989$, $L(0.1) \approx 0.3509$ — closer match is $L(-0.2) \approx 0.3910$ and the table needs interpolation. In typical KTU problems, this is given.)

**Step 5 (0.5 mark):** Safety stock:

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{DLT} \approx 0.1 \cdot 33.94 \approx 3.4 \text{ units}$$

*(Note: a fill rate of 98% with $Q = 2500$ requires surprisingly low safety stock because most demand is already absorbed by cycle stock.)*

---

**Part (b) — Cycle Service Level Approach (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Find $z$ for 99% CSL: $z = 2.33$.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Safety stock:

$$SS = 2.33 \cdot 33.94 \approx 79.1 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Reorder point:

$$ROP = \bar{D} \cdot L + SS = (50000/250) \cdot 8 + 79.1 = 200 \cdot 8 + 79.1 = 1600 + 79.1 = 1679.1 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 (2.5 marks):** Trade-off comment:

- A **99% CSL** delivers virtually no stockout cycles but at the cost of **~23× more safety stock** (79 vs. 3.4 units).
- The 98% **fill rate** protects customer satisfaction (98% of units demanded are met from stock) at a tiny inventory cost.
- **Marginal analysis:** the firm must weigh the holding cost of the additional 75.7 units against the marginal reduction in stockouts. For high-margin or critical items, the CSL approach is preferable; for low-margin bulk items, the fill rate approach yields dramatic savings.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing up CSL and Fill Rate:** Using $z = 1.65$ directly for a fill-rate problem — KTU examiners *specifically* deduct 1–2 marks for this. Always check whether the question specifies a probability or a fraction of demand.
> 2. **Forgetting the lead-time variability term:** When $\sigma_L > 0$, you **must** use the full $\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2\sigma_L^2}$ formula. Many students omit the second term.
> 3. **Confusing $\sqrt{n}$ with $n$:** In aggregation problems, the safety stock ratio is $1/\sqrt{n}$, **not** $1/n$. This is a very common KTU trap.
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Daily vs. annual demand. Convert consistently before computing $\sigma_{DLT}$.
> 5. **Reporting $ROP$ without units:** Always state $ROP$ in *units* and $SS$ in *units*, not just numbers.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ **Safety stock formula:** $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{DLT}$ — the single most important equation.
- ✅ **Combined uncertainty:** $\sigma_{DLT} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \sigma_D^2 + \bar{D}^2 \sigma_L^2}$.
- ✅ **Special case** (constant lead time): $\sigma_{DLT} = \sigma_D \sqrt{\bar{L}}$.
- ✅ **Reorder point:** $ROP = \bar{D} \cdot \bar{L} + SS$.
- ✅ **Square-root law:** Pooled $SS = z \sigma \sqrt{n} \sqrt{\bar{L}}$; per-location reduction = $1/\sqrt{n}$.
- ✅ **CSL (Type 1) vs. Fill Rate (Type 2):** CSL uses $z$ directly; Fill Rate requires inverting the **loss function** $L(z)$.
- ✅ **Optimal availability is not 100%** — it is set by equating marginal holding cost with marginal stockout cost.
- ✅ **Six factors** drive optimal availability: demand variability, lead-time variability, average lead time, target service level, item margin/criticality, and degree of aggregation.
- ✅ **Aggregation** produces huge savings (e.g., 9 locations → 89% reduction in safety stock) but reduces responsiveness.
- ✅ **Lead-time variability** has a *quadratic* effect on $\sigma_{DLT}$ — supplier reliability is more impactful than demand forecasting for supply chains with high $\sigma_L$.
- ✅ **KTU favourite numerical:** "Given $\bar{D}$, $\sigma_D$, $\bar{L}$, $\sigma_L$, and CSL — find SS and ROP." Practice this template thoroughly.
- ✅ **For a 95% CSL use $z = 1.65$; for 97% use $z = 1.88$; for 99% use $z = 2.33$; for 99.9% use $z = 3.09$.** Memorise these.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation Networks and Sourcing: Role of Transportation - Modes and Their Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transportation Networks and Sourcing: Role of Transportation - Modes and Their Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Transportation** in a supply chain context refers to the **physical movement of goods, raw materials, work-in-process inventory, and finished products between nodes** in the supply network — from suppliers to manufacturers, manufacturers to warehouses, warehouses to distribution centers, and finally to the end consumer.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme definition framework, transportation is one of the **four key drivers of supply chain performance** (alongside **Inventory, Location, and Information**), and it directly influences the responsiveness and efficiency of the entire chain. The role of transportation is to **convert inventory from one location to another, thereby creating place utility and time utility** for the product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Transportation as a Strategic Lever**
> Transportation decisions affect: (a) **Customer Service Levels** (Lead Time), (b) **Operating Costs** (typically 6%–12% of revenue), (c) **Inventory Investment** (faster transport = less pipeline inventory), and (d) **Supplier/Customer Network Design**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Think of transportation as the **"circulatory system" of a supply chain**, just like blood vessels in the human body. Imagine a Kerala-based spice company, *Eastern Spices Ltd.*:

- **Raw materials (pepper, cardamom)** must travel from the Idukki farms → to the Kochi processing plant.
- **Finished products** must travel from Kochi → to wholesalers in Bangalore, Delhi, and Dubai.
- **Replenishment orders** must travel from retail stores back to the warehouse.

In each of these journeys, transportation is the **"lifeblood"** that keeps products flowing. If the trucks are slow (poor transport), the products arrive late (low responsiveness). If trucks are too expensive, the company bleeds money (high cost). The challenge of supply chain managers is to find the **right balance — the perfect "heart rate"** — where products move at the right speed, cost, and reliability.

## 1.3 Why Transportation is a Critical Supply Chain Driver

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Strategic Role of Transportation** can be summarized in five dimensions:
> 1. **Cost Driver** — A major component of landed cost (often 2/3 of total logistics cost).
> 2. **Service Driver** — Determines delivery lead time and on-time performance.
> 3. **Inventory Driver** — Pipeline inventory in transit.
> 4. **Risk Driver** — Damage, loss, delays, and disruptions.
> 5. **Sustainability Driver** — Carbon footprint, ESG compliance.

## 1.4 The Performance Trade-off Triangle

A fundamental KTU concept: transportation decisions involve a **trade-off triangle** between **Cost, Speed, and Consistency (Reliability)**. No single mode dominates in all three. This is known as the **Transportation Trade-off Triangle**.

| Objective | Description | KTU Exam Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Minimize Cost** | Lowest freight rate per unit | Cost efficiency |
| **Minimize Lead Time** | Fastest transit | Responsiveness |
| **Maximize Consistency** | Low variance in transit time | Reliability |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Primary Modes of Transportation

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires mastery of all major transportation modes. Each mode has a distinct **performance profile** based on five key metrics: **Cost, Speed, Reliability, Capacity, and Accessibility**.

### 2.1.1 Road Transportation (Trucking / Highways)

- **Description**: Movement of goods overland using trucks, trailers, and containerized vehicles.
- **Performance Profile**:
  - **Cost**: Moderate (high for small shipments).
  - **Speed**: Moderate to High (50–80 km/h average).
  - **Reliability**: Moderate (subject to traffic, weather).
  - **Capacity**: Limited (typically 20–25 tons per truck).
  - **Accessibility**: **Highest** — door-to-door service available.
- **Best For**: Short to medium hauls, high-value goods, last-mile delivery, FTL (Full Truck Load) and LTL (Less-than-Truck Load) shipments.
- **KTU Example**: Kerala's spice exports via road to neighboring Tamil Nadu and Karnataka warehouses.

### 2.1.2 Rail Transportation

- **Description**: Movement of freight using freight trains, containerized rail cars, and intermodal yards.
- **Performance Profile**:
  - **Cost**: Low per ton-mile (highly economical for bulk).
  - **Speed**: Moderate (30–50 km/h).
  - **Reliability**: High (fixed schedules, less affected by traffic).
  - **Capacity**: **Very High** (a single train = 100+ truck equivalent).
  - **Accessibility**: Low — requires rail siding and intermodal transfer.
- **Best For**: Bulk commodities (coal, grain, ore), long-haul inter-city freight, and heavy industrial materials.

### 2.1.3 Air Transportation

- **Description**: Movement via commercial cargo aircraft, dedicated freighters, and air couriers.
- **Performance Profile**:
  - **Cost**: **Very High** per kg (typically 4–8× road cost).
  - **Speed**: **Very High** (800–900 km/h).
  - **Reliability**: High (strict schedules).
  - **Capacity**: Low (volume and weight restricted).
  - **Accessibility**: Moderate — only between airport nodes.
- **Best For**: High-value, low-weight, time-sensitive products (electronics, pharmaceuticals, perishables, e-commerce).

### 2.1.4 Water Transportation (Maritime & Inland)

- **Description**: Movement via ocean-going vessels, container ships, barges, and coastal cargo.
- **Performance Profile**:
  - **Cost**: **Lowest** per ton-mile (economies of scale are massive).
  - **Speed**: Slow (20–40 km/h for ships, 10–15 km/h for barges).
  - **Reliability**: Moderate (weather delays, port congestion).
  - **Capacity**: **Highest** (Ultra Large Container Vessels carry 20,000+ TEU).
  - **Accessibility**: Low — limited to ports and inland waterways.
- **Best For**: International trade, bulk raw materials, low-value high-volume goods (e.g., oil, cement, grain).
- **KTU Example**: Kochi Port → Singapore → Rotterdam container route for Kerala's cashew and coir exports.

### 2.1.5 Pipeline Transportation

- **Description**: Movement of liquids, gases, and slurries through fixed pipelines.
- **Performance Profile**:
  - **Cost**: **Lowest** per unit (after high fixed installation cost).
  - **Speed**: Slow but continuous (3–8 km/h typical).
  - **Reliability**: **Very High** (uninterrupted, weather-independent).
  - **Capacity**: High (continuous flow).
  - **Accessibility**: Very Low — fixed route, limited products.
- **Best For**: Crude oil, natural gas, refined petroleum, water, and some chemicals.

### 2.1.6 Intermodal & Multimodal Transportation

- **Description**: Combination of two or more modes, typically road + rail + sea, using standardized containers (ISO 20'/40' containers).
- **Performance Profile**:
  - **Cost**: Lower than pure road/air.
  - **Speed**: Medium.
  - **Reliability**: High (with proper coordination).
  - **Capacity**: High.
  - **Accessibility**: High.
- **Best For**: Long-distance international and inter-regional shipments.

## 2.2 Detailed Performance Comparison Matrix

| Mode | Cost/ton-mile | Speed (km/h) | Reliability | Capacity (tons) | Flexibility | Door-to-Door |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Road** | Medium | 50–80 | Medium | 20–25 | High | Yes |
| **Rail** | Low | 30–50 | High | 1000+ | Low | No |
| **Air** | Very High | 800–900 | Very High | 2–100 | Medium | No |
| **Water** | Very Low | 20–40 | Medium | 1000–200,000 | Low | No |
| **Pipeline** | Lowest | 3–8 | Very High | Continuous | Very Low | No |
| **Intermodal** | Low–Medium | 40–60 | High | High | Medium | Partial |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "RRAWP+I"** for ordering modes by **Speed**: **A**ir > **R**oad > **I**ntermodal > **W**ater > **R**ail > **P**ipeline.
> 
> **KTU Memory Trick — "PRAWRA"** for ordering modes by **Cost** (low to high): **P**ipeline < **R**ail < **W**ater < **R**oad < **A**ir.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU 2024 Scheme high-yield** and frequently tested in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

### Formula 1: Transportation Cost per Unit

$$C_{unit} = \frac{F + (V \times d)}{Q}$$

Where:
- $C_{unit}$ = Cost per unit transported
- $F$ = Fixed cost of transport (loading, documentation)
- $V$ = Variable cost per unit-distance
- $d$ = Distance of haul (km)
- $Q$ = Quantity shipped (units or tons)

### Formula 2: Pipeline Inventory in Transit

$$I_{pipeline} = D_{avg} \times T$$

Where:
- $I_{pipeline}$ = Pipeline inventory (units)
- $D_{avg}$ = Average daily demand (units/day)
- $T$ = Transit time (days)

### Formula 3: Total Landed Cost (TLC)

$$TLC = P + T_{cost} + I_{cost} + W_{cost} + S_{cost}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Purchase price
- $T_{cost}$ = Transportation cost
- $I_{cost}$ = Inventory carrying cost (in transit)
- $W_{cost}$ = Warehousing cost
- $S_{cost}$ = Shrinkage, damage, spoilage cost

### Formula 4: Breakeven Distance (Mode Comparison)

The distance at which two transportation modes have equal total cost:

$$D_{breakeven} = \frac{F_2 - F_1}{V_1 - V_2}$$

Where:
- $F_1, F_2$ = Fixed costs of Mode 1 and Mode 2
- $V_1, V_2$ = Variable costs per km of Mode 1 and Mode 2
- $D_{breakeven}$ = Distance at which total costs are equal

### Formula 5: Freight Cost Index (Lane Cost Comparison)

$$FCI = \frac{C_{lane}}{C_{baseline}} \times 100$$

Used to compare the cost-effectiveness of transportation lanes.

### Formula 6: On-Time Delivery (OTD) Performance

$$OTD = \frac{N_{on-time}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$

A core KPI for transportation performance evaluation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are used by **logistics engineers, supply chain analysts, and operations managers** in:
- **Freight tendering and contract negotiations**.
- **Network design optimization** (choosing warehouse and plant locations).
- **Mode selection decisions** (when to switch from road to rail).
- **Customer SLA (Service Level Agreement) design**.
- **Carbon footprint calculation** for ESG reporting (CO₂ per ton-km).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Faster transport modes reduce **pipeline inventory** but increase **freight cost**. The optimal mode is the one that **minimizes total landed cost (TLC)**, not just freight cost alone.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Breakeven Analysis Between Two Transport Modes

This is a **KTU 2024 High-Yield derivation** — frequently asked in Part B questions.

### Problem Setup

A KTU logistics case study: A company in Kochi must ship **500 tons of processed spices** to a distributor in Delhi every month. Two transportation options exist:

- **Mode A — Road (Refrigerated Truck)**
  - Fixed cost: $F_1 = ₹50{,}000$ per shipment
  - Variable cost: $V_1 = ₹15$ per ton-km
  - Distance: Kochi → Delhi = $d = 2{,}500$ km

- **Mode B — Rail (Container Freight)**
  - Fixed cost: $F_2 = ₹1{,}20{,}000$ per shipment
  - Variable cost: $V_2 = ₹8$ per ton-km

Find the breakeven distance and determine which mode is optimal for the 2,500 km route.

### Step 1: Write the Total Cost Equations

For any shipment of quantity $Q$ over distance $d$:

$$TC_{Mode} = F + (V \times d \times Q)$$

**Total cost by Road:**

$$TC_{Road} = F_1 + (V_1 \times d \times Q)$$

$$TC_{Road} = 50{,}000 + (15 \times 2{,}500 \times 500)$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Variable Cost Component (Road)

We compute the per-shipment variable cost:

$$V_1 \times d \times Q = 15 \times 2{,}500 \times 500$$

$$= 15 \times 1{,}250{,}000$$

$$= ₹1{,}87{,}50{,}000$$

### Step 3: Total Road Cost

$$TC_{Road} = 50{,}000 + 1{,}87{,}50{,}000 = ₹1{,}88{,}00{,}000$$

### Step 4: Total Cost by Rail

$$TC_{Rail} = F_2 + (V_2 \times d \times Q)$$

$$= 1{,}20{,}000 + (8 \times 2{,}500 \times 500)$$

### Step 5: Calculate Variable Cost (Rail)

$$V_2 \times d \times Q = 8 \times 2{,}500 \times 500$$

$$= 8 \times 1{,}250{,}000$$

$$= ₹1{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

### Step 6: Total Rail Cost

$$TC_{Rail} = 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}01{,}20{,}000$$

### Step 7: Comparison at d = 2,500 km

$$TC_{Road} = ₹1{,}88{,}00{,}000$$

$$TC_{Rail} = ₹1{,}01{,}20{,}000$$

$$\Delta TC = TC_{Road} - TC_{Rail} = ₹86{,}80{,}000$$

### Step 8: Derivation of Breakeven Distance

To find $D_{breakeven}$, equate the two total cost equations:

$$F_1 + V_1 \times D_{breakeven} \times Q = F_2 + V_2 \times D_{breakeven} \times Q$$

Rearranging to isolate $D_{breakeven}$:

$$F_1 - F_2 = (V_2 - V_1) \times D_{breakeven} \times Q$$

$$D_{breakeven} = \frac{F_1 - F_2}{(V_2 - V_1) \times Q}$$

### Step 9: Substituting Values

$$D_{breakeven} = \frac{50{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000}{(8 - 15) \times 500}$$

$$= \frac{-70{,}000}{-7 \times 500}$$

$$= \frac{-70{,}000}{-3{,}500}$$

$$D_{breakeven} = 20 \text{ km}$$

### Step 10: Interpretation of the Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since the breakeven distance is only **20 km**, Rail is more cost-effective for **any distance > 20 km**. For the 2,500 km Kochi–Delhi route, **Rail is the obvious cost-optimal choice**, saving ₹86,80,000 per shipment.

### Decision Rule for Students:

- If $d > D_{breakeven}$: choose the mode with **lower variable cost** (Mode B / Rail here).
- If $d < D_{breakeven}$: choose the mode with **lower fixed cost** (Mode A / Road here).
- If $d = D_{breakeven}$: modes are **indifferent** in cost — choose based on speed/reliability.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mode Selection Decision Framework

| Decision Criterion | Weight | Road Score (1–5) | Rail Score (1–5) | Air Score (1–5) | Water Score (1–5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | 30% | 3 | 5 | 1 | 5 |
| **Speed** | 25% | 3 | 2 | 5 | 1 |
| **Reliability** | 20% | 3 | 5 | 5 | 3 |
| **Capacity** | 15% | 2 | 5 | 1 | 5 |
| **Accessibility** | 10% | 5 | 1 | 3 | 1 |
| **Weighted Total** | 100% | **3.0** | **3.8** | **2.7** | **3.2** |

*Score 1 = Poor, 5 = Excellent. Weights are scenario-dependent.*

## 3.3 Pipeline Inventory Calculation Example

A Bangalore-based electronics firm ships 200 smartphones daily from Shenzhen to Bangalore. Air transit time = 3 days; Sea transit time = 28 days. Which option is better?

**By Air:**

$$I_{pipeline,air} = D_{avg} \times T_{air} = 200 \times 3 = 600 \text{ units}$$

**By Sea:**

$$I_{pipeline,sea} = D_{avg} \times T_{sea} = 200 \times 28 = 5{,}600 \text{ units}$$

**Inventory Carrying Cost (Sea, @ 15% of $300/unit/yr):**

$$IC_{sea} = 5{,}600 \times 300 \times 0.15 = ₹2{,}52{,}000 \text{ per shipment}$$

This illustrates the **inventory-vs-transport trade-off** — the core insight of Chopra & Meindl's framework.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transportation Network Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block maps the **functional architecture of a transportation network** within a supply chain. Each node represents a key role, and the edges represent material/information/cost flows.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SC[Supply Chain Network] --> TR[Transportation System]
    TR --> M1[Mode 1 - Road]
    TR --> M2[Mode 2 - Rail]
    TR --> M3[Mode 3 - Air]
    TR --> M4[Mode 4 - Water]
    TR --> M5[Mode 5 - Pipeline]
    TR --> M6[Mode 6 - Intermodal]
    M1 --> P1[Performance: Cost Moderate]
    M1 --> P2[Performance: Speed High]
    M1 --> P3[Performance: Flexibility High]
    M2 --> P4[Performance: Cost Low]
    M2 --> P5[Performance: Capacity Very High]
    M2 --> P6[Performance: Speed Moderate]
    M3 --> P7[Performance: Cost Very High]
    M3 --> P8[Performance: Speed Very High]
    M3 --> P9[Performance: Reliability Very High]
    M4 --> P10[Performance: Cost Lowest]
    M4 --> P11[Performance: Capacity Highest]
    M4 --> P12[Performance: Speed Slow]
    M5 --> P13[Performance: Cost Lowest]
    M5 --> P14[Performance: Continuous Flow]
    M6 --> P15[Performance: Door-to-Door]
    M6 --> P16[Performance: Containerized]
    SC --> DEC[Decision Factors]
    DEC --> D1[Product Characteristics]
    DEC --> D2[Distance and Geography]
    DEC --> D3[Cost vs Speed Tradeoff]
    DEC --> D4[Reliability Requirements]
    DEC --> D5[Sustainability Goals]
    DEC --> OUT[Optimal Mode Selection]
```

## 4.2 Mode Selection Decision Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start: Shipment Required] --> Q1{Is shipment time-critical?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is product high-value low-weight?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is shipment bulk commodity?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| AIR[Choose Air Transport]
    Q2 -->|No| RD[Choose Road Transport]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Long distance inter-state?}
    Q3 -->|No| RD2[Choose Road Transport]
    Q4 -->|Yes| RAIL[Choose Rail Transport]
    Q4 -->|No| PIPE[Consider Pipeline or Rail]
    AIR --> END[Optimal Mode Selected]
    RD --> END
    RD2 --> END
    RAIL --> END
    PIPE --> END
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Transportation Decision

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT[Input Layer: Shipment Data] --> PROC[Processing Layer: Cost-Speed-Reliability Analysis]
    PROC --> FORM[Formula Application: TLC and Breakeven]
    FORM --> OPT[Optimization: Mode Selection]
    OPT --> OUT[Output Layer: Mode Allocation]
    OUT --> KPI[Monitoring: OTD, Cost per ton-km, Pipeline Inventory]
    KPI --> FEED[Feedback Loop to PROC]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams**: The architecture shows a **closed-loop system** where transportation performance is continuously monitored (OTD, cost, pipeline inventory) and fed back into the decision engine for continuous improvement — a core principle of **Closed-Loop Supply Chain Management**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the term "Pipeline Inventory" in the context of supply chain transportation. How is it related to transit time?**

**Model Answer:**

Pipeline inventory refers to the **inventory that is in transit** between supply chain nodes (e.g., between a manufacturer and a warehouse) and has not yet reached its destination. It represents goods that are physically committed to the supply chain but are not yet available for sale or use.

The relationship is:

$$I_{pipeline} = D_{avg} \times T$$

Where $D_{avg}$ is the average daily demand and $T$ is the transit time.

**Implication**: Faster transport modes (Air) **reduce** pipeline inventory, while slower modes (Sea) **increase** it. However, faster modes have higher freight cost, illustrating the **transport-inventory trade-off**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: List any three performance criteria used to compare transportation modes. State which mode is best for each criterion.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Best Mode |
|---|---|
| **Lowest Cost per ton-km** | Water / Pipeline |
| **Highest Speed** | Air |
| **Highest Capacity** | Water (Ultra Large Container Vessels) |
| **Best Door-to-Door Accessibility** | Road |
| **Highest Reliability** | Pipeline / Air |

Other criteria include **flexibility, safety, frequency, and environmental impact**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern**: Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** between two full-length questions. Each question typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students must answer ONE full question.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain in detail the various modes of transportation available in a supply chain. Compare their performance with respect to cost, speed, capacity, and reliability. (14 marks)**

**Sub-part (a)**: Explain the five primary modes of transportation with their characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**Sub-part (b)**: Construct a comparative performance matrix and identify the optimal mode for: (i) Kerala spices to Germany, (ii) Coal from Jharkhand to a Kerala thermal plant, (iii) Emergency medical supplies during a pandemic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — The Five Primary Modes (7 marks):**

1. **Road Transport**: Door-to-door service, moderate cost, high flexibility, limited capacity (20–25 tons). Best for short-medium hauls.

2. **Rail Transport**: Low cost per ton-mile, high capacity, moderate speed, low flexibility (fixed routes). Best for bulk commodities and long hauls.

3. **Air Transport**: Highest cost, highest speed, low capacity, high reliability. Best for high-value, time-sensitive goods.

4. **Water Transport (Maritime/Inland)**: Lowest cost, highest capacity, slowest speed, weather-dependent. Backbone of international trade.

5. **Pipeline Transport**: Lowest operating cost (after high capex), continuous flow, very limited to liquids/gases. Best for oil, gas, water.

6. **Intermodal Transport**: Combines two or more modes using ISO containers, balancing cost, speed, and accessibility.

**Part (b) — Comparative Performance & Application (7 marks):**

| Scenario | Recommended Mode | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Kerala spices → Germany (international, bulk, non-urgent) | **Sea (Container Ship)** | Lowest cost per ton; spices are non-perishable if properly packed; high volume justifies containerization. |
| (ii) Coal → Kerala thermal plant (domestic, bulk, long-haul) | **Rail** | Low cost per ton-km, very high capacity, reliable for bulk solids, dedicated freight corridors available. |
| (iii) Emergency medical supplies (urgent, high-value) | **Air** | Time-criticality overrides cost; speed and reliability essential; low volume makes cost manageable. |

**Key Takeaway**: Mode selection is **product, distance, and urgency specific**. There is no universally "best" mode.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: A KTU-based FMCG company in Thrissur needs to ship 1,000 tons of detergent monthly to a distribution center in Nagpur (distance = 1,800 km). Two modes are available:**

- **Mode X (Road)**: Fixed cost = ₹80,000/shipment; Variable cost = ₹12/ton-km
- **Mode Y (Rail)**: Fixed cost = ₹1,50,000/shipment; Variable cost = ₹6/ton-km

**(a)** Calculate the total cost of each mode and recommend the optimal choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the breakeven distance formula and determine at what distance the company would be indifferent between the two modes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Total Cost Calculation (7 marks):**

**Total Cost by Road (Mode X):**

$$TC_{Road} = F_X + (V_X \times d \times Q)$$

$$= 80{,}000 + (12 \times 1{,}800 \times 1{,}000)$$

$$= 80{,}000 + 2{,}16{,}00{,}000$$

$$= ₹2{,}16{,}80{,}000$$

**Total Cost by Rail (Mode Y):**

$$TC_{Rail} = F_Y + (V_Y \times d \times Q)$$

$$= 1{,}50{,}000 + (6 \times 1{,}800 \times 1{,}000)$$

$$= 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}08{,}00{,}000$$

$$= ₹1{,}09{,}50{,}000$$

**Recommendation**: **Rail (Mode Y) is optimal**, saving:

$$\Delta TC = 2{,}16{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}09{,}50{,}000 = ₹1{,}07{,}30{,}000 \text{ per shipment}$$

**Part (b) — Breakeven Derivation (7 marks):**

Set $TC_{Road} = TC_{Rail}$:

$$F_X + (V_X \times D_{BE} \times Q) = F_Y + (V_Y \times D_{BE} \times Q)$$

Rearranging:

$$F_X - F_Y = (V_Y - V_X) \times D_{BE} \times Q$$

$$D_{BE} = \frac{F_X - F_Y}{(V_Y - V_X) \times Q}$$

Substituting values:

$$D_{BE} = \frac{80{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000}{(6 - 12) \times 1{,}000}$$

$$= \frac{-70{,}000}{-6 \times 1{,}000}$$

$$= \frac{-70{,}000}{-6{,}000}$$

$$D_{BE} \approx 11.67 \text{ km}$$

**Interpretation**: Since the actual distance (1,800 km) is **far greater** than the breakeven distance (~12 km), Rail is decisively more cost-effective. The company should choose **Rail (Mode Y)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply Variable Cost by Quantity ($Q$)** — Variable cost is "per ton-km", so you must multiply by both distance and quantity. Many students only multiply by distance, losing 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Sign error in breakeven formula** — Ensure the numerator is $(F_1 - F_2)$ and denominator is $(V_2 - V_1)$. A negative-over-negative cancelation is correct only if $F_1 < F_2$ when $V_1 > V_2$.
> 3. **Not stating units in the final answer** — Always mention km, ₹, tons, etc.
> 4. **Confusing "Pipelines" with "Pipeline Inventory"** — They are different concepts.
> 5. **Ignoring qualitative factors** — In scenario-based questions, you must justify mode choice with both cost AND non-cost factors (speed, reliability, perishability).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section before entering the KTU exam hall.**

✅ **Transportation is one of the 4 supply chain drivers** (Inventory, Location, Transportation, Information).

✅ **Six modes**: Road, Rail, Air, Water, Pipeline, Intermodal.

✅ **Trade-off Triangle**: Cost ↔ Speed ↔ Reliability (you can usually optimize only 2 of 3).

✅ **Memory Trick — Modes by Cost (low to high)**: **P**ipeline < **R**ail < **W**ater < **R**oad < **A**ir.

✅ **Memory Trick — Modes by Speed (low to high)**: **P**ipeline < **W**ater < **R**ail < **I**ntermodal < **R**oad < **A**ir.

✅ **Pipeline Inventory Formula** (must memorize): $I_{pipeline} = D_{avg} \times T$.

✅ **Total Landed Cost (TLC)** = Purchase + Transport + Inventory + Warehousing + Shrinkage.

✅ **Breakeven Distance Formula** (must memorize): $D_{BE} = \dfrac{F_1 - F_2}{(V_2 - V_1) \times Q}$.

✅ **Road is best for**: door-to-door, short-medium haul, high-value goods.

✅ **Rail is best for**: bulk commodities, long-haul, inter-city freight.

✅ **Air is best for**: high-value, low-weight, time-critical shipments.

✅ **Water is best for**: international trade, low-value high-volume, non-urgent shipments.

✅ **Pipeline is best for**: continuous flow of liquids/gases (oil, gas, water).

✅ **Intermodal is best for**: long-distance international shipments using containers.
