✅ **Transportation Cost Components**: Fixed (loading, documentation) + Variable (per ton-km).

✅ **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)**: OTD (%), Cost per ton-km, Pipeline Inventory, Damage Rate.

✅ **Trade-off Insight**: Faster transport = Less pipeline inventory BUT more freight cost → optimize TLC, not freight cost alone.

✅ **Sustainability Note (KTU 2024 trend)**: Air > Road > Rail > Water in CO₂ per ton-km. ESG reporting now factors transport mode choice.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation Infrastructure and Policies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transportation Infrastructure and Policies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: TRANSPORTATION INFRASTRUCTURE AND POLICIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transportation Infrastructure (Definition):**
> *Transportation Infrastructure refers to the entire physical, organizational, and institutional framework — including roads, railways, ports, airports, inland waterways, pipelines, vehicles, terminals, information systems, and regulatory bodies — that enables the movement of raw materials, work-in-process inventory, and finished goods across the supply chain network from suppliers to end customers.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transportation Policy (Definition):**
> *Transportation Policy constitutes the set of government regulations, legal frameworks, tariff structures, safety standards, environmental norms, and strategic initiatives formulated by central, state, and local authorities to govern the planning, construction, operation, and pricing of transportation services within a national and global context.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Transportation as the "Circulatory System" of an Economy:**
> Just as blood vessels carry oxygen and nutrients to every cell in the human body, transportation infrastructure carries raw materials to factories and finished goods to consumers. The **heart** is the manufacturing hub, the **arteries** are highways and expressways, the **veins** are return logistics routes, and the **blood** is the cargo. If arteries get clogged (traffic congestion, port delays, regulatory bottlenecks), the entire body (economy) suffers. Transportation **policies** are akin to the medical guidelines that ensure clean blood flow, proper pressure, and healthy circulation.

### 1.3 Scope of Transportation in Supply Chain

Transportation accounts for **approximately 8–10%** of the GDP of a developing economy and is the **largest single cost component** in most logistics budgets. The key infrastructure components are:

| Infrastructure Element | Description | Strategic Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Roadways (Highways)** | National, State, and rural roads | Last-mile delivery; primary mode for short-haul freight |
| **Railways** | Dedicated freight corridors, container trains | Bulk movement; long-haul cost efficiency |
| **Airways (Aviation)** | Cargo airports, freighters | High-value, time-sensitive, perishables |
| **Waterways (Shipping)** | Seaports, inland waterways, container shipping | International trade; bulk commodities |
| **Pipelines** | Crude oil, natural gas, refined products | Continuous flow of liquids/gases |
| **Terminals & Hubs** | ICDs, CFS, dry ports, multimodal hubs | Mode-changeover points; consolidation |
| **ITS (Intelligent Transport Systems)** | GPS, RFID, TMS, automated tracking | Real-time visibility and optimization |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> The syllabus explicitly maps this topic to understanding **Logistics Performance Index (LPI)** indicators — Customs, Infrastructure, Shipments, Logistics Competence, Tracking, and Timeliness. India's rank improvement on the World Bank LPI is a frequently tested data point.

### 1.4 Five Primary Modes of Transportation (Standard Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Memorization):**
> - **Cost per ton-km** — lower means cheaper (Road is highest cost; Water is lowest)
> - **Speed (km/hr)** — higher means faster (Air is fastest; Water is slowest)
> - **Capacity** — volume/weight per consignment
> - **Reliability** — consistency in transit time
> - **Flexibility** — ability to reach any point
> - **Carbon Footprint** — emissions per ton-km

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Transportation Decision

Transportation decisions in SCM are governed by a **hierarchical decision structure** consisting of four layers:

### Layer 1: Service Selection (Mode Choice)
The shipper decides **which mode** to use. This depends on:
- Product characteristics (value, weight, volume, perishability)
- Market characteristics (distance, urgency, customer dispersion)
- Cost-vs-service trade-offs

### Layer 2: Service Provider Selection (Carrier Choice)
The shipper decides **which carrier** within the chosen mode (e.g., Maersk vs MSC for ocean shipping).

### Layer 3: Shipment Size / Consolidation Decisions
- **Small shipments** → higher per-unit cost but lower inventory
- **Large shipments** → lower per-unit transport cost but higher inventory carrying cost
- Optimal quantity is found by **trade-off analysis**.

### Layer 4: Routing and Scheduling
- Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)
- Load planning, backhauls, milk-runs

## 2.2 Comparative Analysis of Transportation Modes

| Mode | Cost/Ton-km (Relative) | Speed | Reliability | Flexibility | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Roadways** | **High** (₹1.5–3.0) | Moderate (40–60 km/hr) | Moderate | **Very High** | Short-haul, FMCG, e-commerce |
| **Railways** | Low (₹0.8–1.2) | Moderate (25–50 km/hr) | High | Low | Bulk, coal, cement, long-haul |
| **Airways** | **Very High** (₹15–25) | **Very High** (800+ km/hr) | High | Moderate | High-value, perishables, urgent |
| **Waterways** | **Lowest** (₹0.3–0.6) | **Lowest** (15–30 km/hr) | Moderate | Low | Bulk commodities, international |
| **Pipelines** | Low (after fixed cost) | Low (5–8 km/hr) | **Very High** | Very Low | Crude, gas, refined products |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The "Rule of Thumb" for mode selection is the **Value-to-Weight Ratio**:
> - **High** value/weight → **Air**
> - **Low** value/weight → **Water/Rail**
> - **Medium** → **Road**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TC = F + (v \times D)$ | Total Transport Cost (Fixed + Variable × Distance) | ₹ |
| 2 | $C_{tk} = \frac{TC}{W \times D}$ | Cost per ton-km (Tk) | ₹/ton-km |
| 3 | $EOQ_{trans} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times A \times S}{i \times C}}$ | Economic Order Quantity with transport cost | Units |
| 4 | $C_{total} = C_{transport} + C_{inventory} + C_{order}$ | Total logistics cost function | ₹ |
| 5 | $LPI_{score} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \times s_i$ | Weighted LPI score (6 dimensions) | Index 1–5 |
| 6 | $T_{transit} = \frac{D}{S_{avg}} + T_{handling}$ | Transit time = Distance/Speed + Handling | Hours |
| 7 | $\text{Backhaul Ratio} = \frac{\text{Loaded Return}}{\text{Total Outbound}}$ | Backhauling efficiency | Ratio 0–1 |
| 8 | $\eta_{load} = \frac{\text{Actual Load}}{\text{Max Capacity}} \times 100$ | Vehicle load factor | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Trap Note (No Vertical Pipes):** All absolute values and mod conditions in this module are written as $\vert x \vert$ using `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

## 2.4 Transportation Policy Framework — Indian Context

> [!NOTE]
> **National Logistics Policy (NLP) 2022 — Key Pillars:**
> 1. **Integration** of logistics across modes
> 2. **Digitalization** through ULIP (Unified Logistics Interface Platform)
> 3. **Standardization** of warehousing and transport
> 4. **Skill Development** in logistics workforce
> 5. **Sustainability** — green logistics and EV adoption

**PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan (2021):**
- ₹100 lakh crore infrastructure investment
- 16 ministries on a single GIS-based platform
- Targets to **reduce logistics cost from 14–16% of GDP to ~9%** (global benchmark)

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **E-commerce giants** (Amazon, Flipkart) rely on multi-modal last-mile infrastructure.
- **Automotive OEMs** use Just-in-Time (JIT) supported by reliable road and rail networks.
- **Pharma cold chain** depends on air + temperature-controlled road transport.
- **Oil & Gas** sector viability depends on pipeline network efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Cost Trade-off in Transportation

The fundamental trade-off in transport decisions is between **transport cost** and **inventory carrying cost**.

### Step 1: Define the Variables
Let:
- $A$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $S$ = Order cost per order (₹/order)
- $C$ = Unit cost of item (₹/unit)
- $i$ = Inventory carrying rate (fraction/year, typically 0.20–0.30)
- $v$ = Variable transport cost per unit per km (₹/unit-km)
- $D$ = Distance (km)
- $Q$ = Order quantity (units)

### Step 2: Express Transport Cost Per Year
Number of orders per year = $\frac{A}{Q}$

Therefore, annual transport cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{transport} &= \left(\frac{A}{Q}\right) \times S + A \times v \times D \\
&= \frac{A \cdot S}{Q} + A \cdot v \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The first term captures fixed order cost incurred $\frac{A}{Q}$ times; the second term is the variable per-unit distance cost charged for the full annual demand.

### Step 3: Express Inventory Carrying Cost
Average inventory = $\frac{Q}{2}$

Therefore, annual carrying cost:

$$
C_{inventory} = \frac{Q}{2} \times C \times i
$$

### Step 4: Formulate Total Cost Function

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total}(Q) &= C_{transport} + C_{inventory} \\
&= \frac{A \cdot S}{Q} + A \cdot v \cdot D + \frac{Q \cdot C \cdot i}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Minimize Total Cost by Differentiating

Take the first derivative with respect to $Q$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{d\,C_{total}}{d\,Q} = -\frac{A \cdot S}{Q^{2}} + \frac{C \cdot i}{2} = 0
$$

### Step 6: Solve for Optimal Q

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C \cdot i}{2} &= \frac{A \cdot S}{Q^{2}} \\
Q^{2} &= \frac{2 \cdot A \cdot S}{C \cdot i} \\
Q^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot A \cdot S}{C \cdot i}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the classic **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** result, generalized with the transport dimension.

### Step 7: Practical Interpretation

- If transport cost $v \cdot D$ is **high** → use **faster modes** (Air) but **smaller, more frequent** shipments.
- If transport cost is **low** (Water/Rail) → use **larger, less frequent** shipments.
- The optimal $Q^{*}$ balances **transport frequency** with **inventory holding**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mode Selection Decision Tree

| Step | Decision Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is transit time < 24 hrs critical? | **Air** | Go to Step 2 |
| 2 | Is shipment > 500 km and bulk? | **Rail/Water** | Go to Step 3 |
| 3 | Is destination inland with road access? | **Road** | Go to Step 4 |
| 4 | Is it liquid/gas? | **Pipeline** | Consider **Intermodal** |

## 3.3 Cost-Per-Ton-Km Worked Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

> **Question:** A consignment of **50 tonnes** is transported over **400 km**.
> - Mode A (Road): ₹2,400 per tonne total charge
> - Mode B (Rail): ₹1,600 per tonne total charge
> Calculate the cost per ton-km for both modes.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{tk}^{Road} &= \frac{50 \times 2400}{50 \times 400} = \frac{120000}{20000} = 6.0 \; \text{₹/ton-km} \\
C_{tk}^{Rail} &= \frac{50 \times 1600}{50 \times 400} = \frac{80000}{20000} = 4.0 \; \text{₹/ton-km} \\
\Delta C &= 6.0 - 4.0 = 2.0 \; \text{₹/ton-km} \\
\text{Savings on Rail} &= 2.0 \times 50 \times 400 = ₹40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Rail is **33.33% cheaper** per ton-km than road for this consignment — a classic KTU numerical.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Intermodal Transportation Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Supplier Warehouse] --> B[Road First Mile]
    B --> C[Inland Container Depot ICD]
    C --> D[Rail Long Haul]
    D --> E[Port Terminal]
    E --> F[Ocean Shipping]
    F --> G[Destination Port]
    G --> H[Customs Clearance]
    H --> I[Rail or Road Last Mile]
    I --> J[Customer Distribution Center]
    J --> K[End Customer]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block represents a node in the supply chain. The arrows show cargo flow. This is the **canonical intermodal freight architecture** in international trade. Document handover occurs at every node (LR, RR, BoL, BoE, Delivery Challan).

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Hierarchical Transportation Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Transportation Decision Problem] --> M2{Service Selection: Which Mode}
    M2 --> M3[Road]
    M2 --> M4[Rail]
    M2 --> M5[Air]
    M2 --> M6[Water]
    M2 --> M7[Pipeline]
    M3 --> M8{Provider Selection: Which Carrier}
    M4 --> M8
    M5 --> M8
    M6 --> M8
    M7 --> M8
    M8 --> M9{Shipment Size Decision}
    M9 --> M10[Routing and Scheduling]
    M10 --> M11[Execution and Monitoring]
    M11 --> M12[Performance Review]
```

> **Interpretation:** Layer 1 decisions (mode) constrain Layer 2 (carrier). A wrong mode choice cannot be fixed by a better carrier.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Policy Stack of Indian Transportation Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[National Logistics Policy 2022] --> P2[PM Gati Shakti Master Plan]
    P1 --> P3[Motor Vehicles Act 1988 Amended 2019]
    P1 --> P4[Indian Railways Act 1989]
    P1 --> P5[Carriage of Goods by Sea Act 1925]
    P1 --> P6[Air Cargo Policy]
    P2 --> P7[Gati Shakti Multi-Modal Cargo Terminals]
    P2 --> P8[National Highway Expansion Programme]
    P2 --> P9[Dedicated Freight Corridors]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The National Logistics Policy (NLP) 2022 is the **apex policy** that cascades into mode-specific acts and infra initiatives. Understanding this hierarchy is critical for KTU long-answer questions on "Discuss the policy framework governing Indian transportation."

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Multimodal Transport Document Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Quotation and Booking] --> R1[LR Lorry Receipt for Road]
    R1 --> S[Railway Receipt RR]
    S --> T1[BoL Bill of Lading for Sea]
    T1 --> T2[Airway Bill AWB]
    T2 --> U[Customs Bill of Entry]
    U --> V[Delivery Order]
    V --> W[Proof of Delivery POD]
```

> **Note:** Each document has legal significance under the relevant Carriage Act. Loss of cargo liability shifts based on the document in possession at the time of loss.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define transportation infrastructure. List any four major components of transportation infrastructure with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for components):**

Transportation infrastructure refers to the physical, organizational, and institutional framework that enables the movement of goods and people across geographies. It includes all fixed assets, networks, terminals, vehicles, and information systems supporting the flow of cargo.

**Four major components:**
1. **Roadways** — e.g., National Highway 44 (Srinagar to Kanyakumari)
2. **Railways** — e.g., Dedicated Freight Corridor (Eastern & Western)
3. **Seaports** — e.g., JNPT Mumbai, Mundra, Visakhapatnam
4. **Air Cargo Terminals** — e.g., IGI Cargo Terminal, Delhi

*(1.5 marks for listing with examples + 0.5 mark each for additional quality)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the National Logistics Policy (NLP) 2022? Mention its two key objectives.*

**Model Answer:**

The **National Logistics Policy 2022**, launched by the Government of India, aims to integrate, digitalize, and streamline the logistics sector to **reduce logistics costs from 14–16% of GDP to ~9%**, aligning India with global benchmarks (8–11%).

**Two key objectives:**
1. To provide **integrated logistics infrastructure** through the PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan.
2. To enable **digitalization and standardization** of logistics processes via the Unified Logistics Interface Platform (ULIP).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for objectives)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*(a) Compare the five major modes of transportation in terms of cost, speed, flexibility, reliability, and suitable cargo. (7 marks)*

*(b) Discuss the National Logistics Policy 2022 and the PM Gati Shakti Master Plan in detail. How do they aim to improve India's LPI ranking? (7 marks)*

#### Solution to (a): Comparison of Five Modes (7 marks)

A structured comparison table (4 marks for table + 1 mark for explanation + 2 marks for cargo suitability):

| Mode | Cost/Ton-km | Speed | Flexibility | Reliability | Best Suited Cargo |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Road** | High (₹1.5–3) | 40–60 km/hr | **Highest** | Moderate | FMCG, e-commerce, short-haul |
| **Rail** | Low (₹0.8–1.2) | 25–50 km/hr | Low | **High** | Coal, cement, steel, foodgrains |
| **Air** | **Highest** (₹15–25) | **800+ km/hr** | Moderate | High | Pharma, electronics, perishables |
| **Water** | **Lowest** (₹0.3–0.6) | **Lowest** (15–30 km/hr) | Very Low | Moderate | Crude oil, containers, bulk minerals |
| **Pipeline** | Low (high capex) | Slow (5–8 km/hr) | Very Low | **Highest** | Crude, gas, refined petroleum |

**Explanation (1 mark):** Road is preferred for door-to-door flexibility; rail for long-haul bulk; air for time-sensitive goods; water for international bulk trade; pipeline for continuous liquid/gas flow.

**Suitability (2 marks):** Choice depends on **value-to-weight ratio, distance, and urgency**. Perishable, high-value items → Air; low-value bulk → Water; inland bulk → Rail; short distances → Road; liquids → Pipeline.

#### Solution to (b): NLP 2022 & Gati Shakti (7 marks)

**National Logistics Policy 2022 (3 marks):**
- Launched on 17 September 2022 by the Ministry of Commerce and Industry.
- **Vision:** To make India a logistics-efficient nation by integrating fragmented infrastructure.
- **Four key pillars:**
  1. **Integration** of logistics networks
  2. **Digitalization** (ULIP — Unified Logistics Interface Platform connecting 30+ systems)
  3. **Standardization** of warehousing, transport equipment
  4. **Capacity Building** through Logistics Skill Council

**PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan (3 marks):**
- Launched in 2021; ₹100 lakh crore infrastructure investment plan.
- A **GIS-based digital platform** integrating 16 ministries (Roads, Railways, Aviation, Shipping, Petroleum, Power, Telecom, etc.).
- Identifies **infrastructure gaps** and synchronizes project planning.
- Builds **Multi-Modal Logistics Parks (MMLPs)** and **Multi-Modal Cargo Terminals**.

**Impact on LPI (1 mark):**
- Aims to push India from current LPI rank **38 (2023)** into the **top 25** by 2030.
- Improves six LPI dimensions — especially **Infrastructure, Tracking, and Timeliness** — by enabling seamless mode transfer and real-time cargo visibility.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]**
*(a) Explain the role of intermodal and multimodal transportation in modern supply chains. What are the key legal documents involved? (7 marks)*

*(b) A manufacturing company ships 5,000 units per year. The order cost is ₹800 per order, unit cost is ₹200, and inventory carrying rate is 25% per year. The transport cost is ₹10 per unit. Calculate the EOQ. If transport cost is reduced to ₹6 per unit, recalculate and comment. (7 marks)*

#### Solution to (a): Intermodal & Multimodal Transport (7 marks)

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Intermodal Transport:** Use of two or more modes in an integrated manner, with **separate contracts and separate carriers** for each leg. Each carrier issues its own document (e.g., LR + RR + BoL).
- **Multimodal Transport:** Use of two or more modes under a **single contract** with a **single Multimodal Transport Operator (MTO)** who issues a single **Multimodal Transport Document (MTD)** and assumes **single-point liability**.

**Role in Modern Supply Chains (3 marks):**
1. **Cost optimization** — combines low-cost long-haul (Rail/Water) with flexible first/last mile (Road).
2. **Speed + reach** — Air for long-distance urgent legs + Road for last-mile.
3. **Reduced handling damage** — containerization allows door-to-door sealed movement.
4. **Sustainability** — shifting freight from road to rail/water reduces carbon footprint.
5. **Single-window documentation** (in multimodal) simplifies customs and insurance.

**Key Legal Documents (2 marks):**
- **Lorry Receipt (LR)** — Road (under Carriers Act 1865)
- **Railway Receipt (RR)** — Rail (Indian Railways Act 1989)
- **Bill of Lading (BoL)** — Sea (Carriage of Goods by Sea Act 1925)
- **Airway Bill (AWB)** — Air (Carriage by Air Act 1972)
- **Multimodal Transport Document (MTD)** — Single document under Multimodal Transportation of Goods Act 1993

#### Solution to (b): EOQ Numerical (7 marks)

**Given (1 mark for writing given data):**
- $A = 5000$ units/year
- $S = ₹800$/order
- $C = ₹200$/unit
- $i = 0.25$/year
- $v \cdot D = ₹10$/unit (Case 1), ₹6/unit (Case 2)

**Case 1: Transport cost = ₹10/unit (1 mark for setup, 2 marks for calculation)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{1}^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot A \cdot S}{C \cdot i}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 800}{200 \times 0.25}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{8{,}000{,}000}{50}} \\
&= \sqrt{160{,}000} \\
&\approx 400 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2: Transport cost = ₹6/unit (1 mark for setup, 1 mark for calculation)**

Note: The classic EOQ formula does not include transport cost directly. Transport cost becomes relevant when computing **total landed cost**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total}(Q) &= \frac{A \cdot S}{Q} + A \cdot v \cdot D + \frac{Q \cdot C \cdot i}{2} \\
C_{total}(400) &= \frac{5000 \times 800}{400} + 5000 \times 6 + \frac{400 \times 200 \times 0.25}{2} \\
&= 10{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 10{,}000 \\
&= ₹50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (2 marks):**
When transport cost is ₹10/unit, total cost = ₹10,000 + ₹50,000 + ₹10,000 = **₹70,000**.
When transport cost is ₹6/unit, total cost = **₹50,000**.
A **₹4/unit reduction in transport cost** yields a savings of **₹20,000/year (28.6% reduction)**, demonstrating the critical role of carrier negotiation and route optimization in supply chain cost reduction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Intermodal vs Multimodal** — Single contract & single liability = Multimodal. Multiple contracts = Intermodal. Examiners deduct 2 marks instantly for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in EOQ Numerical** — Always write "units" or "tonnes" after the final answer. A bare "400" without units can cost 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up LR/RR/BoL/AWB** — Each document belongs to a specific mode and a specific Indian Act. Memorize the Act-document mapping.
> 4. **Writing "Transportation = Logistics"** — Logistics is broader (includes warehousing, inventory, packaging, info flow). Transportation is one component.
> 5. **In policy questions, forgetting to mention specific schemes** — Vague answers like "government helps logistics" score poorly. Always name **PM Gati Shakti, NLP 2022, ULIP, MMLP, DFC**.
> 6. **Numerical Errors in Cost/Ton-Km** — Always divide by `tonnes × km`, not just `km`. The examiner checks the denominator.
> 7. **Skipping the 'Comment' in numericals** — Even with correct numbers, a missing business interpretation can cost 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Transportation infrastructure** = physical + institutional + digital framework for movement of goods.
- ✅ **Five modes**: Road, Rail, Air, Water, Pipeline — know cost, speed, flexibility, reliability ranking.
- ✅ **Road** = highest flexibility, highest cost per ton-km.
- ✅ **Water** = lowest cost, slowest, used for international bulk trade.
- ✅ **Air** = highest cost, fastest, used for high-value, perishable, urgent cargo.
- ✅ **Rail** = best for long-haul bulk (coal, cement, foodgrain).
- ✅ **Pipeline** = continuous flow of liquids/gases, very high reliability.
- ✅ **Intermodal** = multiple contracts, multiple carriers. **Multimodal** = single contract, MTO, single liability.
- ✅ **Containerization** is the enabler of modern intermodalism.
- ✅ **EOQ formula** = $\sqrt{\frac{2AS}{Ci}}$ — transport cost affects total cost, not EOQ itself directly.
- ✅ **Total logistics cost** = transport + inventory + ordering + shortage + warehousing.
- ✅ **India's target** = reduce logistics cost from **14–16% to ~9%** of GDP (aligning with global benchmark).
- ✅ **National Logistics Policy 2022** pillars: Integration, Digitalization, Standardization, Skill Development.
- ✅ **PM Gati Shakti** = ₹100 lakh crore, 16 ministries, GIS-based platform.
- ✅ **ULIP** = Unified Logistics Interface Platform — connects 30+ government/industry systems.
- ✅ **Key legal documents**: LR (Road), RR (Rail), BoL (Sea), AWB (Air), MTD (Multimodal).
- ✅ **Relevant Indian Acts**: Motor Vehicles Act 1988, Indian Railways Act 1989, Carriage of Goods by Sea Act 1925, Carriage by Air Act 1972, Multimodal Transportation of Goods Act 1993.
- ✅ **LPI (Logistics Performance Index)** by World Bank — 6 dimensions, India ranked **38 in 2023**.
- ✅ **Backhauling** = utilizing return trips with load — improves load factor and reduces per-unit cost.
- ✅ **Value-to-Weight Ratio** rule: High → Air; Low → Water/Rail; Medium → Road.
- ✅ **Gati Shakti Multi-Modal Cargo Terminals** are being built to reduce cargo handling time.
- ✅ **DFC (Dedicated Freight Corridors)** — Eastern and Western — increase rail share in freight.
- ✅ **Sustainability**: Shift from road to rail/water reduces carbon emissions per ton-km by **60–80%**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Options and Their Trade-offs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Options and Their Trade-offs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supply Chain Design Options** are the strategic structural and operational alternatives available to a firm in configuring its supply chain network, while **Trade-offs** refer to the inherent give-and-take relationships between conflicting performance objectives (e.g., cost vs. service level, inventory vs. responsiveness) where improving one performance dimension necessarily degrades another.

A "design option" in SCM refers to a structural decision such as:
- How many distribution centers (DCs) to operate
- Where to position them
- Whether to manufacture or outsource
- How to push product information and material flow
- What level of inventory to hold at each stage

These decisions are *strategic* (long-term, capital-intensive, hard to reverse), and almost always involve **trade-offs** — improving one performance metric worsens another.

### 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Delivery Dilemma"**

Imagine you are running a pizza delivery chain in Kerala:
- **Centralized kitchen** (one large kitchen in Kochi serving all of Kerala) → Lower rent, fewer chefs, but pizza arrives cold in Kannur, and delivery time is high.
- **Decentralized kitchens** (a kitchen in every district) → Pizza arrives hot, low delivery time, but high fixed costs and idle capacity in low-demand districts.

This is a classic **centralization vs. decentralization trade-off**. You cannot optimize both *cost* and *speed* simultaneously — you must *trade-off* one against the other. This is the essence of every design decision in supply chain management.

### 1.3 Why Design Options and Trade-offs Are Critical

| Strategic Driver | Implication |
|---|---|
| **Customer expectations** rising | Pushes toward agility and responsiveness |
| **Cost pressures** | Pushes toward efficiency and centralization |
| **Globalization** | Opens make-vs-buy and offshoring decisions |
| **Technology disruption** | Enables postponement and mass customization |
| **Risk and uncertainty** | Forces inventory vs. lead-time trade-offs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to *identify*, *analyze*, and *justify* a design option with explicit reference to its trade-offs. Examiners test whether you can argue *both sides* — that is what earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Core Design Options in SCM

Each design option has a **canonical trade-off**. Mastery of these is the *single highest-yield* topic for Module 4 in KTU examinations.

---

#### **Option 1 — Centralized vs. Decentralized Distribution Network**

| Aspect | Centralized | Decentralized |
|---|---|---|
| Number of facilities | Few | Many |
| Fixed facility cost | Low | High |
| Inventory holding | High (safety stock) | Low |
| Transportation cost | High (long-haul) | Low (short-haul) |
| Customer lead time | High | Low |
| Responsiveness | Low | High |
| Risk pooling | Strong | Weak |

**The Trade-off:** *Cost efficiency vs. customer responsiveness.*

**Square Root Law of Safety Stock** — the mathematical heart of this trade-off:

$$SS_{centralized} = SS_{decentralized} \times \sqrt{n}$$

where $n$ = number of decentralized facilities. Centralizing $n$ facilities into 1 reduces safety stock by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$ but increases the distance (and cost) to customers.

---

#### **Option 2 — Push vs. Pull System**

- **Push:** Production and distribution decisions are based on **forecasted** demand. Material flows *into* the market.
- **Pull:** Production is triggered by **actual** customer demand (e.g., Just-In-Time).

**The Trade-off:** *Forecast accuracy vs. lead time*. Push requires accurate forecasts but enables long production runs (economies of scale). Pull eliminates forecast error but exposes the system to lead-time risk.

**The Push-Pull Boundary** is the point in the supply chain where execution switches from forecast-driven to demand-driven.

---

#### **Option 3 — Make vs. Buy (Insourcing vs. Outsourcing)**

- **Make:** Firm produces the component/good internally.
- **Buy:** Firm sources from an external supplier.

**Decision Drivers:** Core competency, cost, capacity, quality control, intellectual property protection, supply risk.

**The Trade-off:** *Vertical integration (control) vs. flexibility (focus)*.

**Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** comparison framework:

$$TCO = C_{purchase} + C_{ordering} + C_{holding} + C_{stockout} + C_{quality} + C_{transport}$$

---

#### **Option 4 — Direct vs. Indirect Distribution**

- **Direct:** Manufacturer → Customer (e.g., Amazon, Dell)
- **Indirect:** Manufacturer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Customer

**The Trade-off:** *Channel control and margin capture vs. market reach and distribution cost*.

---

#### **Option 5 — Standardization vs. Customization**

- **Standardization:** Same product for all customers → economies of scale.
- **Customization:** Tailored product → higher customer satisfaction, higher cost.

**The Trade-off:** *Scale economies vs. customer intimacy*.

The modern resolution: **Postponement** — standardize the *core* but customize the *finish* at the last possible point.

---

#### **Option 6 — Lean vs. Agile Supply Chain**

| Dimension | Lean | Agile |
|---|---|---|
| Demand profile | Stable, predictable | Volatile, unpredictable |
| Inventory | Minimal (JIT) | Buffered (strategic stock) |
| Lead time | Long acceptable | Must be short |
| Variety | Low | High |
| Cost driver | Unit cost | Responsiveness cost |

**The Trade-off:** *Efficiency (cost minimization) vs. Responsiveness (service-level maximization)*.

**Strategic Resolution:** Use **Leagile** — Lean upstream, Agile downstream.

---

#### **Option 7 — Inventory vs. Transportation Trade-off**

A foundational SCM trade-off.

$$Total\ Logistics\ Cost = Inventory\ Holding\ Cost + Transportation\ Cost$$

- More inventory (warehouses) → Less transportation cost (short-haul).
- Less inventory (fewer warehouses) → More transportation cost (long-haul, expedited).

---

#### **Option 8 — Lead Time vs. Inventory (Lot-Sizing) Trade-off**

Smaller lot sizes reduce cycle inventory but increase ordering/transportation frequency:

$$Cycle\ Inventory = \frac{Q}{2}$$

$$Number\ of\ orders\ per\ year = \frac{D}{Q}$$

**The Trade-off:** *Setup/ordering cost vs. holding cost* — optimized via the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU ESE — Module 4**

| Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $SS_{n} = SS_{1} \cdot \sqrt{n}$ | Square-root law for safety stock pooling | Centralization trade-off |
| $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Economic Order Quantity | Lot-sizing trade-off |
| $TC_{inventory} = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | Total relevant cost | EOQ decision |
| $TCO = C_p + C_o + C_h + C_s + C_q + C_t$ | Total Cost of Ownership | Make-vs-Buy |
| $Reorder\ Point\ (ROP) = d \cdot L + SS$ | Inventory reorder point | Pull-system trigger |
| $\rho = \dfrac{c_a - c_b}{c_a}$ | Capacity utilization cushion | Push vs. Pull boundary |
| $Service\ Level = 1 - \dfrac{Stockouts}{Demand\ Cycles}$ | Type-1 / Type-2 service | Inventory trade-off |
| $Z_{SL}$ | Safety factor from normal table | Safety stock calculation |
| $Safety\ Stock = Z_{SL} \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Safety stock under variable LT | Service-level trade-off |
| $Holding\ Cost = i \cdot C \cdot \bar{I}$ | Annual holding cost | Inventory trade-off |

Where:
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $Q$ = Order quantity (units)
- $i$ = Holding cost rate (fraction)
- $C$ = Unit cost (₹)
- $\sigma_{LT}$ = Standard deviation of lead time demand
- $Z_{SL}$ = Standard normal value at desired service level

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

These design options and their trade-offs are foundational to:
- **Site selection decisions** (e.g., Amazon's DC placement)
- **Network rationalization** (e.g., Tata Motors consolidating plants)
- **Reshoring vs. offshoring** (post-COVID global supply chain restructuring)
- **Omnichannel retail design** (e.g., Reliance Retail, Flipkart)
- **Resilience vs. efficiency** (post-pandemic strategic re-balancing)

The **KTU examiner expects you to recognize the underlying trade-off** in any case study, not merely describe the decision.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Square Root Law of Safety Stock

**Premise:** Suppose a firm currently operates $n$ independent warehouses, each holding safety stock to protect against local demand variability. The firm considers consolidating them into a single centralized warehouse.

**Step 1 — Define demand at each warehouse.**

Let demand $D_i$ at warehouse $i$ be a normally distributed random variable:

$$D_i \sim N(\mu_i,\ \sigma_i^2)$$

**Step 2 — Decentralized safety stock.**

If the firm must independently protect each warehouse, the safety stock at each is:

$$SS_i = Z_{SL} \cdot \sigma_i$$

**Step 3 — Total decentralized safety stock.**

$$SS_{decentralized} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} SS_i = Z_{SL} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i$$

**Step 4 — Centralized safety stock.**

If all $n$ warehouses are consolidated into one, demands aggregate:

$$D_{central} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i$$

Variance of the sum (assuming independence):

$$\sigma_{central}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{central} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2}$$

**Step 5 — Centralized safety stock value.**

$$SS_{centralized} = Z_{SL} \cdot \sigma_{central} = Z_{SL} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2}$$

**Step 6 — Simplifying assumption: identical warehouses.**

Assume each warehouse has equal demand variance $\sigma_i = \sigma$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2 = n\sigma^2$$

Therefore:

$$SS_{centralized} = Z_{SL} \cdot \sqrt{n\sigma^2} = Z_{SL} \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n}$$

**Step 7 — Form the ratio.**

$$\frac{SS_{centralized}}{SS_{decentralized}} = \frac{Z_{SL} \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{n}}{n \cdot Z_{SL} \cdot \sigma}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{SS_{centralized}}{SS_{decentralized}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}}$$

Equivalently:

$$SS_{centralized} = \frac{SS_{decentralized}}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A firm operates $n = 4$ regional warehouses, each carrying safety stock = 100 units. Service level = 95%.

$$SS_{centralized} = \frac{4 \times 100}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{400}{2} = 200\ \text{units}$$

vs. Decentralized total = **400 units**. Centralization saves **50%** of safety stock despite serving the same total demand — this is the **risk-pooling effect**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

**Premise:** Trade off fixed ordering cost vs. variable holding cost.

**Step 1 — Total relevant cost function.**

$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Annual ordering cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Annual holding cost}}$$

**Step 2 — Take the derivative and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d\ TC(Q)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Q$.**

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

$$\boxed{EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum total cost.**

$$TC_{min} = \frac{D}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} \cdot S + \frac{\sqrt{2DS/H}}{2} \cdot H$$

$$TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Matching Design Options to Trade-offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is what KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark answer.** Use it as a structured response template.

| # | Design Option | Primary Trade-off | Resolution Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centralized vs. Decentralized | Cost vs. Responsiveness | Hybrid network; risk pooling |
| 2 | Push vs. Pull | Forecast accuracy vs. Lead time | Postponement / Push-Pull boundary |
| 3 | Make vs. Buy | Control vs. Flexibility | TCO analysis; core competency test |
| 4 | Direct vs. Indirect | Control vs. Reach | Omni-channel strategy |
| 5 | Standardization vs. Customization | Scale vs. Variety | Modular design + postponement |
| 6 | Lean vs. Agile | Efficiency vs. Responsiveness | Leagile strategy |
| 7 | Inventory vs. Transportation | Holding cost vs. Freight cost | Cross-docking; milk runs |
| 8 | Lot size: Large vs. Small | Setup cost vs. Holding cost | EOQ; SMED |
| 9 | Single vs. Multiple sourcing | Cost vs. Risk | Dual sourcing; supplier portfolio |
| 10 | Domestic vs. Global | Cost vs. Lead time & Risk | Nearshoring; multi-sourcing |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Inventory vs. Transportation Trade-off

A firm in Kerala ships from a single plant in Bangalore. Current policy: ship in full truckloads (TL) every 14 days.
- Annual demand $D = 50{,}000$ units
- Truckload capacity = 5,000 units
- TL freight = ₹40,000 per truck
- Holding cost $H = ₹100$/unit/year

**Step 1 — Inventory cost under TL policy.**

Cycle inventory = $Q/2 = 5{,}000/2 = 2{,}500$ units.

$$Holding\ cost = 2{,}500 \times 100 = ₹2{,}50{,{000\ /\ \text{year}}}$$

**Step 2 — Transportation cost.**

$$\frac{D}{Q} = \frac{50{,}000}{5{,}000} = 10\ \text{trucks/year}$$

$$Transport\ cost = 10 \times 40{,}000 = ₹4{,}00{,}000\ /\ \text{year}$$

**Step 3 — Total cost (TL policy).**

$$TC_{TL} = 2{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹6{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute EOQ.**

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50{,}000 \times S}{100}}$$

Assume ordering cost $S = ₹40{,}000$ (one truck per order):

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50{,}000 \times 40{,}000}{100}} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{10}} = 20{,}000\ \text{units}$$

Since EOQ exceeds truck capacity, EOQ is infeasible — so use truckload constraint.

**Conclusion:** The trade-off reveals that the firm is locked into a high inventory cost because of high per-order freight. **The trade-off resolution is to negotiate smaller-truck freight pricing or use LTL/rail consolidation.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Trade-off Map — All Design Options

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Supply Chain Design Decisions"] --> node2["Network Structure"]
    node1 --> node3["Information & Flow Logic"]
    node1 --> node4["Sourcing Strategy"]
    node1 --> node5["Product Strategy"]
    node1 --> node6["Operations Strategy"]

    node2 --> node2a["Centralized vs Decentralized"]
    node2a -- "Trade-off" --> node2b["Cost vs Responsiveness"]
    node2 --> node2c["Direct vs Indirect Channel"]
    node2c -- "Trade-off" --> node2d["Channel Control vs Market Reach"]

    node3 --> node3a["Push vs Pull"]
    node3a -- "Trade-off" --> node3b["Forecast Accuracy vs Lead Time"]
    node3 --> node3c["Lean vs Agile"]
    node3c -- "Trade-off" --> node3d["Efficiency vs Responsiveness"]

    node4 --> node4a["Make vs Buy"]
    node4a -- "Trade-off" --> node4b["Vertical Integration vs Flexibility"]
    node4 --> node4c["Single vs Multiple Sourcing"]
    node4c -- "Trade-off" --> node4d["Economies of Scale vs Supply Risk"]

    node5 --> node5a["Standardization vs Customization"]
    node5a -- "Trade-off" --> node5b["Scale Economies vs Variety"]
    node5 --> node5c["Inventory vs Transportation"]
    node5c -- "Trade-off" --> node5d["Holding Cost vs Freight Cost"]

    node6 --> node6a["Lot Size Decision"]
    node6a -- "Trade-off" --> node6b["Setup Cost vs Holding Cost"]
    node6 --> node6c["Lead Time vs Inventory"]
    node6c -- "Trade-off" --> node6d["Speed vs Working Capital"]
```

---

### 4.2 Push-Pull Boundary Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Supplier"] --> nodeB["Manufacturer"]
    nodeB -- "PUSH forecast driven" --> nodeC["Distribution Center"]
    nodeC -- "PUSH forecast driven" --> nodeD["Retailer"]
    nodeD -- "PULL demand driven" --> nodeE["Customer"]

    nodeX["Push-Pull Boundary"]:::boundary
    nodeD -.- nodeX
    nodeX -.- nodeE

    classDef boundary fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:3px;
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Push-Pull Boundary** (shown as the dashed boundary node above) is the strategic point in the supply chain where execution switches from *forecast-based* to *demand-based*. Items held in inventory at this boundary are called **decoupling inventory**. Closer the boundary to the customer → *more responsive, higher inventory*. Closer the boundary to the supplier → *more efficient, lower responsiveness.*

---

### 4.3 Centralization vs. Decentralization Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Network Design Decision"] --> nodeQ1{"Customer Lead Time SLA?"}
    nodeQ1 -- "Tight less than 24 hours" --> nodeQ2{"Demand Volume per Region?"}
    nodeQ1 -- "Relaxed greater than 5 days" --> nodeQ3["Consider Centralized Network"]

    nodeQ2 -- "High volume per region" --> nodeQ4["Decentralized Warehouses"]
    nodeQ2 -- "Low volume per region" --> nodeQ3

    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ5{"Risk Pooling Potential?"}
    nodeQ5 -- "High" --> nodeQ6["Centralize with Risk Pooling"]
    nodeQ5 -- "Low" --> nodeQ7["Hybrid: Centralized plant, Local DCs"]

    nodeQ4 --> nodeQ8["Multiple regional DCs"]
    nodeQ6 --> nodeQ9["1 large DC"]
    nodeQ7 --> nodeQ10["1 plant + 3 to 5 DCs"]
```

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Trade-off Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeInput["Strategic Objective: Maximize Profit + Service"] --> nodeBlock1["Demand Sensing Layer"]
    nodeBlock1 --> nodeBlock2["Network Design Module"]
    nodeBlock2 --> nodeBlock3["Inventory Optimization Module"]
    nodeBlock3 --> nodeBlock4["Transportation Optimization Module"]
    nodeBlock4 --> nodeBlock5["Sourcing and Procurement Module"]
    nodeBlock5 --> nodeOutput["Trade-off Resolution Output"]

    nodeOutput --> nodeOutA["Centralized Hub for Cost Efficiency"]
    nodeOutput --> nodeOutB["Regional DCs for Responsiveness"]
    nodeOutput --> nodeOutC["Push Pull Boundary at Decoupling Point"]
    nodeOutput --> nodeOutD["Lean Upstream, Agile Downstream"]

    nodeBlock2 -. "Trade-off: Cost vs Service" .- nodeBlock3
    nodeBlock3 -. "Trade-off: Inventory vs Transport" .- nodeBlock4
    nodeBlock4 -. "Trade-off: Mode Cost vs Speed" .- nodeBlock5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define "trade-off" in supply chain design. Illustrate with one example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A trade-off in supply chain design is the inherent compromise between two conflicting performance objectives, where improving one worsens the other. **Example:** Centralizing warehouses reduces inventory (via risk pooling) but increases customer lead times and outbound transportation costs. Thus, the firm must *trade off* inventory cost savings against reduced customer responsiveness. (3 marks — *1 for definition, 2 for example with explanation*)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between Push and Pull systems in a supply chain.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Push | Pull |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Forecast | Actual demand |
| Inventory | Higher | Lower |
| Lead-time risk | Lower | Higher |
| Examples | Consumer electronics, FMCG | Custom apparel, JIT |

In a Push system, production and distribution precede demand. In a Pull system, activities are initiated only upon customer order. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the design options related to *Centralization vs. Decentralization* of a distribution network. What are the key trade-offs involved?

**(b) [7 marks]** A retail firm operates 9 equal-sized regional warehouses. Each warehouse holds safety stock of 600 units. The firm is considering consolidating into 1 centralized warehouse. Using the Square Root Law, calculate:
  - (i) Total decentralized safety stock
  - (ii) Centralized safety stock
  - (iii) The percentage reduction in safety stock

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks] — Centralization vs. Decentralization**

- **Centralized Network:** Few large facilities serving a wide geographic area. (1)
- **Decentralized Network:** Many smaller facilities serving local markets. (1)
- **Trade-off 1 — Cost:** Centralized = lower facility cost, higher transportation cost; Decentralized = opposite. (2)
- **Trade-off 2 — Responsiveness:** Centralized = longer customer lead times; Decentralized = shorter lead times. (1)
- **Trade-off 3 — Risk:** Centralized = single-point-of-failure risk; Decentralized = resilience through redundancy. (1)
- **Hybrid / Risk Pooling:** Modern firms adopt hybrid networks — a central hub for bulk storage plus regional DCs for last-mile delivery. (1)

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution**

**Step 1:** Total decentralized safety stock.

$$SS_{decentralized} = n \times SS_{each} = 9 \times 600 = 5{,}400\ \text{units}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Square Root Law.

$$SS_{centralized} = \frac{SS_{decentralized}}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{5{,}400}{\sqrt{9}} = \frac{5{,}400}{3} = 1{,}800\ \text{units}$$

**Step 3:** Percentage reduction.

$$\% Reduction = \frac{5{,}400 - 1{,}800}{5{,}400} \times 100\% = \frac{3{,}600}{5{,}400} \times 100\% = 66.67\%$$

**Conclusion:** Centralization reduces safety stock by **66.67%**, saving 3,600 units of inventory carrying cost — but increases transportation cost and customer lead time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the *Make vs. Buy* design decision. What trade-offs does a firm face when making this strategic choice? Discuss the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) framework.

**(b) [7 marks]** A manufacturer faces an annual demand $D = 12{,}000$ units. Ordering cost $S = ₹2{,}500$ per order. Holding cost $H = ₹150$ per unit per year.
  - (i) Calculate the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ).
  - (ii) Calculate the total annual inventory cost at EOQ.
  - (iii) If the firm currently orders $Q = 1{,}000$ units each time, calculate the extra cost incurred compared to EOQ.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks] — Make vs. Buy Decision**

- **Make (Insourcing):** Firm produces the component in-house. (1)
- **Buy (Outsourcing):** Firm procures from external supplier. (1)
- **Trade-offs:** (3)
  - *Control vs. Flexibility* — Make gives full control over quality/IP but reduces flexibility.
  - *Cost vs. Capability* — Make ties up capital; Buy accesses specialized capability.
  - *Risk vs. Reward* — Buy introduces supplier risk; Make absorbs operational risk.
- **TCO Framework:** Compare purchase price + ordering + holding + stockout + quality + transportation cost for both options. (2)

**(b) [7 marks] — EOQ Numerical Solution**

**Step 1:** Compute EOQ.

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 2{,}500}{150}}$$

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{60{,}000{,}000}{150}} = \sqrt{400{,}000} = 632.46 \approx 632\ \text{units}$$

**Step 2:** Total cost at EOQ.

$$TC_{EOQ} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 2{,}500 \times 150}$$

$$TC_{EOQ} = \sqrt{9 \times 10^9} = ₹94{,}868\ /\ \text{year}$$

**Step 3:** Total cost at Q = 1,000.

$$TC_{1000} = \frac{12{,}000}{1{,}000} \times 2{,}500 + \frac{1{,}000}{2} \times 150$$

$$TC_{1000} = 12 \times 2{,}500 + 500 \times 150 = 30{,}000 + 75{,}000 = ₹1{,}05{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Extra cost.

$$Extra\ cost = 1{,}05{,}000 - 94{,}868 = ₹10{,}132\ /\ \text{year}$$

**Conclusion:** Ordering in lots of 1,000 costs ₹10,132 more annually than ordering at the EOQ of 632 units — illustrating the **lot-sizing trade-off**. **(7 marks)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Defining trade-offs as "choices" only** — Examiner expects *conflicting objectives with mathematical or logical tension*. No tension = no trade-off = 0 marks.
> 2. **Applying Square Root Law with wrong variable** — Many students write $SS_{central} = SS_{decent} \times \sqrt{n}$ (inverted). The correct formula divides by $\sqrt{n}$. This is a *one-mark killer*.
> 3. **Forgetting units in EOQ numericals** — Always state "units/year", "₹/year", etc.
> 4. **Failing to state assumptions** in Square Root Law derivation (normal distribution, independence, identical variance) — Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 5. **Discussing only one side of the trade-off** — e.g., "centralization reduces inventory" without acknowledging "but increases lead time and transportation cost". Both sides are mandatory.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Trade-off** = compromise between conflicting performance objectives; *both sides must always be stated*.
- [x] **Centralization** uses Square Root Law → safety stock reduction by factor $\sqrt{n}$.
- [x] **Decentralization** improves responsiveness at higher facility cost.
- [x] **Push** = forecast-driven, higher inventory, lower lead-time risk.
- [x] **Pull** = demand-driven, lower inventory, higher lead-time risk.
- [x] **Push-Pull Boundary** is set at the *decoupling point*.
- [x] **Make vs. Buy** uses **TCO** framework, not just unit price.
- [x] **Lean vs. Agile** = Efficiency vs. Responsiveness. Resolution: **Leagile**.
- [x] **Standardization vs. Customization** resolved via **Postponement**.
- [x] **EOQ** = $\sqrt{2DS/H}$. Total cost at EOQ = $\sqrt{2DSH}$.
- [x] **Inventory vs. Transportation** is a *continuous* trade-off — total logistics cost curves.
- [x] **Lot-size trade-off** = setup/ordering cost vs. holding cost — solved by EOQ.
- [x] **Hybrid networks** dominate real-world SCM (e.g., Amazon's hub-and-spoke).
- [x] **Risk pooling** is the mathematical foundation of centralization economics.
- [x] **Always** mention the *resolution strategy* after stating a trade-off in your answer — this is what separates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tailored Transportation, Sourcing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tailored Transportation, Sourcing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Tailored Transportation & Sourcing

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Relevance:** Module 4 builds on the logistics network design (Module 3) and pushes the student into *operational decision-making*. Board questions in this area frequently test (a) the ability to **segment transportation strategies** and (b) **quantify sourcing decisions** using weighted scoring and total-cost-of-ownership (TCO). Memorise the Tailored Transportation Grid and the 7-step Strategic Sourcing framework — these are **guaranteed 7-markers**.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Tailored Transportation — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tailored Transportation (also called Tailored Logistics):** A logistics strategy in which the firm designs a **different transportation, inventory, and facility configuration for each product–customer segment** so that the *logistics cost* of serving that segment is minimised while still meeting the *service-level requirement* of the customer. It is the **per-segment customisation** of the supply chain to match product characteristics (value, density, perishability, hazard level) with customer demand patterns (response time, lot size, variety).

**KTU Board-Exam Terminology (must be reproduced verbatim):**
"Matching the *logistics capabilities* (inventory, transportation, information, sourcing, pricing) to the *product characteristics* and *customer segmentation* requirements to achieve the desired *responsive capability* at the *lowest possible cost*."

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Tailored Transportation

Imagine a **multi-cuisine restaurant kitchen**. You do not cook a ₹50 tiffin, a ₹500 biryani and a ₹5,000 kebab platter on the same burner using the same chef. The tiffin uses mass stoves and steel vessels (low cost, high volume), the biryani uses a dedicated tandoor (moderate cost), and the kebab platter uses imported wood, sous-vide baths, and a personal chef (high responsiveness, low volume). **Each dish gets a tailored cooking pipeline.** That is exactly what Tailored Transportation does for products moving through a supply chain — a Fast-Moving Consumer Good (FMCG) gets dense, slow, high-utilisation trucks, while a premium smartphone gets expedited air-freight with GPS-tracked containers.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Dimensions of Tailored Transportation:**
> 1. **Product Characteristics** → value, density, substitutability, perishability, hazard, handling.
> 2. **Customer Requirements** → response time, variety, availability, service level, order size.
> 3. **Logistics Capabilities** → inventory, transportation, facilities, information, sourcing, pricing.

---

### 1.3 Sourcing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sourcing:** The set of activities involved in **identifying, evaluating, qualifying, selecting, contracting, and continuously managing** suppliers that provide the goods, services, or raw materials required by a firm to fulfil its strategic, operational, and financial objectives. Strategic sourcing extends this to a **data-driven, category-by-category procurement process** aimed at lowering Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), mitigating supply risk, and aligning supplier capabilities with the firm's long-term competitive strategy.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Sourcing

Think of **arranging the wedding of your younger sister**. You will not book the cheapest caterer you find on Google. You will (i) decide what kind of wedding you want (make-vs-buy: do you cook at home or hire a caterer?), (ii) list 4–5 caterers, (iii) compare them on **taste, cost, reliability, capacity, reputation** (weighted scoring), (iv) negotiate and lock a contract, and (v) keep monitoring them till the wedding. **That entire process is Sourcing.** The "make-vs-buy" decision is whether the family cooks or hires a caterer. The "supplier selection" is choosing between caterers. The "contract management" is the running coordination till D-day.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Sourcing Vocabulary (Frequently tested in 2-mark KTU questions):**
> - **Make-or-Buy Decision:** Produce in-house vs. procure from external supplier.
> - **Single Sourcing:** One supplier for a part → high risk, low cost.
> - **Dual / Multiple Sourcing:** 2 or more suppliers → lower risk, higher coordination cost.
> - **Strategic Sourcing:** A 7-step data-driven procurement methodology.
> - **Global / Off-shoring Sourcing:** Procuring from suppliers located in foreign countries.
> - **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):** Purchase price + logistics + inventory + quality + risk + administration cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tailored Transportation Grid (Chopra–Meindl Framework)

The Chopra & Meindl textbook visualises tailored logistics on a **2 × 2 Grid**:

| | **Low Demand Uncertainty** | **High Demand Uncertainty** |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Product Value / Low Margin** | **Lean Logistics** — Efficient, low-cost, slow transport (rail/sea), centralised inventory | **Risk-Pooling / Hybrid** — Pooled inventory at regional DCs |
| **High Product Value / High Margin** | **Hybrid** — Mix of centralised + regional warehouses, fast replenishment | **Agile / Responsive Logistics** — Fast transport (air/express), decentralised stock |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Mapping (Memorise this for KTU Board):**
> - **FMCG (low value, low uncertainty)** → Lean: full truckload, 3PL warehousing, JIT replenishment.
> - **Mobile phones (high value, moderate uncertainty)** → Hybrid: bonded warehouses + air freight for launches.
> - **Fresh seafood (low value, high uncertainty)** → Hybrid-Risk Pooling: cold-chain consolidation hubs.
> - **Aerospace spares (high value, high uncertainty)** → Agile: 24×7 air-courier, AOG (Aircraft-on-Ground) priority lane.

### 2.2 Transportation Modes — Comparative Metrics

| Mode | Cost / Unit | Speed (door-to-door) | Best Use Case | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Air** | **Highest** | Fastest (1–3 days) | High-value, perishable, emergency | Capacity, weather-sensitive |
| **Express / Courier** | High | Very fast (1–2 days) | B2C e-commerce, AOG spares | Weight-based pricing penalty |
| **Road (LTL)** | Moderate | Moderate (2–7 days) | Short–medium haul, FMCG | Driver shortage, road taxes |
| **Road (FTL)** | Low per unit | Moderate | High-volume short haul | Return-load problem |
| **Rail** | Low | Slow (5–15 days) | Bulk commodities, long haul | Last-mile feeder cost |
| **Sea (FCL)** | Lowest | Slowest (20–45 days) | Global bulk, non-perishable | Port dwell, demurrage |
| **Pipeline** | Very low | Continuous | Petroleum, gas, slurry | Fixed route, very high CAPEX |
| **Intermodal** | Moderate | Moderate | Long-haul cost+carbon optimisation | Coordination complexity |

### 2.3 Transportation Decision Variables (KTU Favourite)

When selecting a mode, balance:

$$\text{Transportation Cost} = f(\text{Tariff}, \text{Lead Time Penalty}, \text{Inventory-in-Transit}, \text{Loss/Damage}, \text{Carbon Cost})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lead-Time Penalty Formula (Examiner's Favourite):**
> $$\text{Lead-Time Penalty} = \lambda \times \bar{D} \times L$$
> where $\lambda$ = lost-sale cost per unit per day, $\bar{D}$ = average daily demand, $L$ = transit time in days. Faster modes **reduce** this penalty but **increase** tariff.

---

## 2.4 Sourcing — The 7-Step Strategic Sourcing Process

1. **Profile the Category** → Understand internal spend, demand pattern, supplier market.
2. **Understand the Supply Market** → Supplier landscape, bargaining power (Porter's 5 Forces).
3. **Develop Sourcing Strategy** → Single vs multi, global vs local, make vs buy.
4. **Identify & Qualify Suppliers** → RFI (Request for Information) + RFQ (Request for Quotation).
5. **Select Supplier(s)** → Weighted scoring, TCO analysis, negotiation.
6. **Negotiate & Contract** → SLAs, payment terms, exit clauses, IP clauses.
7. **Implement, Monitor & Re-evaluate** → KPIs, supplier scorecards, quarterly business reviews.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside table cells to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables | Unit | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weighted Supplier Score** | $\displaystyle S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \, r_{ij}$ | $S_i$ = score of supplier $i$; $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$; $r_{ij}$ = rating (1–10) | Score (unitless) | Supplier selection (7-marker) |
| 2 | **Total Cost of Ownership** | $\displaystyle TCO = P + L + I_c + Q + R + A$ | $P$ = purchase price; $L$ = logistics; $I_c$ = inventory carrying; $Q$ = quality/defect; $R$ = risk; $A$ = admin | ₹ / unit | Make-vs-Buy, sourcing decision |
| 3 | **Inventory Carrying Cost** | $\displaystyle I_c = h \times \frac{Q}{2} \times C$ | $h$ = holding rate (fraction); $Q$ = order qty; $C$ = unit cost | ₹ / period | EOQ, TCO sub-component |
| 4 | **Lead-Time Penalty** | $\displaystyle \text{LTP} = \lambda \times \bar{D} \times L$ | $\lambda$ = stockout penalty ₹/unit/day; $\bar{D}$ = daily demand; $L$ = lead time | ₹ / order | Mode selection |
| 5 | **Freight Cost per Unit (FOB basis)** | $\displaystyle C_u = \frac{\text{Total Freight}}{\text{Units Shipped}}$ | $C_u$ = freight per unit | ₹ / unit | Mode comparison |
| 6 | **Supplier Risk Score** | $\displaystyle R_s = \sum w_k \times \text{Risk}_k$ | $w_k$ = weight; $\text{Risk}_k$ = 1–10 score | Score | Risk-adjusted sourcing |
| 7 | **Cost of Single Sourcing Risk** | $\displaystyle \text{CSSR} = P(\text{Disruption}) \times \text{Lost Margin}$ | $P$ = probability; lost margin = demand × margin | ₹ / year | Make-vs-Multi decision |
| 8 | **Reorder Point with Lead Time** | $\displaystyle ROP = \bar{D} \times L + z \times \sigma_D \times \sqrt{L}$ | $z$ = service-level z-score; $\sigma_D$ = std dev of demand | Units | Sourcing + Inventory link |
| 9 | **Weighted Cost (Make vs Buy)** | $\displaystyle W_{mb} = \alpha \, C_m + (1-\alpha) C_b + \beta R$ | $\alpha$ = reliability weight; $C_m, C_b$ = make/buy costs; $R$ = risk cost | ₹ / unit | Strategic sourcing |
| 10 | **On-Time Delivery (OTD) %** | $\displaystyle \text{OTD} = \frac{\text{Orders Delivered On-Time}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100$ | — | % | Supplier scorecard KPI |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility of the Sourcing & Tailored Transport System

| Industry | Tailored Transport Application | Sourcing Strategy Used |
|---|---|---|
| **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)** | Tiered: Air + Express for Prime; LTL for bulk; cross-docking for non-Prime | Multi-source regional + global, dual-sourcing for critical SKUs |
| **Pharma (vaccines)** | Cold-chain air with temperature loggers | Single-source with dual-manufacturing redundancy |
| **Automotive (Toyota, Tata)** | Milk-run, FTL, just-in-sequence | Multi-sourcing with keiretsu / TCO-based annual contracts |
| **Aerospace (Boeing, HAL)** | AOG Air-courier for spares | Single-source for FAA-certified parts, dual-source for non-critical |
| **Apparel (Zara, H&M)** | Truck + sea hybrid, postponement | Near-shore + global mix based on season |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Mandate:** All numerical values, weights, and scores used below are self-generated teaching examples — clearly labelled as such. No external company data is fabricated.

---

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — Weighted Supplier Selection (14-Marker Style)

**Problem Statement (KTU Practice Question):**

A B.Tech project company in Kerala needs to select a supplier for **printed circuit boards (PCBs)**. The procurement team has shortlisted three suppliers — **Supplier A, B, C** — and evaluates them on **four criteria**:

| Criterion | Weight $w_j$ |
|---|---|
| Price | 0.30 |
| Quality (PPM defect) | 0.35 |
| On-Time Delivery | 0.20 |
| Technical Support | 0.15 |

Each supplier is rated on a scale of **1 to 10** (10 = best).

| Supplier | Price | Quality | OTD | Tech Support |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Supplier A** | 8 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| **Supplier B** | 9 | 7 | 9 | 7 |
| **Supplier C** | 7 | 8 | 8 | 9 |

**Required:** Calculate the **weighted composite score** for each supplier using the formula $S_i = \sum w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ and **rank them**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Verify that weights sum to 1 (normalisation check).**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{4} w_j = 0.30 + 0.35 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute the weighted score for Supplier A.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= w_{\text{Price}} \cdot r_{A,\text{Price}} \;+\; w_{\text{Quality}} \cdot r_{A,\text{Quality}} \;+\; w_{\text{OTD}} \cdot r_{A,\text{OTD}} \;+\; w_{\text{Tech}} \cdot r_{A,\text{Tech}} \\[4pt]
S_A &= (0.30)(8) \;+\; (0.35)(9) \;+\; (0.20)(7) \;+\; (0.15)(8) \\[4pt]
S_A &= 2.40 \;+\; 3.15 \;+\; 1.40 \;+\; 1.20 \\[4pt]
S_A &= 8.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted score for Supplier B.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.30)(9) \;+\; (0.35)(7) \;+\; (0.20)(9) \;+\; (0.15)(7) \\[4pt]
S_B &= 2.70 \;+\; 2.45 \;+\; 1.80 \;+\; 1.05 \\[4pt]
S_B &= 8.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the weighted score for Supplier C.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.30)(7) \;+\; (0.35)(8) \;+\; (0.20)(8) \;+\; (0.15)(9) \\[4pt]
S_C &= 2.10 \;+\; 2.80 \;+\; 1.60 \;+\; 1.35 \\[4pt]
S_C &= 7.85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rank the suppliers.**

$$S_A = 8.15 \;>\; S_B = 8.00 \;>\; S_C = 7.85$$

$$\boxed{\text{Ranking: Supplier A (1st)} \;>\; \text{Supplier B (2nd)} \;>\; \text{Supplier C (3rd)}}$$

**Step 6 — Recommendation.**

> Supplier **A** is selected. Even though Supplier B is cheaper (rating 9 vs 8) and faster (9 vs 7), the **35% weight on Quality** and **15% weight on Tech Support** tilt the decision toward Supplier A, which excels in both. This illustrates the principle that **sourcing decisions are not about the cheapest price, but about the best Total Value.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — Make-or-Buy Decision with TCO

**Problem Statement:**

A TVS-like manufacturing firm needs a component used in its motor. Two options:

- **Make in-house:** Fixed setup cost ₹ 5,00,000 per year + variable cost ₹ 600 per unit. Capacity = 12,000 units/year.
- **Buy from external supplier:** Quoted price = ₹ 850 per unit. No fixed cost.

**Required:** Determine the **break-even volume** and recommend at $D = 8{,}000$ units/year.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the cost equations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{make}}(Q) &= F \;+\; v_m \cdot Q \\[4pt]
C_{\text{buy}}(Q) &= P_b \cdot Q
\end{aligned}
$$

where $F = 5{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $v_m = 600$ ₹/unit, $P_b = 850$ ₹/unit.

**Step 2 — Find break-even volume $Q^*$ where $C_{\text{make}} = C_{\text{buy}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F + v_m \, Q^* &= P_b \, Q^* \\[4pt]
F &= (P_b - v_m) \, Q^* \\[4pt]
Q^* &= \frac{F}{P_b - v_m} \\[4pt]
Q^* &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{850 - 600} \\[4pt]
Q^* &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{250} \\[4pt]
Q^* &= 2{,}000 \;\text{units/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare costs at $D = 8{,}000$ units.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{make}}(8000) &= 5{,}00{,}000 + (600)(8000) = 5{,}00{,}000 + 48{,}00{,}000 = 53{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹} \\[4pt]
C_{\text{buy}}(8000) &= (850)(8000) = 68{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Recommendation.**

$$\text{Savings from Make} = 68{,}00{,}000 - 53{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹/year}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Recommendation: MAKE in-house. Volume (8000) > Break-even (2000).}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world extension:** A board-answer should add qualitative factors: **strategic control, IP protection, capacity utilisation, supplier reliability, future volume forecasts**. Pure cost analysis alone is incomplete.

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem 3 — Mode Selection Using Lead-Time Penalty

**Problem Statement:**

A firm must ship **5,000 units** from Cochin to Delhi. Each unit's **value = ₹ 1,200**, **lost-sale penalty $\lambda$ = ₹ 50 per unit per day**. Two mode options:

| Mode | Tariff (₹/unit) | Lead Time (days) | Damage % |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Truck (FTL)** | 30 | 5 | 2% |
| **Air** | 110 | 1 | 0.5% |

**Required:** Compute the **total landed cost per unit** (tariff + lead-time penalty + damage cost) and recommend.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute lead-time penalty per unit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LTP}_{\text{truck}} &= \lambda \cdot L = 50 \times 5 = 250 \;\text{₹/unit} \\[4pt]
\text{LTP}_{\text{air}} &= 50 \times 1 = 50 \;\text{₹/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute expected damage cost per unit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Damage}_{\text{truck}} &= 0.02 \times 1200 = 24 \;\text{₹/unit} \\[4pt]
\text{Damage}_{\text{air}} &= 0.005 \times 1200 = 6 \;\text{₹/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total landed cost per unit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{truck}} &= 30 + 250 + 24 = 304 \;\text{₹/unit} \\[4pt]
C_{\text{air}} &= 110 + 50 + 6 = 166 \;\text{₹/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total shipment cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Truck Total} &= 304 \times 5000 = 15{,}20{,}000 \;\text{₹} \\[4pt]
\text{Air Total} &= 166 \times 5000 = 8{,}30{,}000 \;\text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation.**

$$\boxed{\text{Air mode saves } 15{,}20{,}000 - 8{,}30{,}000 = 6{,}90{,}000 \;\text{₹ (45.4\% lower)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lesson:** Naively, "truck is cheaper than air" — but when **lead-time penalty and damage cost** are included, **air wins decisively**. This is the *entire point* of Tailored Transportation: the cheapest mode on paper is not always the cheapest in total cost.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sourcing Decisions

| Decision Variable | Make (In-house) | Buy (Outsource) | Single-Source | Multi-Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost structure** | High fixed, low variable | Low fixed, high variable | Low admin | Higher admin |
| **Capacity flexibility** | Low (cap. constraint) | High (scalable) | Low | High |
| **Risk exposure** | Low supplier risk | High supplier risk | Very high disruption | Spread out |
| **IP / Confidentiality** | High control | Low control | Medium | Low |
| **Strategic fit** | Core competencies | Non-core activities | Unique / patented items | Commodity items |
| **KTU example** | Engine assembly | IT services | Aerospace turbine blade | FMCG packaging |

---
