## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following worked examples are calibrated to the **difficulty level of KTU University Exam (ESE) numerical questions**. Each step is fully expanded; no algebraic jump is skipped.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Weighted Composite Supplier Score

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):**
A procurement manager in Kerala needs to evaluate three suppliers — *Alpha Industries*, *Beta Corp*, and *Gamma Traders* — for a critical machined component. The evaluation criteria, their weights, and the raw scores (on a scale of 1 to 10) are given below:

| Criterion | Weight (W) | Alpha Score | Beta Score | Gamma Score |
|-----------|:----------:|:-----------:|:----------:|:-----------:|
| Price Competitiveness | 0.30 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Quality (Defect Rate) | 0.25 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| On-Time Delivery | 0.20 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| Technical Capability | 0.15 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Responsiveness | 0.10 | 9 | 7 | 8 |

**Step 1 — Verify Weight Normalization**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{5} W_j = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

The weights sum to **1.00**, confirming a valid normalized weight vector.

**Step 2 — Compute Composite Score for Supplier Alpha Industries**

$$
S_{Alpha} = (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 9)
$$

$$
S_{Alpha} = 2.40 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 0.90 = 8.15
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Composite Score for Beta Corp**

$$
S_{Beta} = (0.30 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7)
$$

$$
S_{Beta} = 2.10 + 2.00 + 1.80 + 1.35 + 0.70 = 7.95
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Composite Score for Gamma Traders**

$$
S_{Gamma} = (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_{Gamma} = 2.70 + 1.75 + 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.80 = 7.90
$$

**Step 5 — Final Ranking**

$$
S_{Alpha} = 8.15 \;\; > \;\; S_{Beta} = 7.95 \;\; > \;\; S_{Gamma} = 7.90
$$

**Conclusion:** **Alpha Industries** is the highest-ranked supplier under this weighted model. Although Beta scored higher on Delivery and Technical Capability, Alpha's superior Price and Quality scores drove the composite result.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Defect Rate and PPM Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A supplier delivered 50 batches of an electronic component to a Kochi-based EMS firm. Each batch contained 1,000 units. The Quality Control team rejected 3 entire batches and found 150 additional defective units across the remaining batches. Calculate:
1. The **Rejection Rate (DR)** in percentage.
2. The **Defect Rate (PPM)** for the accepted portion.
3. The **On-Time Delivery Critical Ratio (CR)** if the supplier delivered on the promised date for 42 of the 50 batches.

**Given Data:**
- Total batches = **50**, Total units = $50 \times 1000 = 50{,}000$
- Fully rejected batches = **3** → Rejected units = $3 \times 1000 = 3{,}000$
- Additional defectives in accepted batches = **150**
- Total defectives = $3{,}000 + 150 = 3{,}150$
- On-time deliveries = **42**

**Step 1 — Overall Defect Rate (Including Rejected Batches)**

$$
DR_{overall} = \dfrac{Q_{defective}}{Q_{total}} \times 100 = \dfrac{3{,}150}{50{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
DR_{overall} = 0.063 \times 100 = 6.30 \%
$$

**Step 2 — Parts Per Million (PPM) on Accepted Batches**

The accepted batches = $50 - 3 = 47$ batches → $47{,}000$ units.

$$
PPM = \dfrac{150}{47{,}000} \times 10^6 = 3{,}191.49 \; ppm
$$

**Step 3 — Critical Ratio (On-Time Delivery)**

$$
CR = \dfrac{D_{on\text{-}time}}{D_{total}} \times 100 = \dfrac{42}{50} \times 100 = 84 \%
$$

**Interpretation:**
- The supplier's overall defect rate is **6.30%**, which is unacceptable for high-reliability electronics (typical threshold: **< 1%**).
- The accepted-batch PPM of **3,191 ppm** exceeds the Six Sigma target of **3.4 ppm** by a factor of nearly 1,000×.
- An OTD of **84%** signals a logistics reliability gap. A corrective action plan (CAPA) is mandated before the next PO is released.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to subtract the rejected batches when computing the *accepted-batch* PPM. Always read the wording carefully — "defect rate in accepted lots" ≠ "overall defect rate."

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Comparison

**Problem Statement:**
Compare two suppliers for a ₹10,000 per unit component over a 1,000-unit annual order. Use the data below:

| Cost Element | Supplier X (₹) | Supplier Y (₹) |
|--------------|---------------:|---------------:|
| Purchase Price (per unit) | 10,000 | 9,500 |
| Inbound Freight (per unit) | 200 | 400 |
| Inspection Cost (per unit) | 150 | 300 |
| Rework / Warranty (per unit) | 100 | 500 |
| Downtime Penalty (per unit) | 50 | 250 |
| Salvage Value (per unit, end-of-life) | 300 | 100 |

**Step 1 — TCO Formula Per Unit**

$$
TCO = P_p + C_{freight} + C_{inspect} + C_{rework} + C_{downtime} - V_{salvage}
$$

**Step 2 — TCO for Supplier X**

$$
TCO_X = 10{,}000 + 200 + 150 + 100 + 50 - 300 = 10{,}200 \; \text{per unit}
$$

$$
\text{Annual } TCO_X = 10{,}200 \times 1{,}000 = \; \text{₹} 1{,}02{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — TCO for Supplier Y**

$$
TCO_Y = 9{,}500 + 400 + 300 + 500 + 250 - 100 = 10{,}850 \; \text{per unit}
$$

$$
\text{Annual } TCO_Y = 10{,}850 \times 1{,}000 = \; \text{₹} 1{,}08{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Comparative Insight**

$$
\Delta TCO = TCO_Y - TCO_X = 10{,}850 - 10{,}200 = 650 \; \text{per unit}
$$

**Conclusion:** Although Supplier Y quotes a lower unit price (₹9,500 vs ₹10,000), Supplier X delivers a **₹650 per unit TCO advantage**, translating to **₹6,50,000 annual savings**. This is the central message of TCO analysis — *price ≠ cost*.

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Define Supplier Scoring. List any four criteria commonly used for evaluating suppliers in a manufacturing supply chain.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Supplier Scoring* is a quantitative technique used in procurement and supply chain management to objectively rate and rank suppliers against a set of pre-defined evaluation criteria. It transforms subjective vendor judgments into comparable numerical scores, enabling transparent and defensible sourcing decisions.

Four commonly used evaluation criteria:
1. **Price / Cost Competitiveness** — Quoted price and total cost of ownership.
2. **Quality Performance** — Incoming defect rate, PPM, quality certifications.
3. **Delivery Performance** — On-time delivery rate, lead-time adherence.
4. **Capacity & Capability** — Production volume, technological infrastructure, financial stability.

*(Mark Allocation: Definition = 1M, List with brief explanation = 2M.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Differentiate between Supplier Evaluation and Supplier Assessment with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Supplier Evaluation | Supplier Assessment |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Timing** | Conducted *before* awarding the contract (pre-qualification) | Conducted *after* the contract is in place (post-award monitoring) |
| **Purpose** | To *select* the right supplier | To *monitor and improve* the ongoing supplier |
| **Data Source** | RFP responses, capability audits, site visits | Delivery logs, defect reports, scorecards |
| **Frequency** | One-time / event-driven (new sourcing) | Continuous / periodic (monthly/quarterly) |
| **Example** | Auditing an Indian CNC vendor's factory before issuing a ₹50 lakh PO | Reviewing the same vendor's monthly defect-rate and OTD after 6 months of supply |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When solving weighted-score numericals, students commonly lose **3–4 marks** by:
> (a) forgetting to normalize weights to 1.0,
> (b) writing the formula without substituting values, and
> (c) failing to interpret the *ranking result*. Always conclude with a clear decision statement.

---

#### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Weighted Point Model** for supplier evaluation. List any **five evaluation criteria** with their relative weights as typically used in an automobile industry scorecard.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Bengaluru-based OEM is evaluating three vendors (V1, V2, V3) for a plastic injection-molded component. Using the data below, calculate the **composite weighted score** for each vendor and rank them.

| Criterion | Weight | V1 | V2 | V3 |
|-----------|:------:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Quality | 0.35 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Delivery | 0.25 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Cost | 0.20 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| Service | 0.10 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Innovation | 0.10 | 6 | 8 | 9 |

---

##### Model Solution A(a) — Weighted Point Model Explanation

The **Weighted Point Model (WPM)** is the most widely adopted mathematical technique for supplier selection and scoring in KTU-referenced SCM literature.

**Core Steps:**
1. **Identify Criteria** — Brainstorm all relevant evaluation parameters with a cross-functional team (Procurement, Quality, Engineering, Finance).
2. **Assign Weights** — Allocate percentage importance to each criterion such that total = **100%**.
3. **Define Scale** — Adopt a uniform rating scale (commonly 1 to 5 or 1 to 10) to ensure comparability.
4. **Score Each Vendor** — Rate every supplier on every criterion.
5. **Compute Composite Score** — $S_i = \sum (W_j \times R_{ij})$
6. **Rank and Decide** — Highest composite score = preferred supplier.

**Five Automobile-Industry Criteria and Typical Weights:**

| # | Criterion | Typical Weight |
|---|-----------|--------------:|
| 1 | Quality (Q) — Incoming defect rate, QMS certifications | **30–40%** |
| 2 | Cost (C) — Piece price, TCO, payment terms | **20–25%** |
| 3 | Delivery (D) — On-time, lead-time reliability | **20–25%** |
| 4 | Technical Capability (T) — Process maturity, R&D | **10–15%** |
| 5 | Responsiveness & Service (R) — Communication, flexibility | **5–10%** |

**Advantages of WPM:** Quantitative, transparent, multi-criteria, easy to defend.
**Limitations:** Subjectivity in weight assignment; requires good data hygiene.

---

##### Model Solution A(b) — Numerical Computation

**Step 1 — Verify Weights Sum to 1.00**

$$
\sum W = 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 1.00 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Score for Vendor V1**

$$
S_{V1} = (0.35 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 6)
$$

$$
S_{V1} = 2.80 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 0.80 + 0.60 = 7.85
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Score for Vendor V2**

$$
S_{V2} = (0.35 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_{V2} = 3.15 + 1.75 + 1.60 + 0.90 + 0.80 = 8.20
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Score for Vendor V3**

$$
S_{V3} = (0.35 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 9)
$$

$$
S_{V3} = 2.45 + 2.00 + 1.80 + 0.70 + 0.90 = 7.85
$$

**Step 5 — Ranking and Decision**

$$
S_{V2} = 8.20 > S_{V1} = 7.85 = S_{V3} = 7.85
$$

**Conclusion:** **Vendor V2 is ranked first** and should be awarded the contract. Vendors V1 and V3 are tied for second place; a tie-breaker (e.g., capacity audit or commercial negotiation) is recommended before finalizing the secondary supplier.

---

#### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **concept of Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** in supplier assessment. Why is TCO considered a superior metric compared to the simple purchase price?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Pune-based automotive supplier has supplied 80,000 parts in a quarter. The standard cost per part is ₹250, but the supplier's actual average price was ₹265. Compute the **Price Variance (PV)** and comment on its significance. Additionally, if the supplier's OTD was **88%** and the average defect rate was **1.2%**, compute the **Critical Ratio (CR)** and the **Defect Rate (DR)**, and recommend whether the vendor should be retained, developed, or removed.

---

##### Model Solution B(a) — TCO Concept

**Definition:**
*Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)* is a comprehensive cost-evaluation framework that captures **every cost** associated with procuring, using, and disposing of a product over its entire life-cycle with a supplier — beyond the initial purchase price.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
TCO = P_p + C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{qual} + C_{disposal} - V_{salvage}
$$

Where:
- $P_p$ = Purchase price
- $C_{acq}$ = Acquisition costs (freight, duties, customs)
- $C_{op}$ = Operating costs (training, process adjustments)
- $C_{maint}$ = Maintenance and warranty costs
- $C_{qual}$ = Quality costs (inspection, rework, scrap)
- $C_{disposal}$ = End-of-life disposal cost
- $V_{salvage}$ = Salvage or buy-back value

**Why TCO > Purchase Price:**

1. **Hidden Costs Revealed** — A ₹100 unit price may balloon to ₹300 after adding freight, inspection, and rework.
2. **Decision Quality** — Avoids the trap of choosing the *cheapest* vendor who delivers poor quality and inflates downstream costs.
3. **Supplier Behavior** — Signals to suppliers that *value*, not just *price*, will determine the award.
4. **Strategic Alignment** — Enables apples-to-apples comparison across geographically diverse suppliers (domestic vs. imported).
5. **Risk Quantification** — Captures the cost of supply disruption, late delivery penalties, and warranty claims.

**Real-World Example:** A car manufacturer discovered that switching from the lowest-priced steel vendor (₹52/kg) to a 5%-higher vendor (₹54.6/kg) saved **₹3.2 crore annually** by eliminating defect-related rework and downtime.

---

##### Model Solution B(b) — Variance and Performance Index Calculation

**Step 1 — Price Variance (PV) Calculation**

$$
P_{standard} = \text{₹}250, \quad P_{actual} = \text{₹}265, \quad Q_{total} = 80{,}000
$$

$$
PV_{percent} = \dfrac{265 - 250}{250} \times 100 = \dfrac{15}{250} \times 100 = 6.00 \%
$$

$$
PV_{absolute} = (265 - 250) \times 80{,}000 = 15 \times 80{,}000 = \; \text{₹} 12{,}00{,}000
$$

The supplier's pricing exceeds the standard by **6%**, resulting in a **₹12 lakh overrun** for the quarter.

**Step 2 — Critical Ratio (On-Time Delivery)**

$$
CR = \dfrac{D_{on\text{-}time}}{D_{total}} \times 100 = \dfrac{88}{100} \times 100 = 88 \%
$$

**Step 3 — Defect Rate (DR)**

$$
DR = \dfrac{Q_{defective}}{Q_{total}} \times 100 = 1.20 \%
$$

**Step 4 — Composite Performance Index (SPI)**

Assigning sub-weights (Price = 0.30, Delivery = 0.35, Quality = 0.35) and scoring each metric against an industry benchmark (Price: 100% - 6% = 94/100, Delivery: 88/100, Quality: 100% - 1.2% = 98.8/100):

$$
SPI = (0.30 \times 94) + (0.35 \times 88) + (0.35 \times 98.8)
$$

$$
SPI = 28.20 + 30.80 + 34.58 = 93.58 \;\; \text{out of 100}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision Recommendation**

| Threshold | Classification |
|-----------|---------------|
| SPI ≥ 95 | Preferred Partner |
| 80 ≤ SPI < 95 | Develop and Monitor |
| SPI < 80 | Conditional / Phase Out |

**Conclusion:** With an SPI of **93.58**, the supplier falls in the **"Develop and Monitor"** category. Specifically:
- The **6% price overrun** must be addressed through commercial renegotiation.
- The **88% OTD** must improve to ≥95% within one quarter; otherwise, dual-sourcing is activated.
- The **1.2% defect rate** is borderline acceptable but should be reduced to <0.5% for safety-critical automotive parts.

The supplier is **retained**, but a **formal Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA)** plan is mandated, with re-assessment in **90 days**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read This Carefully

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Supplier Scoring Questions:**
> 1. **Weight Normalization Error:** Forgetting to normalize weights to 1.00 (or 100%). Partial deduction of **2 marks** if normalization step is missing.
> 2. **Formula Without Substitution:** Writing only the formula and skipping the numerical substitution loses **3 marks**. Always substitute values explicitly.
> 3. **Interpretation Omission:** Calculating the score without ranking or making a decision loses **1–2 marks**. Always conclude with a *decision statement*.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing up percentages and decimals (e.g., using 30 instead of 0.30 for weight). Always declare units explicitly.
> 5. **PPM Calculation Trap:** Computing PPM across *all* batches instead of only the *accepted* batches.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section as your final-day revision sheet for KTU ESE Module 4.**

- **Supplier Scoring** = Quantitative ranking of suppliers using weighted criteria.
- **Supplier Assessment** = Continuous monitoring of supplier performance against benchmarks.
- The **Weighted Point Model** is the most-tested numerical method in KTU papers.
- **Five Core Criteria:** Quality, Cost, Delivery, Capacity, Service (Q-C-D-C-S).
- **Critical Ratio (CR)** measures delivery reliability — formula: $\dfrac{\text{on-time}}{\text{total}} \times 100$.
- **Defect Rate (DR)** measures incoming quality — formula: $\dfrac{\text{defective}}{\text{total}} \times 100$.
- **PPM** is the automotive-grade quality metric — formula: $\dfrac{\text{defective}}{\text{total}} \times 10^6$.
- **TCO > Purchase Price** — always emphasize hidden costs in long-answer questions.
- **RPN (FMEA)** quantifies supplier risk: $RPN = S \times O \times D$.
- **Scorecard Threshold:** ≥85% = Preferred, 70–85% = Develop, <70% = Conditional/Exit.
- **Weight Vector MUST sum to 1.00** — verify before any calculation.
- **Composite Score Formula:** $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \times R_{ij}$.
- **Supplier Categorization Quadrant:** Performance (x-axis) vs. Risk (y-axis) → 4 zones.
- **Continuous improvement** is the heart of SRM — assessment is a *loop*, not a *one-shot*.
- **Process Steps:** Identify Criteria → Assign Weights → Define Scale → Collect Data → Compute Score → Rank → Feedback.
- **Differentiator:** Evaluation = pre-contract; Assessment = post-contract monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Partnerships and Trust
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Building Partnerships and Trust # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for Supply Chain Management, **Supply Chain Partnerships** are defined as **long-term, collaborative, inter-organizational relationships** between two or more independent firms in a supply chain who willingly invest resources (time, money, information, and effort) to jointly achieve competitive advantages that neither could achieve alone. **Trust** in a supply chain context is defined as the **willingness of a party to be vulnerable** to the actions of another party, based on the expectation that the other will perform a particular action important to the trustor, irrespective of the ability to monitor or control that other party (formalized as the **Vulnerability-Based Trust Model**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > Building Partnerships and Trust is positioned as a foundational *soft competency* in SCM. It links directly to **Course Outcome CO3** (Analyze supply chain relationships and partnership models) and is tested frequently under **Bloom's Level L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyze)**. --- ## 1.2 The Three Core Dimensions of Trust (3D Trust Model) Trust in any buyer-supplier partnership is rarely one-dimensional. It must be built on three pillars: | Dimension | Academic Definition | Simple Intuition | |---|---|---| | **Integrity** | The belief that the partner adheres to a set of principles considered acceptable by the trustor. | *"Will they keep their word?"* | | **Competence** | The belief that the partner has the technical skills, knowledge, and ability to perform effectively. | *"Can they actually do what they promise?"* | | **Benevolence** | The belief that the partner has intentions and motives beneficial to the trustor beyond profit motive. | *"Do they genuinely care about my success?"* | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The *Marriage vs. Dating* Framework Imagine the supply chain as a community of people forming relationships: - **Dating** = *Transactional Relationship*. You meet a vendor on IndiaMART for one cheap order. You do not share secrets, you do not invest, and you can leave tomorrow with no penalty. **No trust is required because the risk is tiny.** - **Engagement** = *Operational Partnership*. You commit to a single, repeated project. Some trust exists, but it is thin and tested by a contract. - **Marriage** = *Strategic Partnership*. Like a real marriage, you share bank accounts (joint cost data), dreams (long-term roadmaps), and weather storms (demand shocks) together. Trust here is **deep, multi-dimensional, and asset-rich**. > [!NOTE] > **The KTU Exam Trick:** Examiners often describe a scenario where a company "shares forecasts and joint cost data with a single supplier for 5 years." Recognize this immediately as a **Strategic Partnership** built on **High Trust**, not just a contractual agreement. --- ## 1.4 The Trust-Commitment Theory (TCM) The widely cited **Morgan and Hunt (1994) Commitment-Trust Theory** in the KTU syllabus states that relationship marketing success requires both: $$ \text{Relationship Marketing Success} = f(\text{Commitment}, \text{Trust}) $$ Where *commitment* is an enduring desire to maintain a valued relationship, and *trust* is the confidence in the partner's reliability and integrity. They are mutually reinforcing — **trust builds commitment, and commitment deepens trust**. > [!TIP] > Memorize this equation. It is a **favourite 3-mark definition question** in KTU University Examinations. --- ## 1.5 Why Trust is the *Oil* of Supply Chains Without trust, every transaction becomes a **defensive contract** requiring lawyers, audits, inventory buffers, and verification overhead. With trust, these *transaction costs collapse*, enabling: - **Voluntary information sharing** (demand forecasts, capacity plans) - **Joint problem solving** (kaizen with suppliers) - **Reduced opportunistic behavior** (no cheating on quality or price) - **Faster cycle times** (no re-verification at every step) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Building Blocks of Partnership Success A robust supply chain partnership rests on five **operational building blocks**. Memorize these as the KTU-mandated pillars: 1. **Shared Vision and Goals** — Both firms must agree on the *strategic direction* of the relationship (e.g., becoming the lowest-cost producer in Asia). 2. **Mutual Benefit / Win-Win Economics** — The relationship must be profitable for both parties. A partnership that benefits only the buyer is a disguised form of *exploitation* and will eventually collapse. 3. **Information Sharing** — Open exchange of Point-of-Sale (POS) data, forecasts, inventory levels, and capacity plans. The classic example is **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)**. 4. **Joint Planning and Decision Making** — Collaborative forecasting (**CPFR** — Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment) and joint product design. 5. **Shared Risk and Reward** — Both parties bear the consequences of failure and share the upside of success (e.g., gain-sharing on cost reduction). --- ## 2.2 Trust-Building Mechanisms — A 5-Step Process Building trust is not accidental; it follows a structured progression. The KTU syllabus recommends the following **5-Step Trust Development Model**: | Step | Mechanism | What the Firm Does | Resulting Trust Asset | |---|---|---|---| | **Step 1** | *Deterrence-Based Trust* | Builds legal contracts, NDAs, penalties | Compliance-based, shallow | | **Step 2** | *Knowledge-Based Trust* | Shares technical information, forecasts | Competence-trust grows | | **Step 3** | *Identification-Based Trust* | Empathizes with partner goals, shares culture | Benevolence-trust emerges | | **Step 4** | *Calculative Trust* | Demonstrates the cost of breaking trust is higher than maintaining it | Economic-rational trust | | **Step 5** | *High-Performance Trust* | Achieves results repeatedly under stress | Deep, self-sustaining trust | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Logic:** Notice the *upward spiral*. You cannot jump directly from Step 1 to Step 5. Trust, like muscle, must be built through **consistent, repeated small wins** over time. This is why KTU answers must show *progression*, not a single leap. --- ## 2.3 The Partnership Spectrum — From Arms-Length to Strategic Not all partnerships are equal. The KTU module classifies partnerships into a continuum based on **Integration Level** and **Trust Depth**: $$ \text{Integration Level} \propto \frac{\text{Trust} \times \text{Information Sharing}}{\text{Distance and Adversarial Posture}} $$ The four standard types, ranked from weakest to strongest: 1. **Arms-Length Transactional** — Short-term, price-driven, no loyalty (e.g., commodity trading on spot markets). 2. **Ongoing Contractual** — Long-term contract, but adversarial; relationship is mediated entirely by lawyers (e.g., typical FMCG vendor). 3. **Collaborative Operational** — Joint projects, shared information, multiple suppliers (e.g., Toyota's keiretsu lite). 4. **Strategic Alliance / Partnership** — Deep integration, joint investment, few partners, mutual dependence (e.g., Apple-Foxconn, P&G-Walmart Consumer Insights). --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!NOTE] > These are the **only quantitative formulas** you will encounter in this topic. Memorize them with their boundary conditions. | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | Relationship Marketing Success | $S = f(C, T)$ | $S$ = Success, $C$ = Commitment, $T$ = Trust | Valid for buyer-supplier dyads | | Trust Capital Equation | $T_c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot t_i$ | $T_c$ = Trust Capital, $w_i$ = weight of dimension $i$, $t_i$ = score on dimension $i$ | $\sum w_i = 1$, $t_i \in [0,10]$ | | Transaction Cost Reduction | $\Delta TC = TC_{arm} - TC_{partner}$ | $\Delta TC$ = Cost saved by partnership vs. arms-length, in $\text{INR/order}$ | Always $\geq 0$ if partnership is functioning | | Total Cost of Partnership (TCP) | $TCP = C_{setup} + C_{coord} + C_{disruption}$ | All terms in $\text{INR/year}$ | Includes *both* monetary and *opportunity* costs | | Partner Selection Score | $P_s = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \alpha_j \cdot s_j$ | $\alpha_j$ = weight of criterion $j$, $s_j$ = partner score on $j$, $\sum \alpha_j = 1$ | Used in RFP evaluation | | Trust Threshold Equation | $\text{Information Shared} \leq k \cdot T_c$ | $k$ = proportionality constant, $T_c$ = Trust Capital | Information shared is bounded by trust level | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Note:** Notice the use of `\vert` (instead of `\vert` shown in source) and `\in` for set membership to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In **manufacturing and operations**, building partnerships and trust has measurable engineering consequences: - **Bullwhip Effect Reduction** — Trust enables **forecast sharing**, which dampens demand variability upstream (true for any engineering supply chain, from semiconductors to automotive). - **Lower Safety Stock** — High-trust partnerships rely on reliable supplier delivery, allowing smaller buffers. - **Faster New Product Introduction (NPI)** — Trusting suppliers integrate into design phases earlier (**Design for X** activities). - **Higher First-Pass Quality** — Trust eliminates the need for *incoming inspection* in mature partnerships (e.g., Toyota's zero-defect supplier policy). --- ## 2.6 The Opposite of Trust — *Opportunism* When trust is absent, partners engage in **opportunism** — self-interest seeking with guile. KTU examiners love this contrast. There are two types: - **Active Opportunism** — Cheating, lying, shirking (e.g., supplier delivers sub-standard steel but charges premium grade price). - **Passive Opportunism** — Refusing to share information, withholding innovation, slow response. The expected cost of opportunism for a buyer is: $$ E[\text{Loss}_{opportunism}] = p_{cheat} \times V_{stolen} + (1 - p_{cheat}) \times 0 $$ Where $p_{cheat}$ is the probability the partner cheats, and $V_{stolen}$ is the value at risk. **Trust raises the cost of cheating, thus lowering $p_{cheat}$.** # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Trust Capital Score for Partner Evaluation Suppose a KTU exam asks you to evaluate three potential suppliers (A, B, C) for a strategic partnership using the **Trust Capital Equation**. The three dimensions of trust are scored on a **0 to 10 scale**, with weights assigned by management. **Given Data:** | Supplier | Integrity Score $t_1$ | Competence Score $t_2$ | Benevolence Score $t_3$ | |---|---|---|---| | A | 8 | 9 | 6 | | B | 7 | 7 | 9 | | C | 9 | 6 | 7 | **Weights assigned by management:** - $w_1 = 0.4$ (Integrity — most critical for new product IP) - $w_2 = 0.35$ (Competence — quality is non-negotiable) - $w_3 = 0.25$ (Benevolence — moderately important) --- **Step 1:** Verify the weights sum to unity. $$ \begin{aligned} \sum w_i &= w_1 + w_2 + w_3 \\ &= 0.40 + 0.35 + 0.25 \\ &= 1.00 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ *Logic Check:* The weights must sum to 1, otherwise the Trust Capital score becomes dimensionally inconsistent. This is the *normalization constraint*. --- **Step 2:** Compute the Trust Capital for Supplier A. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{c,A} &= w_1 \cdot t_{1,A} + w_2 \cdot t_{2,A} + w_3 \cdot t_{3,A} \\ &= (0.40)(8) + (0.35)(9) + (0.25)(6) \\ &= 3.20 + 3.15 + 1.50 \\ &= 7.85 \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Each weight multiplies the respective dimension score. The result is the weighted trust capital on a 0–10 scale. --- **Step 3:** Compute the Trust Capital for Supplier B. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{c,B} &= w_1 \cdot t_{1,B} + w_2 \cdot t_{2,B} + w_3 \cdot t_{3,B} \\ &= (0.40)(7) + (0.35)(7) + (0.25)(9) \\ &= 2.80 + 2.45 + 2.25 \\ &= 7.50 \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Supplier B's benevolence is highest, but it does not fully compensate for lower integrity. --- **Step 4:** Compute the Trust Capital for Supplier C. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{c,C} &= w_1 \cdot t_{1,C} + w_2 \cdot t_{2,C} + w_3 \cdot t_{3,C} \\ &= (0.40)(9) + (0.35)(6) + (0.25)(7) \\ &= 3.60 + 2.10 + 1.75 \\ &= 7.45 \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Supplier C leads on integrity but underperforms on competence. --- **Step 5:** Rank the suppliers by Trust Capital. $$ T_{c,A} = 7.85 \;>\; T_{c,B} = 7.50 \;>\; T_{c,C} = 7.45 $$ **Decision:** Supplier A is the most trustworthy partner under the given strategic weighting. However, the **margin is thin (only 0.35 over Supplier B)**, so a real-world manager would also examine cost, capacity, and risk — not trust alone. --- **Step 6:** Sensitivity Analysis — what if management re-weights? If benevolence becomes the most important (e.g., for a long-term co-development deal), the weights become $w_1 = 0.25$, $w_2 = 0.25$, $w_3 = 0.50$: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{c,A}^{new} &= (0.25)(8) + (0.25)(9) + (0.50)(6) = 2.00 + 2.25 + 3.00 = 7.25 \\ T_{c,B}^{new} &= (0.25)(7) + (0.25)(7) + (0.50)(9) = 1.75 + 1.75 + 4.50 = 8.00 \\ T_{c,C}^{new} &= (0.25)(9) + (0.25)(6) + (0.50)(7) = 2.25 + 1.50 + 3.50 = 7.25 \end{aligned} $$ **Revised Decision:** Supplier B now wins decisively (8.00 vs. 7.25). *This is a critical KTU lesson: **the choice of weights drives the choice of partner**.* --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Trust vs. Risk Trade-Off The **Trust-Risk Matrix** is a 2x2 framework for deciding how much information to share with a partner: | | **Low Trust** | **High Trust** | |---|---|---| | **Low Risk** (low stakes if leaked) | Share freely — *cost of caution exceeds benefit* | Share freely — *both gain* | | **High Risk** (sensitive IP, prices) | Share almost nothing — *legal protection only* | Share strategically with NDAs — *maximum value* | The optimal information-sharing policy is: $$ \text{Share} = \begin{cases} \text{Full} & \text{if } T_c \geq T_{\text{threshold}} \text{ AND } R_{\text{leak}} \leq R_{\text{tolerance}} \\ \text{Partial (NDA-bound)} & \text{if } T_c \geq T_{\text{threshold}} \text{ AND } R_{\text{leak}} > R_{\text{tolerance}} \\ \text{None} & \text{if } T_c < T_{\text{threshold}} \end{cases} $$ Where: - $T_c$ = Current trust capital of the partner - $T_{\text{threshold}}$ = Minimum trust level required to share - $R_{\text{leak}}$ = Risk that shared information is leaked/misused - $R_{\text{tolerance}}$ = Maximum acceptable risk --- ## 3.3 The Trust Escalation Function (Optional Advanced) Many SCM researchers model trust as a **sigmoid function of cumulative interactions**: $$ T(t) = \frac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}} $$ Where: - $T(t)$ = Trust level at time $t$ - $L$ = Maximum trust ceiling (typically 10 in our scale) - $k$ = Steepness of trust growth (depends on partner reliability) - $t_0$ = Inflection point (time at which trust reaches half its ceiling) > [!TIP] > **KTU Insight:** This S-curve explains why partnerships often show **slow initial trust growth**, then rapid acceleration after a few successful projects, then plateau. Examiners love drawing this curve and asking "identify the phases." --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Trust and Transaction Cost A manufacturing firm considers two sourcing options for a precision component: - **Option X (Arms-Length):** Order spot-market price, no trust investment. Transaction cost per order = **INR 50,000** (verification, inspection, contracting). - **Option Y (Strategic Partnership):** Sign 3-year agreement, share forecasts, joint quality team. Fixed setup cost = **INR 30,00,000**, but recurring transaction cost drops to **INR 5,000** per order. **Compute the breakeven number of orders.** $$ \begin{aligned} TC_X &= 50{,}000 \cdot n \\ TC_Y &= 30{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}000 \cdot n \\ \text{Breakeven:} \quad TC_X &= TC_Y \\ 50{,}000n &= 30{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}000n \\ 45{,}000n &= 30{,}00{,}000 \\ n &= \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{45{,}000} \\ n &= 66.67 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** Beyond **67 orders**, the strategic partnership becomes cheaper. *This is the classic KTU logic for proving the financial case for trust-based partnerships.* # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Trust-Building Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Stage 1: Initial Encounter] --> stageB[Stage 2: Contractual Setup
Deterrence Based Trust] stageB --> stageC[Stage 3: Information Exchange
Knowledge Based Trust] stageC --> stageD[Stage 4: Joint Problem Solving
Identification Based Trust] stageD --> stageE[Stage 5: Shared Vision and Risk
High Performance Trust] stageE --> stageF[Stage 6: Strategic Partnership
Sustained Mutual Benefit] style startA fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style stageB fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style stageC fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style stageD fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c style stageE fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style stageF fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f ``` *Figure 4.1: The Six-Stage Trust Development Pathway. Each color shift represents a deeper tier of trust, moving from legal compliance to strategic mutual commitment.* --- ## 4.2 The 3D Trust Dimension Model ```mermaid flowchart LR subCore[TRUST] integ[INTEGRITY
Honesty and
Ethical Conduct] comp[COMPETENCE
Technical Skill
and Capability] bene[BENEVOLENCE
Genuine Care
for Partner Welfare] integ --- subCore comp --- subCore bene --- subCore style subCore fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px style integ fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style comp fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style bene fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f ``` *Figure 4.2: The Three Pillars of Trust. Removing any one pillar collapses the entire trust structure, much like removing a leg from a tripod.* --- ## 4.3 Partnership Spectrum — Block-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR n1[LEVEL 1
Arms Length
Spot Market] n2[LEVEL 2
Contractual
Long Term Contract] n3[LEVEL 3
Collaborative
Shared Forecasting] n4[LEVEL 4
Strategic Alliance
Joint Investment] n1 -->|Trust grows| n2 n2 -->|Trust grows| n3 n3 -->|Trust grows| n4 info1[Information Sharing: 0 percent] -.-> n1 info2[Information Sharing: 20 percent] -.-> n2 info3[Information Sharing: 60 percent] -.-> n3 info4[Information Sharing: 95 percent] -.-> n4 style n1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style n2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style n3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style n4 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c ``` *Figure 4.3: Trust-Information Coupling. As trust deepens, the volume of operational information shared with the partner grows non-linearly.* --- ## 4.4 The Trust-Commitment Relationship Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD a[Successful Transaction] --> b[Increased Trust T] b --> c[Increased Commitment C] c --> d[Greater Investment in Relationship] d --> e[Improved Joint Performance] e --> a f[Relationship Failure] --> g[Trust Erosion] g --> h[Withdrawal of Commitment] h --> i[Reduced Information Sharing] i --> j[Further Performance Decline] j --> f style a fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style b fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style c fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style d fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style e fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style f fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style g fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style h fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style i fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style j fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c ``` *Figure 4.4: The Virtuous Cycle of Trust vs. the Vicious Cycle of Distrust. KTU examiners use this to test whether students understand that trust is *self-reinforcing* in both directions.* --- ## 4.5 Partner Selection Decision Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD sA[Step A: Define Partnership Objectives] sB[Step B: Identify Evaluation Criteria] sC[Step C: Assign Weights to Criteria] sD[Step D: Score Each Candidate Supplier] sE[Step E: Calculate Trust Capital and Total Score] sF[Step F: Validate with Reference Checks] sG[Step G: Final Selection and Contract Drafting] sA --> sB sB --> sC sC --> sD sD --> sE sE --> sF sF --> sG style sA fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b style sB fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b style sC fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b style sD fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b style sE fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b style sF fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b style sG fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 x 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]` **Q: Define "Supply Chain Partnership" and list any THREE characteristics of a successful partnership.** **Model Answer:** A **Supply Chain Partnership** is a long-term, collaborative agreement between independent firms in a supply chain where parties willingly invest resources to achieve mutual competitive advantage that they could not attain independently. The three characteristics of a successful partnership are: 1. **Mutual Trust and Commitment** — Both parties consistently honour promises, share risks, and avoid opportunism. 2. **Open Information Sharing** — Firms exchange demand forecasts, inventory data, and capacity plans without fear of exploitation. 3. **Joint Planning and Shared Benefits** — Collaborative decision-making (e.g., CPFR) with a clear gain-sharing mechanism for cost savings and innovations. > [!TIP] > KTU accepts any three reasonable characteristics from: *shared vision, win-win economics, joint problem solving, long-term orientation, transparent communication*. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Similar Pattern]` **Q: Explain the THREE dimensions of trust in a supply chain partnership.** **Model Answer:** According to the widely accepted **3D Trust Model**, trust in supply chain partnerships has three dimensions: 1. **Integrity** — The belief that the partner will adhere to acceptable moral and ethical principles. It answers the question: *"Will they keep their word and act honestly?"* 2. **Competence** — The belief that the partner possesses the technical skills, knowledge, and operational capability to deliver. It answers the question: *"Can they actually perform as promised?"* 3. **Benevolence** — The belief that the partner genuinely cares about the trustor's welfare beyond profit motives. It answers the question: *"Do they have my best interests at heart?"* A partnership is considered robust only when **all three dimensions score high**; deficiency in any one dimension erodes the entire trust foundation. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) (1 x 14 = 14 Marks) ### CHOICE A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted Pattern]` **Question A:** **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss in detail the **FIVE STEPS through which trust is built** in a supply chain partnership. Use an example from the Indian automotive industry (Tata, Maruti, or Mahindra) to illustrate your answer. **(b)** [7 Marks] What is **opportunism**? Explain its two types with suitable examples. Also describe **THREE mechanisms** that firms use to safeguard against opportunism in partnerships. --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks Trust-building follows a structured five-step progression: **Step 1 — Deterrence-Based Trust:** Initially, trust is purely legalistic. Contracts, NDAs, and penalty clauses ensure compliance. *Example:* Tata Motors signs a strict supply contract with new axle suppliers, with INR 10 lakh penalties for late delivery. **Step 2 — Knowledge-Based Trust:** As the relationship matures, both parties exchange technical information (drawings, forecasts, capacity). The buyer gains confidence in the supplier's *competence*. *Example:* Tata and Bosch share engine calibration data to co-develop BS6 emission-compliant parts. **Step 3 — Identification-Based Trust:** Partners begin to empathize with each other's strategic goals, embedding themselves into each other's culture. *Example:* Maruti Suzuki's vendor parks — suppliers literally live on Maruti's campus, building identity-based trust. **Step 4 — Calculative Trust:** Each party calculates that the cost of breaking the relationship (lost future business, reputational damage) outweighs any short-term gain from cheating. *Example:* Mahindra's long-term steel supplier refuses to cheat even when steel prices spike globally because losing the Mahindra contract would mean bankruptcy. **Step 5 — High-Performance Trust:** Partners achieve reliable, superior outcomes even under stress. They voluntarily assist each other during crises. *Example:* During the 2020 COVID lockdown, Tata's tier-1 suppliers voluntarily delivered safety masks and sanitisers to Tata's workers — without any contractual obligation. *Closing remark for full marks:* Trust, like muscle, must be exercised consistently; it cannot be bought through a single contract. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Opportunism** is defined as *self-interest seeking with guile* — the act of exploiting a partnership for unfair gain. **Two Types of Opportunism:** 1. **Active Opportunism** — Deliberate cheating: lying about quality, falsifying certifications, short-shipping orders, overcharging. *Example:* A Bengaluru-based electronics supplier ships refurbished chips labelled as "new original" to a mobile phone OEM. 2. **Passive Opportunism** — Withholding effort, slow response, refusing to share innovation, foot-dragging on joint projects. *Example:* A logistics partner delays implementing a GPS tracking system despite a contractual commitment, citing "technical issues." **Three Safeguard Mechanisms:** 1. **Comprehensive Contracts with Penalty Clauses** — Clearly defined SLAs (Service Level Agreements) with measurable penalties. Deterrence-based but necessary at the start. 2. **Performance Monitoring Systems** — Use of *Vendor Scorecards* tracking quality, delivery, and responsiveness over time. Early detection of opportunism. 3. **Reputation and Trust Networks** — In tight industrial clusters (e.g., Pune Auto Cluster, Chennai Auto Hub), suppliers who cheat are quickly blacklisted across the entire cluster. This social network is a powerful deterrent. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > Students often lose marks in part (a) by listing only the *names* of the five steps without explaining the **logical progression** — how each step builds upon the previous. You must show **causal flow**, not a bullet list. In part (b), students forget to give **Indian-context examples**; foreign examples like "Enron" are accepted but Indian examples often earn bonus marks. --- ### CHOICE B: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted Pattern]` **Question B:** **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Morgan and Hunt Commitment-Trust Theory**. Discuss its **FOUR key constructs** and how they interact in a supply chain partnership. **(b)** [7 Marks] A buyer is evaluating THREE suppliers (X, Y, Z) for a strategic partnership. The scores on Integrity ($t_1$), Competence ($t_2$), and Benevolence ($t_3$) on a 10-point scale are given below. The weights are $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$. Calculate the Trust Capital of each supplier using $T_c = \sum w_i t_i$ and recommend the best partner. Comment on how the ranking changes if benevolence is weighted at 0.5 instead. | Supplier | Integrity $t_1$ | Competence $t_2$ | Benevolence $t_3$ | |---|---|---|---| | X | 9 | 8 | 5 | | Y | 7 | 9 | 8 | | Z | 8 | 7 | 9 | --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks The **Morgan and Hunt Commitment-Trust Theory of Relationship Marketing (1994)** is one of the foundational theories in supply chain partnership literature. **Core Premise:** Relationship marketing success requires two foundational variables — **Commitment** (an enduring desire to maintain a valued relationship) and **Trust** (confidence in a partner's reliability and integrity). **FOUR Key Constructs and their Interactions:** 1. **Relationship Termination Costs** — Switching costs and lost investments that make exiting the relationship expensive. *Interaction:* Higher termination costs → higher commitment. 2. **Relationship Benefits** — Profits, learning, and competitive advantages gained from the partnership. *Interaction:* Higher benefits → higher commitment AND higher trust. 3. **Shared Values** — Common beliefs about appropriate behavior, ethics, and goals. *Interaction:* Shared values → higher trust, which → higher commitment. 4. **Communication** — The formal and informal sharing of meaningful, timely information. *Interaction:* Open communication → higher trust, which → reduces opportunism and increases commitment. **The Mediation Effect:** Trust and commitment mediate between these antecedent factors (termination costs, benefits, shared values, communication) and key outcomes like *acquiescence*, *propensity to leave*, *cooperation*, and *functional conflict*. In a supply chain context, when a buyer trusts the supplier: - They are more willing to share sensitive forecast data (no fear of opportunism). - They commit to long-term contracts (reducing transaction costs). - Joint innovation and cost-reduction initiatives flourish. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Case Weights (Original):** $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$. Verify $\sum w_i = 1.0 \;\checkmark$ **Step 1 — Trust Capital for Supplier X:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{c,X} &= (0.5)(9) + (0.3)(8) + (0.2)(5) \\ &= 4.50 + 2.40 + 1.00 \\ &= 7.90 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Trust Capital for Supplier Y:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{c,Y} &= (0.5)(7) + (0.3)(9) + (0.2)(8) \\ &= 3.50 + 2.70 + 1.60 \\ &= 7.80 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Trust Capital for Supplier Z:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{c,Z} &= (0.5)(8) + (0.3)(7) + (0.2)(9) \\ &= 4.00 + 2.10 + 1.80 \\ &= 7.90 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Ranking Under Original Weights:** $$ T_{c,X} = T_{c,Z} = 7.90 > T_{c,Y} = 7.80 $$ Suppliers X and Z are tied at 7.90. The buyer should select either X or Z based on *non-trust factors* like price, capacity, or geographic location. **Step 5 — Re-computation With $w_3 = 0.5$ (benevolence dominant):** New weights: $w_1 = 0.25$, $w_2 = 0.25$, $w_3 = 0.50$. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{c,X}^{new} &= (0.25)(9) + (0.25)(8) + (0.50)(5) = 2.25 + 2.00 + 2.50 = 6.75 \\ T_{c,Y}^{new} &= (0.25)(7) + (0.25)(9) + (0.50)(8) = 1.75 + 2.25 + 4.00 = 8.00 \\ T_{c,Z}^{new} &= (0.25)(8) + (0.25)(7) + (0.50)(9) = 2.00 + 1.75 + 4.50 = 8.25 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Revised Ranking:** $$ T_{c,Z}^{new} = 8.25 > T_{c,Y}^{new} = 8.00 > T_{c,X}^{new} = 6.75 $$ **Conclusion:** | Weights | Winner | Runner-Up | Insight | |---|---|---|---| | Integrity-dominant ($w_1 = 0.5$) | X & Z (tied at 7.90) | Y at 7.80 | Integrity and benevolence trade off | | Benevolence-dominant ($w_3 = 0.5$) | Z (8.25) | Y (8.00) | Supplier Z's high benevolence dominates | **Recommendation:** If the strategic partnership requires *ethical long-term co-development* (where benevolence is critical), choose **Supplier Z**. If the priority is *reliable quality at the lowest integrity risk*, choose **Supplier X**. Supplier Y is consistently a strong second choice but rarely the first — a classic *"safe but never spectacular"* profile. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > Students commonly forget to **verify that weights sum to 1**. KTU deducts **1 mark** for failing to normalize. Also, students often skip Step 4 (the comparative ranking) and jump to conclusions. Always show **all three computations in parallel** before ranking — examiners award partial credit for the procedure. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - **Definition Core:** Partnership = long-term, resource-investing, collaborative relationship. Trust = willingness to be vulnerable based on positive expectations. - **Three Pillars of Trust:** *Integrity, Competence, Benevolence*. Memorize in this exact order. - **Five-Step Trust Progression:** *Deterrence → Knowledge → Identification → Calculative → High-Performance*. Show progression, not a list. - **Four Partnership Levels:** *Arms-Length → Contractual → Collaborative → Strategic*. The deeper the trust, the higher the level. - **Five Building Blocks:** *Shared Vision, Mutual Benefit, Information Sharing, Joint Planning, Shared Risk and Reward*. - **Morgan-Hunt Equation:** $S = f(C, T)$ — Relationship Marketing Success is a function of Commitment and Trust. - **Trust Capital Formula:** $T_c = \sum w_i t_i$, where $\sum w_i = 1$ (always normalize weights). - **Opportunism Types:** *Active (deliberate cheating) and Passive (withholding effort)*. - **Real-World Anchor:** Always cite an **Indian example** (Tata, Maruti, Mahindra, Flipkart, UPI) — examiners reward cultural relevance. - **Sigmoid Insight:** Trust grows like an S-curve — slow, then fast, then plateau. - **Trust-Risk Trade-Off:** Never share more information than the partner's trust capital can protect. The Trust Threshold Equation is your safety check. - **Breakeven Logic:** Partnerships beat arms-length only beyond a critical volume — show the breakeven calculation. - **Examiner's Trap:** Forgetting to define terms before using them in formulas costs 1–2 marks per question. *Define first, derive second.* - **Word Economy:** A 7-mark answer should be ~250–350 words with at least one labeled diagram or equation. Don't exceed 400 words. - **Cross-Link:** Connect this topic to **Module 3 (Sourcing Decisions)** and **Module 5 (Supply Chain Coordination)** — KTU rewards integrated answers. - **Final Tip:** When asked about *building* partnerships, the answer must always include the word **"trust"** explicitly. It is the connective tissue of every SCM partnership model.

topics.title: Continuous Replenishment and Vendor-Managed Inventories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Continuous Replenishment and Vendor-Managed Inventories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Continuous Replenishment (CRP)

**Continuous Replenishment Process (CRP)** is a supply chain partnership strategy in which a retailer and a supplier (manufacturer/vendor) agree to maintain agreed-upon inventory levels of the supplier's products at the retailer's location (store or warehouse) through a system of automatic, ongoing replenishment, eliminating the need for discrete, periodic purchase orders.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard)**
> Continuous Replenishment is an inventory management technique in which a retailer's information system is integrated with the supplier's information system to enable the automatic triggering of replenishment orders whenever stock falls to a pre-defined level. The supplier is responsible for ensuring that the agreed service levels and inventory targets are continuously met at the retailer's premises.

In essence, instead of the retailer placing a purchase order every week or every month, the **act of selling a product (Point-of-Sale / POS data)** automatically generates a replenishment signal to the vendor. The vendor then ships the required quantity to maintain inventory within an agreed upper and lower band.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Never-Empty Refrigerator"

Imagine you have a smart refrigerator at home. Every time you take out a milk carton, a tiny sensor detects the drop in level and **automatically places an order** with the dairy. The dairy delivers a fresh carton the very next morning so that you never run out. You never go to a store, never place an order, and never think about inventory. The **dairy is continuously replenishing** your fridge based on your consumption.

In this analogy:
- **You (the customer/retailer)** = consumer of inventory
- **The smart fridge** = automated information system (POS + EDI)
- **The sensor detecting milk level** = reorder point trigger
- **The dairy** = the supplier/vendor
- **The auto-delivery** = continuous replenishment

The key idea is that **information replaces inventory** — by sharing real-time sales data, the supplier can deliver "just-in-time" without the retailer ever having to formally order.

> [!NOTE] **CRP is a foundational building block of ECR (Efficient Consumer Response)** and is a stepping stone toward the more advanced **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** model discussed in Section 1.2.

---

## 1.2 Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)

**Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** is a supply chain initiative in which the **supplier (vendor) takes full responsibility for managing the inventory levels of its products at the customer's (retailer's) location**. Under VMI, the supplier decides *when* to reorder, *how much* to ship, and *where* to position stock — decisions that traditionally belonged to the retailer.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard)**
> Vendor-Managed Inventory is a business model in which the supplier creates orders for its customers based on demand information (POS data, forecasts) shared by the customer. The vendor is accountable for maintaining specified inventory targets and service levels at the customer's location, transforming the traditional buyer-driven replenishment model into a supplier-driven one.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Personal Dietician"

Think about how a **personal dietician** manages your kitchen. You don't decide what to buy, when to buy, or how much to stock. The dietician visits your kitchen, checks what is consumed, and refills your pantry with the exact items and quantities you need. You just consume. The dietician has the **expertise, the data, and the authority** to manage your stock.

In the VMI analogy:
- **You** = the retailer (customer of the supplier)
- **The dietician** = the vendor/supplier
- **The pantry** = the retailer's shelf/warehouse
- **The dietician's visit & refill** = the vendor's replenishment decision
- **Your consumption** = POS/sales data fed to the vendor

The **transfer of decision-making authority** from buyer to seller is what fundamentally distinguishes VMI from traditional purchasing and even from CRP.

---

## 1.3 Quick Comparison: CRP vs. VMI

| Dimension | Continuous Replenishment (CRP) | Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI) |
|---|---|---|
| **Who decides to reorder?** | Trigger is automatic, but order logic is often pre-set jointly | The vendor decides fully |
| **Who determines order quantity?** | Retailer's system (rule-based) or jointly agreed | The vendor |
| **Data flow** | Retailer's POS → Supplier | Retailer's POS + forecasts → Supplier |
| **Decision authority** | Shared / collaborative | Transferred to vendor |
| **Risk of stockout** | Shared | Primarily on vendor |
| **Information sharing depth** | Moderate (POS data) | Deep (POS + forecasts + promotions) |
| **Level of trust required** | High | Very high |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Memory Hook:** *CRP is "automated ordering with shared decisions."* *VMI is "automated ordering with the vendor fully in charge."*

Both are forms of **Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment (CPFR)**-aligned initiatives and represent the shift from **transactional buyer-seller relationships** to **partnership-based supply chain integration**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Board-Exam Point:** The difference between CRP and VMI is *not* the presence of continuous replenishment (both have it). The difference is **who owns the decision to replenish** and **who bears the inventory risk**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Continuous Replenishment Process — Theoretical Breakdown

CRP works on the principle of **information substitution for inventory**. Let us break down the operational logic step by step:

### Step 1 — Data Capture at the Retail End
Each time a customer purchases a product, the **Point-of-Sale (POS)** system records the transaction: SKU (Stock Keeping Unit), quantity, timestamp, store ID, and price. This data is captured at the **stockkeeping unit level** — the granular SKU data is the lifeblood of CRP.

### Step 2 — Data Transmission
The POS data is transmitted, typically at the end of the day, to the retailer's **central information system**. From there, it is pushed to the supplier via **Electronic Data Interchange (EDI)**, **API calls**, or **Vendor portals**.

### Step 3 — Reorder Point Calculation
At the supplier's end, an inventory model evaluates:
- Current stock at the retailer's shelf/warehouse
- Sales velocity (units sold per day)
- Lead time for replenishment
- Safety stock requirements

A **reorder trigger** fires when the **available stock** falls to or below the **reorder point (ROP)**.

### Step 4 — Order Generation
The system automatically generates a replenishment order. The order quantity is typically calculated using the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** or a **Min–Max model**.

### Step 5 — Shipment & Replenishment
The supplier ships the product to the retailer. Upon receipt, the cycle continues.

> [!NOTE] **Why CRP Works (The "Why")**
> Traditional ordering requires the retailer to *anticipate* demand. Errors in forecasting lead to either **stockouts (lost sales)** or **overstocks (carrying costs)**. CRP shifts the model from "forecast and order" to "**sense and respond**" — actual consumption data drives replenishment. This dramatically reduces the **bullwhip effect** and the **safety stock** needed.

---

## 2.2 The Vendor-Managed Inventory Model — Theoretical Breakdown

VMI extends CRP by **transferring not just information but also decision rights** to the vendor. The operational logic is:

### Step 1 — Information Sharing Agreement
The retailer and vendor sign a **VMI partnership agreement** that specifies:
- Target inventory levels (min, max, on-hand, in-transit)
- Service level (e.g., 98% fill rate)
- Performance metrics (KPIs)
- Roles and responsibilities
- Pricing and payment terms

### Step 2 — Data Visibility
The vendor gains **real-time visibility** into:
- Daily/weekly POS data
- Inventory on-hand at each store
- Inventory in-transit
- Promotional calendars
- Forecasts (jointly developed or vendor-generated)

### Step 3 — Vendor-Side Planning
Using this data, the vendor runs its own **inventory optimization model** (often an MRP/DRP system) to determine:
- **What** to ship (SKU mix)
- **When** to ship (timing)
- **How much** to ship (lot size)

### Step 4 — Vendor-Initiated Replenishment
The vendor places the order (or simply ships) without waiting for a purchase order from the retailer. The retailer is billed based on **actual receipts**.

### Step 5 — Continuous Review
KPIs are reviewed regularly. The vendor is often **penalized for stockouts** and **rewarded for inventory efficiency**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **The "Why" Behind VMI**
> The vendor typically has **better forecasting capability** (knows market trends, seasonal patterns, upstream supply) and **better economies of scale** (consolidates shipments across many retailers). By centralizing the replenishment decision at the vendor, the **total system cost** of inventory is reduced even if the vendor's per-unit cost is higher than the retailer's naive plan.

---

## 2.3 Mathematical Foundation: The Reorder Point (ROP) Model

In both CRP and VMI, the operational core is the **reorder point calculation**. The classical model is:

$$\text{ROP} = (\bar{d} \times L) + \text{SS}$$

Where:
- $\bar{d}$ = **average daily demand** (units/day)
- $L$ = **lead time** in days
- $\text{SS}$ = **safety stock**

Safety stock, under a probabilistic demand model, is:

$$\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

Where:
- $z$ = **Z-score** corresponding to the desired service level (e.g., $z = 1.65$ for 95%, $z = 2.33$ for 99%)
- $\sigma_d$ = **standard deviation of daily demand**
- $L$ = lead time in days

Combining both:

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

### Modified ROP for VMI (with shared data)

In VMI, the vendor may have access to **demand forecasts** generated jointly with the retailer. The modified ROP becomes:

$$\text{ROP}_{\text{VMI}} = \bar{d}_{\text{forecast}} \cdot L_{\text{vendor}} + z \cdot \sigma_{d,\text{pooled}} \cdot \sqrt{L_{\text{vendor}}}$$

The key insight: when the vendor pools demand across **multiple retail locations**, the pooled standard deviation $\sigma_{d,\text{pooled}}$ is **lower** than the sum of individual $\sigma_d$'s, reducing safety stock.

> [!NOTE] **Pooled Standard Deviation (Square Root Law of Inventory)**
> For $n$ independent retail locations with identical demand variability $\sigma$:
> $$\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$
> This is why a vendor managing inventory for 50 stores can hold **far less safety stock** than 50 individual retailers each holding their own.

---

## 2.4 Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — Lot Size in CRP/VMI

The replenishment quantity in CRP/VMI is often derived from the classical **EOQ (Wilson) Model**:

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D K}{h}}$$

Where:
- $D$ = annual demand (units/year)
- $K$ = ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $h$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $Q^*$ = optimal order quantity (units)

The corresponding **Total Cost** at optimal $Q^*$ is:

$$\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{2 D K h}$$

The **cycle time** (time between orders) is:

$$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} \quad \text{(in years)} = \frac{365 \cdot Q^*}{D} \quad \text{(in days)}$$

### Production Order Quantity (POQ) for VMI

In VMI, the vendor often produces in batches rather than ordering from outside. The **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)** model is used:

$$Q_p^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D K}{h}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{p - u}}$$

Where:
- $p$ = production rate (units/day)
- $u$ = usage/demand rate (units/day)
- All other variables as in EOQ

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reorder Point (Deterministic) | $\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot L$ | $\bar{d}$ = avg daily demand (units/day); $L$ = lead time (days) |
| 2 | Reorder Point (Probabilistic) | $\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | $z$ = service-level z-score; $\sigma_d$ = std. dev. of daily demand |
| 3 | Safety Stock | $\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Same as above |
| 4 | EOQ (Wilson) | $Q^* = \sqrt{2 D K / h}$ | $D$ = annual demand; $K$ = order cost (₹); $h$ = holding cost/unit/year (₹) |
| 5 | EOQ Total Cost | $\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{2 D K h}$ | ₹/year |
| 6 | EOQ Cycle Time | $T^* = Q^* / D$ | Years (multiply by 365 for days) |
| 7 | EPQ | $Q_p^* = \sqrt{2 D K / h} \cdot \sqrt{p / (p-u)}$ | $p$ = production rate; $u$ = demand rate (both units/day) |
| 8 | Pooled Std. Dev. | $\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$ | $n$ = number of independent locations |
| 9 | Service Level → Z-score | 90% → 1.28, 95% → 1.65, 97.5% → 1.96, 99% → 2.33 | Standard normal table |
| 10 | Reorder Frequency | $N = D / Q^*$ | Orders per year |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Exam Tip (KTU):** Always state the **units** of each variable before substituting. Most valuation errors arise from unit mismatches (e.g., using weekly demand with a daily lead time).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of CRP/VMI in Engineering & Management

1. **FMCG (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods):** Walmart, P&G, and Tesco use VMI to manage billions of dollars in retail inventory.
2. **Automotive Industry:** Toyota's *Just-in-Time* (JIT) system is an extreme variant of VMI where the supplier delivers parts to the assembly line on a near-hourly basis.
3. **Pharmaceuticals:** Hospitals use CRP to ensure critical drugs are always available without manual ordering.
4. **E-commerce:** Amazon's anticipatory shipping model uses a VMI-like logic where inventory is pre-positioned based on demand predictions.
5. **Food & Beverage:** Cold-chain management for dairy, fresh produce, and frozen goods uses CRP to minimize spoilage.

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Connection:** CRP/VMI is closely linked to **Industrial Engineering** concepts — production planning, inventory control, supply chain optimization, and operations research. The mathematical models (EOQ, ROP) are direct applications of operations research techniques.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EOQ Formula (Wilson's Formula)

The EOQ model is the foundation of lot-sizing in CRP/VMI. Let us derive it rigorously.

### Setting Up the Cost Function

**Assumptions:**
1. Demand $D$ is **known and constant** (units/year).
2. Lead time $L$ is **known and constant**.
3. Order quantity $Q$ is **fixed** each cycle.
4. No stockouts are allowed.
5. Ordering cost $K$ is **fixed per order** (₹/order).
6. Holding cost $h$ is **proportional to average inventory** (₹/unit/year).

**Total Annual Cost (TC)** is the sum of:
- Annual Ordering Cost = (Number of orders per year) × (Cost per order) = $\frac{D}{Q} \cdot K$
- Annual Holding Cost = (Average inventory) × (Holding cost per unit) = $\frac{Q}{2} \cdot h$

Therefore:

$$\text{TC}(Q) = \frac{D K}{Q} + \frac{h Q}{2}$$

### Minimizing TC(Q)

Differentiate with respect to $Q$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d(\text{TC})}{dQ} = -\frac{D K}{Q^2} + \frac{h}{2} = 0$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$\frac{h}{2} = \frac{D K}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2 D K}{h}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D K}{h}}$$

### Second-Order Condition (Verification of Minimum)

$$\frac{d^2(\text{TC})}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D K}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{for all } Q > 0$$

This confirms that $Q^*$ is a **minimum** of the total cost function.

### Substituting Back to Get TC*

$$\text{TC}^* = \frac{D K}{\sqrt{2 D K / h}} + \frac{h \cdot \sqrt{2 D K / h}}{2}$$

$$\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{\frac{D K h}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{D K h}{2}} = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D K h}{2}}$$

$$\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{2 D K h}$$

### Cycle Time

Number of orders per year = $D / Q^*$. The time between consecutive orders (cycle time) is:

$$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{1}{D} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 D K}{h}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{D h}}$$

The cycle time in days is $T^*_{\text{days}} = 365 \cdot T^*$.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example 1 — EOQ for a CRP System

**Problem:** A retailer's POS data shows an annual demand of **12,000 units** for a particular SKU. The ordering cost is **₹150 per order** and the holding cost is **₹20 per unit per year**. The lead time is **6 days**. The retailer uses a CRP system with the supplier.

**Find:**
1. The optimal EOQ
2. The total annual cost
3. The cycle time (in days)
4. The reorder point

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Variables**
- $D = 12{,}000$ units/year
- $K = ₹150$ per order
- $h = ₹20$ per unit per year
- $L = 6$ days
- Average daily demand: $\bar{d} = D / 365 = 12{,}000 / 365 \approx 32.88$ units/day

**Step 2 — Compute EOQ**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D K}{h}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 150}{20}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{3{,}600{,}000}{20}} = \sqrt{180{,}000}$$

$$Q^* \approx 424.26 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Annual Cost**

$$\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{2 D K h} = \sqrt{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 150 \times 20}$$

$$\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{72{,}000{,}000} \approx ₹8{,}485.28 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Cycle Time**

$$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{424.26}{12{,}000} \approx 0.03536 \text{ years}$$

$$T^*_{\text{days}} = 0.03536 \times 365 \approx 12.91 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Reorder Point (Deterministic)**

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot L = 32.88 \times 6 \approx 197.26 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

The retailer should reorder **every ~13 days** in the quantity of **~424 units** when stock falls to **~197 units**. In a CRP setup, this calculation is done by the system automatically.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example 2 — VMI with Safety Stock

**Problem:** A vendor manages inventory for a retailer under a VMI agreement. The **average daily demand** is **50 units**, the **standard deviation of daily demand** is **10 units**, and the **lead time is 4 days**. The desired **service level is 95%**.

**Find:** The ROP and the safety stock.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Variables**
- $\bar{d} = 50$ units/day
- $\sigma_d = 10$ units
- $L = 4$ days
- $z = 1.65$ (for 95% service level)

**Step 2 — Compute Safety Stock**

$$\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.65 \times 10 \times \sqrt{4}$$

$$\text{SS} = 1.65 \times 10 \times 2 = 33 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Reorder Point**

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot L + \text{SS} = (50 \times 4) + 33 = 200 + 33 = 233 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

The vendor should initiate a replenishment order (or shipment) **when the retailer's stock falls to 233 units**. The 33 units of safety stock protects against demand variability during the 4-day lead time.

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example 3 — Pooled Inventory in Multi-Store VMI

**Problem:** A vendor supplies 25 retail stores. Each store has an **average daily demand of 20 units** and a **standard deviation of 5 units**. Each store currently holds its own safety stock with a 4-day lead time and 95% service level.

**Find:**
1. Total safety stock **without VMI** (each store self-supplies)
2. Safety stock per store **with VMI** (vendor pools demand)
3. Total safety stock **with VMI** for all 25 stores
4. Total savings

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Without VMI (Each Store Independent)**

Per store:

$$\text{SS}_{\text{per store}} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.65 \times 5 \times \sqrt{4} = 1.65 \times 5 \times 2 = 16.5 \text{ units}$$

Total across 25 stores:

$$\text{SS}_{\text{total, no VMI}} = 16.5 \times 25 = 412.5 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — With VMI (Pooled)**

When the vendor pools demand across all 25 stores, the **pooled standard deviation per day** is:

$$\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \frac{\sigma_d}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{5}{5} = 1 \text{ unit/day}$$

The pooled safety stock (per shipment cycle) is:

$$\text{SS}_{\text{VMI}} = z \cdot \sigma_{\text{pooled}} \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.65 \times 1 \times 2 = 3.3 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Total Safety Stock with VMI**

In the VMI setup, the vendor still services 25 stores, so the total inventory held (across the system) is scaled:

$$\text{SS}_{\text{total, VMI}} = 3.3 \times 25 = 82.5 \text{ units}$$

Wait — let us reconsider. The pooled inventory means the vendor can hold **one consolidated safety stock** rather than 25 distributed ones. The total system safety stock with VMI is:

$$\text{SS}_{\text{total, VMI (consolidated)}} = z \cdot \sigma_{\text{pooled}} \cdot \sqrt{L} = 3.3 \text{ units}$$

(Note: In a true pooled model, one central warehouse holds the safety stock for all stores; replenishment is dispatched to stores as needed.)

**Step 4 — Savings**

$$\text{Savings} = 412.5 - 3.3 = 409.2 \text{ units}$$

This is a **99.2% reduction** in safety stock — a powerful demonstration of the **Square Root Law of Inventory** in VMI.

> [!NOTE] **KTU Insight:** The Square Root Law is *the* most cited justification for VMI in board examinations. Memorize the formula $\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$ and the implication that centralization reduces total inventory.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: CRP vs. VMI — Decision Framework

| Decision Criterion | Choose CRP if... | Choose VMI if... |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust level between partners** | Moderate | High (vendor needs retailer's data) |
| **Vendor's forecasting ability** | Limited | Strong (vendor has market insight) |
| **Retailer's inventory management maturity** | High (retailer can set min-max rules) | Low to moderate (vendor takes over) |
| **Product complexity (SKUs)** | Low to moderate | High (vendor has SKU expertise) |
| **Demand variability** | Moderate | High (vendor can pool across locations) |
| **Lead time** | Long and stable | Variable (vendor can absorb shocks) |
| **Implementation cost** | Lower (EDI integration) | Higher (system integration, contracts) |
| **Industry example** | Small retailers with single supplier | Walmart-P&G, Amazon vendors |

---

## 3.6 Challenges and Limitations

> [!WARNING] **Critical Pitfalls to Know for KTU Exams**

1. **Information Asymmetry:** If the retailer does not share accurate POS data, the vendor's replenishment decisions will be sub-optimal. **Trust and data integrity are prerequisites.**
2. **Loss of Retailer Control:** Some retailers resist VMI because they fear losing control over their shelf space and assortment decisions.
3. **Demand Distortion:** If the vendor's forecasting model is biased, errors propagate across all retail locations.
4. **Switching Costs:** Once a VMI system is implemented, switching back to traditional ordering is operationally and financially difficult.
5. **Legal/Contractual Issues:** VMI agreements must clearly specify liability for stockouts, spoilage (perishables), and obsolescence.

---

## 3.7 Implementation Roadmap (KRTU-Style Answer Structure)

A typical **KTU 14-mark question** may ask: *"Explain the steps to implement a VMI system in a retail supply chain."*

The examiner expects a **structured 5–6 step answer**:

1. **Partner Selection & Agreement** — Identify strategic vendors, negotiate VMI contracts with clear SLAs.
2. **Information System Integration** — Establish EDI/API links for POS, inventory, and forecast data exchange.
3. **Joint Definition of Inventory Policies** — Set min, max, safety stock, and service levels collaboratively.
4. **Pilot Implementation** — Run VMI in 1–2 stores or SKUs to test and refine.
5. **Vendor-Side Replenishment Activation** — Vendor's system takes over order generation and shipping.
6. **Performance Monitoring & Continuous Improvement** — Track KPIs (fill rate, inventory turnover, stockouts) and refine.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
