# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI). How does it differ from Continuous Replenishment Process (CRP)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI) is a supply chain model in which the supplier (vendor) takes responsibility for monitoring and replenishing the inventory of its products at the customer's (retailer's) location. The vendor uses shared data (such as POS, inventory levels, and forecasts) to determine the order quantity, timing, and shipment — decisions that traditionally rested with the buyer.

**Difference (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | CRP | VMI |
|---|---|---|
| Decision rights | Shared between buyer & supplier | Transferred to vendor |
| Data flow | POS data shared | POS + forecasts + promotions shared |
| Risk | Shared | Primarily on vendor |
| Implementation | Less invasive | Requires deeper integration & trust |

> [!NOTE] **Examiner's View:** A clean definition with a small comparison table fetches full 3 marks. Avoid writing paragraphs of fluff.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the "Square Root Law of Inventory" and why is it significant in VMI?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Square Root Law of Inventory states that when demand is pooled across $n$ independent locations, the pooled standard deviation of demand is reduced by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$:
$$\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Significance in VMI (1.5 marks):**
In VMI, the vendor typically pools demand across multiple retail stores or warehouses. As a result, the required safety stock — which is proportional to demand variability — drops sharply. For example, pooling across 25 stores reduces safety stock to $1/5$ of what each store would need independently. This results in:
- Lower total inventory holding cost
- Higher inventory turnover
- Better service level with less stock

Hence, the Square Root Law is a **key driver** of cost savings in VMI implementations.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions have an **internal choice**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts (typically 7 marks each).

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the Continuous Replenishment Process (CRP) in detail. Discuss the key steps, benefits, and limitations of CRP with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retailer uses CRP with a supplier. Annual demand for a product is 9,000 units. The ordering cost is ₹200 per order, holding cost is ₹30 per unit per year, and the lead time is 8 days. The standard deviation of daily demand is 6 units and the desired service level is 97.5%.
**Compute:**
(i) The EOQ
(ii) The reorder point
(iii) The total annual inventory cost
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** CRP is a partnership between retailer and supplier wherein the retailer's POS data automatically triggers replenishment orders to maintain agreed inventory levels at the retailer's location.

**Key Steps (3 marks):**
1. **POS Data Capture:** Each sale at the retailer's checkout is recorded with SKU, quantity, and time.
2. **Data Transmission:** POS data flows to the supplier's system via EDI or web services, usually daily.
3. **Inventory Check:** The supplier's system checks the retailer's current stock against the agreed-upon minimum.
4. **Order Generation:** If stock falls to or below the reorder point, the system generates an order using EOQ or Min-Max logic.
5. **Shipment & Receipt:** The supplier ships; the retailer receives and updates inventory. The cycle repeats.

**Benefits (2 marks):**
- **Reduced stockouts** due to automatic triggers
- **Lower safety stock** requirements thanks to real-time data
- **Reduced bullwhip effect** in the supply chain
- **Lower administrative cost** (no manual purchase orders)
- Example: Procter & Gamble's CRP with Walmart

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Requires high level of trust and data sharing
- IT integration costs can be significant
- Dependent on data accuracy (garbage in, garbage out)
- Limited applicability for slow-moving items

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $D = 9{,}000$ units/year
- $K = ₹200$/order
- $h = ₹30$/unit/year
- $L = 8$ days
- $\sigma_d = 6$ units/day
- Service level 97.5% → $z = 1.96$

**Average daily demand:**

$$\bar{d} = \frac{D}{365} = \frac{9{,}000}{365} \approx 24.66 \text{ units/day}$$

**(i) EOQ (2 marks):**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D K}{h}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9{,}000 \times 200}{30}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{3{,}600{,}000}{30}} = \sqrt{120{,}000} \approx 346.41 \text{ units}$$

**(ii) Reorder Point (2 marks):**

$$\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

$$\text{ROP} = 24.66 \times 8 + 1.96 \times 6 \times \sqrt{8}$$

$$\text{ROP} = 197.26 + 1.96 \times 6 \times 2.828$$

$$\text{ROP} = 197.26 + 33.26 = 230.52 \text{ units}$$

**(iii) Total Annual Inventory Cost (3 marks):**

$$\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{2 D K h} = \sqrt{2 \times 9{,}000 \times 200 \times 30}$$

$$\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{108{,}000{,}000} = ₹10{,}392.30 \text{ per year}$$

**Final Answers:**
- EOQ ≈ **346 units**
- ROP ≈ **231 units**
- Total annual cost ≈ **₹10,392.30**

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** What is Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)? Explain its benefits, challenges, and implementation steps in a modern supply chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vendor manages inventory for 16 retail outlets under VMI. Each outlet has an average daily demand of 30 units and a standard deviation of 8 units. Lead time is 5 days and service level is 95%.
**Calculate:**
(i) The safety stock at each outlet **without VMI** (independent case)
(ii) The pooled safety stock with VMI
(iii) The total safety stock across all 16 outlets with VMI
(iv) Comment on the savings. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** VMI is a supply chain model in which the vendor is responsible for maintaining agreed-upon inventory levels of its products at the retailer's premises. The vendor uses shared sales and inventory data to decide on replenishment timing and quantities.

**Benefits (2 marks):**
- Reduced stockouts and overstocks
- Lower total inventory holding cost
- Better demand forecasting by the vendor
- Improved customer service level
- Reduced bullwhip effect
- Example: Barilla's VMI with Italian retailers (the "JMI" case)

**Challenges (2 marks):**
- High trust required between partners
- Data sharing concerns (retailer may be reluctant)
- Loss of retailer's decision autonomy
- Implementation cost (IT systems, contracts)
- Vendor bears stockout risk → financial exposure

**Implementation Steps (2 marks):**
1. **Partner selection & contract** — identify strategic vendors; agree on SLAs, KPIs, and roles.
2. **IT integration** — establish EDI/API for real-time data exchange.
3. **Joint planning** — set inventory targets, service levels, and promotional calendars.
4. **Pilot rollout** — test on 1–2 SKUs or stores; refine.
5. **Full deployment** — vendor takes over replenishment.
6. **Performance review** — track fill rate, inventory turnover, cost.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $n = 16$ outlets
- $\bar{d} = 30$ units/day per outlet
- $\sigma_d = 8$ units/day per outlet
- $L = 5$ days
- Service level 95% → $z = 1.65$

**(i) Safety Stock Per Outlet (Without VMI) (2 marks):**

$$\text{SS}_{\text{per outlet}} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.65 \times 8 \times \sqrt{5}$$

$$\text{SS}_{\text{per outlet}} = 1.65 \times 8 \times 2.236 = 29.51 \text{ units}$$

**(ii) Pooled Safety Stock (With VMI) (2 marks):**

Pooled standard deviation per day:

$$\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \frac{\sigma_d}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{16}} = \frac{8}{4} = 2 \text{ units/day}$$

$$\text{SS}_{\text{pooled}} = z \cdot \sigma_{\text{pooled}} \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.65 \times 2 \times \sqrt{5}$$

$$\text{SS}_{\text{pooled}} = 1.65 \times 2 \times 2.236 \approx 7.38 \text{ units}$$

**(iii) Total Safety Stock with VMI (1.5 marks):**

In a true consolidated VMI setup, the vendor holds **one** central safety stock for all 16 outlets:

$$\text{SS}_{\text{total, VMI}} = 7.38 \text{ units}$$

**(iv) Comment on Savings (1.5 marks):**

| Scenario | Total Safety Stock |
|---|---|
| Independent (16 outlets) | $16 \times 29.51 = 472.16$ units |
| Consolidated VMI | $7.38$ units |
| **Savings** | **464.78 units (98.4% reduction)** |

**Comment:** The Square Root Law of Inventory leads to a dramatic reduction in safety stock under VMI. The savings translate directly into lower holding cost and improved inventory turnover, demonstrating the strategic value of centralization in VMI.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in CRP/VMI Questions**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing weekly demand with daily lead time (or vice versa). Always convert to a **common time unit** (days).
> 2. **Z-Score Mismatch:** Using $z = 1.65$ for 99% service level (correct $z = 2.33$).
> 3. **Forgetting Safety Stock:** In ROP calculations, some students compute $\bar{d} \cdot L$ only and omit the $z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ term. This is a **complete mark-loss** for probabilistic ROP questions.
> 4. **Confusing EOQ with EPQ:** EOQ assumes instantaneous replenishment; EPQ allows for finite production rate. Use the right one.
> 5. **CRP ≠ VMI:** Many students define both identically. The **decision rights** is the discriminator.
> 6. **Not Showing Substitution Steps:** In numericals, the examiner wants to see **the actual numbers plugged in** and the intermediate calculations. Skipping the substitution costs 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Vague Benefits:** "Reduces cost" is too generic. Specify **which** cost (holding, ordering, stockout) and **how** (e.g., "reduces safety stock by $\sqrt{n}$ factor via pooling").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Summary — Read This Before the Exam**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **CRP** = automated, continuous replenishment triggered by POS data; decision rights are **shared**.
- ☐ **VMI** = vendor takes full responsibility for inventory at the retailer's location; decision rights are **transferred**.
- ☐ The fundamental shift in both is **information replacing inventory** and **partnership replacing transaction**.
- ☐ Both are enablers of **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment)** and **ECR (Efficient Consumer Response)**.

### Key Differences
- ☐ CRP = **Data sharing + automatic triggers**, but retailer's system or joint rules decide.
- ☐ VMI = **Data sharing + vendor decision-making** for replenishment quantity and timing.
- ☐ VMI is a **deeper form** of partnership than CRP.

### Mathematical Tools
- ☐ **EOQ:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2 D K / h}$
- ☐ **Total Cost at EOQ:** $\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{2 D K h}$
- ☐ **ROP (Deterministic):** $\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot L$
- ☐ **ROP (Probabilistic):** $\text{ROP} = \bar{d} \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$
- ☐ **Safety Stock:** $\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$
- ☐ **Square Root Law:** $\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$
- ☐ **Service Level → Z:** 90% → 1.28 | 95% → 1.65 | 97.5% → 1.96 | 99% → 2.33

### Real-World Examples
- ☐ **Walmart – P&G:** Classic VMI partnership in FMCG.
- ☐ **Barilla (Italy):** Vendor-managed grocery replenishment.
- ☐ **Toyota JIT:** Extreme VMI variant with hourly deliveries.

### Implementation Steps
- ☐ Partner selection → IT integration → Joint planning → Pilot → Full deployment → KPI review.

### Benefits
- ☐ Lower stockouts, lower safety stock, lower bullwhip effect, lower administrative cost, better service level.

### Challenges
- ☐ Trust, data sharing, IT cost, loss of retailer's autonomy, vendor's forecasting errors propagate.

### Key Exam Triggers
- ☐ If a question mentions "**POS data**" and "**automatic trigger**" → think **CRP**.
- ☐ If a question mentions "**vendor decides the order quantity**" → think **VMI**.
- ☐ If a question mentions "**multiple retail locations, centralization**" → think **Square Root Law + VMI savings**.
- ☐ If a question gives demand, ordering cost, holding cost, lead time, service level → solve with **EOQ + ROP** framework.

### One-Liner Definitions (For 1-mark sub-parts)
- ☐ **CRP:** "A system where POS data automatically triggers replenishment orders to a supplier."
- ☐ **VMI:** "A model where the vendor — not the retailer — decides when and how much to replenish."
- ☐ **Square Root Law:** "Pooling $n$ independent demands reduces the variability of aggregate demand by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$."

### Final Mantra
- ☐ **CRP shares data; VMI shares decisions.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collaborative Planning and Replenishment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT832 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Collaborative Planning and Replenishment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Collaborative Planning and Replenishment (CPR)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collaborative Planning and Replenishment (CPR)** is a Supply Chain Management (SCM) initiative originally developed by the **Voluntary Interindustry Commerce Standards (VICS)** association (now operating under **GS1 US**). CPR is a structured framework that enables trading partners — typically a *retailer* and its *supplier* — to jointly plan, forecast, replenish, and execute ordering activities using **shared demand signals, sales data, and inventory visibility**, thereby replacing traditional, siloed, order-based replenishment systems with cooperative, forecast-driven planning.

CPR is formally recognized as the **progenitor framework** of the more advanced **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment)** model, which extends CPR's principles across multi-tier supply networks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Neighborhood Vegetable Cooperative

Imagine two neighbors:
- **Neighbor A (Retailer)** runs a small grocery shop.
- **Neighbor B (Farmer)** supplies fresh vegetables.

In the **old system (Traditional Replenishment)**, Neighbor A would call Neighbor B *only when stock ran low* and place an order. Neighbor B had **no idea** about upcoming festivals, weather, or local events. Result: Either *too much* (wastage) or *too little* (stockouts).

In the **CPR system**, the two neighbors now:
1. Meet every Saturday over tea to **discuss expected demand** (festivals, weddings, rain).
2. **Share** their calendars and customer footfall data.
3. **Plan together** what to grow, harvest, and deliver for the week.
4. Agree on **exception triggers** ("If sales of tomatoes drop by 20%, call me before sending more").

This is CPR in essence — **shared visibility, joint planning, and exception-based execution**.

## 1.3 Why CPR Was Created

> [!NOTE]
> Traditional replenishment suffers from the **Bullwhip Effect** — small fluctuations in consumer demand amplify upstream, causing excessive inventory, stockouts, and inefficiencies. CPR was created to **dampen the Bullwhip Effect** through inter-firm collaboration.

## 1.4 The Eight Foundational Principles of CPR

| # | Principle | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Consumer/Customer Focus** | Plan based on *actual end-consumer* demand, not internal bias. |
| 2 | **Joint Business Planning** | Retailer and supplier jointly set goals, strategies, and metrics. |
| 3 | **Joint Forecasting & Planning** | A **single shared forecast** replaces separate, conflicting forecasts. |
| 4 | **Exception-Based Management** | Only **abnormal events** are flagged; routine items auto-proceed. |
| 5 | **Data Synchronization** | Both partners use **the same clean, consistent data** (e.g., via UCCnet/GS1 GDSN). |
| 6 | **Joint Performance Metrics** | KPIs are **shared**, not adversarial (e.g., On-Time-In-Full, Forecast Accuracy). |
| 7 | **Collaborative Execution** | The replenishment **order is generated jointly**, not unilaterally. |
| 8 | **Continuous Improvement** | Partners regularly review and refine their CPR process. |

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Definitions)

- **Retailer** — The downstream trading partner (e.g., a supermarket chain).
- **Supplier/Manufacturer** — The upstream trading partner producing the goods.
- **Replenishment** — The act of refilling inventory to meet anticipated demand.
- **Trading Partner Agreement (TPA)** — A formal pact defining the rules, roles, and tools of the CPR engagement.
- **Shared Data** — POS (Point-of-Sale) data, inventory levels, promotion calendars, and forecasts exchanged between partners.
- **VICS** — Voluntary Interindustry Commerce Standards, the body that birthed CPR guidelines.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board exams, **always define CPR in one sentence using the phrase "joint planning and shared visibility"** — examiners actively reward this precise vocabulary.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The CPR Process Flow — A Layered Breakdown

CPR is not a single event; it is a **continuous, cyclical process** broken into four interlocking stages. Each stage has clear inputs, activities, and outputs.

### 🔷 Stage 1: Joint Business Planning (Strategic Layer)

- **Time Horizon:** Typically **annual**, revisited quarterly.
- **Who:** Senior management of both retailer and supplier.
- **Activities:**
  - Agree on **joint objectives** (e.g., 15% reduction in out-of-stocks, 10% inventory reduction).
  - Define **category roles** (e.g., "Your brand is the category captain for dairy").
  - Establish **shared KPIs** and **Trading Partner Agreement (TPA)**.
- **Output:** A signed **Joint Business Plan** with annual targets.

### 🔷 Stage 2: Joint Forecasting & Planning (Tactical Layer)

- **Time Horizon:** **Weekly to monthly** rolling cycles.
- **Who:** Demand planners, category managers from both sides.
- **Activities:**
  - Retailer shares **POS data, promotion plans, and store-level events**.
  - Supplier contributes **production capacity, lead times, and constraints**.
  - Both sides build a **consensus forecast**.
- **Output:** A single, **unified forecast** that drives downstream replenishment.

### 🔷 Stage 3: Collaborative Replenishment (Operational Layer)

- **Time Horizon:** **Daily to weekly**.
- **Who:** Replenishment analysts and order management teams.
- **Activities:**
  - System generates **projected orders** based on shared forecast + inventory positions.
  - Both parties review the order recommendation.
  - **Order exceptions** (e.g., a sudden demand spike) are flagged for human review.
- **Output:** **Jointly approved replenishment order** sent to the supplier's ERP.

### 🔷 Stage 4: Collaborative Execution & Continuous Improvement

- **Activities:**
  - **Monitor** order fulfillment (On-Time-In-Full, fill rate).
  - **Measure** against agreed KPIs.
  - **Review** weekly/monthly to refine the forecast model.
  - **Iterate** the Joint Business Plan at the next planning cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The transition from *Stage 2 → Stage 3* is where the **Bullwhip Effect is most significantly dampened**, because the order is no longer a *reaction* to a depleted warehouse but a *projection* based on shared demand insight.

## 2.2 The Replenishment Logic — Quantitative Lens

CPR is not purely a soft, relational framework; it rests on concrete replenishment math. The order recommendation in Stage 3 is computed using an **Inventory Position Model**.

### 2.2.1 The Core Replenishment Equation

The **Projected Order Quantity (POQ)** is calculated as:

$$
POQ = D_{L+LT} - IP + SS
$$

Where:

- $D_{L+LT}$ = Forecasted demand over the **Lead Time + Review Period**
- $IP$ = Current **Inventory Position** (On-hand + On-order − Backorders)
- $SS$ = **Safety Stock** buffer for demand variability

### 2.2.2 Safety Stock (SS) Formula

$$
SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = Z \cdot \sqrt{LT \cdot \sigma_d^{\,2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{\,2}}
$$

Where:

- $Z$ = Service-level Z-score (e.g., **1.65 for 95%** service, **2.33 for 99%**)
- $\sigma_{LT}$ = Standard deviation of demand during lead time
- $\sigma_d$ = Standard deviation of daily/period demand
- $\sigma_{LT}$ = Standard deviation of lead time itself
- $\bar{d}$ = Average demand

> [!NOTE]
> Under CPR, the **forecast variance $\sigma_d$ shrinks** because both partners use the same consensus forecast. Smaller $\sigma_d$ → smaller $SS$ → **lower inventory carrying cost** — a direct monetary win.

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | Used In |
|--------|---------|--------------|---------|
| $POQ$ | Projected Order Quantity | Units / Cases | Stage 3 calculation |
| $D_{L+LT}$ | Demand over Review + Lead Time | Units / Cases | Order trigger |
| $IP$ | Inventory Position | Units / Cases | Net availability check |
| $SS$ | Safety Stock | Units / Cases | Buffer against variability |
| $Z$ | Service Level Z-Score | Dimensionless | Probability lookup |
| $\sigma_{LT}$ | Std. Dev. of Lead-Time Demand | Units | Variability modeling |
| $\sigma_d$ | Std. Dev. of Daily Demand | Units / Day | Demand variability |
| $\bar{d}$ | Average Daily Demand | Units / Day | Mean forecast |
| $LT$ | Lead Time | Days | Replenishment delay |
| $L$ | Review Period | Days | Order frequency |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

CPR math underpins:

- **ERP Auto-Replenishment Modules** (SAP, Oracle, MS Dynamics).
- **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** systems in retail and FMCG.
- **E-commerce fulfillment** at scale (Amazon, Flipkart use shared-forecast principles).
- **Pharmaceutical cold chains** where *out-of-stock* is life-threatening.

## 2.5 CPR vs. CPFR — A Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> KTU exams often test this difference directly. Do **not** confuse them.

| Dimension | **CPR** | **CPFR** |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| **Scope** | Bilateral (1 retailer ↔ 1 supplier) | Multi-tier, multi-echelon |
| **Origin** | VICS, 1990s | VICS, 1998 onward |
| **Stages** | 4 (Joint Plan → Forecast → Replenish → Improve) | 9 (more granular, includes *Sales Forecasting*, *Order Forecasting*, *Order Generation*) |
| **Complexity** | Moderate | High |
| **Data Sharing** | POS + Inventory | POS + Inventory + Promotions + Assortment + Capacity |

# 📙 Step-by-Step Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Model KTU Numericals)

> A regional supermarket **"FreshKart"** and its dairy supplier **"MilkWell"** are operating under a **CPR agreement**.
>
> **Given Data:**
> - Average daily demand of milk pouches: $\bar{d} = 400$ pouches/day
> - Standard deviation of daily demand: $\sigma_d = 50$ pouches/day
> - Lead time: $LT = 4$ days
> - Standard deviation of lead time: $\sigma_{LT} = 0.5$ days
> - Review period: $L = 2$ days
> - Desired service level: **95%** → $Z = 1.65$
> - Current on-hand inventory: $OH = 300$ pouches
> - On-order inventory: $OO = 200$ pouches
> - Backorders: $BO = 50$ pouches
>
> **Required:**
> 1. Calculate the **Inventory Position (IP)**.
> 2. Calculate the **Safety Stock (SS)**.
> 3. Calculate the **Projected Order Quantity (POQ)**.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

### ✅ Step 1: Calculate Inventory Position (IP)

The Inventory Position accounts for *all* stock that is potentially available, including stock already in transit.

$$
IP = OH + OO - BO
$$

$$
IP = 300 + 200 - 50
$$

$$
IP = 450 \text{ pouches}
$$

> **Interpretation:** FreshKart effectively has **450 pouches** of available inventory once in-transit stock arrives and backorders are netted off.

---

### ✅ Step 2: Calculate Forecasted Demand over Review + Lead Time

$$
D_{L+LT} = \bar{d} \cdot (L + LT)
$$

$$
D_{L+LT} = 400 \cdot (2 + 4)
$$

$$
D_{L+LT} = 400 \cdot 6 = 2400 \text{ pouches}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Over the combined **review + lead time window of 6 days**, demand is projected to be **2400 pouches**.

---

### ✅ Step 3: Calculate Safety Stock (SS)

Using the **combined variability formula** that accounts for both demand and lead-time variance:

$$
SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT\,demand}
$$

Where the standard deviation of demand over the lead time is:

$$
\sigma_{LT\,demand} = \sqrt{LT \cdot \sigma_d^{\,2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{\,2}}
$$

**Sub-step 3a:** Calculate the demand-variability term.

$$
LT \cdot \sigma_d^{\,2} = 4 \cdot (50)^2 = 4 \cdot 2500 = 10000
$$

**Sub-step 3b:** Calculate the lead-time-variability term.

$$
\bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{\,2} = (400)^2 \cdot (0.5)^2 = 160000 \cdot 0.25 = 40000
$$

**Sub-step 3c:** Sum and take the square root.

$$
\sigma_{LT\,demand} = \sqrt{10000 + 40000} = \sqrt{50000}
$$

$$
\sigma_{LT\,demand} \approx 223.607 \text{ pouches}
$$

**Sub-step 3d:** Apply the Z-score.

$$
SS = 1.65 \cdot 223.607
$$

$$
SS \approx 368.95 \approx 369 \text{ pouches}
$$

> **Interpretation:** FreshKart must hold **~369 pouches** as a buffer to maintain a 95% service level against demand and supply variability.

---

### ✅ Step 4: Calculate the Projected Order Quantity (POQ)

$$
POQ = D_{L+LT} - IP + SS
$$

$$
POQ = 2400 - 450 + 369
$$

$$
POQ = 2319 \text{ pouches}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Under the **CPR agreement**, the joint system recommends that FreshKart place an order for **2319 pouches** with MilkWell at the next review cycle.

---

## 3.3 Sensitivity Insight (Examiner's Favorite Follow-up)

> [!TIP]
> **Question:** *If the CPR program improves forecast accuracy such that $\sigma_d$ drops from 50 to 30 pouches/day, what is the new Safety Stock?*

**Recompute:**

$$
LT \cdot \sigma_d^{\,2} = 4 \cdot (30)^2 = 4 \cdot 900 = 3600
$$

$$
\sigma_{LT\,demand} = \sqrt{3600 + 40000} = \sqrt{43600} \approx 208.80
$$

$$
SS_{new} = 1.65 \cdot 208.80 \approx 344.5 \text{ pouches}
$$

**Savings:** $369 - 344.5 = 24.5$ pouches of safety stock eliminated per cycle.

> **Insight:** This is the **quantitative proof** that CPR reduces inventory carrying costs — a guaranteed 4–5 mark answer in KTU 14-mark problems.

## 3.4 Quick Numerical Recap Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Inventory Position | $IP$ | **450 pouches** |
| Forecasted Demand | $D_{L+LT}$ | **2400 pouches** |
| Safety Stock | $SS$ | **~369 pouches** |
| Projected Order Qty | $POQ$ | **2319 pouches** |
| SS After CPR Forecast Improvement | $SS_{new}$ | **~344.5 pouches** |
| Inventory Reduction | $\Delta SS$ | **24.5 pouches** |

# 🗂️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CPR Process Flow — Mermaid Schematic

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **four-stage CPR cycle** and its feedback loops. It is renderable in GitHub, GitLab, VS Code, and most modern markdown engines.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Joint Business Planning Annual Strategy and KPIs] --> B[Stage 2: Joint Forecasting and Planning Consensus Demand Build]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Collaborative Replenishment System Generates Projected Order]
    C --> D{Exception Triggered Sudden Spike or Drop}
    D -- Yes --> E[Manual Joint Review Exception Management]
    D -- No --> F[Auto Approval of Projected Order]
    E --> G[Stage 4: Execution Order Sent to Supplier ERP]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Monitor Order Fulfillment OTF and Fill Rate]
    H --> I[Measure Against Shared KPIs]
    I --> J[Continuous Improvement Review]
    J --> A
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- **A → B → C → G** is the *forward flow* (strategic → tactical → operational).
- **D** is the *exception node* — this is where the human judgment enters.
- **J → A** is the *feedback loop* — continuous improvement is what makes CPR adaptive, not static.

## 4.2 Data Synchronization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RPOS[Retailer POS Data] --> RDS[Retailer Data Sync Layer]
    RINV[Retailer Inventory] --> RDS
    RPROM[Retailer Promotion Calendar] --> RDS
    RDS --> SCP[Shared Collaborative Platform]
    SCAP[Supplier Capacity Plan] --> SCP
    SCONST[Supplier Constraints] --> SCP
    SCP --> ENG[Forecasting Engine]
    ENG --> FCST[Consensus Forecast]
    FCST --> ORDGEN[Order Recommendation Generator]
    ORDGEN --> REVIEW{Joint Review}
    REVIEW -- Approved --> SUPP_ERP[Supplier ERP]
    REVIEW -- Rejected --> ENG
```

**Functional Architecture Insight:**
- The **Shared Collaborative Platform (SCP)** is the digital heart of CPR — typically a **cloud-based portal** (e.g., SAP IBP, E2open, Kinaxis).
- The **Forecasting Engine** consumes *retailer data + supplier data* — a key reason CPR reduces the Bullwhip Effect.

## 4.3 CPR vs. Traditional Replenishment — Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD[Traditional Order Based]
        T1[Retailer Places Order] --> T2[Supplier Fulfills Blindly]
        T2 --> T3[Reactive Adjustments]
        T3 --> T1
    end
    subgraph COL[CPR Collaborative]
        C1[Shared Data Exchange] --> C2[Joint Forecast Build]
        C2 --> C3[Consensus Replenishment Plan]
        C3 --> C4[Joint Execution]
        C4 --> C5[Shared KPIs Review]
        C5 --> C1
    end
```

> **Key Architectural Difference:** In the *Traditional* model, information flows **one-way** (Retailer → Supplier) and is **reactive**. In CPR, information flows **bidirectionally** and is **anticipatory**.

## 4.4 Exception Management Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Monitor KPIs Fill Rate Forecast Error OOS Rate] --> N{Variance Exceeds Threshold}
    N -- No --> O[Continue Auto Replenishment]
    N -- Yes --> P[Trigger Exception Alert]
    P --> Q[Root Cause Analysis Joint Team]
    Q --> R{Resolution Type}
    R -- Demand Surge --> S[Emergency Production Ramp Up]
    R -- Forecast Error --> T[Forecast Model Recalibration]
    R -- Supply Issue --> U[Alternate Sourcing Activation]
    S --> V[Document Lesson in CPR Repository]
    T --> V
    U --> V
    V --> M
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Exception-Based Management** principle (Principle 4 of CPR) in action. The system does **not** flag every transaction — only statistically significant deviations.

# 🧪 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 📌 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** Define Collaborative Planning and Replenishment (CPR). State any four of its foundational principles. **[3 Marks]** *`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Bank]`*

**Model Answer:**

> **Collaborative Planning and Replenishment (CPR)** is a supply chain framework developed by the **Voluntary Interindustry Commerce Standards (VICS)** association that enables retailers and suppliers to jointly plan demand, share data, and execute replenishment based on a **consensus forecast**, replacing traditional siloed, order-based systems.

**Four Foundational Principles:**
1. **Consumer/Customer Focus** — Plans are anchored to actual end-consumer demand.
2. **Joint Forecasting and Planning** — A single shared forecast replaces conflicting individual forecasts.
3. **Exception-Based Management** — Only abnormal events require human review.
4. **Data Synchronization** — Both partners operate on the same clean, standardized data (e.g., via GS1 standards).

---

### **Q2.** Differentiate between CPR and CPFR. Why was CPR considered the precursor to CPFR? **[3 Marks]** *`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **CPR** | **CPFR** |
|---------|---------|----------|
| Scope | Bilateral (1 retailer ↔ 1 supplier) | Multi-tier, multi-echelon |
| Complexity | 4-stage process | 9-stage process |
| Data Exchange | POS + Inventory | POS + Inventory + Promotions + Capacity + Assortment |
| Industry Adoption | FMCG, Retail (initial) | Broader (Pharma, Electronics, Apparel) |

**Why CPR is the Precursor:**
CPR introduced the **fundamental shift** from reactive, order-based replenishment to proactive, forecast-based collaboration. CPFR **builds upon** this by extending collaboration across multiple supply-chain tiers, adding more granular stages (such as *Sales Forecasting* and *Order Generation Forecasting* as separate steps), and incorporating a wider data scope.

---

## 📌 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**. Both sub-parts must be answered in sequence.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four stages of the CPR process** with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of the **Trading Partner Agreement (TPA)** in Stage 1. **[7 Marks]** *`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`*

**Model Solution:**

The CPR process consists of **four interconnected stages** that operate as a continuous cycle:

**Stage 1 — Joint Business Planning (Strategic Layer):**
- Conducted at the **senior management level**, typically on an **annual cycle** with quarterly reviews.
- Both partners align on **shared business objectives** (e.g., reduce stockouts by 15%, increase category sales by 10%).
- They sign a **Trading Partner Agreement (TPA)** that formally defines:
  - Roles and responsibilities of each partner.
  - Scope of collaboration (SKUs, categories, regions).
  - Data-sharing protocols and confidentiality terms.
  - Joint KPIs and their measurement methodology.
  - Conflict-resolution mechanisms.
- **Output:** A *Joint Business Plan* with annual targets.

**Stage 2 — Joint Forecasting and Planning (Tactical Layer):**
- Conducted at the **planner level** on a **weekly or monthly rolling cycle**.
- Retailer shares: **POS data, store-level promotions, local events, and assortment changes**.
- Supplier shares: **production capacity, raw-material availability, lead-time commitments**.
- Both teams collaborate to build a **single consensus forecast**.
- **Output:** A *consensus demand forecast* feeding into replenishment.

**Stage 3 — Collaborative Replenishment (Operational Layer):**
- Conducted at the **replenishment analyst level**, daily or weekly.
- The collaborative system computes the **Projected Order Quantity (POQ)** using:

$$
POQ = D_{L+LT} - IP + SS
$$

- Both partners review the order recommendation.
- **Exceptions** (deviations beyond agreed thresholds) trigger joint human review.
- **Output:** A *jointly approved replenishment order*.

**Stage 4 — Execution, Monitoring & Continuous Improvement:**
- The approved order flows into the supplier's ERP for fulfillment.
- Partners monitor **On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)**, **fill rate**, **forecast accuracy**, and **inventory turns**.
- Periodic **business reviews** identify process gaps and feed improvements back into Stage 1.

```
Stage 1 → Stage 2 → Stage 3 → Stage 4
                                  ↓
                            (Feedback Loop)
                                  ↓
                              Stage 1
```

**(b)** Discuss the **role of data synchronization** in CPR. Explain how the **GS1 GDSN (Global Data Synchronization Network)** supports this. **[7 Marks]** *`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`*

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Data Synchronization in CPR:**

Data synchronization is the **fifth foundational principle** of CPR and is critical because:

1. **Single Source of Truth:** Both retailer and supplier refer to the **same master data** — SKU codes, descriptions, dimensions, pricing, and case-pack configurations.
2. **Eliminates Mismatch Errors:** Without synchronization, a single SKU listed as "12 units/case" by the supplier but "10 units/case" by the retailer creates **order and shipment errors**.
3. **Enables Automated Replenishment:** Standardized data allows **machine-to-machine (M2M)** communication between ERP systems.
4. **Enriches Forecasting:** Clean attribute data (e.g., product hierarchy, brand, category) improves demand-pattern recognition in the forecast engine.
5. **Enables Exception Detection:** When data is synchronized, *anomalies* (e.g., a sudden 30% sales drop) are real signals — not artifacts of a data-entry error.

**GS1 GDSN — How It Works:**

The **Global Data Synchronization Network (GDSN)** is the world's largest product data network, operated by **GS1**, providing:

| GDSN Component | Function |
|----------------|----------|
| **GS1 Global Registry** | Central index of all products and their data sources. |
| **Data Pools** | Certified repositories (e.g., **1WorldSync, NielsenIQ**) where suppliers publish and retailers subscribe to product data. |
| **GDSN Standards** | Defined XML message formats (e.g., **GDSN Trade Item**, **GDSN Price**) for data exchange. |
| **GS1 Keys** | Unique identifiers — **GTIN (Global Trade Item Number)**, **GLN (Global Location Number)**, **SSCC (Serial Shipping Container Code)**. |

**Workflow:**
1. Supplier publishes product data to a certified **GDSN Data Pool**.
2. The **GS1 Global Registry** indexes the data.
3. The retailer's subscribed Data Pool retrieves the data automatically.
4. Both parties operate on **identical, up-to-date information**.

> **Outcome:** GDSN makes CPR's Principle 5 (**Data Synchronization**) operationally feasible at scale, enabling **Coca-Cola, Procter & Gamble, Walmart, and Tesco** to run CPR programs with thousands of suppliers seamlessly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** With a suitable numerical example, explain how the **Projected Order Quantity (POQ)** is computed under a CPR replenishment system. Clearly state the formula and the meaning of each variable. **[7 Marks]** *`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`*

**Model Solution:**

Under CPR, replenishment is **forecast-driven**, and the order quantity is determined by a **mathematical inventory model** that balances forecasted demand, current inventory position, and a safety buffer.

**Formula:**

$$
POQ = D_{L+LT} - IP + SS
$$

**Variable Definitions:**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $POQ$ | Projected Order Quantity (units to be ordered this cycle) |
| $D_{L+LT}$ | Forecasted demand over the Review Period ($L$) plus Lead Time ($LT$) |
| $IP$ | Inventory Position = On-hand + On-order − Backorders |
| $SS$ | Safety Stock = Buffer for demand and lead-time variability |

**Worked Example:**

> A retailer and supplier have agreed on the following CPR parameters:
> - $\bar{d} = 300$ units/day (average daily demand)
> - $\sigma_d = 40$ units/day (demand std. deviation)
> - $LT = 5$ days (lead time)
> - $\sigma_{LT} = 1$ day (lead time std. deviation)
> - $L = 3$ days (review period)
> - $Z = 1.65$ (95% service level)
> - $OH = 200$ units (on-hand), $OO = 400$ units (on-order), $BO = 0$ units

**Step 1: Compute Inventory Position**

$$
IP = OH + OO - BO = 200 + 400 - 0 = 600 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Forecasted Demand**

$$
D_{L+LT} = \bar{d} \cdot (L + LT) = 300 \cdot (3 + 5) = 300 \cdot 8 = 2400 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Safety Stock**

$$
\sigma_{LT\,demand} = \sqrt{LT \cdot \sigma_d^{\,2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{\,2}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{5 \cdot 1600 + 90000 \cdot 1} = \sqrt{8000 + 90000} = \sqrt{98000} \approx 313.05
$$

$$
SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT\,demand} = 1.65 \cdot 313.05 \approx 516.5 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4: Compute POQ**

$$
POQ = D_{L+LT} - IP + SS = 2400 - 600 + 516.5 = 2316.5 \text{ units}
$$

**Recommendation:** The CPR system will suggest ordering **2317 units** (rounded up) at the next review cycle.

---

**(b)** What is the **Bullwhip Effect**? Explain how CPR helps in **dampening** it. Provide **three quantitative benefits** of CPR adoption. **[7 Marks]** *`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`*

**Model Solution:**

**The Bullwhip Effect:**

The **Bullwhip Effect** refers to the phenomenon where **small variations in end-consumer demand** progressively amplify as they move upstream through the supply chain — from retailer → wholesaler → manufacturer → supplier. This results in:

- Excessive **safety stock** at each tier.
- Frequent **stockouts** and lost sales.
- **Inflated** production and inventory carrying costs.
- **Erosion of forecast accuracy** at each echelon.

**How CPR Dampens the Bullwhip Effect:**

| CPR Mechanism | Dampening Effect |
|---------------|------------------|
| **Shared POS Data** | Supplier sees *real* end-demand, not distorted order data. |
| **Joint Consensus Forecast** | Single forecast replaces multi-echelon forecasting errors. |
| **Exception-Based Ordering** | Prevents over-reaction to small demand fluctuations. |
| **Reduced Lead-Time Variance** | Joint planning stabilizes production schedules. |
| **Data Synchronization (GDSN)** | Eliminates errors that would otherwise trigger false demand signals. |

**Three Quantitative Benefits of CPR:**

1. **Reduction in Safety Stock:** As demonstrated in earlier worked examples, improved forecast accuracy from CPR can reduce safety stock by **15–30%**, directly lowering carrying costs.

2. **Improved Forecast Accuracy:** Industry case studies (e.g., **Walmart–P&G**) show forecast accuracy improvements from ~60% (traditional) to ~85–90% (CPR), a **40% relative improvement**.

3. **Lower Order-to-Delivery Cycle Time:** With synchronized data and joint planning, **order processing time** drops by 30–50%, and **On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)** rates climb from ~80% to ~95%.

4. **Inventory Turnover Increase:** Many CPR adopters report a **20–50% increase in inventory turnover**, freeing up working capital.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
>
> In 14-mark CPR questions, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by:
> 1. **Confusing CPR with CPFR** — Use the correct framework name as per the question.
> 2. **Omitting the Trading Partner Agreement (TPA)** when explaining Stage 1 — TPA is a *direct KTU-board mark-grabber*.
> 3. **Writing POQ formula without variable definitions** — Always define every symbol in a formula question; definitions carry marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the service-level Z-score** when calculating safety stock — Examiners deduct marks for using an arbitrary multiplier instead of a Z-score.
> 5. **Failing to mention GS1/GDSN** when discussing data synchronization — this is a current, KTU 2024-scheme-relevant industry standard and is expected.
> 6. **Writing "CPR helps reduce costs"** without quantification — Always include a numeric or percentage-based benefit; vague statements lose marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **CPR = Collaborative Planning and Replenishment** — a VICS-born framework.
- ✅ CPR is the **direct precursor to CPFR** (do not interchange).
- ✅ **Four stages:** Joint Business Plan → Joint Forecast → Collaborative Replenishment → Execution & Improvement.
- ✅ **Eight principles:** Consumer Focus, Joint Business Planning, Joint Forecasting, Exception Management, Data Synchronization, Joint KPIs, Collaborative Execution, Continuous Improvement.
- ✅ **TPA (Trading Partner Agreement)** is signed in Stage 1 — defines rules, KPIs, and conflict resolution.
- ✅ **Core formula:** $POQ = D_{L+LT} - IP + SS$
- ✅ **Safety Stock:** $SS = Z \cdot \sqrt{LT \cdot \sigma_d^{\,2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{LT}^{\,2}}$
- ✅ **Service Level Z-values to memorize:** 90% → 1.28, 95% → 1.65, 97.5% → 1.96, 99% → 2.33.
- ✅ **GS1 GDSN** enables Principle 5 (Data Synchronization) using GTIN, GLN, and SSCC keys.
- ✅ **Bullwhip Effect** is the primary problem CPR solves.
- ✅ CPR **dampens** bullwhip via shared POS, consensus forecast, and exception-based ordering.
- ✅ **Quantitative benefits:** 15–30% SS reduction, 40% forecast accuracy gain, 20–50% inventory turnover increase.
- ✅ **Bidirectional data flow** distinguishes CPR from traditional one-way ordering.
- ✅ **VICS** (now GS1 US) is the originating body — frequently asked as a 1-mark factual.
